name: HUMAN AND ORGANIZATIONAL FACTORS IN SAFETY

topics.title: Occupational Safety and Industrial Safety
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT524 - HUMAN AND ORGANIZATIONAL FACTORS IN SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Occupational Safety and Industrial Safety # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Occupational Safety** is the multidisciplinary discipline concerned with preserving and protecting human and facility resources in the workplace through the prevention of accidents, injuries, illnesses, and fatalities. It encompasses the systematic identification, evaluation, and control of workplace hazards arising from physical, chemical, biological, ergonomic, and psychosocial sources. **Industrial Safety** is a specialized subdomain of occupational safety that focuses specifically on the safety of workers, machinery, processes, and the environment within manufacturing, construction, processing, and heavy engineering industries. It deals with the management of operational risks associated with industrial activities. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Module 1.1):** According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, occupational safety and industrial safety form the foundational framework upon which Human and Organizational Factors in Safety (PESFT524) is constructed — without these baseline concepts, advanced topics like safety culture, human reliability, and organizational behaviour cannot be understood. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition > [!TIP] > **The "House Analogy" for Occupational Safety:** > Imagine your body is a house. Occupational safety is the **fire alarm, security system, fire extinguishers, and structural inspections** you install to keep the house (your body and the workplace) safe from damage. > - The **house** = the workplace and workers > - The **fire alarm** = safety procedures and PPE > - The **locks on doors** = engineering controls and machine guards > - The **insurance policy** = the safety policy and emergency response plan > - **Ignoring safety** is like leaving the front door open during a storm — you may be safe today, but the first time a hazard walks in, damage is guaranteed. ### Real-World Example A worker in a chemical plant without proper Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) is like a soldier going into battle without body armor. The probability of harm from a chemical splash, falling object, or heat exposure is exponentially higher without proper safety systems. --- ## 1.3 Core Safety Terminology (KTU High-Yield Definitions) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mandatory KTU Glossary — Memorize these terms for the ESE:** | Term | Definition | |---|---| | **Hazard** | Any source, situation, or act with the potential to cause harm in terms of human injury or ill-health, damage to property, damage to the environment, or a combination of these. | | **Risk** | The combination of the probability of an occurrence of a hazardous event or exposure and the severity of injury or ill-health that can be caused by such event or exposure. | | **Accident** | An undesired, unplanned event that results in harm to people, damage to property, or loss to process. | | **Incident** | An occurrence arising out of or in the course of work that could or does result in injury, ill-health, or fatality. | | **Near Miss** | An event that did not result in injury, illness, or damage but had the potential to do so. | | **Unsafe Act** | A violation of an accepted safe procedure or standard practice. | | **Unsafe Condition** | A physical condition in the workplace that can cause or contribute to an accident. | | **PPE** | Personal Protective Equipment — devices worn by workers to minimize exposure to specific occupational hazards. | --- ## 1.4 Why Occupational & Industrial Safety Matter > [!NOTE] > **The Three Pillars of Safety Justification (KTU Module 1 Theme):** > 1. **Moral Obligation** — Every worker has a right to a safe working environment (ILO Convention 155). > 2. **Legal Compliance** — The Factories Act 1948, BOCW Act 1996, OSH Code 2020 (India). > 3. **Economic Necessity** — Accidents cost companies money through direct costs (medical, compensation) and indirect costs (lost production, reputational damage). The standard **safety metric** in industry is the **Accident Frequency Rate (AFR)**: $$ \text{AFR} = \frac{\text{Total number of accidents} \times 1{,}000{,}000}{\text{Total man-hours worked}} $$ The **global benchmark** for AFR in heavy industry is **< 2.0**; the **world-class** target is **< 0.5**. --- ## 1.5 Scope of Industrial Safety (KTU 2024 Outline) > [!IMPORTANT] > The KTU 2024 syllabus frames the scope of industrial safety around four overlapping domains: > 1. **Mechanical Safety** — Machines, tools, conveyors, lifting equipment > 2. **Electrical Safety** — Wiring, earthing, lockout/tagout (LOTO) > 3. **Chemical Safety** — Hazardous substances, MSDS, fire protection > 4. **Ergonomic & Behavioural Safety** — Human factors, posture, work design # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 The Theoretical Foundation — Three Pillars of Industrial Safety Industrial safety rests upon three theoretical pillars. Each pillar has a specific contribution to the overall safety system: ### Pillar 1: Engineering (Inherent Safety) - **Order of Priority:** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering Controls → Administrative Controls → PPE - **KTU Mnemonic:** "**E-S-E-A-P**" — **E**liminate, **S**ubstitute, **E**ngineer, **A**dminister, **P**rotect. - **Goal:** Build the hazard OUT of the system. Example: Replacing a toxic solvent with a water-based one. ### Pillar 2: Administrative - **Definition:** Procedural controls that reduce the frequency or duration of exposure. - **Examples:** Job rotation, training, signage, work permits, safety inspections. - **Effectiveness:** Generally **lower than engineering controls** because they depend on human compliance. ### Pillar 3: Behavioural (PPE) - **Definition:** The last line of defence after the hazard has been identified and other controls have been applied. - **Effectiveness:** Lowest reliability, but critical when other controls fail. > [!TIP] > **The "Swiss Cheese Model" of Accident Causation (Reason, 1990):** > Imagine each safety layer as a slice of Swiss cheese. Each layer has holes (latent failures and active failures). An accident occurs only when the holes in all layers momentarily align, allowing a hazard to reach the worker. Safety management's job is to **reduce the size and number of holes in each slice**. --- ## 2.2 Accident Causation Theories (KTU Module 1 — High Yield) ### 2.2.1 Heinrich's Domino Theory (1931) Heinrich proposed that **88% of accidents are caused by unsafe acts of people, 10% by unsafe conditions, and 2% by acts of God**. The theory uses a five-domino model: > [!NOTE] > **Heinrich's Five Dominoes:** > **Domino 1:** Ancestry and Social Environment (heredity, upbringing) > **Domino 2:** Fault of Person (carelessness, recklessness) > **Domino 3:** Unsafe Act / Mechanical / Physical Hazard > **Domino 4:** Accident (the direct event) > **Domino 5:** Injury (the consequence) **Theory Conclusion:** Removing **Domino 3** (the unsafe act or condition) prevents the chain reaction. Hence, the focus must be on **correcting unsafe acts and unsafe conditions**. ### 2.2.2 Bird & Germain's Loss Causation Model (1985) This updated model (used in modern KTU curriculum) shows that **management system failures** are the root cause of 85% of accidents, not worker behaviour directly. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Modern KTU questions (2023–2024) emphasize Bird's model because it aligns with the **human and organizational factors** course focus. Heinrich's model is older but still tested as a baseline. ### 2.2.3 Reason's Swiss Cheese Model (1990) Already explained above — relevant for **organizational safety culture** and **high-reliability organizations (HROs)**. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Safety Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — KTU Module 1** | Formula | Equation | Unit | Application | |---|---|---|---| | Accident Frequency Rate (AFR) | $\text{AFR} = \dfrac{N \times 10^6}{H}$ | per million man-hours | Industry-wide frequency tracking | | Accident Severity Rate (ASR) | $\text{ASR} = \dfrac{L \times 10^6}{H}$ | days lost per million man-hours | Severity of injuries | | Incidence Rate (IR) | $\text{IR} = \dfrac{N \times 10^5}{W}$ | per 100,000 workers | Population-based risk | | Safety Pyramid Ratio | $1 : 30 : 300 : 3000$ | fatal : serious : minor : near-miss | Heinrich's Triangle | | Total Recordable Incident Rate (TRIR) | $\text{TRIR} = \dfrac{\text{Recordable injuries} \times 200{,}000}{H}$ | per 100 FTE workers | OSHA global benchmark | | Fatal Accident Rate (FAR) | $\text{FAR} = \dfrac{F \times 10^8}{H}$ | per 100 million man-hours | High-risk industries | **Where:** - $N$ = Number of accidents - $H$ = Total man-hours worked - $L$ = Total man-days lost - $W$ = Average number of workers - $F$ = Number of fatalities > [!WARNING] > **CRITICAL KTU Formula Pitfall:** Always use the **standard multiplier** ($10^6$ or $10^8$) as specified in the question. Mixing up the multiplier (using $10^5$ where $10^6$ is required) is the **#1 reason students lose marks** in safety engineering numericals. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility **Case Study — Bhopal Disaster (1984):** The Bhopal gas tragedy is the world's worst industrial accident. **40+ tonnes** of methyl isocyanate (MIC) leaked from Union Carbide's plant, killing **3,800+ people** immediately and affecting **500,000+** long-term. Root cause: **management failure** in maintenance, training, and emergency response — not worker error. This single event transformed industrial safety legislation worldwide, leading to India's **Manufacture, Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules, 1989** and the OSH Code 2020. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Connection:** Bhopal is a frequently asked case study in Module 1 and Module 5 of PESFT524. Always mention the **organizational and management failure** as the root cause, not just the technical leak. --- ## 2.5 Industrial Safety Hierarchy of Controls The hierarchy of controls (used by NIOSH, OSHA, and KTU syllabus) ranks safety interventions by effectiveness: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hierarchy of Controls (Most → Least Effective):** > 1. **Elimination** — Physically remove the hazard (e.g., stop using benzene) > 2. **Substitution** — Replace with less hazardous material (e.g., benzene → toluene) > 3. **Engineering Controls** — Machine guards, ventilation, interlocks > 4. **Administrative Controls** — Training, signage, work-rest cycles > 5. **PPE** — Respirators, helmets, gloves (last line of defence) **KTU Module 1 Insight:** The most effective safety systems are designed at the **engineering level**, not the **behavioural level**. PPE is the least reliable because it depends on continuous human compliance. # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example #1: Accident Frequency Rate > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A manufacturing plant employs 250 workers working 8-hour shifts, 6 days a week. During the year, there were 4 reportable accidents resulting in a total of 45 lost workdays. Calculate the **AFR**, **ASR**, and **IR** assuming the plant operates 50 weeks per year. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Calculate total man-hours worked per year** $$ H = \text{Workers} \times \text{Hours/day} \times \text{Days/week} \times \text{Weeks/year} $$ $$ H = 250 \times 8 \times 6 \times 50 $$ $$ H = 250 \times 8 = 2000 \text{ worker-hours/day} $$ $$ H = 2000 \times 6 = 12{,}000 \text{ worker-hours/week} $$ $$ H = 12{,}000 \times 50 = 600{,}000 \text{ man-hours/year} $$ **Conversion logic:** We multiply all exposure dimensions (workers × hours × days × weeks) to obtain the cumulative exposure time during which accidents could occur. --- **Step 2 — Calculate Accident Frequency Rate (AFR)** $$ \text{AFR} = \frac{N \times 10^6}{H} $$ $$ \text{AFR} = \frac{4 \times 10^6}{600{,}000} $$ $$ \text{AFR} = \frac{4{,}000{,}000}{600{,}000} $$ $$ \text{AFR} = 6.67 \text{ per million man-hours} $$ **Conversion logic:** AFR expresses the number of accidents normalized to a standard exposure of one million man-hours. The ratio $4/600{,}000$ is multiplied by $10^6$ to convert to the standard unit. --- **Step 3 — Calculate Accident Severity Rate (ASR)** $$ \text{ASR} = \frac{L \times 10^6}{H} $$ $$ \text{ASR} = \frac{45 \times 10^6}{600{,}000} $$ $$ \text{ASR} = \frac{45{,}000{,}000}{600{,}000} $$ $$ \text{ASR} = 75 \text{ man-days lost per million man-hours} $$ **Conversion logic:** ASR captures the **severity dimension** of accidents (lost workdays) on the same standardized exposure scale. A high ASR with low AFR indicates few but serious accidents. --- **Step 4 — Calculate Incidence Rate (IR)** $$ \text{IR} = \frac{N \times 10^5}{W} $$ $$ \text{IR} = \frac{4 \times 10^5}{250} $$ $$ \text{IR} = \frac{400{,}000}{250} $$ $$ \text{IR} = 1600 \text{ per 100,000 workers} $$ **Conversion logic:** IR expresses accidents per 100,000 workers, allowing comparison between plants of different sizes. --- > [!TIP] > **Interpretation:** The AFR of **6.67** is **3.3 times higher** than the world-class benchmark of **2.0**. This plant has serious safety deficiencies that need root-cause investigation. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example #2: Heinrich's Safety Pyramid > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A company recorded **2 fatalities** in 2024. Using Heinrich's pyramid ratio, estimate the number of **serious injuries**, **minor injuries**, and **near-misses** that should have occurred. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Apply Heinrich's Pyramid Ratio** $$ 1 : 30 : 300 : 3000 \quad \text{(Fatal : Serious : Minor : Near-Miss)} $$ **Conversion logic:** Heinrich's empirical research showed that for every fatal injury, approximately 30 serious injuries, 300 minor injuries, and 3000 near-misses occur in the workplace. --- **Step 2 — Calculate each level of the pyramid** $$ \text{Serious injuries} = 2 \times 30 = 60 $$ $$ \text{Minor injuries} = 2 \times 300 = 600 $$ $$ \text{Near-misses} = 2 \times 3000 = 6000 $$ **Conversion logic:** Each tier of the pyramid is a constant multiple of the tier above it, reflecting the statistical distribution of accident severity in industry. --- > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Modern research (Bird, 2003) has updated the ratio to **1 : 10 : 30 : 600** for higher-precision reporting. The KTU syllabus accepts both classical Heinrich (1931) and updated Bird ratios — always state which one you are using. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Accident Causation Theories > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Module 1 — Theory Comparison Table (Required for 14-mark questions)** | Aspect | Heinrich (1931) | Bird & Germain (1985) | Reason Swiss Cheese (1990) | |---|---|---|---| | **Focus** | Worker behaviour | Management systems | Organizational failure | | **Dominoes/Layers** | 5 dominos | 6 dominos | Multiple layers with holes | | **Root Cause** | Unsafe acts/conditions | Lack of management control | Latent + active failures | | **Worker Blame** | 88% of accidents | 15% of accidents | Conditional on defences | | **Intervention Level** | Worker training | Management system design | Organizational culture | | **Modern Relevance** | Outdated but historic | Industry standard | HROs, aviation, healthcare | | **KTU Module** | Module 1.2 | Module 1.2 | Module 4 (Safety Culture) | --- ## 3.4 Derivation of Indirect-to-Direct Cost Ratio > [!NOTE] > **Heinrich's Iceberg Theory of Accident Cost:** > The visible (direct) cost of an accident is only the "tip of the iceberg." The hidden (indirect) costs are **4 to 10 times** the direct cost. **Mathematical representation:** $$ C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{direct}} + C_{\text{indirect}} $$ $$ C_{\text{indirect}} = k \cdot C_{\text{direct}}, \quad \text{where } k \in [4, 10] $$ $$ C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{direct}} + k \cdot C_{\text{direct}} = (1 + k) \cdot C_{\text{direct}} $$ **Example:** If a workplace injury has a direct medical cost of ₹50,000, with $k = 6$: $$ C_{\text{total}} = (1 + 6) \times 50{,}000 = 7 \times 50{,}000 = \text{₹}3{,}50{,}000 $$ **Conversion logic:** The 4–10× multiplier accounts for lost productivity, equipment damage, investigation time, training replacement workers, insurance premium hikes, and reputational damage. # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Heinrich's Domino Theory (Accident Causation) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Domino 1
