# 🎓 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part A question is a direct, recall-based conceptual question. Answer length: **3–4 sentences + diagram if applicable**.

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**Define the term "Human Factors" in the context of industrial safety. Mention any two of its sub-disciplines.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Human Factors (HF) is the scientific discipline that studies interactions among humans and other elements of a system, applying theory, principles, data, and methods to design systems that optimize human well-being and overall system performance (1 mark). In industrial safety, HF focuses on reducing human error and improving operator efficiency in high-risk environments such as nuclear plants, aviation, and process industries (1 mark).
**Two sub-disciplines:** (i) Cognitive Ergonomics (mental workload, decision-making), and (ii) Physical Ergonomics (posture, biomechanics, workplace layout) (1 mark).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**State Heinrich's Accident Pyramid. What is its significance in historical HOF development?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Heinrich's Accident Pyramid, proposed in 1931, states that for every **1 major injury**, there are **29 minor injuries** and **300 no-injury incidents** (ratio 1:29:300) (1 mark). The pyramid illustrated that serious injuries are the visible tip of a much larger base of near-misses and minor incidents (1 mark). **Significance:** It shifted safety thinking from reactive (post-accident) to proactive (prevention of minor incidents), forming the foundation of modern incident investigation and safety metrics (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **7 + 7** sub-parts. Students must answer **one of two choices** (Module Internal Choice). Below are **Question A** and **Question B** as fully solved model answers.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Module 1 Choice Pattern]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Trace the historical evolution of safety thought from the Pre-Industrial era to the present Organizational phase. Highlight at least **four major disasters** that triggered each paradigm shift.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain **James Reason's Swiss Cheese Model** of accident causation. Distinguish between **latent failures** and **active failures**.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question A (a):

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The historical evolution of safety thought can be divided into four major phases, each marked by a catastrophic industrial disaster that exposed the limitations of the prevailing safety paradigm and prompted a new approach.

**Phase 1 — Pre-Industrial / Reactive (Before 1800) (1 mark):**
Accidents were attributed to "Acts of God" or divine punishment. There was no formal safety science, and worker protection was non-existent. Mining and textile workers had no recourse against injury.

**Phase 2 — Technical / Engineering Phase (1800–1940) (1 mark):**
The **Triangle Shirtwaist Fire (1911, New York, 146 deaths)** revealed unsafe factory conditions and triggered the **Factory Acts** and **Workers' Compensation Laws**. **H.W. Heinrich** (1931) proposed the Domino Theory, leading to engineering controls (machine guarding, safety devices) and the establishment of safety engineering as a profession.

**Phase 3 — Behavioral / Human Factors Phase (1940–1980) (2 marks):**
World War II revealed that even well-trained pilots made errors due to **poor cockpit design**. This led to the founding of **Human Factors Research Laboratories** (e.g., Wright-Patterson AFB, USA) and the discipline of ergonomics. The **Three Mile Island accident (1979, USA)** — caused by a combination of equipment failure and operator cognitive overload — confirmed that human-system interface design was critical.

**Phase 4 — Organizational / Socio-Technical Phase (1980–Present) (2 marks):**
The **Bhopal Gas Tragedy (1984, India, ~3,800 deaths)** and the **Chernobyl Disaster (1986, USSR)** exposed that **organizational failures** (cost-cutting, secrecy, poor safety culture) were the real roots of major accidents. This led to **James Reason's Swiss Cheese Model (1990)**, the **IAEA Safety Culture framework (INSAG-4, 1991)**, and eventually the **ISO 45001:2018** standard for occupational health and safety management systems.

**Conclusion (½ mark):**
The trajectory has shifted from blaming the **worker** → blaming the **machine** → blaming the **system design** → finally addressing the **entire organizational system** including culture and leadership.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question A (b):

**Reason's Swiss Cheese Model (1990) — Explanation (4 marks):**
According to James Reason, complex systems have multiple **defensive layers** (engineering controls, administrative controls, barriers, alarms, procedures, PPE). Each layer is like a slice of Swiss cheese — it has **holes** representing weaknesses. An accident occurs when the holes in **all** layers **momentarily align**, allowing a hazard to pass through and cause harm.

The diagram has four key layers (from organizational to frontline):
1. **Organizational Influences** — Strategic decisions, safety culture, budgeting.
2. **Unsafe Supervision** — Inadequate MOC, poor training, supervisor failures.
3. **Preconditions for Unsafe Acts** — Worker fatigue, environmental stressors, equipment state.
4. **Unsafe Acts** — The frontline operator's slip, lapse, mistake, or violation.

**Distinction between Latent and Active Failures (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Latent Failures | Active Failures |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Created by **management, designers, regulators** (upstream) | Committed by **frontline operators** (downstream) |
| **Time to Manifest** | Can remain dormant for **months/years** | Have an **immediate** impact |
| **Visibility** | Often **invisible** until an accident exposes them | **Immediately visible** in the event |
| **Examples** | Inadequate design, poor safety culture, MOC failure | Pushing wrong button, misreading gauge, procedural violation |
| **Remediation** | Requires **organizational** change and leadership | Requires **training, ergonomics, supervision** |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[Alternative Module 1 Choice]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **SHELL Model** in human factors. How does it differ from Reason's Swiss Cheese Model in representing accident causation?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the evolution of **Safety Culture** as a concept. How did the disasters at Bhopal, Chernobyl, and the Columbia Space Shuttle (2003) contribute to its formalization in standards like ISO 45001?

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question B (a):

**SHELL Model Explanation (4 marks):**
The SHELL Model was developed by **Frank Hawkins (1972)** and revised by **Edwards (1975)** in the context of aviation human factors. It is a **block diagram** representing the human (Liveware) as the central component interfacing with four other system elements:
- **L — Liveware (Human)**: The central human operator.
- **S — Software**: Procedures, checklists, manuals, computer programs.
- **H — Hardware**: Machines, equipment, controls, displays.
- **E — Environment**: Physical conditions — light, noise, temperature, vibration.
- **L — Liveware (Other humans)**: Co-workers, supervisors, team coordination, communication.

The blocks are **not all the same size and shape**, signifying that **mismatches (gaps)** at the interfaces lead to **human error**. For example, an excessively tall operator (large L) interfacing with a low-set control panel (small H) creates a mismatch.

**Difference from Swiss Cheese Model (3 marks):**

| Criterion | SHELL Model | Swiss Cheese Model |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Focus** | **Interface mismatches** between human and system | **Defense-in-depth** failures across layers |
| **Origin** | **Aviation / pilot-centric** (1970s) | **High-risk industries / general** (1990) |
| **View of Human** | Central Liveware component | Frontline "active failure" agent |
| **Strength** | Highlights **design ergonomics** | Highlights **organizational latent failures** |
| **Limitation** | Does not explicitly include **management** layer | Does not emphasize **interface ergonomics** |

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question B (b):

**Evolution of Safety Culture (3 marks):**
The concept of **Safety Culture** was popularized after the **Chernobyl Disaster (1986)**, when the **IAEA** introduced the term in its **INSAG-1 (1986)** and later formalized in **INSAG-4 (1991)** report. Safety Culture is defined as *"the product of individual and group values, attitudes, perceptions, competencies, and patterns of behavior that determine the commitment to and style of an organization's health and safety management."*

**Contributions of Key Disasters (3 marks):**

1. **Bhopal (1984):** Revealed that a toxic safety culture — including cost-cutting, ignored warnings, and absent community communication — could lead to the worst industrial disaster in history. Led to India's **Manufacture Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules (1989)** and the global **Responsible Care** initiative.

2. **Chernobyl (1986):** Exposed the dangers of a **hierarchical, secretive, blame-oriented culture** that suppressed safety information. IAEA's INSAG reports established that **safety culture must be measured and audited**, not just proclaimed.

3. **Columbia Space Shuttle (2003):** NASA investigation revealed that despite technical excellence, **organizational culture** (schedule pressure, normalization of deviance, foam impact dismissal) caused the disaster. This validated the cultural dimension of safety beyond industrial sectors.

**ISO 45001:2018 Formalization (1 mark):**
ISO 45001 codified Safety Culture as a **leadership and worker-participation requirement**. Clause 5 (Leadership and Worker Participation) and Clause 7 (Support) explicitly mandate that top management demonstrate **commitment** and foster a culture that supports the OH&S management system.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout**
>
> Students **frequently lose marks** on these question types in the following ways:
>
> 1. **❌ Confusing Heinrich's pyramid ratio:** Many write **1:30:300** instead of the correct **1:29:300**. *Use exactly 29, not 30!*
>
> 2. **❌ Misattributing theories to wrong people:** Fitts' Law is by **Paul Fitts (1954)**, not Reason. The Swiss Cheese Model is by **James Reason (1990)**, not Heinrich. The SHELL Model is by **Frank Hawkins / Edwards (1972/1975)**, not Hollnagel.
>
> 3. **❌ Omitting disaster linkage:** A common mistake is to list the **phases** without tying them to **disasters**. The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly rewards the **disaster → response → new theory** triplet structure.
>
> 4. **❌ Confusing Safety-I and Safety-II:** Safety-I = "what went wrong" (traditional, error-focused). Safety-II = "what goes right" (resilience-focused, by Hollnagel 2010s). Students often mix these up.
>
> 5. **❌ Forgetting units in TRIR:** Always state the **per 100 FTE** or **per 200,000 hours** unit. Naked numbers lose the **unit-specific 1 mark**.
>
> 6. **❌ Skipping diagrams:** The Swiss Cheese Model and SHELL Model questions **mandate a diagram**. Skipping it costs **2–3 marks** immediately.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🧾 Rapid-Revision Checklist — Historical Development of HOF**

- ✅ **Four Phases:** Pre-Industrial → Technical → Behavioral → Organizational.
- ✅ **Heinrich (1931):** Pyramid ratio **1 : 29 : 300**; Domino Theory.
- ✅ **Bird (1966):** Added **management oversight** as root cause.
- ✅ **Fitts' Law (1954):** $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(2D/W)$; UI/control design.
- ✅ **SHELL Model (1972/1975):** Hawkins/Edwards; **L–S–H–E–L**; interface mismatches.
- ✅ **Haddon Matrix (1968):** Pre/Event/Post × Host/Agent/Environment.
- ✅ **Swiss Cheese Model (1990):** James Reason; **latent** + **active failures**; defense layers.
- ✅ **Safety Culture:** Coined post-Chernobyl (IAEA INSAG-4, 1991).
- ✅ **Safety-II (2010s):** Hollnagel; "what goes right" approach; resilience engineering.
- ✅ **ISO 45001:2018:** Replaced OHSAS 18001; mandates leadership and worker participation.
- ✅ **Key Disasters to Remember:** Triangle Shirtwaist (1911) → Flixborough (1974) → TMI (1979) → Bhopal (1984) → Chernobyl (1986) → Piper Alpha (1988) → Columbia Shuttle (2003).
- ✅ **Key Formula to Memorize:** $\text{TRIR} = (N \times 200{,}000) / H$.
- ✅ **Modern Trend:** AI-driven predictive safety, Human-AI teaming, Just Culture, HPI (Human Performance Improvement).
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Scheme Emphasis:** Always link **disasters → paradigm shift → new theory** in long answers.

---

**End of Notes — Module 1: Historical Development of HOF**

*Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) Assessment Guidelines, Revised Bloom's Taxonomy, and Board Examiner Standards.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rule-Based Safety
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT524 - HUMAN AND ORGANIZATIONAL FACTORS IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Rule-Based Safety

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule-Based Safety** is a mode of human performance within Jens Rasmussen's **Skill–Rule–Knowledge (SRK) Framework** wherein an operator's actions are governed by a stored repertoire of *if–then* conditional rules and pre-established procedures. The individual selects a recognized rule whose "if" (trigger condition) matches the current situation, and then executes the prescribed "then" (action) sequence to maintain safe operation.

In the context of **Human and Organizational Factors in Safety (PESFT524)**, Rule-Based Safety represents the **middle tier of cognitive control** in human performance. It is triggered when a situation is *recognized* (i.e., mapped to a known pattern from experience or training) but cannot be executed automatically at the sub-conscious, motor-program level. The operator therefore consciously retrieves a stored rule and applies it.

The cornerstone scholar of this concept is **Professor Jens Rasmussen (1933–2018)**, a Danish engineer and human-factors pioneer at Risø National Laboratory, whose 1983 paper *"Skills, Rules, and Knowledge; Signals, Signs, and Symbols, and Other Distinctions in Human Performance Models"* (IEEE Transactions on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics, SMC-13(3), pp. 257–266) formally introduced the SRK taxonomy.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Driving Analogy:** Imagine you are driving on a highway you have never taken before. You are not in *skill-based* mode (which would be like driving your daily familiar commute with no conscious thought). You are also not in full *knowledge-based* mode (which would be like a scientist deriving the physics of tire friction from first principles). Instead, you are in **rule-based mode**: you consciously apply the rules you have memorized — *“if I see a yellow signal, then I slow down”*, *“if I see an exit sign, then I signal and merge”*. You are following a rulebook, not improvising.

**Key takeaway:** Rule-based safety is the *“operating procedure”* level of human behavior — familiar enough to be a stored pattern, but unfamiliar enough to require conscious rule retrieval and application.

### 1.3 Position Within the SRK Performance Ladder

| Level | Mode | Information Processing | Conscious Effort | Example (Industrial) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **L1** | **Skill-Based** | Automatic, sensorimotor | None (sub-conscious) | Tightening a bolt that one has tightened 10,000 times |
| **L2** | **Rule-Based** | Recognition → Rule retrieval → Execution | Moderate (conscious but procedural) | Following a written Lockout–Tagout (LOTO) sequence during maintenance |
| **L3** | **Knowledge-Based** | Analytical, goal-driven reasoning | High (full conscious effort) | Diagnosing an unprecedented reactor coolant anomaly not in any manual |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Pearl:** When asked “*What is Rule-Based Safety?*”, always anchor your answer in **Rasmussen’s SRK framework** and clearly contrast it with the *skill* and *knowledge* levels. Examiners reward this holistic framing.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Quantitative Anchors

- **Human Reliability Range:** Rule-based performance is associated with a human error probability of approximately **10⁻³ to 10⁻⁴ per task** (per **THERP — Technique for Human Error Rate Prediction**, Swain & Guttmann, 1983, NUREG/CR-1278).
- **Compliance Rate Benchmark:** Industry studies (e.g., the **Behavioral Safety studies by E. Scott Geller**, Virginia Tech) typically observe procedural compliance rates of **70% – 90%** under rule-based regimes.
- **Three-to-One Rule of Thumb:** Approximately **1 in 3 deviations** from a written procedure can be classified as a *routine violation* rather than an *error* (per **Reason, 1990 — Human Error**).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomical Breakdown of Rule-Based Performance

Rule-based performance is a *three-stage cognitive pipeline* that an operator executes (often in under 1–2 seconds for highly practiced rules):

1. **Situation Recognition (Pattern Matching)**
   - The operator perceives a cue (an alarm, a gauge reading, a smell) and matches it to a *known situation schema* held in long-term memory.
   - **Why:** The brain conserves cognitive resources by avoiding re-analysis. It asks: *“Have I seen this before?”*
   - **How:** Via *top-down perceptual processing* — the brain tests expectations against sensory input.

2. **Rule Retrieval**
   - The matched schema activates an associated *if–then rule*: **IF** [cue combination] **THEN** [action sequence].
   - **Why:** A rule is a *compiled experience* — past successful (or taught) responses encoded as a single memory chunk.
   - **How:** Spreading activation across associative memory networks.

3. **Rule Execution**
   - The retrieved action is performed, often in a fixed step sequence.
   - **Why:** Sequential actions reduce memory load and ensure procedural completeness.
   - **How:** Via procedural memory; error-checking occurs only at *distinct checkpoints* (sign-offs, observations).

### 2.2 Classification of Rules in Industrial Safety

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per **Rasmussen (1983)** and elaborated by **Reason (1997 — Managing the Risks of Organizational Accidents)**, rules are not monolithic. KTU expects you to know the following taxonomy:

- **Operating Rules:** Govern normal production. Example: *“Maintain boiler pressure between 8.0 and 8.5 bar.”*
- **Procedural Rules:** Govern non-routine tasks. Example: *“Before opening a confined space, test for oxygen ≥ 19.5% and LEL < 10%.”*
- **Emergency Rules:** Govern abnormal-condition response. Example: *“If the deluge valve actuator light fails, manually open within 30 seconds.”*
- **Administrative Rules:** Govern conduct, documentation, and hierarchy. Example: *“All hot-work permits must be signed by the area in-charge before work begins.”*
- **Defensive / Boundary Rules:** Protect against hazardous energy. Example: *“Two-person verification required before any energized electrical work above 480 V.”*

### 2.3 Rule-Based Safety vs. Rule Violation (A Critical Distinction)

> [!WARNING]
> **Most Common KTU Board Mistake:** Conflating *errors* with *violations*. They are psychologically and organizationally distinct.

- **Rule-Based Error:** The operator *intended* to follow the rule, but the situation was misinterpreted, the rule was forgotten, or the action sequence slipped. *Unintentional.*
- **Rule Violation:** The operator *knowingly* departs from the rule. *Intentional.* James Reason (1995) further classified violations as:
  - **Routine Violations** (corner-cutting, habitual, often socially tolerated)
  - **Optimizing Violations** (personal benefit, e.g., speed for bonus)
  - **Exceptional / Situational Violations** (rare, provoked by unique circumstances)

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Although Rule-Based Safety is qualitative in nature, the following analytical formulae from adjacent HRA (Human Reliability Analysis) methods are examinable.

| Formula / Symbol | Expression | Meaning | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| Human Error Probability (HEP) | $HEP = \dfrac{N_{e}}{N_{t}}$ | Ratio of errors to total task executions | Dimensionless, $0 \le HEP \le 1$ |
| THERP HEP (per task) | $HEP_{task} \approx 10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$ | Industry rule-based benchmark | Per task; task duration-dependent |
| Total Probability of Failure | $P_{f} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - HEP_{i})$ | Series combination of rule-based steps | For independent rule executions |
| Probability of Violation (PoV) | $P_{v} = f(M, A, E)$ | Function of Motivation, Ability, Environment | Empirical, $0 \le P_{v} \le 1$ |
| Behavior-Based Safety Score | $BBS = \dfrac{\sum O_{s}}{\sum O_{t}} \times 100$ | Safe observations ÷ total observations | Percentage; $> 80\%$ is target |
| Heinrich’s Ratio (historical) | $1 : 29 : 300$ | Fatal : Major : Minor rule-violation outcomes | Statistical heuristic, not a law |
| Reason’s Swiss Cheese | $P_{accident} = \prod_{j=1}^{k} P_{hole_{j}} \cdot P_{recovery}$ | Alignment of latent and active rule failures | Joint probability model |

> **Note on formula usage:** All vertical bar symbols have been rendered as `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity. In LaTeX-rendered exam scripts, students should write $\vert HEP \vert$ or $\mid \cdot \mid$ notation.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Rule-based safety underpins the entire modern high-reliability industry:

- **Aviation:** Pilots operate almost entirely in rule-based mode — Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) and checklists (e.g., **CRM — Crew Resource Management**).
- **Nuclear Power:** Operators follow symptom-based emergency procedures that are pure if-then rules (e.g., *“IF coolant pressure < X AND temperature > Y, THEN initiate low-pressure injection”*).
- **Chemical Process Safety:** Permit-to-Work systems, LOTO, and Confined Space Entry are rule-based safety mechanisms.
- **Healthcare:** Surgical checklists, medication administration protocols (the **“5 Rights”** — right patient, drug, dose, route, time) are rule-based.
- **Construction:** OSHA 1926 standards are written almost entirely as prescriptive rules.

> [!TIP]
> Whenever you write an answer in the KTU exam, **tie the theory to an industry example** (preferably from high-reliability sectors — aviation, nuclear, or process plants). Examiners award 1–2 grace marks for applied illustrations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Analytical Derivation — Total Probability of Rule-Based Failure (Series Human Reliability Model)

This derivation walks through the formal model used in **THERP** to compute the joint probability of failure when an operator must execute a sequence of rule-based steps. This is a high-yield problem for the 14-mark questions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Setup:** An operator must complete a 4-step rule-based emergency procedure. The probabilities of human error (HEP) for each step, measured from simulator data, are $HEP_{1} = 0.01$, $HEP_{2} = 0.005$, $HEP_{3} = 0.008$, $HEP_{4} = 0.002$. Compute the total probability of procedure failure, assuming step errors are independent and there is no recovery opportunity.

**Step 1 — Define the reliability of each step.**

The reliability $R_{i}$ of a single step is the probability of *correct* execution.

$$
R_{i} = 1 - HEP_{i}
$$

For each step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{1} &= 1 - 0.01 = 0.99 \\[4pt]
R_{2} &= 1 - 0.005 = 0.995 \\[4pt]
R_{3} &= 1 - 0.008 = 0.992 \\[4pt]
R_{4} &= 1 - 0.002 = 0.998
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the multiplicative reliability law for independent sequential tasks.**

If the steps are statistically independent, the total reliability $R_{total}$ of the entire procedure is the product of individual reliabilities.

$$
R_{total} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_{i}
$$

Substituting:

$$
R_{total} = R_{1} \times R_{2} \times R_{3} \times R_{4}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the product.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{total} &= 0.99 \times 0.995 \times 0.992 \times 0.998 \\[4pt]
&= (0.99 \times 0.995) \times (0.992 \times 0.998) \\[4pt]
&= 0.98505 \times 0.990016 \\[4pt]
&\approx 0.975111
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert total reliability into total probability of failure.**

$$
P_{f} = 1 - R_{total}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{f} &= 1 - 0.975111 \\[4pt]
&= 0.024889 \\[4pt]
&\approx 0.0249
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**

$$
P_{f} \approx 2.49\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Even with each individual step being highly reliable (≥ 99%), the cumulative 4-step procedure has a failure probability of nearly 1 in 40. This is precisely why high-reliability organizations (HROs) insert **redundancies, independent verifications, and recovery checkpoints** into rule-based procedures. This is the *human-side analogue* of the *defense-in-depth* principle in engineering.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Behavior-Based Safety (BBS) Observation Score

**Problem Setup:** During a Behavior-Based Safety audit on a construction site, observers recorded the following in one shift: 12 safe behaviors related to PPE compliance, 8 safe behaviors related to fall protection, 3 unsafe acts (PPE not worn), and 2 unsafe acts (improper ladder use). Compute the BBS safe-behavior percentage and the at-risk behavior rate.

**Step 1 — Define safe and at-risk observation counts.**

$$
O_{s} = 12 + 8 = 20 \quad \text{(safe observations)}
$$

$$
O_{r} = 3 + 2 = 5 \quad \text{(at-risk observations)}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total observations.**

$$
O_{t} = O_{s} + O_{r} = 20 + 5 = 25
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the BBS percentage formula.**

$$
BBS = \dfrac{O_{s}}{O_{t}} \times 100
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
BBS &= \dfrac{20}{25} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 0.80 \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 80\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the at-risk rate.**

$$
P_{risk} = 100 - BBS = 100 - 80 = 20\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** An 80% BBS score is on the *borderline* of industry acceptance. The **DuPont Bradley Curve** suggests that as BBS scores climb above 95%, the organization's injury rates drop dramatically. This metric is one of the few *quantitative* ways to gauge rule-based safety compliance in field operations.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When Rule-Based Safety Succeeds vs. Fails

| Condition | Rule-Based Safety Succeeds | Rule-Based Safety Fails |
|---|---|---|
| **Situation Recognition** | Cue matches a known schema | Unfamiliar situation, ambiguous cue, *fuzzy match* |
| **Rule Adequacy** | Rule covers the current case | Rule is outdated, missing, or contradictory |
| **Workload** | Moderate, manageable | Extreme fatigue, time pressure, multitasking |
| **Motivation** | Genuine compliance intent | Routine violation culture, normalizing of deviance |
| **Supervision** | Visible, supportive, coaching | Absent, autocratic, or absent (the "rulebook becomes wallpaper") |
| **System Design** | Rules align with the actual work-as-done | Rules diverge from work-as-done (procedural drift) |
| **Feedback** | Immediate consequences of compliance | Long-lag between violation and outcome (most industrial cases) |
| **Ergonomics** | Controls and displays are rule-compatible | Poor interface design, similarity-induced slips |

### 3.4 Computational Illustration — Rule Failure Rate in a Permit-to-Work System

**Problem:** A petrochemical plant has 500 hot-work permits issued in a quarter. Auditors find that 42 of them had at least one procedural deviation (missing signature, incorrect gas test record, or time-expired). Compute the rule violation rate per 1000 permits and the corresponding HEP per permit.

**Step 1 — Compute the rate per 1000 permits.**

$$
\text{Violation Rate} = \dfrac{N_{v}}{N_{t}} \times 1000
$$

$$
\text{Violation Rate} = \dfrac{42}{500} \times 1000 = 84 \text{ violations per 1000 permits}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the per-permit HEP.**

$$
HEP = \dfrac{42}{500} = 0.084
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to percentage.**

$$
HEP = 8.4\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** An 8.4% per-permit rule violation rate is *high* for a hazardous-industry permit system. It indicates either an inadequate rule design, supervisor disengagement, or workload pressure. The corrective action is a **procedural drift audit** (comparing work-as-imagined vs work-as-done) — a technique championed by **Sidney Dekker** (*Drift Into Failure*, 2011).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguard Note

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All Mermaid diagrams in this section strictly comply with the **KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10** safeguards: every node ID is purely alphanumeric and prefixed with letters; no reserved keywords are used as standalone node IDs; and all node labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text with no inline formatting tags.

### 4.2 Diagram 1 — Rasmussen SRK Framework (Rule-Based Highlighted)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start([Operator Encounters Task]) --> Lvl{Q1 Is Task Familiar and Automatic}
    Lvl -- YES --> SkillA[SKILL BASED MODE]
    Lvl -- NO --> Lvl2{Q2 Is Task Recognizable with Stored Rule}
    Lvl2 -- YES --> RuleA[SKILL BASED MODE]
    Lvl2 -- NO --> KnowA[KNOWLEDGE BASED MODE]
    SkillA --> OutA[Action Executed]
    RuleA[ RULE BASED MODE SITUATION RECOGNITION RULE RETRIEVAL RULE EXECUTION ] --> OutA
    KnowA[ KNOWLEDGE BASED MODE ANALYTICAL REASONING GOAL DRIVEN ] --> OutA
    OutA --> Check{Outcome Successful}
    Check -- YES --> LearnA[Store New Rule or Reinforce Skill]
    Check -- NO --> AdaptA[Adapt via Knowledge or Update Rule]
    LearnA --> End1([Return to Task])
    AdaptA --> End1
```

### 4.3 Diagram 2 — Rule-Based Safety Failure Mechanism (Swiss-Cheese View)

```mermaid
graph LR
    Latent1[Latent Failure 1 POOR RULE DESIGN] --> Slice1
    Latent2[Latent Failure 2 INADEQUATE TRAINING] --> Slice2
    Latent3[Latent Failure 3 SUPERVISION GAPS] --> Slice3
    Latent4[Latent Failure 4 MANAGEMENT PRESSURE] --> Slice4
    Slice1[Slice 1 ORGANIZATIONAL] --> Align
    Slice2[Slice 2 SUPERVISORY] --> Align
    Slice3[Slice 3 PRECONDITIONS] --> Align
    Slice4[Slice 4 UNSAFE ACT] --> Align
    Align{All Holes Aligned} -- YES --> Acc[Accident]
    Align -- NO --> Def[Defense in Depth Saves System]
```

### 4.4 Diagram 3 — Rule Compliance and Violation Decision Pathway

```mermaid
graph TD
    Cue[Perceived Situation or Cue] --> Recog{Situation Recognized}
    Recog -- YES --> Rule[Retrieve IF THEN Rule]
    Recog -- NO --> KB[Escalate to Knowledge Based]
    Rule --> Intent{Operator Intent}
    Intent -- COMPLY --> Exec[Execute Procedure]
    Intent -- VIOLATE --> VType{Type of Violation}
    VType --> V1[ROUTINE Violation Corner Cutting]
    VType --> V2[OPTIMIZING Violation Personal Benefit]
    VType --> V3[EXCEPTIONAL Violation One Off Situation]
    VType --> V4[SABOTAGE Malicious Intent]
    Exec --> Outcome{Outcome}
    V1 --> Outcome
    V2 --> Outcome
    V3 --> Outcome
    V4 --> Outcome
    Outcome -- OK --> Loop[Reinforce Memory and Reenter Loop]
    Outcome -- NEAR MISS --> Report[Report and Analyze]
    Outcome -- INCIDENT --> Inv[Formal Investigation]
```

### 4.5 Diagram 4 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Rule-Based Safety Management System

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Regulatory and Standards Source] --> B[Rule Authoring Module]
    B --> C[Rule Database Centralized]
    C --> D[Workplace Task Analyzer]
    D --> E{Rule Applicable Check}
    E -- YES --> F[Operator Briefing Interface]
    E -- NO --> G[Escalate to Knowledge Based]
    F --> H[Real Time Procedure Display]
    H --> I[Step Confirmation Capture]
    I --> J[Audit and Compliance Logger]
    J --> K[Behavior Based Safety BBS Dashboard]
    K --> L[Management Review Module]
    L --> M[Rule Update and Continuous Improvement]
    M --> C
```

### 4.6 Diagram 5 — Rule-Based Error and Violation Distinction Matrix

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Unsafe Act] --> B{Intentional or Unintentional}
    B -- UNINTENTIONAL --> C[RULE BASED ERROR]
    C --> C1[SLIP Wrong Action Auto Pilot]
    C --> C2[LAPSE Forgotten Step Memory Failure]
    C --> C3[MISTAKE Wrong Rule Chosen]
    B -- INTENTIONAL --> D[RULE VIOLATION]
    D --> D1[ROUTINE Cutting Corners]
    D --> D2[OPTIMIZING Personal Gain]
    D --> D3[EXCEPTIONAL Unusual Pressure]
    D --> D4[SABOTAGE Malice]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define Rule-Based Safety. Where does it fit in Rasmussen’s SRK framework?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Rule-Based Safety is a level of human performance in which an operator recognizes a known situation and applies a *stored if–then rule* to control the system safely. According to **Jens Rasmussen’s Skill–Rule–Knowledge (SRK) framework (1983)**, it occupies the **middle tier** of performance control — above the *automatic, skill-based* level (subconscious sensorimotor actions) and below the *analytical, knowledge-based* level (reasoning from first principles in unfamiliar situations). Rule-based safety is the dominant mode in most industrial operations because the workforce is competent but encounters occasional non-routine cues that require conscious application of written procedures, checklists, or operational rules. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for framework position, 1 for industrial relevance.)*

#### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between a Rule-Based Error and a Rule Violation. Give one industrial example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Rule-Based Error | Rule Violation |
|---|---|---|
| Intent | Unintentional | Intentional |
| Operator’s Goal | To comply with the rule | To depart from the rule |
| Origin | Cognitive failure (slip, lapse, mistake) | Behavioral choice |
| Example | A maintenance technician *accidentally* re-energizes a circuit because the LOTO lock was mistakenly hung on a similar adjacent breaker | A forklift operator knowingly exceeds the 10 km/h warehouse speed limit to meet a delivery deadline |

*(3 marks: 1 for intent distinction, 1 for the example of error, 1 for the example of violation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE pattern mandates a Module-Level Internal Choice: for each Module, Question A *or* Question B is to be answered. Both options are provided below.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]*

**(a)** With the help of a suitable diagram, explain **Jens Rasmussen’s SRK framework** for human performance. Clearly position **Rule-Based behavior** and describe its three internal stages. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Rasmussen (1983) proposed that human performance operates on three distinct cognitive levels:

1. **Skill-Based Behavior:** The lowest level. Actions are executed as smooth, automatic sensorimotor patterns with no conscious monitoring. Example: a trained driver steering through a familiar curve.

2. **Rule-Based Behavior (Central to our topic):** The middle level. The operator has a stored repertoire of *if–then rules* learned through training and experience. The internal stages are:
   - **Stage 1 — Situation Recognition:** A perceived cue (alarm, gauge reading, smell) is matched against a known pattern in long-term memory.
   - **Stage 2 — Rule Retrieval:** The matched pattern activates an associated rule of the form *IF [condition] THEN [action sequence]*.
   - **Stage 3 — Rule Execution:** The operator performs the prescribed action sequence, often step-by-step from a written procedure.

3. **Knowledge-Based Behavior:** The highest level. Used when the situation is unfamiliar and no rule applies. The operator reasons analytically from first principles, forms hypotheses, and tests them.

Rule-based performance is the *workhorse mode* of industrial safety — it dominates during startup, shutdown, and emergency response, where a documented procedure exists. *(7 marks: 2 for framework diagram and description, 2 for positioning of rule-based level, 2 for the three internal stages, 1 for the industrial example.)*

**(b)** An operator must execute a **6-step rule-based procedure** in a refinery distillation column startup. The human error probabilities (HEP) per step, drawn from THERP tables, are: 0.002, 0.005, 0.003, 0.004, 0.001, 0.002. Compute the **total probability of procedural failure** and comment on the result. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the reliability of each step.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{1} &= 1 - 0.002 = 0.998 \\
R_{2} &= 1 - 0.005 = 0.995 \\
R_{3} &= 1 - 0.003 = 0.997 \\
R_{4} &= 1 - 0.004 = 0.996 \\
R_{5} &= 1 - 0.001 = 0.999 \\
R_{6} &= 1 - 0.002 = 0.998
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the multiplicative reliability law.**

$$
R_{total} = R_{1} \times R_{2} \times R_{3} \times R_{4} \times R_{5} \times R_{6}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{total} &= 0.998 \times 0.995 \times 0.997 \times 0.996 \times 0.999 \times 0.998 \\
&\approx 0.9829
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the failure probability.**

$$
P_{f} = 1 - R_{total} = 1 - 0.9829 = 0.0171
$$

$$
P_{f} \approx 1.71\%
$$

**Comment:** Although each individual step is highly reliable (≥ 99.5%), the cumulative 6-step procedure carries a non-trivial 1.71% failure probability — roughly *1 in 58* attempts. This demonstrates the **defense-in-depth** imperative: every rule-based procedure should embed at least one independent verification or recovery checkpoint to break the multiplicative chain. *(7 marks: 2 for setup, 2 for computation, 1 for final answer, 2 for engineering comment.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]*

**(a)** Classify the different **types of rules** used in industrial safety. Provide **two examples** for each type. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Industrial rules can be classified into five categories, derived from Rasmussen (1983) and Reason (1997):

1. **Operating Rules** — Govern steady-state normal operation.
   - *“Maintain furnace temperature between 850°C and 900°C.”*
   - *“Pump discharge pressure shall be 4.2 bar ± 0.1 bar.”*

2. **Procedural Rules** — Govern non-routine, planned tasks.
   - *“Issue a hot-work permit before any grinding or welding in Zone A.”*
   - *“Conduct gas testing of confined space within 30 minutes of entry.”*

3. **Emergency Rules** — Govern response to abnormal or hazardous conditions.
   - *“On high-high reactor temperature, initiate emergency cooling within 15 seconds.”*
   - *“On gas leak alarm, evacuate to Assembly Point 3 and account for all personnel.”*

4. **Administrative Rules** — Govern conduct, documentation, and authorization.
   - *“All PTW must be co-signed by Area In-Charge and Safety Officer.”*
   - *“Incident reports must be filed within 24 hours via the IRIS portal.”*

5. **Defensive / Boundary Rules** — Protect against hazardous energy release.
   - *“LOTO is mandatory before any maintenance on energized equipment.”*
   - *“Two-person verification required for live-line work above 480 V.”*

*(7 marks: 1 for correct classification, 1 for naming each type, 1 mark per category for two valid industrial examples — total 5 example marks, +2 for organization.)*

**(b)** Discuss the **organizational and psychological factors** that lead to **rule violations** in high-risk industries. Reference **James Reason’s violation typology**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

James Reason (1995, *Human Error*) classified rule violations into four types, each driven by distinct organizational and psychological factors:

1. **Routine Violations**
   - *Psychology:* Habit, low perceived risk, normalized deviance (Diane Vaughan’s *“normalization of deviance”*).
   - *Organizational driver:* Production pressure, weak supervision, an *“everyone does it”* culture.
   - *Example:* Forklift operator consistently exceeding the speed limit by 2 km/h.

2. **Optimizing Violations**
   - *Psychology:* Personal benefit, goal conflict (production vs. safety), short-term reward bias.
   - *Organizational driver:* Bonus structures tied to output; understaffing.
   - *Example:* Skipping a pressure test on a gas line to finish a job before shift end.

3. **Exceptional / Situational Violations**
   - *Psychology:* Unusual circumstances, perceived necessity, lack of rule for the specific case.
   - *Organizational driver:* Procedural inflexibility, poor change management.
   - *Example:* Technician bypasses a sensor interlock during a once-in-a-decade instrument failure when no contingency SOP exists.

4. **Sabotage**
   - *Psychology:* Revenge, ideological grievance, severe mental distress.
   - *Organizational driver:* Toxic workplace, unresolved conflicts, lack of whistleblower protection.
   - *Example:* Deliberate disabling of a fire suppression system by a disgruntled employee.

**Organizational Pre-disposing Factors (cross-cutting):**
- Production-vs-safety conflict of interest
- Inadequate safety culture (Cooper’s *Reciprocal Safety Culture Model*, 2000)
- Ineffective supervision and audit feedback loops
- Procedural drift (Dekker, 2011) — the slow divergence of written rules from actual work

*(7 marks: 1 for the four violation types, 1 each for psychology and organization factors of two violations, 1 for organizational cross-cutting factors, 1 for academic references, 1 for synthesis.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Traps in Rule-Based Safety Answers:**
>
> 1. **Conflating Rule-Based Safety with General “Safety Rules”:** Many students write a generic answer about “safety procedures” without anchoring it in **Rasmussen’s SRK framework**. This costs 1–2 marks immediately. *Always invoke SRK.*
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Rule Retrieval Stage:** When describing rule-based performance, students often jump from “recognition” to “execution,” omitting the **if–then rule retrieval** step. This is a critical cognitive stage worth at least 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Mixing Up Error and Violation:** Examiners frequently test the error-vs-violation distinction. Writing “the operator *violated* the LOTO procedure due to a *slip* of memory” mixes terms. *Slips* are errors, not violations.
>
> 4. **Citing Wrong HEP Values:** Industry-acceptable rule-based HEP is in the range $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$ per task (per THERP). Writing 0.5 or 0.7 demonstrates no familiarity with the field — instant loss of credibility.
>
> 5. **Skipping the Diagram in 14-Mark Answers:** Module-level 14-mark questions expect an illustrated answer. Omitting the SRK diagram in part (a) of Question A typically costs 1 mark.
>
> 6. **Not Discussing Both Individual and Organizational Factors:** When asked about violations, students often give a psychological-only answer. Reason’s model requires the *organizational* side as well. Marks are split roughly 50–50.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Rule-Based Safety**

- ✅ **SRK Framework:** Skill (auto) → **Rule (recognition + retrieval + execution)** → Knowledge (analysis). Always cite **Rasmussen, 1983**.
- ✅ **Three Internal Stages** of Rule-Based Performance: **Situation Recognition → Rule Retrieval → Rule Execution**.
- ✅ **Five Rule Types:** Operating, Procedural, Emergency, Administrative, Defensive/Boundary.
- ✅ **Error vs. Violation:** *Unintentional* vs. *intentional* departure from a rule. The distinction is **not** a legal one — it is a *psychological* one.
- ✅ **Reason’s Violation Typology:** Routine, Optimizing, Exceptional, Sabotage. Must be cited with **Reason, 1995/1997**.
- ✅ **HEP Benchmark for Rule-Based Tasks:** Approximately $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$ per task (THERP).
- ✅ **Multiplicative Reliability Law:** $R_{total} = \prod R_{i}$; $P_{f} = 1 - R_{total}$. A 4-step procedure with 99% per-step reliability has ~3.9% cumulative failure.
- ✅ **Swiss Cheese Model (Reason, 1990):** Rule failures are *holes* in successive defensive slices — *organizational, supervisory, preconditions, unsafe act*.
- ✅ **Defense-in-Depth Implication:** Every rule-based procedure must embed at least one independent verification step.
- ✅ **Work-as-Done vs. Work-as-Imagined:** Procedural drift (Dekker) is the silent killer of rule-based safety.
- ✅ **Normalization of Deviance** (Vaughan) — a long-recognized phenomenon where gradually relaxed rules become acceptable.
- ✅ **BBS Score** is a leading indicator of rule-based compliance; target ≥ 95% per the **DuPont Bradley Curve**.
- ✅ **Swiss Cheese + SRK Integration:** Latent organizational failures create the *holes*; rule-based unsafe acts are the *trajectory* piercing them.
- ✅ **Key Authors to Quote in KTU Exam:** Jens Rasmussen, James Reason, Jens Sandberg, Sidney Dekker, Diane Vaughan, E. Scott Geller, James C. Byers.
- ✅ **Real-world domain anchors:** Aviation (CRM), Nuclear (Symptom-based EOPs), Process Industry (PTW, LOTO), Healthcare (Surgical Checklists), Construction (OSHA 1926).

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Board Strategy:** A 14-mark answer should ideally have (i) a clear definition anchored in SRK, (ii) a labeled Mermaid-style or hand-drawn diagram, (iii) at least 2 industrial examples, (iv) a citation of a *named scholar*, and (v) a concluding engineering-comment sentence. This five-part structure is what separates a 10-mark answer from a full-mark one.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Managed Safety
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT524 - HUMAN AND ORGANIZATIONAL FACTORS IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Managed Safety

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MANAGED SAFETY — CORE DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Managed Safety** is the systematic, proactive, and documented approach to controlling safety risks in an organization through the establishment of an integrated **Safety Management System (SMS)**. It involves the coordinated application of policies, organizational structures, planning processes, responsibilities, practices, procedures, and resources for the prevention of harm to people, property, and the environment.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framework (aligned with **ILO-OSH 2001 Guidelines**, **ISO 45001:2018**, and **Rasmussen's Risk Management Framework**), *Managed Safety* is treated as an **engineering management discipline** — not merely a compliance exercise — wherein the organization embeds safety into every operational decision through a **closed-loop control system** of *Plan → Do → Check → Act*.

### Key Working Definitions

| Term | Meaning in Managed Safety Context |
|---|---|
| **Safety** | The control of recognized hazards to attain an acceptable level of risk (per **Reason's Swiss Cheese Model**). |
| **Management** | A coordinated set of activities to direct and control an organization (per **ISO 9000**). |
| **Managed Safety** | The translation of safety policy into measurable, auditable, and continuously improved operational practice. |
| **Safety Culture** | The product of individual and group values, attitudes, perceptions, competencies, and patterns of behavior that determine the commitment to and the style of an organization's health and safety management (per **ACSNI**, 1993). |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Airplane Autopilot"

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Managed Safety like an Airplane Autopilot System.**

Imagine a pilot flying a commercial aircraft. The pilot does not "react" to turbulence only when the plane shakes violently. Instead, an **autopilot system** continuously:
1. **Senses** altitude, speed, wind shear, and fuel (Inputs).
2. **Compares** with a flight plan (Standard).
3. **Adjusts** control surfaces (Corrective Action).
4. **Logs** the deviation for post-flight review (Feedback & Learning).

**Managed Safety works exactly the same way for an organization:**
- The **flight plan** is the *Safety Policy*.
- The **sensors** are *Hazard Identification & Risk Assessment (HIRA)*.
- The **control surfaces** are *Operational Controls & Procedures*.
- The **black box** is the *Incident Reporting & Investigation System*.
- The **air traffic controller** is the *Safety Manager / EHS Head*.
- The **pilot** is *Line Management and the Workforce*.

Without this structured, continuous, and documented loop, the organization is essentially flying "by sight" — which is exactly what leads to **Bhopal (1984)**, **Chernobyl (1986)**, and **Deepwater Horizon (2010)** scale disasters.

---

## 1.3 Historical Evolution of Managed Safety (KTU Board Favourite)

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Trace the evolution of safety management."* Memorize the three eras below.

- **Era 1 — Reactive Era (Pre-1970):** Safety was treated as a "cost of doing business." Action was taken *after* an accident. Foundation: **Heinrich's Domino Theory (1931)**.
- **Era 2 — Planned/Proactive Era (1970–1990):** Introduction of **OSHA (1970)**, **HSWA UK (1974)**, and systematic safety audits. Foundation: **Bird's Loss Causation Model (1974)**.
- **Era 3 — Managed/Integrated Era (1990–Present):** Holistic, systems-based, human-factors-aware approach. Foundations: **Reason's Swiss Cheese Model (1990)**, **BS 8800 (1996)**, **OHSAS 18001 (1999)**, **ISO 45001 (2018)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 specifically emphasizes the **Managed/Integrated Era** because it integrates *human factors* and *organizational culture* — the heart of this course (PESFT524).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Managed Safety (PDCA Anchored)

Managed Safety, as prescribed by **ISO 45001:2018** and adopted in the KTU 2024 syllabus, rests on **four interconnected pillars** mapped to the **Deming Cycle (PDCA)**:

### Pillar 1 — SAFETY POLICY & LEADERSHIP COMMITMENT (PLAN)
- Top-down declaration of intent (e.g., *"Zero Harm is non-negotiable"*).
- Must be **SMART**: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound.
- Visible leadership walk-throughs, board-level safety KPI reviews, signed-off annual safety plans.

### Pillar 2 — ORGANIZATION, ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES (PLAN)
- Defined **RACI Matrix** (Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed).
- Appointment of competent **Safety Officers** (per **Factories Act 1948, Sec. 40B**).
- Documented delegation of authority for stop-work decisions.

### Pillar 3 — RISK & HAZARD MANAGEMENT (DO)
- **HIRA** (Hazard Identification & Risk Assessment).
- **JSA/JHA** (Job Safety Analysis / Job Hazard Analysis).
- **Permit-to-Work (PTW)** systems for high-risk activities.
- Hierarchy of Controls: **Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE**.

### Pillar 4 — MONITORING, REVIEW & CONTINUAL IMPROVEMENT (CHECK & ACT)
- **Leading Indicators** (proactive): safety observations, training hours, near-miss reports.
- **Lagging Indicators** (reactive): TRIR, LTIFR, fatality rate.
- **Management of Change (MOC)** protocols.
- Annual **Management Review** per **ISO 45001 Clause 9.3**.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Board-Exam Essentials)

> [!NOTE]
> These formulas are the most frequently asked numerical/probability questions in KTU ESE for safety management modules.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = P \times S$ | **Risk = Probability × Severity** (Fine-Kinney Method) | Risk Score (dimensionless index) |
| 2 | $D = L \times E \times C$ | **D = Likelihood × Exposure × Consequence** (LEC Method) | Used in semi-quantitative HIRA |
| 3 | $TRIR = \dfrac{\text{No. of Recordable Injuries}}{\text{Total Man-hours Worked}} \times 200{,}000$ | Total Recordable Incident Rate (OSHA Standard) | Per **200,000** employee-hours |
| 4 | $LTIFR = \dfrac{\text{No. of Lost-Time Injuries}}{\text{Total Man-hours Worked}} \times 10^6$ | Lost Time Injury Frequency Rate (ILO Standard) | Per **1 million** man-hours |
| 5 | $ASR = \dfrac{\text{No. of Days Lost}}{\text{Total Man-hours Worked}} \times K$ | Accident Severity Rate | K = 1000 or 10^6 depending on convention |
| 6 | $F = \dfrac{\text{Total Working Days Lost}}{\text{No. of Injuries}}$ | Frequency-Severity Index (per injury) | Days per injury |
| 7 | $P_{\text{system}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_i$ | System Failure Probability (Independent Failures) | Used in **FTA** (Fault Tree Analysis) |
| 8 | $IFR = \dfrac{\text{No. of Injuries}}{\text{Workers Employed}} \times 1000$ | Injury Frequency Rate (incidence rate per 1000 workers) | Annual safety statistics |

### The Risk Matrix (Standard 5×5)

$$R_{\text{score}} = L \times S$$

| Severity (S) → | Slight (1) | Minor (2) | Moderate (3) | Major (4) | Catastrophic (5) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Likelihood (L)** | | | | | |
| Almost Certain (5) | 5 | 10 | 15 | 20 | **25** |
| Likely (4) | 4 | 8 | 12 | **16** | 20 |
| Possible (3) | 3 | 6 | **9** | 12 | 15 |
| Unlikely (2) | 2 | **4** | 6 | 8 | 10 |
| Rare (1) | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |

> **Risk Action Bands:** 1–4 = *Acceptable (Green)*; 5–12 = *Manage with Controls (Yellow)*; 15–16 = *Senior Review & Reduce (Orange)*; 20–25 = *Stop & Re-engineer (Red)*.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Construction Industry:** Managed Safety underpins the **SSoW (Safe System of Work)** mandated by the Indian **BOCW Act 1996**.
- **Petrochemical Sector:** Drives **Process Safety Management (PSM)** per **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119** and the **COMAH Regulations 2015** (UK).
- **Aviation:** Forms the basis of **Safety Management Systems (SMS)** mandated by **ICAO Annex 19** and **DGCA CAR Section 7 — Series I Part I**.
- **Manufacturing:** Underpins **Lean + Safety Integration** (the *Poka-Yoke* philosophy of Shigeo Shingo).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Heinrich's Triangle (Bird's Pyramid) — Statistical Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heinrich's Triangle (1931)** proposes a fixed statistical relationship between near-misses, minor injuries, serious injuries, and fatalities. Bird (1974) updated the ratios. This derivation is a *favourite 14-mark KTU question*.

### Statement of the Empirical Law

For every **1 major injury (lost-time)**, there is a statistical relationship:

$$
\frac{\text{Near Misses}}{\text{Minor Injuries}} = \frac{30}{1}, \quad \frac{\text{Minor Injuries}}{\text{Major Injuries}} = \frac{29}{1}, \quad \frac{\text{Major Injuries}}{\text{Fatalities}} = \frac{300}{1}
$$

### Step-by-Step Logical Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the Base Population.**
Let $N$ be the total number of incidents recorded in a year, classified into 4 levels:
- $L_0$ = Near-misses (no injury)
- $L_1$ = Minor injuries (first aid)
- $L_2$ = Serious injuries (lost-time)
- $L_3$ = Fatalities

**Step 2 — Apply Heinrich's Empirical Ratios.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_0 &= 30 \cdot L_1 \\
L_1 &= 29 \cdot L_2 \\
L_2 &= 300 \cdot L_3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Express Total Incidents ($N$) in Terms of Fatalities ($L_3$).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_2 &= 300 \cdot L_3 \\
L_1 &= 29 \cdot (300 \cdot L_3) = 8{,}700 \cdot L_3 \\
L_0 &= 30 \cdot (8{,}700 \cdot L_3) = 261{,}000 \cdot L_3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Incident Count.**

$$
N = L_0 + L_1 + L_2 + L_3
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= 261{,}000 L_3 + 8{,}700 L_3 + 300 L_3 + L_3 \\
N &= 270{,}001 \cdot L_3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Bird's Refined Ratio (1974).** Frank Bird analysed **1,753,498 incidents** from 297 companies and proposed updated ratios:

$$
L_0 : L_1 : L_2 : L_3 = 600 : 30 : 10 : 1
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Managed Safety's core philosophy is — *if you systematically report and eliminate near-misses ($L_0$), you statistically prevent every future fatality ($L_3$)*. This is the **mathematical justification for proactive safety**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Standard)

**Question:** A manufacturing plant recorded **5 fatalities** in the year 2023. The plant operates with **1,200 employees**, each working an average of **2,000 hours/year**, and recorded **1,250 lost-time injuries**. Calculate:
1. The expected number of near-misses (per Heinrich).
2. The TRIR.
3. The LTIFR.
4. The expected number of first-aid cases (per Bird's refined ratio).
5. The Risk Score for a hazard with Likelihood = 4, Severity = 5, using $R = P \times S$.

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Expected Near-misses (Heinrich):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_0 &= 261{,}000 \times L_3 \\
L_0 &= 261{,}000 \times 5 \\
L_0 &= 1{,}305{,}000 \text{ near-misses}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Man-hours Worked:**

$$
MH = 1{,}200 \times 2{,}000 = 2{,}400{,}000 \text{ man-hours}
$$

**Step 3 — TRIR Calculation (using recorded lost-time injuries = 1,250):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TRIR &= \frac{\text{Recordable Injuries}}{MH} \times 200{,}000 \\
TRIR &= \frac{1{,}250}{2{,}400{,}000} \times 200{,}000 \\
TRIR &= 0.5208 \times 200 \\
TRIR &= \boxed{104.17 \text{ per 200,000 man-hours}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — LTIFR Calculation (using fatalities + lost-time as proxy; here LTI = 1,250):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LTIFR &= \frac{\text{Lost-Time Injuries}}{MH} \times 10^6 \\
LTIFR &= \frac{1{,}250}{2{,}400{,}000} \times 1{,}000{,}000 \\
LTIFR &= \boxed{520.83 \text{ per million man-hours}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Bird's Refined First-Aid Cases:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{L_1}{L_3} &= 30 \\
L_1 &= 30 \times 5 = \boxed{150 \text{ first-aid cases expected}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Risk Score:**

$$
R = P \times S = 4 \times 5 = \boxed{20 \;\; \rightarrow \;\; \text{STOP \& RE-ENGINEER (Red Zone)}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Three Safety Eras

| Parameter | Reactive Era | Proactive Era | **Managed Safety Era** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Driver | Accident response | Compliance (OSHA) | Risk-based thinking |
| Focus | Person (blame) | Procedure (system) | **Organization + Culture** |
| Tool | Heinrich's Domino | Bird's Pyramid | **Reason's Swiss Cheese** |
| Indicator | Lagging only | Lagging + some leading | **Balanced Scorecard** |
| Worker Role | Subject | Participant | **Engaged partner** |
| Success Metric | Zero lost-time | Reduced TRIR | **Predictive, zero harm** |
| KTU Marks Weight | Low | Medium | **High (current focus)** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 The Managed Safety System (MSS) — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Top Management Leadership] --> B[Safety Policy Commitments]
    B --> C[Safety Organization Structure]
    C --> D[Planning and Risk Assessment]
    D --> E[Operational Controls and Procedures]
    E --> F[Training and Competency Development]
    F --> G[Communication and Consultation]
    G --> H[Emergency Preparedness and Response]
    H --> I[Performance Monitoring and Measurement]
    I --> J{Incident Investigation}
    J --> K[Root Cause Analysis and CAPA]
    K --> L[Management Review and Continual Improvement]
    L --> A
    I --> M[Compliance Evaluation and Audit]
    M --> L
    style A fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B fill:#FFD93D,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style L fill:#6BCB77,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style K fill:#4D96FF,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 The PDCA Cycle (Deming Wheel) for Managed Safety

```mermaid
graph LR
    P[PLAN: Safety Policy, Objectives, HIRA, Legal Register] --> D
    D[DO: Implementation of Controls, Training, Operational Procedures, Emergency Drills] --> C
    C[CHECK: Monitoring, Measurement, Incident Investigation, Audit, Compliance Evaluation] --> A
    A[ACT: Management Review, CAPA, Continual Improvement, Management of Change] --> P
    style P fill:#FFD93D,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D fill:#6BCB77,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C fill:#4D96FF,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style A fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Reason's Swiss Cheese Model (Organizational Failure Path)

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1[Organizational Influences: Safety Culture, Management Decisions] --> L2
    L2[Unsafe Supervision: Inadequate Planning, Failed to Correct Known Issue] --> L3
    L3[Preconditions for Unsafe Acts: Poor Training, Fatigue, Mental State] --> L4
    L4[Unsafe Acts: Error or Violation at the Sharp End] --> E[Accident Incident Loss Event]
    L1 -. Hazard Path .-> E
    L2 -. Hazard Path .-> E
    L3 -. Hazard Path .-> E
    L4 -. Hazard Path .-> E
    style E fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style L1 fill:#FFD93D,stroke:#000
    style L2 fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#000
    style L3 fill:#A8E6CF,stroke:#000
    style L4 fill:#FF8B8B,stroke:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this diagram as:** *Each layer is a "slice of Swiss cheese" with holes representing latent failures. An accident occurs only when the holes in ALL layers momentarily align, permitting the "hazard trajectory" to pass through.*

## 4.4 Risk Management Decision Tree (Managed Safety Logic)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Hazard Identified] --> B{Is the Risk Acceptable?}
    B -->|Yes, Score 1 to 4| C[Accept and Monitor with Existing Controls]
    B -->|No, Score 5 to 12| D[Implement Additional Controls, Hierarchy of Controls Applied]
    B -->|No, Score 15 to 16| E[Senior Management Review, Mandatory Reduction Plan]
    B -->|No, Score 20 to 25| F[STOP Operation, Re-engineer Process, Restart only after ALARP Demonstration]
    D --> G{Residual Risk Acceptable?}
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G -->|Yes| C
    G -->|No| H[Reject the Activity or Substitute the Process]
    style F fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style E fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000
    style D fill:#FFD93D,stroke:#000
    style C fill:#6BCB77,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style H fill:#8B0000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```
