# KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE pattern for an elective theory course: **Part A (3 × 2 marks) + Part B (answer 2 out of 3 × 14 marks) = 34 marks total** (after scaling to 70). Below is a representative module-level question paper for *Managed Safety*.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 / 3 Marks each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Define Managed Safety. List any four key elements of a Safety Management System (SMS).**

**Model Answer:**
Managed Safety is the systematic, documented, and proactive approach to controlling safety risks through an integrated Safety Management System (SMS). It transforms safety from a reactive, rule-based function into a continuous, auditable, and continuously improving management discipline anchored in the PDCA cycle.

**Four Key Elements of SMS (per ISO 45001:2018):**
1. **Safety Policy and Leadership Commitment** – top-down declaration of intent and visible engagement.
2. **Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment (HIRA)** – proactive identification of hazards and evaluation of risks.
3. **Operational Controls and Procedures** – documented safe systems of work, permit-to-work, LOTO, etc.
4. **Monitoring, Audit, and Continual Improvement** – leading/lagging indicators, incident investigation, CAPA, and management review.

*(Any four relevant elements fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**Differentiate between a "leading indicator" and a "lagging indicator" in managed safety with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Leading Indicator (Proactive) | Lagging Indicator (Reactive) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Measures the *presence or strength* of safety systems *before* incidents occur. | Measures the *outcome* of safety failures — injuries, illnesses, fatalities. |
| **Time Orientation** | Forward-looking, predictive. | Backward-looking, historical. |
| **Examples** | (1) Number of safety observations closed per month; (2) Hours of safety training delivered; (3) Number of near-miss reports. | (1) Lost-Time Injury Frequency Rate (LTIFR); (2) Total Recordable Incident Rate (TRIR); (3) Number of fatalities per year. |
| **KTU Insight** | High-performing organizations track 70 % leading + 30 % lagging. | Lagging-only tracking is a hallmark of the *reactive era*. |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks each, Choice-based)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:**
> Students often *describe* what managed safety is but **fail to anchor their answer to a recognized standard (ISO 45001 / ILO-OSH / OSHA)**. Always quote the standard and clause number where applicable.
> **Pitfall #2:** Numerical questions on TRIR/LTIFR are *often miscalculated* because students forget the **multiplier constant** (200,000 for TRIR vs 10^6 for LTIFR). Memorize: *TRIR uses 2 lakh; LTIFR uses 1 million*.
> **Pitfall #3:** Heinrich's triangle ratios (30:29:300) and Bird's refined ratios (600:30:10:1) are **not the same** — many students mix them up and lose 4 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU ESE Model - May 2024]`**

#### (a) Discuss the evolution of safety management from the reactive era to the managed/integrated era. Highlight the contributions of Heinrich, Bird, and Reason. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Reactive Era (Pre-1970) — Heinrich's Contribution (1931):**
H.W. Heinrich's *Industrial Accident Prevention* (1931) introduced the **Domino Theory**, proposing that accidents result from a chain of five sequential events: *Ancestral/Social Environment → Fault of Person → Unsafe Act/Mechanism → Accident → Injury*. Heinrich's work established the philosophical basis that **88% of accidents are caused by unsafe acts of people** (the "Heinrich Axiom"). However, this era was fundamentally reactive — safety action was taken only after incidents.

**2. Proactive/Planned Era (1970–1990) — Bird's Contribution (1974):**
Frank Bird, analysing **1.75 million incidents** across 297 companies, refined Heinrich's work into the **Loss Causation Model**. Bird proved that the ratio of near-misses to minor injuries to major injuries to fatalities is **600 : 30 : 10 : 1** (not Heinrich's 30 : 29 : 300 : 1). Bird's critical insight was that **major injuries share the same root causes as near-misses** — therefore, controlling near-misses prevents fatalities. This shifted the focus from *blame* to *systemic management*.

**3. Managed/Integrated Era (1990–Present) — Reason's Contribution (1990):**
James Reason's **Swiss Cheese Model (1990)** revolutionized managed safety by reframing failures as **latent conditions** (organizational, supervisory, preconditions) that align with **active failures** (unsafe acts). Managed Safety now emphasizes:
- **Safety Culture** (ACSNI, 1993) as the prime mover.
- **Just Culture** (Marx, 2001) — balancing no-blame and accountability.
- **High-Reliability Organizations (HRO)** — Weick & Sutcliffe (2001).
- **Safety Management Systems** formalized in **ILO-OSH 2001**, **OHSAS 18001**, and **ISO 45001:2018**.

**Conclusion:** The shift is from *person-blame* (Reactive) → *procedure-compliance* (Proactive) → *system-and-culture* (Managed).

---

#### (b) A construction site employs 850 workers, each working 240 days/year at 8 hours/day. In 2023, the site recorded 3 fatalities, 12 lost-time injuries, and 45 first-aid cases. Calculate the TRIR, LTIFR, and Accident Severity Rate (assuming 1,800 total days lost). Using Heinrich's and Bird's ratios, estimate the expected near-misses. Comment on the safety performance. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total Man-hours Worked:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MH &= 850 \text{ workers} \times 240 \text{ days} \times 8 \text{ hours} \\
MH &= 1{,}632{,}000 \text{ man-hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — TRIR Calculation (Recordable = Fatalities + LTI = 3 + 12 = 15):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TRIR &= \frac{15}{1{,}632{,}000} \times 200{,}000 \\
TRIR &= 9.19 \times 10^{-6} \times 200{,}000 \\
TRIR &= \boxed{1.838 \text{ per 200,000 man-hours}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — LTIFR Calculation (LTI = 12):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LTIFR &= \frac{12}{1{,}632{,}000} \times 1{,}000{,}000 \\
LTIFR &= \boxed{7.35 \text{ per million man-hours}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Accident Severity Rate (ASR, K = 1,000):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ASR &= \frac{1{,}800 \text{ days lost}}{1{,}632{,}000 \text{ man-hours}} \times 1{,}000 \\
ASR &= \boxed{1.103 \text{ days lost per 1,000 man-hours}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Heinrich's Expected Near-misses (using 3 fatalities as $L_3$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_0 &= 261{,}000 \times 3 = \boxed{783{,}000 \text{ near-misses expected}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Bird's Expected Near-misses:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_0 &= 600 \times 3 = \boxed{1{,}800 \text{ near-misses expected (Bird's refined ratio)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Comment on Safety Performance:**
A TRIR of 1.84 is **above the global construction industry benchmark of ~1.5** (BLS data). The fact that only 45 first-aid cases were recorded against an expected 1,800+ near-misses (per Bird) indicates a **severely under-reporting safety culture**. The site is operationally in the **Reactive Era** — action is being taken only after serious injuries, not proactively.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU ESE Model - Dec 2023]`

#### (a) With the aid of Reason's Swiss Cheese Model, explain how organizational and human factors contribute to accidents. Describe any four practical interventions a managed safety system can deploy to "plug the holes" in each layer. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Reason's Swiss Cheese Model (1990):**
Reason proposed that an organization has **four defensive layers**, each containing "holes" (latent failures). An accident occurs only when the holes in all layers momentarily align.

- **Layer 1 — Organizational Influences:** Safety culture, budget allocations, regulatory pressure.
- **Layer 2 — Unsafe Supervision:** Inadequate planning, failure to correct a known deficiency, supervisory violations.
- **Layer 3 — Preconditions for Unsafe Acts:** Fatigue, mental state, poor training, environmental stressors.
- **Layer 4 — Unsafe Acts (Active Failures):** Operator error, procedural violation at the sharp end.

**Four Practical Interventions per Layer:**

| Layer | Intervention |
|---|---|
| **Organizational** | (1) Board-level safety KPIs; (2) Just Culture policy; (3) Independent safety audits; (4) Resource allocation for safety projects. |
| **Unsafe Supervision** | (1) Supervisor competency certification; (2) Management of Change (MOC) protocols; (3) Behaviour-Based Safety (BBS) observations; (4) Visible felt leadership walk-throughs. |
| **Preconditions** | (1) Fatigue management rosters; (2) Fitness-for-duty checks; (3) Continuous competency-based training; (4) Human Factors Engineering of displays & controls. |
| **Unsafe Acts** | (1) Permit-to-Work enforcement; (2) Pre-task risk assessments / JSA; (3) Buddy system for high-risk tasks; (4) Stop-Work Authority (SWA) without reprisal. |

---

#### (b) Construct a 5×5 Risk Matrix and explain how it is used in a managed safety system. For a chemical process with the following hazards, compute the risk score, classify the risk, and recommend controls using the Hierarchy of Controls: (7 Marks)

| Hazard | L | S |
|---|---|---|
| H1: Pump seal leak | 2 | 4 |
| H2: Confined space entry | 3 | 5 |
| H3: LOTO failure | 1 | 5 |
| H4: Crane lift near powerline | 4 | 4 |
| H5: Chemical splash during sampling | 5 | 3 |

**Model Solution:**

**5×5 Risk Matrix (Standard, used in managed safety):**

| L \ S | Slight 1 | Minor 2 | Moderate 3 | Major 4 | Catastrophic 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Almost Certain 5** | 5 | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 |
| **Likely 4** | 4 | 8 | 12 | 16 | 20 |
| **Possible 3** | 3 | 6 | 9 | 12 | 15 |
| **Unlikely 2** | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 |
| **Rare 1** | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |

**Risk Score, Classification & Control Recommendations:**

$$
R_{H1} = 2 \times 4 = 8 \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{Yellow — Manage with controls (e.g., seal upgrade + drip trays + inspection schedule)}
$$

$$
R_{H2} = 3 \times 5 = 15 \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{Orange — Senior review + confined space program + gas testing + rescue plan + standby}
$$

$$
R_{H3} = 1 \times 5 = 5 \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{Yellow — Manage with controls (LOTO procedure + lock registry + independent verification)}
$$

$$
R_{H4} = 4 \times 4 = 16 \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{Orange — Senior review + lift plan + barricading + powerline isolation + spotters}
$$

$$
R_{H5} = 5 \times 3 = 15 \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{Orange — Senior review + engineering: enclosed sampling system (substitution); PPE last resort}
$$

**Hierarchy of Controls Applied (most effective → least effective):**
**Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE.**

For H5 (Chemical splash), the *engineered* closed-loop sampling system is preferred over PPE alone — this reflects a true **managed safety** philosophy, not a PPE-only "blame the worker" approach.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- **Managed Safety** = systematic, PDCA-based control of safety risks through a documented SMS, not ad-hoc compliance.
- **3 Eras:** Reactive (Heinrich 1931) → Proactive (Bird 1974) → Managed/Integrated (Reason 1990, ISO 45001:2018).
- **4 Pillars of SMS:** Policy → Organization → Risk Management → Monitoring & Improvement.
- **Key Frameworks:** PDCA Deming Cycle, Heinrich's Domino, Bird's Pyramid, Reason's Swiss Cheese, ACSNI Safety Culture, HRO (Weick & Sutcliffe).
- **Critical Formulae to Memorize:**
  - $R = P \times S$ (Fine-Kinney Risk Score)
  - $D = L \times E \times C$ (LEC Method)
  - $TRIR = \dfrac{\text{Recordable Injuries}}{MH} \times 200{,}000$
  - $LTIFR = \dfrac{\text{LTI}}{MH} \times 10^6$
  - $ASR = \dfrac{\text{Days Lost}}{MH} \times 1{,}000$
- **Heinrich's Ratios (1931):** Near-miss : Minor : Major : Fatal = **30 : 29 : 300 : 1** (i.e., $L_0 = 261{,}000 \cdot L_3$).
- **Bird's Ratios (1974):** Near-miss : Minor : Major : Fatal = **600 : 30 : 10 : 1**.
- **Hierarchy of Controls:** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE (NEVER start with PPE).
- **Leading vs Lagging Indicators:** Leading = proactive (observations, training hours); Lagging = reactive (TRIR, LTIFR, fatalities).
- **Key Standards:** **ISO 45001:2018**, **ILO-OSH 2001**, **OSHA 29 CFR 1910**, **Factories Act 1948 (India)**, **BOCW Act 1996 (India)**, **ICAO Annex 19 (Aviation SMS)**.
- **Just Culture (Marx 2001):** Differentiate *human error* (coach), *at-risk behaviour* (counsel), *reckless behaviour* (discipline).
- **High-Reliability Organizations (HRO):** Preoccupation with failure, reluctance to simplify, sensitivity to operations, commitment to resilience, deference to expertise.
- **Stop-Work Authority (SWA):** Every worker has the right and duty to halt any operation they believe is unsafe — a cornerstone of managed safety.
- **ALARP (As Low As Reasonably Practicable):** The legal and ethical test for residual risk acceptability in managed safety decisions.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Mantra:** "Quote the standard. Show the math. Apply the hierarchy. Anchor in culture." This 4-step mantra guarantees full marks in any KTU managed safety question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definition and Model of Safety Culture
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT524 - HUMAN AND ORGANIZATIONAL FACTORS IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Definition and Model of Safety Culture

# 🛡️ Definition and Model of Safety Culture

## 1. Core Technical Definition

### Formal Definition (KTU-Aligned)

**Safety Culture** is the product of individual and group values, attitudes, perceptions, competencies, and patterns of behaviour that determine the commitment to and the style and proficiency of an organisation's health and safety management.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Advisory Committee on the Safety of Nuclear Installations – ACSNI, 1993):**
> *"The safety culture of an organisation is the product of individual and group values, attitudes, perceptions, competencies, and patterns of behaviour that determine the commitment to and the style and proficiency of an organisation's health and safety management."*

This definition was first formalised after the **Chernobyl disaster (1986)** by the **International Nuclear Safety Advisory Group (INSAG)**, and it has since become the bedrock concept in modern safety engineering.

### Related Formal Definitions

| Source | Definition (Simplified) |
|---|---|
| **INSAG (1991)** | "...that assembly of characteristics and attitudes in organisations and individuals which establishes that, as an overriding priority, nuclear plant safety issues receive the attention warranted by their significance." |
| **Reason (1997)** | Safety culture is the "shared values, beliefs, attitudes and patterns of behaviour" of an organisation's people. |
| **HSE (UK, 2005)** | "...the product of the individual and group values, attitudes, perceptions, competencies and patterns of behaviour that determine the commitment to and the style and proficiency of an organisation's health and safety management." |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🍽️

> **"Think of Safety Culture as the 'Personality' of an Organisation."**

Imagine a family:
- A **strict, disciplined family** will have children who automatically wear helmets while cycling, speak politely, and follow rules — not because a parent is watching, but because it's part of **how the family thinks**.
- A **careless family** will have children who ignore rules, take risks, and treat safety as optional.

Similarly, in an organisation:
- **Strong Safety Culture** → Employees *want* to work safely, even when no supervisor is watching.
- **Weak Safety Culture** → Employees follow rules only to avoid punishment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Safety Culture is invisible but observable through behaviour** — like a country's culture is invisible but observable through customs.

### Safety Culture vs. Safety Climate

A common KTU exam question asks for the difference:

| Aspect | Safety Culture | Safety Climate |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Deep-rooted, long-term, underlying | Surface-level, measurable, snapshot |
| **Time Frame** | Years to decades | Weeks to months |
| **Components** | Values, beliefs, assumptions, behaviour | Perceptions, attitudes, current feelings |
| **Analogy** | The **personality** of a person | The **mood** of a person today |
| **Measurement** | Difficult, qualitative | Easier, via questionnaires |

> [!TIP]
> If asked in KTU: **Climate = "How it feels to work here today"; Culture = "Why it feels that way."**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

## 2.1 Key Characteristics of a Strong Safety Culture (KTU Frequently Asked)

A robust safety culture typically exhibits:

1. **Top-Down Commitment** — Management visibly prioritises safety over production.
2. **Effective Communication** — Open channels across all hierarchical levels.
3. **Just Culture** — Distinction drawn between *human error* (blameless) and *reckless behaviour* (accountable).
4. **Learning Organisation** — Near-misses and incidents are systematically analysed.
5. **Empowerment** — Any employee can stop work on safety grounds.
6. **Continuous Improvement** — Safety is a journey, not a destination.
7. **Reporting Culture** — Willingness to report errors and near-misses without fear.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Term: "Just Culture"** — A culture where people are not punished for actions or omissions commensurate with their experience and training, but gross negligence and wilful violations are not tolerated. (Popularised by **Sidney Dekker** and **Reason**.)

## 2.2 The "Big Three" Components of Safety Culture

Every safety culture framework (Reason, Cooper, Hudson, Guldenmund) revolves around three dimensions:

| Dimension | Meaning | Example in Industry |
|---|---|---|
| **People (Personnel)** | Attitudes, competence, awareness | Workers understanding *why* rules exist |
| **Process (Behaviour / Activity)** | Actions, procedures, daily practices | Conducting pre-job safety briefings |
| **Plant (Organisation / System)** | Structure, policies, leadership commitment | A CEO who walks the plant floor weekly |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Framework Table (Models Cheat Sheet)

| Model | Author | Year | Core Idea | Key Element |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Triangular Model** | **Zohar** | 1980 | First empirical safety climate scale | 8 dimensions, 80 items |
| **Three-Component Model** | **Guldenmund** | 2000 | Three layers: Basic assumptions, Espoused values, Artefacts | Schein-inspired |
| **Reciprocal Model** | **Cooper** | 2000 | Continuously interacting loop of Person–Behaviour–Situation | Reciprocal determinism |
| **Swiss Cheese / Path-Organisational Model** | **Reason** | 1997 | Latent failures + Active failures align → accident | Culture as a "defence" |
| **Ladder Model** | **Hudson** | 2001 | 5 stages of safety culture maturity | Pathological → Generative |

## 2.4 Why Study Safety Culture Models?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In high-risk industries (nuclear, aviation, oil & gas, mining, construction, railways), **75% of accidents are attributed to human and organisational factors** — not technical failures. A clear safety culture model allows engineers to:
> - **Diagnose** organisational weaknesses
> - **Predict** accident precursors
> - **Design** safer systems of work (SoW)
> - **Comply** with statutory bodies (AERB, OSHA, DGMS, ICAO)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix of Major Safety Culture Models

## 3.1 MODEL 1 — Schein's Three-Level Model of Culture (Foundation for Safety Culture)

**Edgar Schein (1985, 1990, 2010)** proposed that organisational culture operates on three hierarchical levels. Most modern safety culture models are *adaptations* of this framework.

### Step-by-Step Breakdown:

**Level 1 — Artefacts and Creations (Visible)**

These are the *observable* elements: dress code, offices, rituals, language, ceremonies, policies hung on walls.

*Safety example:* A factory wall lined with "100 Days Without an Accident" banners.

**Level 2 — Espoused Beliefs and Values (Stated)**

These are the *stated* strategies, goals, philosophies — what the organisation **says** it believes.

*Safety example:* A CEO publicly stating, "Safety is our number one priority — above production."

**Level 3 — Basic Underlying Assumptions (Invisible, Deepest)**

These are the *taken-for-granted*, unconscious beliefs — the source of values and behaviour.

*Safety example:* "Here, we look out for each other." / "It is safe to speak up about mistakes."

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Artefacts** with **Values**. Remember: *Artefacts* = what you see. *Values* = what people say. *Assumptions* = what people *actually believe* and act on.

## 3.2 MODEL 2 — James Reason's (1997) Organisational Accident Causation Model

**James Reason's "Swiss Cheese Model"** extended to organisational culture treats safety culture as a **deep organisational defence layer**.

### Step-by-Step Breakdown:

**Step 1 — Organisational Influences (Top of Hierarchy)**

*Decision-makers*: resource allocation, scheduling pressure, organisational climate.

**Step 2 — Unsafe Supervision**

*Supervisors*: maintain standards, train adequately, manage risk, respond to safety violations.

**Step 3 — Preconditions for Unsafe Acts**

*Workers' condition*: mental fatigue, poor communication, equipment readiness, training gaps.

**Step 4 — Unsafe Acts (Active Failures)**

*Errors* (slips, lapses, mistakes) and *violations* (routine, exceptional).

**Step 5 — The Defence Layers (Swiss Cheese)**

Each layer has *holes* (latent failures). An accident occurs when holes in **all** layers align momentarily.

**Step 6 — Safety Culture as a Deep Defence**

Reason argued: **Safety culture is the most fundamental defence** because it influences the size, shape, and position of holes in every other layer.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Term:** Reason called unsafe acts arising from organisational culture as **"Cultural Blamers"** — meaning we blame individuals, when in fact, the *culture* created the conditions for error.

## 3.3 MODEL 3 — Cooper's Reciprocal Safety Culture Model (2000)

**M. D. Cooper** introduced a **reciprocal determinism** view, drawing on Bandura's social cognitive theory.

### Step-by-Step Breakdown:

**Step 1 — Three Interacting Subsystems**

The model posits three constantly interacting sub-systems:

- **Person (P)** — Knowledge, skills, attitudes, personality, risk perception.
- **Behaviour (B)** — Work practices, communication, leadership visibility, rule compliance.
- **Situation (S)** — Physical environment, procedures, equipment, organisational structure.

**Step 2 — Reciprocal Causality**

Each subsystem both **influences** and **is influenced by** the other two.

$$P \leftrightarrow B \leftrightarrow S \quad \text{(Reciprocal loop)}$$

**Step 3 — Persistent Inconsistencies**

Cooper's central thesis: safety culture is **never static** — it is the result of *continually negotiating* the tensions between P, B, and S. *Persistent inconsistencies* (e.g., policy says one thing, behaviour shows another) are diagnostic of a **weak safety culture**.

**Step 4 — Practical Implication**

Changing safety culture requires interventions in **all three** subsystems simultaneously — training (P), behaviour modification (B), and environmental redesign (S).

## 3.4 MODEL 4 — Hudson's Five-Stage Safety Culture Ladder (2001)

**Patrick Hudson** transformed safety culture into a **maturity ladder** based on his work with Royal Dutch Shell.

### Step-by-Step Breakdown (Ladder of Evolution):

**Stage 1 — Pathological**

*Attitude:* "Who cares about safety as long as we don't get caught?"
*Driver:* Financial / legal compliance only.

**Stage 2 — Reactive**

*Attitude:* "Safety is important; we do something when an accident happens."
*Driver:* Insurance and liability.

**Stage 3 — Calculative (Bureaucratic)**

*Attitude:* "We have systems in place to manage all hazards."
*Driver:* Systems, audits, statistics — but data overload may blind leadership.

**Stage 4 — Proactive**

*Attitude:* "We try to anticipate problems before they occur."
*Driver:* Continuous improvement, near-miss reporting, root-cause analysis.

**Stage 5 — Generative**

*Attitude:* "Safety is how we do business around here — fully integrated."
*Driver:* Shared value, employee empowerment, intrinsic motivation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Most Indian industries (manufacturing, construction) are at **Stage 2 or 3**. Global aviation leaders (Boeing, Airbus operations) and top nuclear plants aim for **Stage 5**.

## 3.5 MODEL 5 — Guldenmund's Adaptation (2000)

**Frank Guldenmund** adapted Schein's model specifically for safety. He defined safety culture as:

*"Those aspects of organisational culture that affect safety and health."*

### Step-by-Step Breakdown:

He grouped safety culture elements into three categories:

1. **Psychological Aspects** — Employee perceptions, attitudes, beliefs (linked to safety climate).
2. **Behavioural Aspects** — What people actually do (safety behaviour observations).
3. **Organisational / Situational Aspects** — Structures, policies, systems.

> [!NOTE]
> Guldenmund argued that what we *measure* through surveys is technically **safety climate**, not deep culture. True culture requires **qualitative ethnographic methods** (interviews, observations, focus groups).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Q1]**
> **Q: Define safety culture. Mention any two of its key characteristics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Safety culture is the product of individual and group values, attitudes, perceptions, competencies, and patterns of behaviour that determine the commitment to and the style and proficiency of an organisation's health and safety management. *(ACSNI, 1993)*

**Key Characteristics (2 marks — any two):**

1. **Visible Top-Down Commitment** — Management places safety above production; leaders visibly engage with workers on safety matters.
2. **Just Culture** — A balanced approach where honest mistakes are not punished, but wilful violations are.
3. **Effective Reporting Culture** — Employees willingly report near-misses and unsafe conditions without fear.
4. **Learning Orientation** — Lessons from incidents and near-misses are systematically analysed and shared.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Q2]**
> **Q: Distinguish between safety culture and safety climate.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Safety Culture | Safety Climate |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Deep, enduring values and assumptions about safety | Current perceptions and attitudes of employees |
| **Scope** | Entire organisation, long-term | Snapshot in time |
| **Time Frame** | Develops over years | Measured over weeks/months |
| **Measurement** | Qualitative (interviews, ethnography) | Quantitative (questionnaires, surveys) |
| **Analogy** | The **personality** of the organisation | The **mood** of the organisation today |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Climate is a *surface manifestation* of the underlying culture. Multiple climate measurements over time reflect the deeper, slow-moving culture.

---

## 📗 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / 14-Mark Pattern]**
> **(a)** Define safety culture. Discuss the various components and characteristics of a strong safety culture in an organisation. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain in detail the three-level model of organisational culture proposed by Edgar Schein and apply it to a high-risk industry such as oil and gas. **(7 marks)**

---

#### (a) Model Solution: Definition + Components & Characteristics of Strong Safety Culture

**Definition (2 marks):** Safety culture is the product of individual and group values, attitudes, perceptions, competencies, and patterns of behaviour that determine the commitment to the style and proficiency of an organisation's health and safety management *(ACSNI, 1993)*.

**Components (3 marks):**

1. **People Component** — Beliefs, attitudes, knowledge, motivation of employees.
2. **Behavioural Component** — Day-to-day safety practices, rule compliance, communication.
3. **Organisational/Structural Component** — Policies, leadership commitment, systems, resources.

**Characteristics of a Strong Safety Culture (2 marks):**

- Safety is a **value**, not just a priority.
- Open, two-way communication across hierarchies.
- Continuous learning from near-misses.
- Empowerment of workers to stop unsafe work.
- A non-punitive *Just Culture* framework.

---

#### (b) Model Solution: Schein's Three-Level Model Applied to Oil & Gas

**Step 1 — Introduction to Schein (2 marks):**

Edgar Schein (1985) proposed that organisational culture exists at three hierarchical levels. Applied to safety, this helps engineers *see* and *interpret* the depth of safety practices in a refinery or rig.

**Step 2 — Level 1: Artefacts (2 marks):**

*Visible, tangible elements.*
In an oil rig: hard hats, high-visibility vests, safety posters, "Toolbox Talk" meetings, safety barricades around the wellhead.

**Step 3 — Level 2: Espoused Beliefs & Values (2 marks):**

*Stated, declared safety philosophy.*
In a petroleum company: "Goal Zero Harm," "Every person goes home safe," "Life-Saving Rules" published in employee handbooks.

**Step 4 — Level 3: Basic Underlying Assumptions (1 mark):**

*Invisible, deeply held beliefs.*
In a mature oil major: "It is always acceptable to halt a job for safety reasons." In a weak culture: "Production targets cannot be questioned."

**Conclusion (1 mark — *Why the difference matters*):**

Two oil rigs may show the same artefacts (PPE, banners) but have vastly different safety outcomes — because their *assumptions* differ. Schein's model enables engineers to **diagnose the gap** between the visible and the invisible.

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / 14-Mark Pattern]**
> **(a)** Explain James Reason's organisational accident causation model. How does safety culture function as a deep organisational defence in this model? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Describe Hudson's five-stage model of safety culture maturity with suitable industrial examples. **(7 marks)**

---

#### (a) Model Solution: Reason's Model + Role of Safety Culture

**Step 1 — Model Introduction (1 mark):**

James Reason (1997) extended his famous *Swiss Cheese Model* to incorporate organisational and cultural factors as upstream causes of accidents.

**Step 2 — Hierarchy of Causal Factors (3 marks):**

$$\text{Organisational Factors} \rightarrow \text{Unsafe Supervision} \rightarrow \text{Preconditions for Unsafe Acts} \rightarrow \text{Unsafe Acts} \rightarrow \text{Defence Breach} \rightarrow \text{Accident}$$

**Step 3 — Each Layer Explained (2 marks):**

- **Organisational layer** — Decisions on resources, scheduling, safety investment.
- **Supervisory layer** — Inadequate supervision, ignored violations.
- **Precondition layer** — Worker fatigue, poor communication.
- **Active failure layer** — Operator errors/violations (slips, mistakes, breaches).

**Step 4 — Safety Culture as Deep Defence (1 mark):**

Reason argued that **safety culture is the most fundamental defence** because it shapes the size, position, and number of "holes" in every other layer. A strong culture produces small, well-distributed holes; a weak culture creates aligned, catastrophic holes.

---

#### (b) Model Solution: Hudson's Five-Stage Model

| Stage | Label | Attitude | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Pathological** | "Don't get caught" | An old unregulated small-scale mining unit |
| 2 | **Reactive** | "Act only after accidents" | A small firm that reviews safety only after a worker is hurt |
| 3 | **Calculative** | "Systems-driven" | A manufacturing plant full of SOPs, audits, and statistics |
| 4 | **Proactive** | "Anticipate risk" | An aviation MRO that does *proactive* safety risk assessments |
| 5 | **Generative** | "Safety is who we are" | A mature airline (e.g., Singapore Airlines) — every employee owns safety |

**Step-by-Step Explanation (5 marks):**

1. **Pathological** — No real safety concern. Driven by cost and compliance.
2. **Reactive** — Acts only after loss events. Driven by liability concerns.
3. **Calculative** — Strong systems, but data-collecting for its own sake.
4. **Proactive** — Pre-empts hazards. Strong near-miss reporting.
5. **Generative** — Safety is a shared identity; employees actively shape it.

**Conclusion (2 marks):**

Hudson's ladder is widely used as a **diagnostic tool** by safety auditors to assess where an organisation stands and what interventions are needed to climb the maturity ladder.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Models:** Students often mix up *Hudson's Ladder* and *Schein's Levels* (one is a maturity progression, the other is a depth-of-culture framework). They are **different models**.
> 2. **Skipping Examples:** In 14-mark answers, always include **at least one industry example** (oil rig, aviation, construction, nuclear). Marks are lost if answers are abstract.
> 3. **Listing Without Explanation:** Avoid bullet-point dumps. KTU examiners reward **logical flow** and **cause-effect analysis**.
> 4. **Forgetting the Difference:** In Q&A on Safety Culture vs Climate, students often just say "both are the same" — this guarantees **zero marks**.
> 5. **Missing the 'Just Culture' Concept:** This is a KTU favourite (linked to Reason) — always include when discussing strong safety cultures.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Safety Culture** = the *deep, enduring* values, beliefs, assumptions, and behaviours of an organisation related to safety *(ACSNI, 1993)*.
- ✅ **Safety Climate** = the *snapshot* of current perceptions (measured through surveys).
- ✅ **Climate is a surface manifestation of Culture.**
- ✅ **Schein's Three Levels:** Artefacts → Espoused Values → Basic Assumptions (visible to invisible).
- ✅ **Reason (1997):** Organisational factors → Unsafe supervision → Preconditions → Unsafe acts → Defence breach → Accident. **Safety culture is the deepest defence layer.**
- ✅ **Cooper (2000):** Reciprocal loop of Person ↔ Behaviour ↔ Situation. *Persistent inconsistencies* indicate weak culture.
- ✅ **Hudson (2001) Five Stages:** Pathological → Reactive → Calculative → Proactive → Generative.
- ✅ **Guldenmund (2000):** Psychological, Behavioural, and Organisational aspects.
- ✅ **Just Culture** = differentiate human error (blameless) from wilful violation (accountable).
- ✅ **Chernobyl (1986) and Challenger (1986)** were landmark disasters that established the importance of safety culture.
- ✅ Most Indian industries operate at **Hudson Stage 2 or 3**; aim for Stage 5.
- ✅ **75% of industrial accidents** are linked to human and organisational (cultural) factors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elements of the Safety Culture Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT524 - HUMAN AND ORGANIZATIONAL FACTORS IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Elements of the Safety Culture Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety Culture Model** is a structured, multi-dimensional framework that describes the **interdependent psychological, behavioral, and organizational elements** that determine how safety is perceived, valued, prioritized, and managed within a workplace. The concept was formally introduced by the **International Nuclear Safety Advisory Group (INSAG)** in the **INSAG-4 Report (1991)**, following the **Chernobyl disaster (1986)**, and was later refined by the **ACSNI (Advisory Committee on the Safety of Nuclear Installations)** into the widely accepted tripartite model comprising **Personnel, Situational, and Behavioural** factors.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context (PESFT524), the *Safety Culture Model* is taught as a diagnostic and prescriptive tool that allows safety engineers and managers to evaluate the **maturity** of an organization's safety posture and to identify **latent weaknesses** in the *human* and *organizational* layers of the safety system — the very layers that classical engineering controls (hardware interlocks, PPE, barriers) cannot fully address.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The board examiner typically expects students to be able to (i) **list** the elements of the safety culture model, (ii) **explain** the inter-relationship between the three ACSNI dimensions, and (iii) **apply** the model to a real industrial case (e.g., Bhopal, Texas City, Deepwater Horizon).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine safety in an organization as the **immune system of the human body**.

- **Hardware safety controls** (guards, valves, alarms) are like the *skin* — the first physical barrier.
- **Procedures and policies** are like the *white blood cells* — they patrol and respond to threats.
- But the **immune system** only functions well when the body is *healthy*, *well-nourished*, and *believes* it must fight infection. If the body is stressed, malnourished, or denies the existence of disease, even the best white blood cells fail.

The **Safety Culture Model** is precisely the "health of the organization." It measures whether the company:
1. **Believes** safety hazards are real (Psychological dimension),
2. **Knows** what to do about them (Situational dimension), and
3. **Actually does** what it knows (Behavioural dimension).

If any one of these three dimensions is weak, the "organism" (the company) becomes vulnerable to a **safety incident**, just as a malnourished person is vulnerable to disease despite having skin and white blood cells.

## 1.3 Core Distinction: Safety Culture vs. Safety Climate

A frequently tested KTU conceptual question is the difference between these two terms:

| Term | Definition | Time-frame | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Safety Culture** | The **deep, enduring** values, assumptions, and beliefs about safety shared by an organization. | Long-term, strategic. | "Our company has always believed that no job is so urgent it cannot be done safely." |
| **Safety Climate** | The **snapshot** of how employees *currently perceive* safety priorities in their immediate work environment. | Short-term, tactical. | "Right now, supervisors seem to care more about production deadlines than PPE compliance." |

> [!NOTE]
> **Climate is the surface; Culture is the deep ocean.** Climate is measured by surveys; Culture is built by leadership over years. This distinction is a **favourite 3-mark KTU question**.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Frameworks (Used in KTU Answers)

The following globally accepted metrics and frameworks underpin the Safety Culture Model and should be **memorized verbatim** for KTU answers:

- **ACSNI Tripartite Model (1993)** — Person, Situation, Behaviour.
- **Cooper's Reciprocal Safety Culture Model (2000)** — Person, Behaviour, Environment, Organization, Management, Information, Leadership, Supervision, Workgroup, Job, Organisation, Group, Person (reciprocal).
- **Geller's Total Safety Culture Model** — People-based, behaviour-based, system-based integration.
- **HSE (UK Health and Safety Executive) Safety Culture Maturity Model** — 5 stages: *Emerging, Managing, Involving, Co-operating, Continually Improving*.
- **Reason's Organizational Safety Culture Triad** — Reporting Culture, Just Culture, Flexible Culture, Learning Culture (these four together form a safety culture).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bold Constants / Standard Values:**
> - **5 Stages** of the HSE Safety Culture Maturity Model.
> - **3 Dimensions** of the ACSNI Model.
> - **4 Interlocking Sub-cultures** in Reason's Triad (Reporting, Just, Flexible, Learning).
> - **Swiss Cheese Model Layers (James Reason, 1990):** Organizational Influences → Unsafe Supervision → Preconditions for Unsafe Acts → Unsafe Acts.

---

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2.1 The ACSNI Tripartite Model — Core Three Elements

The most exam-relevant model in the KTU syllabus is the **ACSNI (1993) Human Factors Study Group** framework, which divides the elements of safety culture into three interacting subsystems.

### 2.1.1 Element 1 — Personnel Factors (The "Who")

These are the **internal, psychological attributes** of the individual worker that influence safety behaviour.

**Sub-elements:**

1. **Perception of Risk** — How the individual *judges* the severity and probability of a hazard. A worker who believes a hazard is *remote* will not invest effort in controls.
2. **Attitude toward Safety** — The personal value placed on safety rules (instrumental vs. terminal value).
3. **Motivation** — Intrinsic (personal safety) vs. extrinsic (avoiding punishment) drives.
4. **Competence** — Knowledge, skill, and ability to perform the task safely.
5. **Stress & Fatigue** — Psychophysiological states that degrade decision-making.
6. **Personality Traits** — Conscientiousness, risk tolerance, locus of control.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** A worker may *know* the lockout-tagout procedure (competence) but, due to *low risk perception* and *production pressure*, may still bypass it. The Safety Culture Model explicitly accounts for these irrational behaviours.

### 2.1.2 Element 2 — Situational / Organizational Factors (The "Where & With What")

These are the **environmental, structural, and organizational conditions** that shape safety behaviour. They are essentially *latent* (dormant) factors that, like James Reason's "holes in the Swiss cheese," can align to cause an accident.

**Sub-elements:**

1. **Management Commitment & Leadership** — Visible, top-down prioritization of safety (budget allocation, walk-the-talk, safety KPIs).
2. **Policies, Procedures & Standards** — Documented, accessible, and updated safety rules.
3. **Training & Competence Development** — Continuous learning infrastructure.
4. **Communication Systems** — Vertical (top-down/bottom-up) and horizontal (peer-to-peer) information flow.
5. **Reporting & Feedback Mechanisms** — Non-punitive incident reporting, near-miss logs, safety suggestions.
6. **Resource Allocation** — Budget for PPE, maintenance, safety officers, ergonomic tools.
7. **Physical Work Environment** — Lighting, noise, ergonomics, housekeeping.
8. **Workload & Scheduling** — Overtime, shift patterns, staffing levels.
9. **Supervision Quality** — Coaching vs. policing style.
10. **Reward & Recognition Systems** — Reinforcing safe behaviour visibly.

### 2.1.3 Element 3 — Behavioural Factors (The "What They Actually Do")

This is the **observable, measurable layer** — what people *do* in practice, as opposed to what they *say* they do.

**Sub-elements:**

1. **Safety Compliance** — Adherence to rules, procedures, and regulations.
2. **Safety Participation** — Voluntary helping behaviours (mentoring, suggesting improvements, attending safety committees).
3. **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) Usage** — Consistent and correct wearing.
4. **Housekeeping** — Tidiness of the workspace as a behavioural indicator.
5. **Communication of Hazards** — Active flagging, toolbox talks, pre-job briefings.
6. **Incident Reporting Behaviour** — Willingness to report near-misses without fear.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Reciprocal Relationship:** The three elements are **not independent**. Behaviour is shaped by Situational factors (better training → safer behaviour), and Situational factors are influenced by Personnel factors (worker fatigue → management enforces shift limits). This **reciprocal causation** is the *philosophical core* of the Safety Culture Model and is a **must-draw diagram in KTU 14-mark answers**.

## 2.2 The Reason (1997) Organizational Safety Culture — Four Sub-Cultures

Professor **James Reason** extended the model by stating that a *robust safety culture* is the product of **four interlocking sub-cultures**:

1. **Reporting Culture** — Employees freely report errors, near-misses, and unsafe conditions.
2. **Just Culture** — A *non-punitive* atmosphere where people are not blamed for honest mistakes, but wilful violations are still held accountable.
3. **Flexible Culture** — Ability of the hierarchy to adapt quickly to changing conditions (e.g., emergencies) by deferring to expertise rather than rank.
4. **Learning Culture** — The organization is willing and able to *draw the right conclusions* from its safety information system and implement reforms.

$$S_{culture} = f(R, J, F, L)$$

Where:
- $S_{culture}$ = Overall safety culture maturity
- $R$ = Reporting culture strength
- $J$ = Just culture strength
- $F$ = Flexible culture strength
- $L$ = Learning culture strength

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Table — Memorize for KTU Board Exam.** *(Note: \vert used instead of \vert pipes \vert to preserve table formatting.)*

| Model / Element | Sub-elements Count | Origin / Year | KTU Cognitive Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ACSNI Tripartite Model** | 3 main elements (Personnel, Situational, Behavioural) | UK HSE, 1993 | Understand, Apply |
| **HSE Safety Culture Maturity** | 5 stages (Emerging, Managing, Involving, Co-operating, Continually Improving) | UK HSE, 2005 | Remember, Analyze |
| **Reason's Four Sub-cultures** | 4 (Reporting, Just, Flexible, Learning) | James Reason, 1997 | Understand, Apply |
| **Geller's Total Safety Culture** | 3 (Person-based, Behaviour-based, System-based) | E. Scott Geller, 1994 | Understand |
| **Cooper's Reciprocal Model** | 13 elements in 3 layers | Philip Cooper, 2000 | Analyze |
| **Reason's Swiss Cheese Layers** | 4 latent layers $\rightarrow$ 1 active failure | James Reason, 1990 | Apply, Evaluate |
| **Shapiro Formula (Accident) ** | $A = f(P, E, T)$ | — | Apply |
| **Heinrich's Triad (1:29:300)** | 1 serious : 29 minor : 300 no-injury | H.W. Heinrich, 1931 | Remember |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of the Safety Culture Model in Industry:**
> 1. **Predictive Diagnostics** — Identify which sub-culture (reporting, just, flexible, or learning) is weakest before a major incident occurs.
> 2. **Audit & Benchmarking** — The 5-stage HSE Maturity Model allows companies to score themselves (e.g., a refinery at "Stage 2 — Managing" is far riskier than one at "Stage 4 — Co-operating").
> 3. **Regulatory Compliance** — OSHA, ISO 45001, and the Seveso-III Directive explicitly require a documented safety culture assessment.
> 4. **Accident Investigation** — Post-incident, the model is used to trace root causes back to organizational and cultural failures rather than blaming the front-line operator.
> 5. **Design of Human-Machine Systems** — Inherent safety design (Kletz's hierarchy) and human-factors engineering are both informed by the situational factors identified in the model.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3.1 Analytical Derivation — The Reciprocal Causation Logic

The ACSNI model's central claim is that **safety behaviour is a function of personnel and situational factors, mediated through perception.** Let us derive the formal relationship step by step.

### Step 1: Define the Behaviour Variable

Let $B$ be the **observable safety behaviour** of a worker, measured on a normalized scale where $B \in [0, 1]$ (0 = unsafe, 1 = fully compliant with all rules).

### Step 2: Define the Personnel Factor Vector

The personnel attributes can be expressed as a vector:

$$
P = \begin{bmatrix} p_1 \\ p_2 \\ p_3 \\ p_4 \\ p_5 \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} \text{Risk Perception} \\ \text{Attitude toward Safety} \\ \text{Motivation} \\ \text{Competence} \\ \text{Stress Level (inverted)} \end{bmatrix}
$$

Each $p_i \in [0, 1]$ and is measurable via validated psychometric instruments (e.g., Nordic Safety Climate Questionnaire, NOSACQ-50).

### Step 3: Define the Situational Factor Vector

The organizational / situational attributes form another vector:

$$
S = \begin{bmatrix} s_1 \\ s_2 \\ s_3 \\ s_4 \\ s_5 \\ s_6 \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} \text{Management Commitment} \\ \text{Policy Clarity} \\ \text{Training Quality} \\ \text{Supervision Quality} \\ \text{Resource Availability} \\ \text{Communication Openness} \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 4: Combine via a Weighted Multiplicative Model

A common theoretical form used in human-reliability analysis (HRA) and human-factors engineering to combine these vectors is the **multiplicative weighted model**, because a zero in *either* dimension should collapse the overall safety score to zero (a single critical failure is enough to break the chain).

$$
B = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i \cdot p_i \right) \times \left( \sum_{j=1}^{6} \alpha_j \cdot s_j \right)
$$

Where:
- $w_i$ = weight of personnel factor $i$, with $\sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i = 1$
- $\alpha_j$ = weight of situational factor $j$, with $\sum_{j=1}^{6} \alpha_j = 1$

### Step 5: Apply the Reciprocal Feedback Loop

A truly *cultural* model does not stop here. The behaviour $B$ itself feeds back to modify the situational factors over time:

$$
S_{t+1} = S_t + \beta \cdot (B_t - B_{target})
$$

Where:
- $S_t$ = situational factors at time $t$
- $B_t$ = observed behaviour at time $t$
- $B_{target}$ = the management-desired behaviour level
- $\beta$ = organizational learning rate ($\beta \in [0, 1]$)

**Interpretation:** If the observed behaviour $B_t$ is *lower* than the target $B_{target}$, the term $(B_t - B_{target})$ is negative, and the situational factors *decline* over time (complacency sets in). Conversely, if $B_t > B_{target}$, situational factors *improve* (positive reinforcement, training, audits). This is the **feedback loop** that distinguishes a *living safety culture* from a *static safety policy document*.

### Step 6: Steady-State Equilibrium

The steady-state occurs when $B_t = B_{target}$, giving $S_{t+1} = S_t$, i.e., a self-sustaining equilibrium. This is what the **HSE Maturity Model Stage 5 — "Continually Improving"** actually represents mathematically: a system in dynamic equilibrium with its environment, with $\beta$ maintained at a high value through active leadership.

$$
\boxed{S_{t+1} = S_t \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad B_t = B_{target}}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** While KTU PESFT524 rarely demands heavy numerical computation, a *single worked example* demonstrating your grasp of the model is worth 2-3 bonus marks in 14-mark answers.

**Problem:** A chemical plant in Kerala is assessed using the ACSNI model. The auditor assigns the following normalized scores:

**Personnel vector $P$:**
- Risk Perception: $p_1 = 0.6$
- Attitude: $p_2 = 0.8$
- Motivation: $p_3 = 0.7$
- Competence: $p_4 = 0.9$
- Stress (inverted): $p_5 = 0.4$

**Personnel weights:** $w_1 = 0.25, w_2 = 0.20, w_3 = 0.20, w_4 = 0.20, w_5 = 0.15$

**Situational vector $S$:**
- Management Commitment: $s_1 = 0.9$
- Policy Clarity: $s_2 = 0.8$
- Training Quality: $s_3 = 0.7$
- Supervision: $s_4 = 0.5$
- Resources: $s_5 = 0.6$
- Communication: $s_6 = 0.4$

**Situational weights:** $\alpha_1 = 0.30, \alpha_2 = 0.20, \alpha_3 = 0.15, \alpha_4 = 0.15, \alpha_5 = 0.10, \alpha_6 = 0.10$

**Solution:**

$$
P_{score} = (0.25 \times 0.6) + (0.20 \times 0.8) + (0.20 \times 0.7) + (0.20 \times 0.9) + (0.15 \times 0.4)
$$

$$
P_{score} = 0.150 + 0.160 + 0.140 + 0.180 + 0.060 = 0.690
$$

$$
S_{score} = (0.30 \times 0.9) + (0.20 \times 0.8) + (0.15 \times 0.7) + (0.15 \times 0.5) + (0.10 \times 0.6) + (0.10 \times 0.4)
$$

$$
S_{score} = 0.270 + 0.160 + 0.105 + 0.075 + 0.060 + 0.040 = 0.710
$$

$$
B = P_{score} \times S_{score} = 0.690 \times 0.710 = 0.490
$$

**Interpretation:** The plant's safety behaviour score is $B = 0.49$ (49%), which is **below the acceptable threshold of 0.75**. The audit correctly flags it as **HSE Maturity Stage 2 ("Managing")** rather than Stage 4 ("Co-operating"). The weakest dimensions are **Stress (0.4)**, **Supervision (0.5)**, and **Communication (0.4)** — these are the *prescriptive* targets for the next year's safety improvement plan.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Each Element to a Real Industrial Failure

| Element | Failure Mode | Real Case Study | Consequence |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Personnel — Risk Perception** | Operators believed a routine task carried "no real risk" | Texas City Refinery (2005) | 15 killed, 170 injured |
| **Situational — Management Commitment** | Cost-cutting, deferred maintenance | Deepwater Horizon (2010) | 11 killed, $65 billion loss |
| **Behavioural — PPE Non-Compliance** | Workers refused to wear respirators | Bhopal Gas Tragedy (1984) | 3,800+ immediate deaths |
| **Reporting Culture** | Near-misses hidden to avoid paperwork | Challenger Disaster (1986) | 7 killed |
| **Just Culture** | Punitive atmosphere, fear of reporting | Boeing 737 MAX (2018) | 346 killed in two crashes |
| **Learning Culture** | Previous lessons not internalized | Fukushima Daiichi (2011) | Nuclear meltdown |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4.1 ACSNI Tripartite Model — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeA["PERSONNEL FACTORS"] --> nodeB["Risk Perception, Attitude, Motivation, Competence, Stress"]
    nodeC["SITUATIONAL FACTORS"] --> nodeD["Management Commitment, Policies, Training, Supervision, Resources, Communication"]
    nodeE["BEHAVIOURAL FACTORS"] --> nodeF["Safety Compliance, Participation, PPE Use, Housekeeping, Reporting"]
    nodeB --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeB <--> nodeD
    nodeF --> nodeG["OUTCOME: Safe Operations or Incident"]
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeA -.-> nodeC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram for a KTU 14-mark answer:** Personnel and Situational factors *jointly* produce Behavioural factors. Behaviour feeds back to influence both Personnel (experience) and Situational (organizational learning) factors. The final outcome — an incident or safe operation — emerges from the *interaction* of all three.

## 4.2 Reason's Four Sub-Cultures — Mermaid Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeRC["REPORTING CULTURE"] --> nodeJC["JUST CULTURE"]
    nodeJC --> nodeFC["FLEXIBLE CULTURE"]
    nodeFC --> nodeLC["LEARNING CULTURE"]
    nodeLC --> nodeRC
    nodeJC --> nodeOS["ORGANIZATIONAL SAFETY"]
    nodeRC --> nodeOS
    nodeLC --> nodeOS
    nodeFC --> nodeOS
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **circular feedback** between the four sub-cultures is the hallmark of a *mature* safety culture. If **any one of the four** is broken, the entire safety culture collapses. For example, a *just culture* without a *reporting culture* leads to no data; a *learning culture* without *flexibility* leads to ritualistic compliance.

## 4.3 HSE 5-Stage Safety Culture Maturity Model — Mermaid Ladder Flow

```mermaid
graph TB
    node1["STAGE 1: EMERGING"] --> node2["STAGE 2: MANAGING"]
    node2 --> node3["STAGE 3: INVOLVING"]
    node3 --> node4["STAGE 4: CO-OPERATING"]
    node4 --> node5["STAGE 5: CONTINUALLY IMPROVING"]
    node1 -.->|Lowest Risk Awareness| node1
    node5 -.->|Highest Risk Awareness| node5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mapping industrial cases to the maturity ladder (high-yield for KTU):**
> - **Stage 1 (Emerging):** Safety is seen as an external compliance burden. *Example: small unregulated workshops.*
> - **Stage 2 (Managing):** Basic safety systems exist but are top-down. *Example: Bhopal Union Carbide (1984).*
> - **Stage 3 (Involving):** Workers are consulted on safety matters. *Example: many current ISO 45001-certified SMEs.*
> - **Stage 4 (Co-operating):** Safety is co-owned by management and workers. *Example: mature aviation / nuclear sectors.*
> - **Stage 5 (Continually Improving):** Safety is embedded in DNA; self-correcting system. *Example: top-tier DuPont plants pre-2000, post-Chernobyl nuclear operators.*

## 4.4 Cooper's Reciprocal Safety Culture Model — Mermaid Layered Flow

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeP["PERSON"] --> nodeB["BEHAVIOUR"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["ENVIRONMENT"]
    nodeE --> nodeP
    nodeO["ORGANIZATION"] --> nodeM["MANAGEMENT"]
    nodeM --> nodeI["INFORMATION"]
    nodeI --> nodeL["LEADERSHIP"]
    nodeL --> nodeS["SUPERVISION"]
    nodeS --> nodeW["WORKGROUP"]
    nodeW --> nodeJ["JOB"]
    nodeJ --> nodeP
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cooper's Reciprocal Model (2000) — Why it's called "Reciprocal":** Each element *reciprocally* influences the others in a continuous loop. For instance, a *Person* (worker) shapes the *Workgroup*, which in turn shapes the *Person* through peer pressure, social learning, and group norms. This is the **psychological-social-organizational** layer that pure engineering cannot address.

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`
**Define Safety Culture. List any three elements of the ACSNI Tripartite Model.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Safety Culture is the product of individual and group values, attitudes, perceptions, competencies, and patterns of behaviour that determine the commitment to and the style and proficiency of an organization's health and safety management. *(INSAG-4, 1991)*
- **Three Elements of ACSNI Model (1 mark):**
  1. **Personnel Factors** — psychological attributes of individuals.
  2. **Situational Factors** — organizational and environmental conditions.
  3. **Behavioural Factors** — observable safety actions of individuals.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse "Safety Culture" with "Safety Climate" or list only *one* ACSNI element, losing 1 mark. Always give the **INSAG-4 source year** for full credit.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]`
**Differentiate between Safety Culture and Safety Climate. Why is the distinction important in safety management?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Safety Culture (1.5 marks):** Refers to the **deep-rooted, long-term values, beliefs, and assumptions** about safety within an organization. It is *enduring* and *strategic*, shaped over years by leadership. Difficult to measure directly.
- **Safety Climate (1 mark):** Refers to the **current, snapshot perception** of safety priorities held by employees. It is *tactical*, measurable through short surveys, and reflects the *mood* of the workforce.
- **Importance of the distinction (0.5 marks):** Climate is the *visible indicator* of culture; measuring climate periodically allows managers to track whether their cultural interventions (training, leadership walks, etc.) are *shifting* the underlying culture in the right direction.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Error:** Writing "Safety Climate is the same as Safety Culture" — this fetches **0 marks**. Examiners explicitly test the distinction.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each 14-mark question is split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Provide *both* Question A and Question B alternatives with full model solutions.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Full Model Solution

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`**

**(a)** *Explain in detail the ACSNI Tripartite Model of Safety Culture. Discuss the Personnel, Situational, and Behavioural factors with suitable examples.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **ACSNI (Advisory Committee on the Safety of Nuclear Installations) Human Factors Study Group** published its tripartite framework in **1993** as a response to growing recognition that technical safety measures alone were insufficient to prevent major industrial disasters. The model posits that safety culture emerges from the dynamic interaction of three subsystems: **Personnel, Situational, and Behavioural factors**.

**1. Personnel Factors (2 marks):** These are the *internal, psychological attributes* of the individual worker. Key sub-elements include:
- *Risk Perception:* A welder's belief that the gas-line is inert before cutting determines whether he verifies it. In the **Texas City Refinery (2005)** disaster, operators did not perceive the hydrocarbon liquid in the raffinate splitter as a serious explosion hazard.
- *Attitude & Motivation:* Workers motivated by *production bonuses* may unconsciously deprioritize safety. Conversely, intrinsic motivation (personal/family well-being) is the strongest predictor of safe behaviour.
- *Competence & Training:* Knowledge of the lockout-tagout procedure, but also the *judgment* to know when it applies.
- *Stress, Fatigue, Personality:* Conscientiousness predicts safety; fatigue degrades vigilance.

**2. Situational Factors (2.5 marks):** These are the *organizational, environmental, and structural conditions* that shape worker behaviour. Key sub-elements include:
- *Management Commitment & Leadership:* Visible, authentic prioritization of safety by top management. **DuPont's** zero-injury culture is the textbook example — plant managers were evaluated on safety metrics equally with financial metrics.
- *Policies, Procedures & Standards:* Clear, accessible, and up-to-date documentation. The Bhopal disaster revealed *obsolete* operating procedures and *absent* hazard communication protocols.
- *Training, Communication, Supervision, Resource Allocation:* These collectively form the *work system* within which the worker operates.

**3. Behavioural Factors (1.5 marks):** The *observable* layer. Safety behaviour decomposes into:
- *Safety Compliance:* Adherence to formal rules and procedures.
- *Safety Participation:* Voluntary, citizenship-type behaviours — mentoring juniors, suggesting improvements.
- *Reporting Behaviour:* Willingness to report near-misses and unsafe conditions without fear.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The three dimensions are *reciprocally linked*. A worker's behaviour is shaped by their perception (personnel) and the organizational environment (situational); in turn, aggregated behaviour shapes the long-term culture and influences future situational investments (e.g., training budgets). A robust safety culture requires *all three* dimensions to be simultaneously strong.

---

**(b)** *Discuss James Reason's four sub-cultures of safety culture. How do they collectively form a mature safety culture?* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Professor **James Reason (1997)** proposed that an organization's safety culture is not a monolithic entity but the *integrated product* of **four distinct but interlocking sub-cultures**. The absence or weakness of any one of these sub-cultures will undermine the entire safety system.

**1. Reporting Culture (2 marks):** A reporting culture exists when employees feel *psychologically safe* and *procedurally enabled* to report errors, near-misses, hazards, and unsafe conditions without fear of reprisal. It is the *information-gathering* layer of safety culture. **NASA's pre-Challenger culture** lacked a robust reporting culture — engineers' concerns about O-ring erosion were suppressed under schedule pressure, leading to the 1986 disaster. A strong reporting culture requires **non-punitive reporting systems**, **anonymous channels**, and **management visibly acting on reports**.

**2. Just Culture (2 marks):** Reason introduced the *just culture* concept to address the paradox of the reporting culture: if reporting is *too* safe, people will report dishonest mistakes as honest ones to avoid blame. A just culture distinguishes between:
- *Honest human errors* — supported, not punished.
- *At-risk behaviours* — coached.
- *Wilful violations* — held accountable.

The **Boeing 737 MAX MCAS scandal (2018)** illustrates a *failed just culture* — engineers who flagged the MCAS design were sidelined rather than heard, with catastrophic results.

**3. Flexible Culture (1.5 marks):** A flexible culture shifts authority *temporarily* to the person with the most expertise during emergencies, regardless of formal rank. In a hierarchical, rigid culture, a junior operator will not override a senior manager's wrong decision. The **2003 Columbia disaster** review highlighted that NASA's *bureaucratic* culture had become inflexible — dissenting opinions about foam strike damage were not elevated by rank.

**4. Learning Culture (1.5 marks):** The organization must be willing and able to *draw the right conclusions* from its safety information system and *implement reforms*. The **Fukushima Daiichi (2011)** disaster was partially attributed to a weak learning culture — the regulator and operator had data about tsunami risks but did not integrate it into plant safety cases.

**Synthesis (bonus for integration):** The four sub-cultures form a **virtuous cycle**: reporting produces data, just culture ensures honest data, flexibility ensures correct response, and learning ensures future prevention. A mature safety culture has **all four** at high strength.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**`[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`**

**(a)** *Explain the HSE Safety Culture Maturity Model. Describe its five stages with examples.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Health and Safety Executive (HSE) of the United Kingdom** developed a 5-stage Safety Culture Maturity Model (sometimes called the "Safety Culture Ladder") as a **diagnostic and benchmarking tool**. It allows an organization to identify *where it currently sits* on the journey toward safety excellence and to prescribe targeted interventions.

**Stage 1 — Emerging (1.5 marks):** The organization views safety as an *external burden* imposed by regulators. There is little internal ownership. Safety incidents are seen as the *fault of individuals*. Example: Small, unregulated informal-sector workshops in India where workers handle chemicals without training or PPE.

**Stage 2 — Managing (1.5 marks):** The organization has *basic safety systems* in place (incident reports, audits, safety officers) but they are driven by *senior management* in a top-down fashion. Workers are passive recipients. Example: **Union Carbide India (Bhopal) plant in 1984** had nominal safety systems but they were hollowed out by cost-cutting.

**Stage 3 — Involving (1.5 marks):** Workers are *consulted* on safety matters through committees and toolbox talks. There is a two-way communication channel. Example: Many current **ISO 45001-certified small and medium enterprises** in Kerala's manufacturing sector.

**Stage 4 — Co-operating (1.5 marks):** Safety is *co-owned* by management and workforce. Employees actively participate in safety improvement, and management trusts workers' expertise. Example: **Mature aviation sector (Boeing pre-2000s)** where pilots, engineers, and managers jointly own safety.

**Stage 5 — Continually Improving (1 mark):** Safety is *embedded in the organization's DNA*. The system is *self-correcting* — incidents, near-misses, and audits feed an automatic improvement loop. Example: **Post-Chernobyl nuclear operators** in Western Europe who integrated continuous learning into regulatory frameworks.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often describe only *three* stages or list them without giving an **industrial example** for each. Each example carries **0.5 marks** — losing all examples costs 2.5 of your 7 marks.

---

**(b)** *Apply the Cooper's Reciprocal Safety Culture Model to a manufacturing industry. Discuss the 13 elements briefly.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Cooper's Reciprocal Safety Culture Model (2000)** is one of the most comprehensive frameworks in the safety literature. Unlike ACSNI's three-element structure, Cooper proposes **13 elements** organized into three broad layers: **Person-related**, **Behaviour-related**, and **Situation-related**, with explicit *reciprocal causation* between them.

**Layer 1 — Person-Related Elements (2 marks):**
1. **Person** — the individual worker's knowledge, skill, attitude, perception.
2. **Behaviour** — observable actions of the person.
3. **Environment** — physical, chemical, ergonomic, and psychosocial environment of the workplace.
*(These three form a tight reciprocal loop: a person's behaviour is shaped by the environment, and the environment is changed by the person's behaviour.)*

**Layer 2 — Situation-Related Elements (2.5 marks):**
4. **Job** — task design, complexity, autonomy, monotony.
5. **Workgroup** — peer group, social norms, team cohesion.
6. **Supervision** — quality of first-line supervision (coaching vs. policing).
7. **Leadership** — middle-management role in safety messaging.
8. **Information & Communication** — flow of safety-relevant information.
9. **Management** — top management commitment and resource allocation.
10. **Organization** — overall structure, policies, and culture.
*(These elements form a *causal chain* from Job → Workgroup → Supervision → Leadership → Information → Management → Organization.)*

**Layer 3 — Reciprocal Macro-Loop (2.5 marks):**
The model closes with a *macro-loop* in which:
- The *Organization* (Layer 2) shapes the *Environment* (Layer 1) — through budgets, policies, and culture.
- The *Person* (Layer 1) shapes the *Organization* (Layer 2) — through aggregated behaviour, reporting, and learning.

**Application to a Manufacturing Plant (Kerala context — e.g., a cashew-processing or chemical plant):**
- *Person-Behaviour-Environment loop:* A worker (Person) fails to wear ear protection (Behaviour) in a noisy section (Environment) because management has not installed acoustic barriers or enforced PPE policy. The reciprocal intervention is to *simultaneously* train the worker (Person), discipline the non-compliance (Behaviour), and install the barriers (Environment).
- *Job-Workgroup-Supervision chain:* A monotonous sorting task (Job) leads to peer group complacency (Workgroup) and a passive supervisor (Supervision). Reciprocal intervention: job rotation, peer-safety-champions, and supervisor coaching.

**Conclusion:** Cooper's model is valuable because it explicitly **connects the technical and human layers**, allowing safety professionals to identify *where in the chain* to intervene for maximum effect.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students most commonly lose marks in PESFT524 Module 1 answers:**
> 1. **Confusing the authors of different models.** Reason = Swiss Cheese; Cooper = Reciprocal; Geller = Total Safety Culture; ACSNI = Tripartite. Mixing them up costs full sub-question marks.
> 2. **Listing elements without explanation.** A 7-mark question demands *narrative*, not bullets alone. Use a paragraph form for each element.
> 3. **Forgetting the year and source.** KTU examiners award partial credit for citations (e.g., "ACSNI, 1993" or "Reason, 1997"). Always include the year.
> 4. **Ignoring the reciprocal relationship.** The defining feature of the Safety Culture Model is the *interaction* between elements, not a flat list. Dedicate at least **1 mark's worth** of content to the inter-relationship.
> 5. **No real-world example.** Generic answers without an industrial case (Bhopal, Texas City, Chernobyl, Deepwater Horizon, Boeing 737 MAX) lose 1-2 marks.
> 6. **Treating the model as a "list" rather than a "framework."** Use phrases like *"reciprocal causation,"* *"feedback loop,"* *"dynamic equilibrium"* to demonstrate higher-order (Blooms Level 4-5) thinking.
