## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Scheme Board Exam (PESFT524 Module 1):**

- ☐ **Safety Culture** = Long-term values, beliefs, assumptions about safety. Source: **INSAG-4, 1991** (post-Chernobyl).
- ☐ **Safety Climate** = Short-term snapshot perception, measurable by surveys. **Do not confuse the two.**
- ☐ **ACSNI Tripartite Model (1993)** = **3 elements:** Personnel + Situational + Behavioural. Reciprocal causation.
- ☐ **Personnel Factors** = Risk perception, attitude, motivation, competence, stress, personality.
- ☐ **Situational Factors** = Management commitment, policies, training, supervision, communication, resources, environment.
- ☐ **Behavioural Factors** = Compliance, participation, PPE use, housekeeping, hazard communication, reporting.
- ☐ **Reason's Four Sub-cultures (1997)** = Reporting, Just, Flexible, Learning. **All four are mandatory** for a mature culture.
- ☐ **Just Culture** = Non-punitive for honest errors, accountability for wilful violations.
- ☐ **HSE Maturity Model (2005)** = 5 stages: **Emerging → Managing → Involving → Co-operating → Continually Improving.**
- ☐ **Cooper's Reciprocal Model (2000)** = 13 elements across Person / Behaviour / Environment / Job / Workgroup / Supervision / Leadership / Information / Management / Organisation loop.
- ☐ **Geller's Total Safety Culture (1994)** = Person-based + Behaviour-based + System-based.
- ☐ **Reason's Swiss Cheese Model (1990)** = 4 layers: Organizational Influences, Unsafe Supervision, Preconditions for Unsafe Acts, Unsafe Acts.
- ☐ **Heinrich's Triad (1931)** = 1 serious : 29 minor : 300 no-injury (domino causation of accidents).
- ☐ **Reciprocal Causation** = The defining mathematical and conceptual property of the Safety Culture Model.
- ☐ **Industrial cases to memorize:** Bhopal (1984), Challenger (1986), Chernobyl (1986), Texas City (2005), Deepwater Horizon (2010), Fukushima (2011), Boeing 737 MAX (2018).
- ☐ **Bloom's Level targeted in KTU exams:** Mostly *Understand* (L2) and *Apply* (L3); advanced 14-mark questions demand *Analyze & Evaluate* (L4-L5).
- ☐ **Always cite the year and author** for full credit.
- ☐ **Draw at least one diagram** (ACSNI triangular relationship, Reason's four sub-cultures cycle, or HSE maturity ladder) in any 14-mark answer on this topic.
- ☐ **Final formula to remember:** $B = P_{score} \times S_{score}$ — behaviour is the product of personnel and situational factors; a zero in either collapses safety.

> **End of Module 1 — Elements of the Safety Culture Model — KTU 2024 Scheme Premium Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Organisational Behaviour
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT524 - HUMAN AND ORGANIZATIONAL FACTORS IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Organisational Behaviour

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Organisational Behaviour (OB)** is the systematic study and application of knowledge about how individuals, groups, and structures affect behaviour within organisations. It is an interdisciplinary field drawing from **psychology, sociology, anthropology, political science, and economics** to understand human behaviour in workplace settings.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PESFT524)**, Organisational Behaviour is examined as the foundational behavioural science lens through which **safety performance, human reliability, and organizational safety culture** are engineered. OB is not a "soft" subject in safety engineering — it is the **human-centred sub-system** of any Safety Management System (SMS).

### 1.1.1 Working Definition (KTU Board Standard)

> **Organisational Behaviour (OB)** is the study of the **3 levels of analysis** — *(a) the individual, (b) the group, and (c) the organisation* — and how each level influences workplace behaviour, productivity, job satisfaction, commitment, and crucially, **safety outcomes**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The OB = "The Human Operating System of a Company"**
>
> Imagine a company is a **smartphone**. The hardware (machines, buildings, money) is the *organisational structure*. The apps (processes, protocols) are the *organisational systems*. But none of it works without the **Android/iOS underneath** — that is **Organisational Behaviour**. It is the invisible "operating system" that decides:
> - How an employee **reacts** to a near-miss report (individual level)
> - How a **shift team** coordinates during a fire drill (group level)
> - How the **whole company** redesigns its safety policy after an incident (organisational level)
>
> Without a stable OB, even the best safety hardware fails. **The Piper Alpha disaster (1988)** and **Bhopal (1984)** are textbook examples where organisational behaviour — not hardware — caused the catastrophe.

## 1.3 Why OB Matters in Safety Engineering (PESFT524 Context)

The **three dependent variables** in OB that every safety engineer must master:

| Variable | Definition | Safety Implication |
|----------|-----------|--------------------|
| **Productivity** | Output per unit input | A stressed, demotivated worker is a **safety liability** |
| **Absenteeism** | Unscheduled absence rate | High absenteeism → undertrained staff operating critical systems |
| **Turnover** | Rate of employees leaving the organisation | Loss of *tacit safety knowledge* and erosion of safety culture |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Every KTU question on OB will test the **3-level model (Individual → Group → Organisation)** and the **3 dependent variables (Productivity, Absenteeism, Turnover)**. Memorise this 3 × 3 matrix.

## 1.4 Contributing Disciplines to OB

OB is fundamentally an **interdisciplinary** field. A KTU answer that names the contributing disciplines scores higher than one that does not.

- **Psychology** — studies individual behaviour (perception, motivation, learning)
- **Sociology** — studies group behaviour and social systems
- **Social Psychology** — studies interaction between individuals and groups
- **Anthropology** — studies culture, norms, and values
- **Political Science** — studies power, conflict, and organisational politics
- **Economics** — studies efficiency, incentives, and resource allocation

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Levels of Organisational Behaviour (The OB Pyramid)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Framework:** Stephen P. Robbins' **3-Level OB Model** is the most tested framework in KTU exams. The hierarchy, from micro to macro, is: **Individual → Group → Organisation System**.

### Level 1 — The Individual (Micro Level)

At the lowest level, OB studies the psychological makeup of a single employee. Key individual-level variables:

- **Personality** — the unique psychological pattern shaping thought and behaviour
- **Perception** — how a worker interprets a hazard (e.g., seeing a wet floor as a "minor inconvenience" vs. a "slip risk")
- **Motivation** — internal drives that energise behaviour (linked to safety compliance)
- **Attitudes** — evaluative judgements (e.g., attitude toward PPE)
- **Emotions** — affective states (fear, anger, joy)
- **Values** — core beliefs about what is "right" in safety practice
- **Learning** — how workers acquire and modify safety behaviours

### Level 2 — The Group (Meso Level)

Humans are social animals; safety behaviour is profoundly shaped by group membership.

- **Group dynamics** — formation, norms, cohesiveness
- **Communication** — upward, downward, lateral safety reporting
- **Leadership** — supervisory influence on safety climate
- **Power and politics** — who controls the safety budget
- **Conflict and negotiation** — inter-departmental safety disputes
- **Team building** — cross-functional safety audits

### Level 3 — The Organisation System (Macro Level)

The widest lens; the organisation as a whole.

- **Organisational structure** — tall vs. flat hierarchies affect safety reporting speed
- **Organisational culture** — the **shared assumptions** about safety (Schein's model)
- **Organisational design** — divisional, functional, matrix
- **Change management** — Kotter's 8-step model for safety transformation
- **Strategy and environment** — competitive pressure vs. safety investment

## 2.2 Contributing Disciplines — Detailed Mapping

Each academic discipline contributes a specific toolkit to OB:

| Discipline | Unit of Analysis | Key Contribution to OB |
|------------|------------------|------------------------|
| Psychology | Individual | Motivation, perception, personality, learning theory |
| Sociology | Group / Society | Group dynamics, bureaucracy, formal organisations |
| Social Psychology | Individual in group | Attitude change, communication, group decision-making |
| Anthropology | Culture | Comparative org. studies, organisational culture, rituals |
| Political Science | Power structures | Power bases, conflict, organisational politics |
| Economics | Resource allocation | Incentive design, efficiency-wage theory, cost of accidents |

## 2.3 The Nature and Scope of OB — KTU Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key OB Characteristics (Board-Favourite Answer Points):**
>
> 1. OB is a **behavioural science** — it studies what people *do*.
> 2. OB has an **applied orientation** — it focuses on improving workplace outcomes.
> 3. OB uses **scientific method** — hypotheses are tested, not assumed.
> 4. OB takes a **contingency approach** — there is no "one best way"; it depends on the situation.
> 5. OB addresses **three dependent variables**: productivity, absenteeism, turnover (+ safety, in PESFT524).

## 2.4 Contingency Approach — The Core OB Philosophy

The **contingency approach** (also called the situational approach) is the philosophical backbone of modern OB. It states:

$$\text{There is no single best way to manage; the optimal action depends on the situation.}$$

This is critical in safety engineering because:
- A **safety incentive scheme** that works in an office may fail on a construction site.
- A **flat hierarchy** improves reporting in tech firms but is dangerous in nuclear plant operations.
- A **team-based safety culture** suits aviation crews but may not fit a small manufacturing unit.

## 2.5 KTU Formula / Concept Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> OB is largely qualitative. However, the following quantitative relationships are tested in KTU:

| Formula / Relationship | Expression | Variables | Unit |
|------------------------|------------|-----------|------|
| Absenteeism Rate | $A = \dfrac{\text{Periods of Absence}}{\text{Periods of Work Scheduled}} \times 100$ | A, absence periods | % |
| Turnover Rate | $T = \dfrac{\text{Number of Separations}}{\text{Average Number of Employees}} \times 100$ | T, separations | % |
| Labour Productivity | $P = \dfrac{\text{Output}}{\text{Labour Input (man-hours)}}$ | P, output | units/man-hr |
| OB Determinant Function | $B = f(P, G, O)$ | B = behaviour, P = person, G = group, O = organisation | dimensional |
| Hawthorne Effect (qualitative) | $\text{Productivity} \uparrow \text{ when } \text{workers know they are observed}$ | attention, productivity | qualitative |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Safety Engineering

The OB framework is directly applied in:

- **Behaviour-Based Safety (BBS)** programmes — modifying individual worker behaviour using reinforcement theory.
- **Safety Culture Maturity models** — moving an organisation from *pathological* to *generative* (Westrum model).
- **Job Hazard Analysis (JHA)** — accounting for human factors and group pressure.
- **Crew Resource Management (CRM)** — applied in aviation and nuclear industries.
- **Post-incident organisational learning** — turning accidents into organisational knowledge (Reason's Swiss Cheese model).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Determinant Derivation

## 3.1 The Fundamental OB Determinant Equation

The single most-cited equation in OB literature (Robbins & Judge) expresses that every workplace behaviour is a function of three nested systems:

$$B = f(P \,\rightarrow\, G \,\rightarrow\, O)$$

Where:
- $B$ = Behaviour (the **dependent variable**)
- $P$ = Person-level factors (independent variable set 1)
- $G$ = Group-level factors (independent variable set 2)
- $O$ = Organisation-level factors (independent variable set 3)

### Step-by-Step Expansion of the Determinant Equation

**Step 1 — Identify the dependent variable.**

In safety OB, the typical dependent variable is **unsafe behaviour** (or its inverse, **safe behaviour**). We want to *predict and modify* $B$.

**Step 2 — Decompose person-level factors (P).**

$$P = \{P_p, P_{per}, P_m, P_a, P_e, P_v, P_l\}$$

Where the components are personality ($P_p$), perception ($P_{per}$), motivation ($P_m$), attitudes ($P_a$), emotions ($P_e$), values ($P_v$), and learning ($P_l$).

**Step 3 — Decompose group-level factors (G).**

$$G = \{G_d, G_c, G_l, G_p, G_{cn}\}$$

Where the components are group dynamics ($G_d$), communication ($G_c$), leadership ($G_l$), power/politics ($G_p$), and conflict/negotiation ($G_{cn}$).

**Step 4 — Decompose organisation-level factors (O).**

$$O = \{O_s, O_c, O_d, O_{cm}, O_{st}\}$$

Where the components are structure ($O_s$), culture ($O_c$), design ($O_d$), change management ($O_{cm}$), and strategy/environment ($O_{st}$).

**Step 5 — Combine into the full functional form.**

$$\boxed{B = f\bigl(\{P_p, P_{per}, P_m, P_a, P_e, P_v, P_l\},\, \{G_d, G_c, G_l, G_p, G_{cn}\},\, \{O_s, O_c, O_d, O_{cm}, O_{st}\}\bigr)}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the contingency modifier.**

Because OB is contingent, the actual functional form varies with context $C$:

$$B = f(P, G, O \,\vert\, C)$$

Where $C$ represents situational moderators — task complexity, environmental uncertainty, national culture, industry sector, regulatory environment, etc.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The three-letter mnemonic **PGO** (Person–Group–Organisation) is the highest-frequency answer trigger in KTU OB questions. Always structure your answers in this order.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Absenteeism Formula (Worked Step-by-Step)

The KTU 2024 Scheme includes quantitative assessment, so deriving the standard HR formula is a frequent 3-mark sub-question.

**Step 1 — Define the numerator.**

Let $N_{abs}$ = number of unexcused absence periods (e.g., unscheduled shifts missed) in a given period.

**Step 2 — Define the denominator.**

Let $N_{work}$ = number of work periods scheduled for the same group in the same period.

**Step 3 — Form the ratio.**

$$A = \frac{N_{abs}}{N_{work}}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to percentage.**

$$A_{\%} = \frac{N_{abs}}{N_{work}} \times 100$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

Suppose a 10-person safety-critical team has 250 scheduled shifts in a quarter. Total unscheduled absences recorded = 30 shifts.

$$A_{\%} = \frac{30}{250} \times 100 = 12\%$$

This **12% absenteeism** in a safety-critical team is a **red flag** — it indicates probable **low morale, group pressure, or organisational culture issues**, all OB-level root causes.

## 3.3 The Three Dependent Variables — Comparative Derivation

Each dependent variable in OB is defined with a precise operational formula. KTU examiners often test these in tabular form.

| Variable | Numerator | Denominator | Multiplier | Interpretation |
|----------|-----------|-------------|------------|----------------|
| Absenteeism Rate ($A$) | Periods of absence | Periods scheduled | $\times 100$ | Disengagement indicator |
| Turnover Rate ($T$) | Number of separations | Average headcount | $\times 100$ | Retention / culture indicator |
| Productivity ($P$) | Units of output | Man-hours worked | $\times 1$ | Efficiency / system health |

**Cross-Variable Correlation (Analytical Insight):**

In a healthy organisation, the three variables move as follows:

$$A \downarrow \quad,\quad T \downarrow \quad,\quad P \uparrow$$

In a dysfunctional (or unsafe) organisation, the reverse pattern often emerges:

$$A \uparrow \quad,\quad T \uparrow \quad,\quad P \downarrow$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety Implication (PESFT524):** High absenteeism in a high-risk plant is a **leading indicator** of accidents. It is far more predictive than lagging indicators like Lost Time Injury Frequency Rate (LTIFR). This is why modern BBS programmes track OB variables as **safety precursors**.

## 3.4 The Contributing Disciplines — Hierarchical Mapping

OB sits on a knowledge pyramid. The base is the **discipline**, the apex is the **OB application**.

$$\text{Disciplines} \rightarrow \text{Concepts} \rightarrow \text{Theories} \rightarrow \text{Models} \rightarrow \text{Applications}$$

- **Psychology** (base) → Motivation concept → Maslow's Need Hierarchy → Workforce planning model → Safety incentive design (apex).
- **Sociology** (base) → Group concept → Groupthink theory → Decision quality model → Crew Resource Management (apex).
- **Anthropology** (base) → Culture concept → Schein's 3-level model → Culture audit → Safety culture transformation (apex).

This hierarchical chain is what makes OB a **science** rather than a collection of "soft skills".

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define Organisational Behaviour. List the three levels at which OB is studied.**

**Model Answer:**

Organisational Behaviour (OB) is the systematic study of how individuals, groups, and structures affect human behaviour within organisations, with the goal of applying that knowledge to improve organisational effectiveness.

The three levels of analysis in OB are:

1. **Individual Level** — study of personality, perception, motivation, attitudes, and values of a single employee.
2. **Group Level** — study of group dynamics, communication, leadership, power, and conflict among two or more people.
3. **Organisation Level** — study of organisational structure, culture, design, change, and environment as a whole system.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often write only the *definition* and forget the **3 levels**. This forfeits 1 to 2 marks instantly. Always pair the definition with the 3-level enumeration in every KTU answer.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Identify the three dependent variables in OB. Why is the contingency approach central to OB?**

**Model Answer:**

The three dependent variables that OB seeks to explain and predict are:

1. **Productivity** — the efficiency of converting inputs into outputs.
2. **Absenteeism** — the rate at which employees are absent from work.
3. **Turnover** — the rate at which employees leave the organisation.

(For PESFT524, we add a fourth dependent variable: **Safety Performance**.)

The **con contingency approach** is central to OB because it rejects the idea of a "one best way" to manage people. Instead, OB holds that optimal managerial action depends on the **situation** — including the task, the team, the organisational culture, and the external environment. This is why a safety incentive scheme that succeeds in an office may fail on an offshore rig.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Writing only the names of the three dependent variables without the **safety performance** addition costs you the 4th mark allotted in PESFT524. Always add the safety lens.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Format)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: "Organisational Behaviour is the study of how individuals, groups, and organisational systems interact to shape workplace behaviour."**
**(a) Explain the three levels of OB in detail, with safety-relevant examples for each level. (7 Marks)**
**(b) Describe the contributing disciplines to OB. How does each discipline strengthen the analysis of safety behaviour in a high-risk industry? (7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Define the framework.** *(1 mark)*

OB is studied at three nested levels: **Individual**, **Group**, and **Organisation**. Each level contains unique variables that influence behaviour, and a complete safety analysis must consider all three.

**Step 2 — Individual Level.** *(2 marks)*

At the individual level, OB examines personal attributes. Key variables include:
- **Personality** — risk tolerance differs by personality. A high-conscientiousness worker is more likely to follow lock-out/tag-out procedures.
- **Perception** — two workers exposed to the same noise level may perceive risk differently; perception influences PPE use.
- **Motivation** — using Herzberg's two-factor theory, hygiene factors (e.g., proper tools) and motivators (e.g., recognition) drive safety compliance.
- **Attitudes and Values** — personal commitment to safety predicts near-miss reporting.

**Safety Example:** A maintenance technician with a high *locus of control* is more likely to inspect a scaffold before use.

**Step 3 — Group Level.** *(2 marks)*

At the group level, OB studies team processes. Key variables include:
- **Group norms** — a strong "we always wear hard hats" norm dramatically improves compliance.
- **Communication** — closed-loop communication prevents misunderstood hazard alerts in refinery control rooms.
- **Leadership** — transformational supervisors inspire proactive safety citizenship.
- **Groupthink** — a dangerous tendency to suppress dissent in safety-critical teams.

**Safety Example:** A shift handover team using structured SBAR (Situation–Background–Assessment–Recommendation) communication reduces shift-misalignment errors by 30–40%.

**Step 4 — Organisation Level.** *(2 marks)*

At the organisation level, OB studies systemic variables. Key variables include:
- **Organisational structure** — flat structures accelerate incident reporting.
- **Organisational culture** — Schein's 3-level model (artefacts, espoused values, underlying assumptions) shapes the safety climate.
- **Change management** — Kotter's 8-step process is used during safety transformation programmes.
- **Strategy and environment** — competitive pressure may push production over safety; OB analyses these trade-offs.

**Safety Example:** Post-Bhopal, Union Carbide's organisational culture change programme is a landmark case of macro-level OB intervention.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Introduction.** *(1 mark)*

OB is interdisciplinary. It draws from six core disciplines, each contributing a unique analytical lens.

**Step 2 — Psychology.** *(1 mark)*

Psychology contributes the **individual-level** understanding: motivation, perception, learning, and personality. **Safety application:** Behaviour-Based Safety (BBS) uses Skinner's operant conditioning to reinforce safe acts.

**Step 3 — Sociology.** *(1 mark)*

Sociology contributes the **group-level** understanding: group dynamics, formal organisations, bureaucracy. **Safety application:** Crew Resource Management (CRM) in aviation uses sociology to flatten hierarchies in cockpit communication.

**Step 4 — Social Psychology.** *(1 mark)*

Social psychology studies the **interaction** between individuals and groups. It covers attitude change, persuasion, and conformity. **Safety application:** Used to design safety signage that overcomes normalisation of deviance.

**Step 5 — Anthropology.** *(1 mark)*

Anthropology contributes the study of **culture, symbols, and rituals**. **Safety application:** Schein's organisational culture model is anthropological in origin and is the foundation of modern safety culture assessments.

**Step 6 — Political Science and Economics.** *(2 marks)*

**Political science** contributes the analysis of **power, conflict, and politics** within organisations. *Safety application:* understanding why a frontline worker may *not* report a supervisor's safety violation requires political-science analysis of power asymmetry.

**Economics** contributes the analysis of **incentives, efficiency, and resource allocation**. *Safety application:* the design of gain-sharing safety bonus schemes uses economic incentive theory to align worker and organisational safety goals.

**Step 7 — Conclusion.** *(1 mark)*

Together, the six disciplines give OB a **360-degree lens** to analyse safety behaviour — from the worker's heart (psychology) to the boardroom (economics). High-reliability organisations (HROs) like nuclear and aviation leverage all six lenses.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: "The success of any Safety Management System rests as much on the science of human behaviour as on the science of engineering."**
**(a) Define Organisational Behaviour and explain the three dependent variables it seeks to predict, with safety-relevant examples. (7 Marks)**
**(b) "There is no one best way to manage." Critically evaluate this statement in the context of the contingency approach to OB, using two contrasting safety-industry examples. (7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Definition of OB.** *(2 marks)*

Organisational Behaviour is the study of individual, group, and organisational-level factors that influence behaviour in workplaces, with the applied goal of improving organisational outcomes through evidence-based interventions.

**Step 2 — Dependent Variable 1: Productivity.** *(1.5 marks)*

Productivity is the ratio of output to input. In safety contexts, **safety productivity** is a useful concept — the number of safe man-hours per man-hour worked. A motivated, well-trained workforce delivers higher safety productivity. Conversely, fatigue and stress reduce both production and safety.

**Step 3 — Dependent Variable 2: Absenteeism.** *(1.5 marks)*

Absenteeism is the unscheduled absence from work. In a refinery, high absenteeism among control-room operators is a **leading indicator of accident risk** because undertrained substitutes may misread a DCS alarm. Tracking and addressing absenteeism is therefore a safety priority.

**Step 4 — Dependent Variable 3: Turnover.** *(1.5 marks)*

Turnover is the rate of employee separations. In high-reliability industries like nuclear power, turnover erodes **tacit safety knowledge** — the kind of know-how that is never written down. Loss of veteran operators increases risk of Low-Level Events (LLEs) that precede major accidents (Perrow's Normal Accident Theory).

**Step 5 — Synthesis.** *(0.5 marks)*

For PESFT524, **Safety Performance** is added as a fourth dependent variable — encompassing TRIR, LTIFR, near-miss reporting rate, and BBS observation closure rate.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — State the contingency approach.** *(2 marks)*

The contingency approach (also called the situational approach) holds that **there is no single best way to manage**; the optimal managerial action is contingent on the internal and external situation. This is the dominant philosophy of modern OB, replacing earlier universalist theories (Theory X, Scientific Management).

**Step 2 — First Industry Example: Aviation (Crew-Centric).** *(2 marks)*

In **aviation**, the contingency approach suggests a **flat hierarchy, crew resource management, and non-punitive reporting**. This works because:
- Tasks are highly interdependent in the cockpit.
- The cost of error is catastrophic.
- Communication must override rank.

A hierarchical "Theory X" approach would be dangerous here, as the co-pilot must be empowered to challenge the captain.

**Step 3 — Second Industry Example: Construction (Supervisor-Centric).** *(2 marks)*

In **construction**, the contingency is different. Workers operate in dispersed locations, tasks vary daily, and supervision is intermittent. The optimal approach is a **Theory-Y mix with strong supervisory presence**:
- Workers are self-directed for routine tasks.
- Supervisors enforce hard-hat and harness rules strictly.
- Behaviour-Based Safety (BBS) is layered on top.

A pure "flat" approach used in aviation would fail in construction because the dispersed, varied tasks require centralised rule enforcement.

**Step 4 — Critical Evaluation.** *(1 mark)*

The contingency approach is correct in principle but is **operationally difficult** because managers must continuously diagnose the situation, which requires training and data. In safety-critical industries, however, the cost of *not* being contingent (i.e., being universalist) is catastrophic — making the contingency approach non-negotiable.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning (Module 1 — OB):**
> - Students often skip the **PGO structure** (Person–Group–Organisation) in long answers. This costs 2–3 marks.
> - Students often write the **contingency approach** as a *definition* without applying it to a safety example. Always give **at least one industry-specific safety example**.
> - Students forget to add the **fourth dependent variable (Safety Performance)** in PESFT524 context. Deduct 1 mark if missing.
> - Numericals on **Absenteeism Rate** and **Turnover Rate** are common; memorise the formula and units.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1 / Organisational Behaviour**

- **Definition of OB:** Systematic study of how individuals, groups, and structures affect behaviour in organisations.
- **Three Levels (PGO):** Individual → Group → Organisation.
- **Three Dependent Variables (Traditional):** Productivity, Absenteeism, Turnover.
- **Fourth Dependent Variable (PESFT524 context):** Safety Performance.
- **Six Contributing Disciplines:** Psychology, Sociology, Social Psychology, Anthropology, Political Science, Economics.
- **Core Philosophy:** Contingency Approach — *there is no one best way; it depends on the situation.*
- **Key Functional Equation:** $B = f(P, G, O \;\vert\; C)$.
- **Absenteeism Formula:** $A_{\%} = \dfrac{N_{abs}}{N_{work}} \times 100$.
- **Turnover Formula:** $T_{\%} = \dfrac{N_{sep}}{N_{avg}} \times 100$.
- **Hawthorne Effect:** Workers' productivity rises when they are being observed.
- **Schein's 3-Level Culture Model:** Artefacts → Espoused Values → Underlying Assumptions.
- **Why OB Matters for Safety:** Behaviour is the proximal cause of 80–90% of industrial accidents. Engineering controls fail when human behaviour bypasses them (Reason's Swiss Cheese Model).
- **Highest-Frequency KTU Triggers:** PGO, 3 dependent variables, contributing disciplines, contingency approach, Schein, Herzberg, Maslow, McGregor's Theory X/Y.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety Outcomes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT524 - HUMAN AND ORGANIZATIONAL FACTORS IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Safety Outcomes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety Outcomes** are the **measurable, observable results** that reflect the effectiveness of an organization's safety management system (SMS), the influence of human factors (HF), and the prevailing organizational safety culture. They represent the *bottom-line consequences* of workplace safety policies, procedures, training, supervision, and engineering controls.

In KTU's Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, safety outcomes are the dual counterpart to **leading safety indicators** — they are the *lagging* or *reactive* measures that quantify harm, loss, or damage that has *already occurred*. They are tracked, benchmarked, and analyzed for continuous improvement as required by standards such as **ISO 45001:2018** and **ILO-OSH 2001**.

The three primary categories of safety outcomes are:

1. **Personal Harm Outcomes** — injuries, fatalities, occupational illnesses.
2. **Asset/Property Outcomes** — equipment damage, material loss, structural damage.
3. **System/Process Outcomes** — production stoppages, process upsets, near-misses that didn't escalate.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Health Report Card"

> [!NOTE]
> Think of safety outcomes like a **patient's medical report card** at the end of a year.
> - The **doctor** = Safety Manager / EHS Department
> - **Blood pressure, cholesterol, BMI** = Leading indicators (your proactive lifestyle)
> - **Heart attack, stroke, hospitalization** = Safety outcomes (the actual events that happened)
> - The report card is what the insurance company, employer, and family *read* to judge health.
> - You cannot change the past outcomes, but you can change tomorrow's by studying today's indicators.

In a workplace, a low **Lost Time Injury Frequency Rate (LTIFR)** is the equivalent of "no heart attacks this year" — a great lagging result, but it must be reinforced by strong leading indicators (safety audits, training hours, toolbox talks) to *sustain* the result.

## 1.3 Why Safety Outcomes Matter in KTU Engineering Practice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety Outcomes are the *language of accountability*** in modern industry. KTU-trained engineers are expected to:
> - Quantify safety performance using **standardized rates per million man-hours worked**.
> - Compare performance across plants, projects, and geographies (a *core competency* in OBE).
> - Justify the **economic ROI of safety investments** to management.
> - Comply with statutory reporting under the **Factories Act 1948**, **Kerala Factories Rules**, and **OSHA** (for multinational exposure).

The **standard reference period** in KTU problems is normally **1,000,000 man-hours** (or 200,000 man-hours for TRIR under OSHA), and the **standard reference population** is the **average number of employees** in that period.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Theoretical Pyramid — Heinrich → Bird → Reason

Safety outcome theory has evolved through three landmark models, each KTU-board-favorite:

### 2.1.1 Heinrich's Triangle (1931 — *Industrial Accident Prevention*)
> [!NOTE]
> Herbert William Heinrich proposed that for every **1 major (fatal/disabling) injury**, there are:
> - **29 minor injuries**
> - **300 no-injury accidents (near-misses)**

This **1 : 29 : 300 ratio** became the foundation of *accident causation theory* and the rationale for **near-miss reporting systems** as a predictive leading indicator of fatal outcomes.

### 2.1.2 Frank Bird's Triangle (1966 — *Practical Loss Control Leadership*)
Bird re-examined Heinrich's data across **297 companies and 1,753,498 accidents** and proposed a more granular, severity-weighted ratio:

> For every **1 serious/lost-time injury**:
> - **10 minor injuries**
> - **30 property damage incidents**
> - **600 near-miss incidents (no visible injury or damage)**

Bird's insight: **the base of the pyramid is dominated by unsafe acts and conditions, NOT by the rare fatalities at the tip.**

### 2.1.3 James Reason's Swiss Cheese Model (1990)
Reason's model explains *why* outcomes occur despite layers of defense. Each organizational layer (training, supervision, engineering controls, PPE) is a slice of Swiss cheese with **holes (latent failures + active failures)**. A safety outcome (accident) occurs only when the holes in all layers momentarily *align*.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following rates are the **core computational toolkit** for KTU 14-mark numerical problems. Memorize the **denominators** — they are the most-frequently-lost marks.

| # | Metric (Abbreviation) | Formula | Standard Denominator | Unit | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|----------------------|---------|----------------------|------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Frequency Rate (FR)** | $\text{FR} = \dfrac{\text{No. of Lost-Time Injuries} \times 1{,}000{,}000}{\text{Man-Hours Worked}}$ | 1,000,000 man-hours | Injuries per million man-hours | ★★★★★ |
| 2 | **Severity Rate (SR)** | $\text{SR} = \dfrac{\text{No. of Days Lost} \times 1{,}000{,}000}{\text{Man-Hours Worked}}$ | 1,000,000 man-hours | Days lost per million man-hours | ★★★★★ |
| 3 | **Incidence Rate (IR)** | $\text{IR} = \dfrac{\text{No. of Injuries} \times 200{,}000}{\text{No. of Man-Days Worked}}$ or $\times 1{,}000$ employees | 200,000 man-hours (≈ 100 full-time workers × 2000 hrs/yr) | Injuries per 100 workers/year | ★★★★ |
| 4 | **LTIFR (Lost Time Injury Frequency Rate)** | $\text{LTIFR} = \dfrac{\text{Lost Time Injuries} \times 1{,}000{,}000}{\text{Man-Hours Worked}}$ | 1,000,000 man-hours | LTI per M man-hours | ★★★★★ |
| 5 | **LTI Severity / LTISR** | $\text{LTISR} = \dfrac{\text{Days Lost} \times 1{,}000{,}000}{\text{Man-Hours Worked}}$ | 1,000,000 man-hours | Days per M man-hours | ★★★★ |
| 6 | **TRIR (Total Recordable Injury Rate, OSHA)** | $\text{TRIR} = \dfrac{\text{Recordable Injuries} \times 200{,}000}{\text{Man-Hours Worked}}$ | 200,000 man-hours | Injuries per 100 FT workers/yr | ★★★★ |
| 7 | **DART Rate** | $\text{DART} = \dfrac{\text{Days Away/Restricted/Transferred cases} \times 200{,}000}{\text{Man-Hours Worked}}$ | 200,000 man-hours | Cases per 100 FT workers/yr | ★★★ |
| 8 | **Fatal Accident Rate (FAR)** | $\text{FAR} = \dfrac{\text{No. of Fatalities} \times 1{,}000}{\text{No. of Employees} \times \text{Years of Exposure}}$ | 1,000 employee-years | Fatalities per 1000 employee-years | ★★★ |
| 9 | **Safe-T Score (Safety Efficiency)** | $\text{SE} = \dfrac{\text{No. of Safe Units Produced}}{\text{Total Units Produced}} \times 100$ | — | Percentage | ★★ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These rates feed directly into *risk registers*, *contractor pre-qualification* (tender eligibility in major EPC projects), *insurance premium calculations* (Workmen's Compensation), and *ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance) reporting* for listed companies.

## 2.3 The Cost of an Accident — Heinrich's Iceberg Model

Heinrich's economic model identifies that **for every $1 of direct/visible cost** (medical, compensation), there are **$4 of indirect/hidden costs** (production loss, investigation time, training replacement, morale, legal). Modern updates by the **National Safety Council (NSC)** and **Petersen** suggest ratios as high as **1 : 10 to 1 : 50** for serious incidents.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Calculation of LTIFR and Severity Rate

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Sample]**
> A construction firm in Kochi employs **500 workers** working **8 hours/day, 6 days/week, 50 weeks/year**. During the year, the firm recorded **12 lost-time injuries** resulting in a total of **180 man-days lost**. Compute:
> (a) The Lost Time Injury Frequency Rate (LTIFR).
> (b) The Severity Rate (SR).
> (c) The Average Duration per Injury (Severity per LTI).

### Step 1 — Compute Total Man-Hours Worked

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Man-Hours} &= \text{Workers} \times \text{Hours/day} \times \text{Days/week} \times \text{Weeks/year} \\
&= 500 \times 8 \times 6 \times 50 \\
&= 1{,}200{,}000 \text{ man-hours/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The standard formula requires total *exposure* — this is the total time workers were at risk of injury.

### Step 2 — Compute LTIFR

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LTIFR} &= \dfrac{\text{No. of LTI} \times 1{,}000{,}000}{\text{Man-Hours Worked}} \\
&= \dfrac{12 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{1{,}200{,}000} \\
&= \dfrac{12{,}000{,}000}{1{,}200{,}000} \\
&= 10.0 \text{ LTI per million man-hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Severity Rate (SR)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SR} &= \dfrac{\text{Days Lost} \times 1{,}000{,}000}{\text{Man-Hours Worked}} \\
&= \dfrac{180 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{1{,}200{,}000} \\
&= \dfrac{180{,}000{,}000}{1{,}200{,}000} \\
&= 150.0 \text{ days lost per million man-hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Average Duration per Injury

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Avg. Duration} &= \dfrac{\text{Total Days Lost}}{\text{No. of LTI}} = \dfrac{180}{12} = 15 \text{ days/injury}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Insight:** The *Average Duration per Injury* is a powerful diagnostic. A high average duration (> 30 days) suggests **severe, possibly systemic hazards** (e.g., falls from height, confined-space exposures). A low average duration (< 5 days) suggests **frequent but minor events** (e.g., cuts, sprains) — pointing to a *behavioral* rather than *engineering* root cause.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Incidence Rate Calculation (Indian Context)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> A manufacturing unit employs **200 workers**, each working **48 hours/week** for **52 weeks/year**. In one year, **5 reportable injuries** occurred. Calculate the **Incidence Rate per 1,000 workers** and the **Frequency Rate** as per standard practice.

### Step 1 — Man-Hours per Year

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Man-Hours} &= 200 \times 48 \times 52 = 499{,}200 \text{ man-hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Incidence Rate (per 1,000 workers)

Using the **NSC Indian standard reference of 1,000 workers per year**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{IR}_{1000} &= \dfrac{\text{No. of Injuries}}{\text{No. of Workers}} \times 1{,}000 \\
&= \dfrac{5}{200} \times 1{,}000 = 25.0 \text{ injuries per 1,000 workers/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Frequency Rate (per million man-hours)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FR} &= \dfrac{5 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{499{,}200} \\
&= 10.016 \approx 10.02 \text{ injuries per million man-hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Heinrich's Pyramid Application

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Bank]**
> A refinery in Kerala recorded **3 fatal accidents** in a year. Using Heinrich's 1:29:300 ratio, estimate:
> (a) The expected number of minor injuries.
> (b) The expected number of near-misses.
> (c) Comment on the **value** of near-miss reporting in preventing the next fatality.

### Step (a) — Minor Injuries

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minor Injuries} &= 3 \times 29 = 87
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step (b) — Near-Misses

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Near-Misses} &= 3 \times 300 = 900
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step (c) — Engineering Interpretation

> **Each of the 900 near-misses is a "free warning shot" of the safety system.** Had the refinery's near-miss reporting been < 5%, it would mean ~855 silent precursors exist undetected — a *latent* Swiss-cheese hole alignment waiting for a fatality. Hence, KTU's OBE expectation: a robust SMS must target *near-miss reporting rates > 30 reports per million man-hours* as a leading indicator.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Cost of Accidents (Heinrich's Iceberg)

> A factory has a direct cost of accidents of **₹8,00,000/year**. Using a conservative 1:4 ratio, estimate total cost. Using the updated NSC 1:10 ratio, recompute. Comment.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Conservative Total} &= 8{,}00{,}000 \times (1 + 4) = \text{₹ } 48{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Updated Total (NSC)} &= 8{,}00{,}000 \times (1 + 10) = \text{₹ } 96{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

*Insight:* The same incident profile can cost **12× the direct amount** — this is the financial lever KTU engineers use to justify safety capex in project feasibility reports.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Heinrich–Bird Safety Outcome Pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["F1 FATALITY SEVERE DISABLING INJURY"] --> B["F2 MINOR INJURIES FIRST AID CASES 10 TO 30"] --> C["F3 PROPERTY DAMAGE INCIDENTS 30"] --> D["F4 NEAR MISS UNSAFE ACTS UNSAFE CONDITIONS 600"]
    classDef tip fill:#8B0000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef mid fill:#FF8C00,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef base fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class A tip
    class B mid
    class C mid
    class D base
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Bottom-up = escalating severity. Top-down = frequency dropping exponentially. **Each tier is a leading indicator of the tier above it.**

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Safety Outcome Measurement Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["ORGANIZATIONAL FACTORS Policy Culture Leadership"] --> B["SAFETY MANAGEMENT SYSTEM ISO 45001 SMS"]
    B --> C["LEADING INDICATORS Audits Training Toolbox Talks"]
    B --> D["LAGGING INDICATORS Injuries Near Misses Property Loss"]
    C --> E["SAFETY OUTCOMES Injury Rates Severity Costs Compliance"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["FEEDBACK LOOP TO MANAGEMENT REVIEW"]
    classDef org fill:#1E40AF,stroke:#000,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef sms fill:#047857,stroke:#000,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef ind fill:#B45309,stroke:#000,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef out fill:#7C2D12,stroke:#000,color:#FFFFFF
    class A org
    class B sms
    class C ind
    class D ind
    class E out
    class F out
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Reason's Swiss Cheese Model (Outcome Causation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1["SLICE 1 ORGANIZATIONAL INFLUENCE"] --> L2["SLICE 2 SUPERVISION"] --> L3["SLICE 3 PRECONDITIONS UNSAFE CONDITIONS"] --> L4["SLICE 4 UNSAFE ACTS ACTIVE FAILURES"]
    L4 --> O["SAFETY OUTCOME ACCIDENT INJURY LOSS"]
    H1["LATENT HOLE 1 Resource Constraints"] -.-> L1
    H2["LATENT HOLE 2 Inadequate Supervision"] -.-> L2
    H3["LATENT HOLE 3 Defective Equipment"] -.-> L3
    H4["ACTIVE HOLE 4 Worker Error"] -.-> L4
    classDef slice fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef hole fill:#FEE2E2,stroke:#B91C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outcome fill:#7F1D1D,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF
    class L1,L2,L3,L4 slice
    class H1,H2,H3,H4 hole
    class O outcome
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** When asked to *"analyse the organisational causes of a safety outcome,"* always reference *latent failures* (the upper three slices) — not just the worker's error. The examiner marks for **systems thinking**, not blame.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Safety Outcome" and distinguish between a *leading indicator* and a *lagging indicator* with one industrial example each.**

**Model Answer:**
A *safety outcome* is the measurable end-result of an organization's safety performance, typically expressed as injury, fatality, property loss, or process upset. A *leading indicator* is a proactive, predictive measure (e.g., number of safety audits completed per month, percentage of employees trained), while a *lagging indicator* is a reactive measure of events that have already occurred (e.g., Lost Time Injury Frequency Rate, number of fatalities). The distinction: *leading indicators drive future outcomes; lagging indicators report past outcomes.*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**State Heinrich's Triangle ratio and explain its significance in modern safety management.**

**Model Answer:**
Heinrich's Triangle states that for every **1 major (fatal/disabling) injury**, there are **29 minor injuries** and **300 no-injury accidents (near-misses)** — a 1:29:300 ratio. Its significance lies in demonstrating that **near-misses are statistically far more frequent** than serious injuries, providing a *vast data pool* for proactive hazard identification. Modern SMS uses this rationale to mandate near-miss reporting as a **leading indicator** of fatal events.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

> *(a)* Define the **Lost Time Injury Frequency Rate (LTIFR)** and the **Severity Rate (SR)**. Derive the formulas and state the standard reference denominator used in industry. *(7 marks)*
> *(b)* A plant in Thrissur employs **800 workers** working **9 hours/day, 5 days/week, 48 weeks/year**. In a calendar year, **18 lost-time injuries** occurred with a total of **240 man-days lost**. Calculate the LTIFR, SR, and the **Average Severity per Injury**. Comment on whether the plant's performance is acceptable, given an industry benchmark LTIFR of **2.0** and SR of **30.0**. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Definitions and Formulas

> **LTIFR (Lost Time Injury Frequency Rate)** is the number of lost-time injuries per million man-hours worked.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LTIFR} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Lost-Time Injuries} \times 1{,}000{,}000}{\text{Total Man-Hours Worked}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Severity Rate (SR)** is the number of man-days lost per million man-hours worked.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SR} = \dfrac{\text{Total Man-Days Lost} \times 1{,}000{,}000}{\text{Total Man-Hours Worked}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **standard reference denominator** in international industry is **1,000,000 man-hours** (≈ 500 workers working 2,000 hours/year — the OECD/ILO convention). The unit is therefore *injuries per million man-hours worked*, enabling cross-plant comparison.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Step 1 — Total Man-Hours:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Man-Hours} &= 800 \times 9 \times 5 \times 48 = 1{,}728{,}000 \text{ man-hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — LTIFR:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LTIFR} &= \dfrac{18 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{1{,}728{,}000} \\
&= \dfrac{18{,}000{,}000}{1{,}728{,}000} \approx 10.42 \text{ LTI per million man-hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Severity Rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SR} &= \dfrac{240 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{1{,}728{,}000} \\
&= \dfrac{240{,}000{,}000}{1{,}728{,}000} \approx 138.89 \text{ days/M man-hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Average Severity per Injury:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Avg. Severity} &= \dfrac{240}{18} \approx 13.33 \text{ days/injury}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment on Performance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LTIFR}_{\text{plant}} &\approx 10.42 \;\; \text{vs. benchmark } 2.0 \\
\text{SR}_{\text{plant}} &\approx 138.89 \;\; \text{vs. benchmark } 30.0
\end{aligned}
$$

The plant's performance is **significantly worse** than the industry benchmark — by a factor of ~5× on LTIFR and ~4.6× on SR. This indicates both a **higher frequency** of injuries and **higher severity** per injury, signalling deep-rooted *systemic failures* (likely inadequate supervision, missing engineering controls, or weak safety culture). Immediate management review and a Heinrich-style near-miss campaign are recommended.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** *(Alternative Choice)*

> *(a)* Explain **Heinrich's Triangle** and **Bird's Triangle**. Compare their ratios and discuss the implications for near-miss reporting systems in a high-risk industry. *(7 marks)*
> *(b)* A heavy engineering company in Ernakulam recorded **2 fatalities, 15 LTI, 80 minor injuries, and 1,200 near-misses** in one year. Apply both Heinrich's and Bird's expected ratios and comment on whether the observed data is *under-reported* or *over-reported* for the lower-severity tiers. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Heinrich vs. Bird Triangle

| Aspect | Heinrich (1931) | Bird (1966) |
|---|---|---|
| **Sample size** | Single-company data | 297 companies, 1.75 M accidents |
| **Fatal : Minor : Near-miss** | 1 : 29 : 300 | 1 : 10 : 30 : 600 (with property damage tier) |
| **Granularity** | 3 tiers | 4 tiers (adds property damage) |
| **Implication** | First empirical pyramid of accident causation | Refined pyramid with severity weighting and economic loss tier |

> **Implication for Near-Miss Reporting:** Both models prove that the *base* (near-misses) is vastly larger than the *tip* (fatalities). For every observed fatality, **hundreds of preventable precursors exist**. A robust SMS in high-risk industries (oil & gas, construction, mining) must therefore mandate **voluntary, non-punitive near-miss reporting** with a target reporting rate > 30 per million man-hours to convert silent precursors into corrective actions.

#### Part (b) — Ratio Application

**Heinrich's Predictions (base = 2 fatalities):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Predicted Minor} &= 2 \times 29 = 58 \;\;(\text{Observed: 80}) \\
\text{Predicted Near-miss} &= 2 \times 300 = 600 \;\;(\text{Observed: 1{,}200})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Bird's Predictions (base = 2 fatalities):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Predicted Minor} &= 2 \times 10 = 20 \;\;(\text{Observed: 80}) \\
\text{Predicted Property Damage} &= 2 \times 30 = 60 \;\;(\text{Observed: 15 LTI treated as proxy}) \\
\text{Predicted Near-miss} &= 2 \times 600 = 1{,}200 \;\;(\text{Observed: 1{,}200}) 
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:**

> **Minor Injuries (80):** *More* than Heinrich's prediction (58) and 4× Bird's prediction (20). This suggests the **reporting culture is healthy at the minor-injury tier** — workers are not hiding first-aid cases.
>
> **Near-Misses (1,200):** *Exactly* matches Bird's prediction, but is 2× Heinrich's. The maturity of the company's SMS can be assessed as *moderately developed* — the data confirms Bird's more modern 1:10:30:600 ratio.
>
> **LTI (15):** Disproportionately high relative to 2 fatalities — i.e., the *LTI-to-fatal* ratio is 7.5, lower than the typical 10–15 found in heavy engineering. This may indicate **under-reporting of near-fatalities** (e.g., some LTI may be genuinely severe but not classified as fatal). A *root-cause audit* is recommended.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not** use 200,000 man-hours as denominator unless explicitly asked (it's the OSHA TRIR convention, not the KTU/ISO convention).
> 2. **Always** show unit conversion: hours/day × days/week × weeks/year. Students lose 2–3 marks by writing only "8 hours/day" without computing the total.
> 3. **Always** comment on the result — a numerical answer *without interpretation* fetches at most 4 out of 7 in part (b).
> 4. In Heinrich/Bird ratio questions, **show the multiplication explicitly**: never write "1:29:300" without binding it to the observed fatalities.
> 5. Avoid confusing **Frequency Rate** with **Incidence Rate** — they use *different denominators* (man-hours vs. workers).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 1, Safety Outcomes**

- ✅ **Safety Outcome = Bottom-line measurable consequence** of safety system performance (injury, fatality, property loss, near-miss).
- ✅ **Leading vs. Lagging indicators**: Leading = proactive (audits, training); Lagging = reactive (LTIs, fatalities, costs).
- ✅ **Three foundational models** to memorize: **Heinrich (1:29:300)**, **Bird (1:10:30:600)**, **Reason (Swiss Cheese — Latent + Active Failures)**.
- ✅ **Five core formulas** for numerical problems: **LTIFR, Severity Rate, Incidence Rate, TRIR, FAR**. Memorize the standard denominators.
- ✅ **Standard reference denominator** in KTU/ISO problems: **1,000,000 man-hours** (unless TRIR — then 200,000 man-hours).
- ✅ **Heinrich's Iceberg Cost Ratio**: Direct : Indirect ≈ **1 : 4 (classical)** or **1 : 10+ (modern NSC)**.
- ✅ **Near-miss reporting rate > 30/M man-hours** is the KTU-cited benchmark for a *mature* safety culture.
- ✅ **Always interpret the numerical result** — comparison to industry benchmark is mandatory for full marks.
- ✅ For Indian statutory context, cite: **Factories Act 1948, Kerala Factories Rules, ISO 45001:2018, ILO-OSH 2001**.
- ✅ **Engineers' OBE expectation**: connect every safety outcome to **human factors (behavior, training, fatigue)** and **organizational factors (culture, supervision, management commitment)** — not just to "bad luck."

> *End of KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 notes for **Safety Outcomes**, Module 1, PESFT524.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Human Errors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT524 - HUMAN AND ORGANIZATIONAL FACTORS IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Human Errors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of safety engineering and human factors, a **Human Error** is defined as a human action (or sequence of actions) that deviates from the prescribed or expected standards, leading to an undesired or unintended system outcome, often resulting in a hazard, near-miss, or accident. According to the KTU 2024 Safety Engineering curriculum, human error is not viewed as the *cause* of an accident, but rather as a **symptom of deeper systemic failures** lying within the organization's design, procedures, training, and supervisory structures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition:** A human error is any mental or physical activity of an individual that fails to achieve its intended outcome due to some unplanned or unexpected mental, physical, or environmental interference, where the failure cannot be attributed to chance or external agent intervention.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Typist on Autopilot:**
> Imagine you are typing an email to your manager. Your mind is already drafting the next line. Suddenly, you type "Dear" instead of "Hi" and then hit send to the wrong client. The error is **not a bug in your brain** — it is a **slip of execution**. Your intention was correct, your keyboard was correct, but the automation of your muscle memory betrayed you. This is the heart of human error research: separating **what we MEANT to do** (intent) from **what we ACTUALLY did** (action).

A more industrial analogy is the **aircraft pilot** engaging the autopilot on final approach. The pilot *intends* to lower the landing gear. The pilot's *hand* (action) instead reaches for the flap lever. The plane is uninjured; the system is fine; the pilot is competent. Yet, the error occurred. This is a textbook **execution failure (Slip)** that is central to safety analysis.

### 1.3 Core Dimensions of Human Error

Every human error studied in safety engineering can be classified along three independent dimensions:

| Dimension | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Cognition** | Mental vs. Physical origin | Forgetting a step (mental) vs. pulling the wrong lever (physical) |
| **Intent** | Unintended vs. Intended | Slips (unintended) vs. Sabotage (intended) |
| **Timing** | Pre-initiation vs. Post-initiation | Plan is wrong (pre) vs. Execution is wrong (post) |

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 scheme specifically tests whether students can distinguish between *unintentional* errors (Slips, Lapses, Mistakes) and *intentional* non-compliance (Violations). Examiners award full marks only when the student explicitly uses these exact terms.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

### 2.1 Master Taxonomy of Human Errors

The KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus requires a thorough understanding of the **James Reason Taxonomy** combined with **Jens Rasmussen's SRK Framework**. Together, they form the spine of modern human reliability analysis (HRA).

#### 2.1.1 Rasmussen's SRK (Skill-Rule-Knowledge) Framework

Every human cognitive activity operates at one of three levels. The **type of error** is determined by the level at which the failure occurred.

1. **Skill-Based Behavior (SBB):** Highly practiced, automated, pattern-matched actions with little conscious attention. The operator acts from stored motor programs.
   - **The Why:** The brain is operating in "autopilot" mode to conserve cognitive resources.
   - **The How:** Errors here are *execution* failures (Slips, Lapses).
2. **Rule-Based Behavior (RBB):** The operator applies a stored *if-then* rule to a perceived situation. The action is selected by rule-matching.
   - **The Why:** The brain has encountered a familiar-but-novel situation that requires a pre-learned solution.
   - **The How:** Errors here are *decision* failures (Rule-Based Mistakes).
3. **Knowledge-Based Behavior (KBB):** Slow, effortful, analytical reasoning used in novel situations where no rules exist.
   - **The Why:** The brain is consciously solving a problem from first principles.
   - **The How:** Errors here are *reasoning* failures (Knowledge-Based Mistakes, sometimes called "Cognitive" or "Decision" errors).

#### 2.1.2 Reason's Generic Error Modeling System (GEMS)

Reason mapped Rasmussen's three behavior levels to specific error types:

- **SBB failures → Slips and Lapses**
- **RBB failures → Rule-Based Mistakes**
- **KBB failures → Knowledge-Based Mistakes**

### 2.2 The Five Core Types of Human Errors (KTU Board Focus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 EXAM ESSENTIAL:** You will be examined on the *exact* difference between these five terms. Memorize the definitions, not the words.

#### 2.2.1 Slip (Execution Error — Observable, Action-Level)

- **Definition:** An error of *execution* where the *action performed* is not the action *intended*, but the *plan* is correct.
- **Mechanism:** Attention capture, distraction, habit intrusion, or motor inaccuracy.
- **KTU Example:** A CNC operator intends to press the **STOP** button but instead presses **START** because the buttons are similarly colored and located adjacent to each other.
- **Cognitive Level:** Skill-Based.
- **Recoverability:** Usually **HIGH** — the operator often self-corrects immediately because their intent was sound.

#### 2.2.2 Lapse (Execution Error — Internal, Memory-Level)

- **Definition:** An error of *internal execution* (memory failure, omission of an internal step) where the plan is correct, but an *intervening cognitive step* is missed.
- **Mechanism:** Working memory failure, inattention, mind-wandering, interruption.
- **KTU Example:** A quality inspector checks five out of six welds on a pipe joint, forgetting the sixth because the foreman asked a question midway.
- **Cognitive Level:** Skill-Based (internal).
- **Recoverability:** Usually **LOW** — lapses are often invisible to the operator and external observers because no visible action was taken.

> [!NOTE]
> **Slip vs. Lapse — The KTU Mnemonic:** *Slips are for the **Hands**; Lapses are for the **Head**.* If you can **see** the wrong action, it is a **Slip**. If you cannot see anything wrong but a step was *missed*, it is a **Lapse**.

#### 2.2.3 Mistake (Planning/Decision Error — Intent-Level)

- **Definition:** An error of *intention* or *planning* where the *plan itself* is inadequate to achieve the desired outcome, even though the execution may be flawless.
- **Mechanism:** Knowledge deficiency, misdiagnosis of the situation, or the application of a wrong rule.
- **KTU Example:** A plant operator deliberately shuts down a critical cooling pump to silence a nuisance alarm, unaware that the alarm was the only warning of an impending runaway reaction.
- **Cognitive Level:** Rule-Based or Knowledge-Based.
- **Recoverability:** Usually **VERY LOW** — the operator is *committed* to the bad plan and will execute it perfectly, leading to system damage.

#### 2.2.4 Violation (Intentional Non-Compliance — Motivational)

- **Definition:** A *deliberate* deviation from a known safety rule, procedure, or regulation.
- **Mechanism:** Motivation (perceived time pressure, cost, peer pressure, lack of enforcement), but **NOT** malicious intent.
- **KTU Example:** A scaffolding worker removes a guardrail to access a work area faster, even though the safety rule explicitly forbids it.
- **Cognitive Level:** Pre-attentive (motivational shortcut).
- **Recoverability:** Variable — depends on managerial response and culture.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Distinction Trap:** Do *not* classify a Violation as an "error" in the strict Reason sense. Reason insists that **violations are intentional** and therefore belong to a different psychological category. The KTU 2024 examiner will deduct marks if you lump violations with slips/lapses/mistakes.

#### 2.2.5 Sabotage (Malicious Intent — Criminal)

- **Definition:** A *deliberate* act to damage, destroy, or disrupt systems, performed with full knowledge and malicious intent.
- **Mechanism:** Personal grievance, ideology, financial motive, or psychological disorder.
- **KTU Example:** A disgruntled employee deliberately introduces a corrosive chemical into a reactor mixing tank to cause a hazardous release.
- **Cognitive Level:** Outside Reason's error model.
- **Recoverability:** Requires **behavioral, security, and HR interventions**, not safety training.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparison Table (Cheat Sheet)

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this table verbatim** — it represents the single most important comparison diagram in Module 2 and appears in 80%+ of KTU university exam questions.

| Error Type | Plan/Intent | Execution | Conscious? | Category | Typical Cause |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Slip** | Correct | Wrong (action) | No | Unintentional — Action | Inattention, distraction |
| **Lapse** | Correct | Wrong (omission) | No | Unintentional — Memory | Working memory failure |
| **Mistake** | Wrong | Correct | Yes | Unintentional — Planning | Knowledge gap, wrong rule |
| **Violation** | Deliberately wrong | Correct | Yes | **Intentional** | Motivation, peer pressure |
| **Sabotage** | Deliberately wrong | Correct | Yes | **Malicious** | Grievance, ideology |

### 2.4 The Swiss Cheese Model (Reason, 1990) — Contextual Framework

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Note:** Although the Swiss Cheese Model is officially introduced in Module 1 of PESFT524, KTU examiners frequently require it as a *contextual reference* in Module 2 questions on error types. Always cite it to earn full marks.

Reason's **Swiss Cheese Model** views accidents as the result of **latent conditions** (organizational holes) aligning with **active failures** (human errors at the sharp end). The four layers of defense are:

1. **Organizational Influences** (top management decisions, budgets, scheduling)
2. **Supervision / Line Management** (training, supervision quality, maintenance culture)
3. **Preconditions for Unsafe Acts** (fatigue, mental state, team communication, workplace stressors)
4. **Unsafe Acts** (the actual Slips, Lapses, Mistakes, and Violations committed by the operator)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Analytical Framework: Step-by-Step Error Classification Procedure

### 3.1 The KTU 5-Step Error Classification Algorithm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Tip:** In KTU 2024 descriptive questions, students often present the *correct* answer using the *wrong terminology*. Use this exact 5-step decision procedure to guarantee marks.

When a KTU question gives you an incident scenario, follow this deterministic sequence:

**Step 1 — Establish Intent:** Ask: *Did the person know what they were doing?*
- If **NO** → The error is **unintentional**. Proceed to Step 2.
- If **YES** → The action is *intentional*. Proceed to Step 4.

**Step 2 — Check the Plan:** Ask: *Was the plan/method chosen by the person correct to achieve the goal?*
- If the plan is **CORRECT** → The error is an *execution* failure. Proceed to Step 3.
- If the plan is **WRONG** → The error is a **Mistake**. (Stop here.)

**Step 3 — Distinguish Slip from Lapse:** Ask: *Is there a visible wrong action?*
- If **YES (visible wrong action)** → **Slip**.
- If **NO (an internal step was omitted or forgotten)** → **Lapse**.

**Step 4 — Distinguish Violation from Sabotage:** Ask: *Was the intent malicious or just non-compliant?*
- If **NON-MALICIOUS** (no intent to harm) → **Violation**.
- If **MALICIOUS** (intent to cause damage) → **Sabotage**.

**Step 5 — Cross-Reference with SRK Level:** Ask: *At which cognitive level did the failure occur?*
- **SBB** → Slip / Lapse
- **RBB** → Rule-Based Mistake
- **KBB** → Knowledge-Based Mistake

### 3.2 Worked Analytical Example (Kerala Petrochemical Plant — Synthetic Scenario)

> **Scenario:** A senior control room operator is monitoring a distillation column during a night shift. The level transmitter in the reflux drum begins to drift upward due to a calibration issue. The operator, trained to interpret rising reflux levels as a sign of flooding, decides to *reduce* the reboiler steam flow. The reduction in steam flow causes the bottom product temperature to fall, triggering an emergency shutdown. The operator had a written SOP that explicitly told him to *increase* steam flow in such conditions, but he did not consult it. The shutdown caused a ₹2.3 crore production loss and a 14-hour plant blackout.

**Applying the 5-Step Algorithm:**

1. **Step 1 — Intent:** The operator *knew* he was reducing steam flow. → **Intentional action**, but is it a violation? Proceed.
2. **Step 2 — Plan:** The operator's *plan* was to use steam reduction to address what he thought was rising reflux level. The plan was *defective* (it would have worsened the situation). → **MISTAKE** (Rule-Based Mistake — applied the wrong rule from memory).
3. **Step 3:** N/A (it is a mistake, not a slip/lapse).
4. **Step 4:** N/A (not a violation or sabotage).
5. **Step 5:** The operator used a stored *if-then* rule ("if reflux level rises, reduce steam"). This is **Rule-Based Behavior**. The wrong rule was selected. → **Rule-Based Mistake**.

**Final Classification:** *Rule-Based Mistake* — a planning failure rooted in misdiagnosis of the situation and failure to consult the SOP.

### 3.3 Quantitative Reliability Impact (Optional KTU Numericals)

While PESFT524 is primarily qualitative, the 2024 scheme may include a 2-mark part in Part A questions asking students to estimate the **Human Error Probability (HEP)**. The standard first-order approximation uses:

$$
HEP = \frac{N_{errors}}{N_{opportunities}}
$$

Where $N_{errors}$ is the number of observed human errors and $N_{opportunities}$ is the total number of opportunities for that error to occur.

> [!NOTE]
> Standard industry benchmarks (e.g., **THERP** — Technique for Human Error Rate Prediction) assume baseline error rates of approximately **1 × 10⁻³** to **1 × 10⁻⁴** for skill-based actions in well-designed interfaces. These constants are *not* provided in the KTU question paper, so do not attempt calculations without explicit data.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Hierarchy of Human Errors

> [!TIP]
> The following Mermaid diagram captures the **complete taxonomy** required by KTU Module 2. Examiners award extra marks for any student who can reproduce a clean version of this hierarchy in their answer sheet.

```mermaid
graph TD
    HE[Human Error]
    HE --> UNINT[Unintentional Errors]
    HE --> INTENT[Intentional Non-Compliance]
    
    UNINT --> EXEC[Execution Failures]
    UNINT --> PLAN[Planning Failures]
    
    EXEC --> SLIP[Slip]
    EXEC --> LAPSE[Lapse]
    
    PLAN --> MISTAKE[Mistake]
    MISTAKE --> RBM[Rule-Based Mistake]
    MISTAKE --> KBM[Knowledge-Based Mistake]
    
    INTENT --> VIOL[Violation]
    INTENT --> SABO[Sabotage]
    
    SLIP -.-> SBB[Skill-Based Level]
    LAPSE -.-> SBB
    RBM -.-> RBB[Rule-Based Level]
    KBM -.-> KBB[Knowledge-Based Level]
    
    style HE fill:#1e40af,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0f172a
    style UNINT fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0f172a
    style INTENT fill:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0f172a
    style EXEC fill:#22c55e,color:#000000,stroke:#0f172a
    style PLAN fill:#f59e0b,color:#000000,stroke:#0f172a
    style SLIP fill:#fde047,color:#000000,stroke:#0f172a
    style LAPSE fill:#fde047,color:#000000,stroke:#0f172a
    style MISTAKE fill:#fb923c,color:#000000,stroke:#0f172a
    style VIOL fill:#ef4444,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0f172a
    style SABO fill:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0f172a
    style SBB fill:#e2e8f0,color:#000000,stroke:#0f172a
    style RBB fill:#e2e8f0,color:#000000,stroke:#0f172a
    style KBB fill:#e2e8f0,color:#000000,stroke:#0f172a
```

### 4.2 Error Mapping Across Rasmussen's SRK Cognitive Levels

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph COG[COGNITIVE BEHAVIOR LEVEL]
        SBB[Skill-Based Behavior]
        RBB[Rule-Based Behavior]
        KBB[Knowledge-Based Behavior]
    end
    
    subgraph ERR[TYPES OF ERRORS]
        SLIP_A[Slip]
        LAPSE_A[Lapse]
        MIST_RB[Rule-Based Mistake]
        MIST_KB[Knowledge-Based Mistake]
    end
    
    SBB --> SLIP_A
    SBB --> LAPSE_A
    RBB --> MIST_RB
    KBB --> MIST_KB
    
    SLIP_A -.->|Visible wrong action| OUT1[Observable Error]
    LAPSE_A -.->|Internal omission| OUT2[Hidden Error]
    MIST_RB -.->|Wrong rule applied| OUT3[Execution proceeds; plan is bad]
    MIST_KB -.->|Analytical gap| OUT4[Slow detection; plan is bad]
    
    style COG fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af
    style ERR fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#a16207
    style OUT1 fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c
    style OUT2 fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c
    style OUT3 fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c
    style OUT4 fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Flow — How an Error Becomes an Accident

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operator Intent Formation] --> B{Plan Correct?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Execution Phase]
    B -- No --> M[MISTAKE Generated]
    
    C --> D{Execution Correct?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Task Completed Safely]
    D -- No, Wrong Action --> F[SLIP Generated]
    D -- No, Omitted Action --> G[LAPSE Generated]
    
    M --> H[Active Failure Pool]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    
    H --> I{Swiss Cheese Defenses}
    I --> J[Layer 1 Organizational]
    J --> K[Layer 2 Supervision]
    K --> L[Layer 3 Preconditions]
    L --> P[Layer 4 Unsafe Acts]
    
    P --> Q{All Holes Aligned?}
    Q -- No --> R[Accident Averted]
    Q -- Yes --> S[ACCIDENT OCCURS]
    
    style M fill:#fb923c,color:#000000
    style F fill:#fde047,color:#000000
    style G fill:#fde047,color:#000000
    style S fill:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
    style R fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 2]**
*Distinguish between a 'Slip' and a 'Mistake' with a suitable industrial example for each. (3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

A **Slip** is an *execution* error where the operator's plan is correct, but the wrong physical action is performed due to inattention or habit intrusion. *Example:* A forklift driver intends to press the brake but accidentally presses the accelerator.

A **Mistake** is a *planning* error where the operator's mental plan itself is incorrect, even though execution may be flawless. *Example:* A maintenance technician applies the wrong troubleshooting procedure from memory, not realizing the symptom-indication pairing is misdiagnosed.

**Key Difference:** Slips are **execution-level failures (correct plan, wrong action)**. Mistakes are **intent-level failures (wrong plan, correct action)**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Retest Paper]**
*Why is 'Violation' not classified as a human error in the strict Reason taxonomy? (3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

Reason's human error taxonomy categorizes only **unintentional** cognitive failures (Slips, Lapses, Mistakes). A **Violation** is a *deliberate* deviation from a known safety rule, often driven by motivational factors such as time pressure, peer influence, or convenience.

Because the individual **intends** to break the rule, the act cannot be a "failure of intention or execution" in the cognitive sense. Violations belong to a different psychological category — they reflect **motivational and cultural** factors, not cognitive unreliability. Therefore, violations are managed through **organizational culture, supervision, and disciplinary systems**, not through training or interface redesign.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Descriptive Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Conceptual + Application*

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set A]**

**(a)** Classify the different types of human errors using James Reason's Generic Error Modeling System (GEMS). Explain how each error type maps to Rasmussen's SRK (Skill-Rule-Knowledge) framework. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A night-shift chemical plant operator notices a high-temperature alarm on a reactor. Instead of initiating the documented emergency cooling sequence (which would take 12 minutes and interrupt production), he manually opens a bypass valve to dump reactants into a quench tank, an action explicitly prohibited by SOP. The bypass valve corrodes and ruptures, releasing toxic vapors. Classify the operator's action using the 5-step error classification algorithm. Justify your classification with reference to intent, plan correctness, and cognitive level. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Classification of Human Errors using GEMS and SRK Mapping:**

| Error Type | SRK Level | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Slip** | Skill-Based | Action is not what was intended; plan is correct | Pressing wrong button on a control panel |
| **Lapse** | Skill-Based | Internal memory step missed; plan is correct | Forgetting to log a parameter after recording it |
| **Rule-Based Mistake** | Rule-Based | Wrong *if-then* rule selected from memory | Misapplying a known SOP to a slightly different fault |
| **Knowledge-Based Mistake** | Knowledge-Based | Analytical reasoning fails in novel situations | Incorrectly diagnosing a new process anomaly from first principles |

The mapping is **structural**: the cognitive level at which the operator is operating (SBB/RBB/KBB) determines the *type* of failure that can occur. Slips and lapses are intrinsic to automated behavior. Mistakes emerge only when conscious decision-making is required.

**(b) Classification of the Night-Shift Operator's Action:**

**Step 1 — Intent:** The operator *knew* he was opening the bypass valve. The action was **intentional** (deliberate). → Not a Slip, Lapse, or Mistake in the strict cognitive sense.

**Step 2 — Plan Correctness:** The SOP explicitly forbade the bypass. The operator's chosen plan was *known to be wrong* by the operator. → This rules out a Mistake (which requires the operator to believe the plan is correct).

**Step 3 — Visible Action:** N/A (not a slip/lapse).

**Step 4 — Malice Test:** The operator's motive was *production continuity*, not destruction. → **Not Sabotage**.

**Step 5 — Final Classification:** The operator **deliberately** bypassed a known safety rule for perceived productivity benefit. → **VIOLATION** (specifically, a *routine violation* or *situational violation* under Reason's sub-classification).

**Conclusion:** The operator's act is a **Violation** rooted in *normalization of deviance* and production pressure, not a cognitive error. The corrective intervention must target **supervisory enforcement, SOP usability, and production scheduling**, not operator training.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set B]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain Reason's Swiss Cheese Model of accident causation. Discuss how this model reframes human error from being the *cause* of accidents to being a *symptom* of systemic failure. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A quality inspector on a packaging line is interrupted by a telephone call mid-inspection. After the call, he returns to the line and approves 200 boxes for shipment, but he has actually inspected only 140 boxes.
