The remaining 60 boxes contain a misprinted batch number that violates regulatory labeling. Using the SRK framework and the Reason taxonomy, classify the inspector's error and explain (i) why it is not a Mistake, (ii) why it is not a Slip, and (iii) what type of procedural redesign would prevent its recurrence. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Swiss Cheese Model and Reframing of Human Error:**

Reason's Swiss Cheese Model proposes that every safety system has multiple defensive layers (organizational, supervisory, preconditional, and individual). Each layer has "holes" — latent conditions or active failures. An accident occurs only when the holes in *all* layers momentarily align, allowing a hazard to penetrate.

The model **reframes** human error because it positions the operator's unsafe act as merely the *final* layer of failure. The deeper, more addressable failures lie in management decisions, supervision, training, and design. Investigators must therefore look *upstream* of the operator to find root causes. Training the operator is insufficient if the systemic latent conditions (e.g., poor SOP, fatigued staff, misaligned incentives) remain unaddressed.

*(See Section 4.3 Mermaid block diagram for the visual schematic.)*

**(b) Classification of the Quality Inspector's Error:**

**Step 1 — Intent:** The inspector *intended* to inspect 200 boxes. He was not deliberately skipping work. → **Unintentional**.

**Step 2 — Plan Correctness:** The plan ("inspect each box and approve") is **correct** and aligned with SOP. → The error is *not* a Mistake.

**Step 3 — Visible Action:** No *visible* wrong action occurred (he approved boxes; that was the intended action). → The error is *not* a Slip.

**Step 4 — Internal Step Missed:** The inspector *omitted* the inspection of 60 boxes — a **memory/execution gap** at the internal level. → This is the textbook definition of a **Lapse**.

**Step 5 — SRK Mapping:** Inspection is a highly practiced, automated task (Skill-Based Behavior). Lapses are characteristic SBB failures.

**Procedural Redesign Recommendations:**
1. **Physical Interlock:** A powered conveyor that *physically* halts if the inspection station is unattended for >30 seconds.
2. **Forced Count-Check System:** A barcode scanner that requires scanning each box ID before the "Approve" button activates; missing scans block approval.
3. **Call-Forwarding Protocol:** All phone calls to inspection stations to be diverted to a back-office coordinator during active inspection cycles.
4. **Checklist with Mandatory Sign-Off:** A physical checklist with checkboxes for every 10-box increment, requiring initialing before proceeding.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
> 1. **Conflating Violations with Errors** — A violation is *not* a human error in the Reason sense. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks if you list violation as a "type of human error" without the qualifier "intentional non-compliance."
> 2. **Mixing up Slips and Lapses** — Slips are *visible wrong actions*; lapses are *hidden omissions*. Many students incorrectly classify "forgot to do X" as a Slip. It is a **Lapse**.
> 3. **Ignoring the SRK Mapping** — KTU 2024 examiners expect you to *cross-reference* the error type with Rasmussen's SRK level. A complete answer always mentions the cognitive level.
> 4. **Using "Silly Mistake" or "Careless" in Descriptives** — These are lay terms. Use the technical terms: *Slip*, *Lapse*, *Mistake*, *Violation*, *Sabotage*.
> 5. **Skipping the Justification** — When classifying an error from a scenario, you must *show your reasoning* (intent → plan → action → cognitive level). A one-word answer earns zero.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Human Error ≠ Cause of Accident.** It is a *symptom* of deeper systemic failures. This is the single most important paradigm shift in safety engineering.
- **Five Types of Human Errors:** Slip, Lapse, Mistake, Violation, Sabotage. *Memorize the order and the definitions.*
- **Slip vs. Lapse — Quick Test:** *Slip = wrong action performed. Lapse = correct action omitted.*
- **Mistake = wrong plan, correct execution.** *Violation = correct knowledge of rule, deliberate breach.*
- **Rasmussen's SRK Framework:** Skill-Based → Slip/Lapse; Rule-Based → Rule-Based Mistake; Knowledge-Based → Knowledge-Based Mistake.
- **Reason's GEMS** is the *bridge* between Rasmussen's behavior levels and observable error types.
- **The Swiss Cheese Model** places operator error at the *sharp end* (Layer 4), with three layers of latent organizational failure above it.
- **Violations and Sabotage are *intentional*** and require different management interventions (culture, discipline, security) compared to unintentional errors (training, interface design, fatigue management).
- **For KTU 2024 exam answers:** Always cite the *cognitive level* (SBB/RBB/KBB) when classifying an error. Always use the *technical term* (Slip, Lapse, etc.) and never lay synonyms.
- **Reasoning chain to memorize for scenario questions:** *Intent → Plan Correctness → Action Type → Cognitive Level → Final Classification.*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Causes and Consequences
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT524 - HUMAN AND ORGANIZATIONAL FACTORS IN SAFETY > - **Course:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Causes and Consequences ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the discipline of **Human and Organizational Factors in Safety**, the term **"Causes and Consequences"** refers to the systematic, causal-chain analysis of workplace accidents, incidents, and near-misses. It is a foundational analytical framework that dissects an undesired event into its **latent (root) causes**, **immediate (proximate) causes**, and the resulting **consequences** (human, property, environmental, and economic). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > *"Causes and Consequences"* examines the multi-layered factors (human, technical, organizational, and environmental) that precipitate accidents, and the cascading physical, financial, psychological, and legal outcomes that follow. It is grounded in classical causation models such as **Heinrich's Domino Theory (1931)**, **Bird's Loss Causation Model (1969)**, and **James Reason's Swiss Cheese Model (1990)**. The discipline operates on the foundational principle that **accidents are not random events**; they are the predictable end-products of a chain of preceding failures within a socio-technical system. The analysis distinguishes between: - **Causes** → Pre-event factors (inputs) - **Consequences** → Post-event outcomes (outputs) ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The "Falling Domino" Metaphor** > Imagine a row of dominoes standing in a line. The first domino (the *root cause* — say, poor management commitment to safety) tips over, hits the second (*immediate cause* — say, a worker bypassing a guard), which hits the third (*unsafe act/condition* — a moving machine part is exposed), and finally crashes into the **last domino** — the *accident itself*, with the **consequence** being the worker getting crushed. Heinrich's Domino Theory works exactly this way: **accidents are the final link in a sequence**, and breaking any earlier link prevents the entire chain. A more modern analogy is the **"Swiss Cheese Model"**: imagine a stack of slices of Swiss cheese (each slice = a layer of defense — management policy, supervision, engineering controls, worker behavior). Each slice has *holes* (latent failures and active failures). An accident occurs only when the holes in *all* slices momentarily align, allowing the "trajectory of accident opportunity" to pass through. ### 1.3 Key Terminology (Bolded Standard Metrics) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Key Terms — Must Memorize:** > - **Hazard**: A source or situation with potential to cause harm (ISO 45001:2018). > - **Risk**: The combination of the **probability** of an event and the **severity** of its consequences. > - **Incident**: A workplace event where no injury or ill-health occurs, but has the potential to cause it (often called a *near-miss*). > - **Accident**: An undesired event resulting in physical harm, property damage, or operational loss. > - **Near-Miss Ratio (Heinrich's Pyramid)**: **1 serious injury : 29 minor injuries : 300 near-misses** (Heinrich, 1931 — *Industrial Accident Prevention*). > - **Total Recordable Incident Rate (TRIR)**: Standard OSHA metric for incident frequency. > - **Lost Time Injury (LTI)**: An injury causing the worker to be absent for ≥1 full shift. > - **Severity Rate**: Number of lost workdays per million man-hours worked. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Causal Architecture — Three Layers of Causes Accident causation is structured in **three distinct hierarchical layers**, which KTU examiners frequently test. #### 2.1.1 Root Causes (Latent / Organizational Causes) These are the deepest, most fundamental causes embedded in the **organizational and management system**. They are often invisible, distant in time, and require systemic investigation to uncover. - **Management System Failures**: Absence of written safety policies, inadequate OSHA-style compliance, lack of safety audits. - **Organizational Culture Deficits**: Poor safety climate, reward systems that prioritize production over safety, "blame culture." - **Inadequate Communication**: Language barriers, missing safety signage, absent toolbox talks. - **Substandard Design & Procurement**: Use of inferior materials, failure to apply ergonomic principles during equipment design. - **Economic Pressure & Cost-Cutting**: Understaffing of safety personnel, deferral of maintenance. - **Training Deficiencies**: Inadequate onboarding, no refresher courses, skill gaps. > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Root causes reside in **"Management's responsibility"** under the *Streso/Heinrich philosophy* and form the *upper tiers* of the Swiss Cheese Model. #### 2.1.2 Immediate Causes (Proximate / Direct Causes) These are the **visible, identifiable unsafe conditions and unsafe acts** that directly precipitate the accident. They are categorized into two classes (per **Heinrich's Domino Sequence**): **A. Unsafe Acts (88–95% of all causes, per Heinrich)** - Operating equipment at unsafe speed - Removing safety devices - Using defective tools - Working under the influence of alcohol/drugs - Failure to use Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) - Improper lifting, horseplay, taking unsafe positions - Distraction, inattention, fatigue **B. Unsafe Conditions (10–15%)** - Inadequate guards or barriers - Defective tools, equipment, or materials - Congested or slippery working surfaces - Excessive noise, poor lighting, poor ventilation - Hazardous atmospheric conditions (toxic gases, oxygen deficiency) - Improper storage of materials > [!IMPORTANT] > **Modern Reinterpretation:** Contemporary research (e.g., **Reason, 1990; Dekker, 2014**) revises Heinrich's percentage, asserting that **latent organizational failures** account for the majority, with human error being a *symptom* of system failure, not a *cause* per se. #### 2.1.3 Contributing Causes These are **multi-factorial amplifiers** that increase the likelihood of immediate causes translating into accidents: - **Human Factors**: Cognitive overload, fatigue, stress, time pressure, complacency. - **Environmental Factors**: Weather, temperature extremes, vibration. - **Procedural Factors**: Inadequate Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs), missing work permits. - **Equipment Aging**: Wear, corrosion, deferred maintenance. ### 2.2 The Consequence Architecture Consequences are classified across **four dimensions**, which form a high-frequency KTU exam topic: #### 2.2.1 Human Consequences - **Fatalities** (immediate or delayed) - **Permanent Total Disability (PTD)** — e.g., quadriplegia - **Permanent Partial Disability (PPD)** — e.g., loss of a finger - **Temporary Total Disability (TTD)** — recoverable injury - **Occupational Diseases** — asbestosis, silicosis, hearing loss - **Psychological Trauma** — PTSD, anxiety, survivor guilt (often overlooked) - **Pain and Suffering** — non-monetary human cost #### 2.2.2 Property & Equipment Consequences - Damage to machinery, vehicles, structures - Loss of tools, materials, finished goods - Replacement/repair cost (direct and indirect) - *Note*: Per industrial safety literature, property damage cost is approximately **4 to 10 times** the direct cost of human injury. #### 2.2.3 Environmental Consequences - Soil, water, and air pollution (Bhopal 1984, Chernobyl 1986) - Long-term ecological damage - Regulatory fines (e.g., under the Indian **Environment Protection Act, 1986**) - *Critical case*: Bhopal Gas Tragedy caused **3,800+ immediate deaths** and long-term contamination affecting **500,000+ people**. #### 2.2.4 Economic & Business Consequences - **Direct Costs**: Medical expenses, compensation, insurance premium hikes, equipment repair. - **Indirect Costs**: Production downtime, lost orders, replacement worker training, investigation time, legal fees, brand reputation damage. - **Hidden Costs**: Morale decline, increased absenteeism, regulatory scrutiny, loss of operating license. > [!NOTE] > **Heinrich's Iceberg Model (1931):** Direct (visible) costs of an accident represent only the **tip of the iceberg**, while **indirect (hidden) costs are 4 to 30 times higher** than the direct costs. This is a heavily tested concept. ### 2.3 The Domino Sequence (Heinrich's Causal Chain) Heinrich's model presents a five-domino sequence, where the **accident is the third domino**, and the **injury is the fifth (final) domino**: 1. **Domino 1 — Ancestry & Social Environment** (Root cause: social conditions, poverty, education) 2. **Domino 2 — Fault of Person** (Root cause: character defects, recklessness, lack of safety knowledge) 3. **Domino 3 — Unsafe Act / Unsafe Condition** (Immediate cause) 4. **Domino 4 — The Accident** (The undesired event itself) 5. **Domino 5 — The Injury** (The final consequence to the worker) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Heinrich's theory states: **"Injury is caused by the accident, and the accident is caused by the unsafe act or condition."** Removing Domino 3 (the unsafe act/condition) breaks the chain and prevents the injury. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Formulas for KTU 2024 Scheme Examination** | # | Formula / Metric | Equation | Standard / Unit | Application | |---|------------------|----------|-----------------|-------------| | 1 | **Frequency Rate (FR)** | $FR = \dfrac{\text{Number of Injuries} \times 10^6}{\text{Total Man-hours Worked}}$ | Injuries per million man-hours | Measures how often injuries occur | | 2 | **Severity Rate (SR)** | $SR = \dfrac{\text{Number of Days Lost} \times 10^6}{\text{Total Man-hours Worked}}$ | Days lost per million man-hours | Measures how severe injuries are | | 3 | **Incidence Rate (IR)** | $IR = \dfrac{\text{Number of Injuries} \times 10^3}{\text{Number of Workers}}$ | Injuries per 1000 workers/year | Worker-population-normalized frequency | | 4 | **Total Recordable Incident Rate (TRIR)** | $TRIR = \dfrac{\text{Recordable Injuries} \times 200{,}000}{\text{Total Man-hours}}$ | Per 100 full-time workers/year | OSHA standard metric | | 5 | **Heinrich's Cost Ratio** | $\text{Indirect Cost} = 4 \times \text{Direct Cost}$ (lower bound); up to $30\times$ (upper) | Ratio (dimensionless) | Estimates hidden accident costs | | 6 | **Risk (R)** | $R = P \times S$ (Probability $\times$ Severity) | Qualitative scale (1–5) or numeric | Risk Assessment Matrix input | | 7 | **DART Rate** | $DART = \dfrac{\text{Days Away/Restricted/Transfer} \times 200{,}000}{\text{Total Man-hours}}$ | Per 100 FTE/year | Severity-adjusted OSHA metric | | 8 | **Heinrich's Pyramid Ratio** | $1 : 29 : 300$ (Fatal : Minor : Near-miss) | Ratio | Predicts accident potential | > **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** The numerical multiplier of **$10^6$** in Frequency and Severity Rates is **non-negotiable** in KTU solutions. Skipping the multiplier = **automatic 1-mark deduction** as per valuation key. ### 2.5 Real-World Utility The **Causes and Consequences** framework is the **backbone of Root Cause Analysis (RCA)** methodologies used in: - **Aviation (NTSB investigations)** - **Nuclear industry (INPO, IAEA)** - **Healthcare (Sentinel Event Analysis)** - **Process industries (Hazard and Operability — HAZOP)** - **Construction (OSHA 29 CFR 1926)** It informs regulatory compliance with **ISO 45001:2018**, **Factories Act 1948 (India)**, and **NFPA standards**, making it indispensable for any Safety Officer, Safety Engineer, or HSE Manager. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Frequency Rate from First Principles > **Problem Statement:** > A construction firm has **250 workers** working for **300 days/year**. During the year, the firm records **3 fatalities** and **12 minor injuries**. The average work hours per day per worker is **8 hours**. Calculate the **Frequency Rate (FR)**, **Severity Rate (SR)**, and the **Incidence Rate (IR)**. Also, given the days lost are **75 days** for the minor injuries, determine if the **Heinrich's pyramid ratio** holds. **Step 1 — Identify the Given Data** - Number of workers, $N = 250$ - Working days, $D = 300$ - Man-hours per day per worker, $H = 8$ - Total fatalities, $F = 3$ - Total minor injuries, $I_{minor} = 12$ - Total lost workdays, $L = 75$ - Standard man-hours (Frequency Rate), $M_{FR} = 10^6$ - Standard worker count (Incidence Rate), $M_{IR} = 10^3$ **Step 2 — Calculate Total Man-Hours Worked** The total man-hours is the product of the number of workers, the number of working days, and the hours per day per worker. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total Man-Hours} &= N \times D \times H \\ &= 250 \times 300 \times 8 \\ &= 600{,}000 \text{ man-hours} \end{aligned} $$ This represents the total exposure of the workforce to occupational hazards during the year. **Step 3 — Calculate the Frequency Rate (FR)** The Frequency Rate measures how often injuries occur, normalized to a standard base of $10^6$ man-hours (one million man-hours). We sum the fatalities and minor injuries to get the total recordable injury count. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total Injuries} &= F + I_{minor} \\ &= 3 + 12 \\ &= 15 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} FR &= \dfrac{\text{Total Injuries} \times 10^6}{\text{Total Man-Hours Worked}} \\ &= \dfrac{15 \times 10^6}{600{,}000} \\ &= \dfrac{15{,}000{,}000}{600{,}000} \\ &= 25 \text{ injuries per million man-hours} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Calculate the Severity Rate (SR)** The Severity Rate normalizes the total days lost due to injuries against the same standard of $10^6$ man-hours. $$ \begin{aligned} SR &= \dfrac{\text{Total Days Lost} \times 10^6}{\text{Total Man-Hours Worked}} \\ &= \dfrac{75 \times 10^6}{600{,}000} \\ &= \dfrac{75{,}000{,}000}{600{,}000} \\ &= 125 \text{ days lost per million man-hours} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Calculate the Incidence Rate (IR)** The Incidence Rate normalizes the injury count per 1000 workers, providing a population-based metric independent of hours worked. $$ \begin{aligned} IR &= \dfrac{\text{Total Injuries} \times 10^3}{\text{Number of Workers}} \\ &= \dfrac{15 \times 10^3}{250} \\ &= \dfrac{15{,}000}{250} \\ &= 60 \text{ injuries per 1000 workers} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Verify Heinrich's Pyramid Ratio** According to Heinrich, the ratio of **1 fatal injury : 29 minor injuries : 300 near-misses** should hold for a stable accident population. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Observed Ratio} &= F : I_{minor} \\ &= 3 : 12 \\ &= 1 : 4 \end{aligned} $$ Compared to Heinrich's predicted ratio of **$1 : 29$**, our firm's observed ratio of **$1 : 4$** is significantly worse (more fatalities per minor injury). This suggests: - **Underreporting of minor injuries** OR - **Severe hazard exposure** (a *red flag* for management intervention). Predicted near-misses (if Heinrich holds): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Predicted Near-Misses} &= F \times 300 \\ &= 3 \times 300 \\ &= 900 \end{aligned} $$ The company should investigate whether 900 near-misses actually occurred but were unreported. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Causes-to-Consequence Mapping > **A KTU-style 2D analytical grid linking causes to their primary consequence categories:** | Cause Category | Example | Human Consequence | Property Consequence | Environmental Consequence | Economic Consequence | |----------------|---------|-------------------|----------------------|---------------------------|---------------------| | **Lack of PPE** | Worker not wearing helmet | Head injury (fatal) | — | — | Compensation, medical | | **Improper Machine Guarding** | Conveyor without guard | Limb amputation | — | — | Production stoppage | | **Confined Space Entry** | H2S exposure | Death by asphyxiation | — | Toxic gas release | Regulatory fines | | **Electrical Fault** | Bare wiring | Electrocution | Equipment damage | — | Replacement cost | | **Flammable Storage** | Oil leak near furnace | Burn injuries | Structural damage | Air pollution | Insurance hike | | **Improper Lifting** | Lifting 50 kg manually | Back injury (TTD) | — | — | Lost workdays | | **Driver Fatigue** | Long-haul trucker | Fatal road crash | Vehicle damage | Fuel spill | Litigation, lost load | | **Hazardous Substance Spill** | Chemical plant leak | Long-term disease | Soil contamination | Soil/water pollution | Cleanup cost (millions) | | **Inadequate Training** | Worker on CNC | Crush injury | Tool damage | — | Training, replacement hire | | **Poor Housekeeping** | Slip on oil | Fracture | — | — | First aid, lost time | ### 3.3 Comparative Analysis of Causation Models | Feature | **Heinrich's Domino (1931)** | **Bird's Loss Causation (1969)** | **Reason's Swiss Cheese (1990)** | |---------|-------------------------------|-----------------------------------|-----------------------------------| | **Layers** | 5 dominos | 5-step loss causation | 4 slices of defenses | | **Focus** | Worker behavior & ancestry | Management control failure | System defenses & latent failures | | **Probability Math** | No | Yes (uses **Domino Ratio**) | No (qualitative) | | **Origin of Failure** | Personal faults | Management systems | Latent conditions + active failures | | **Modern Acceptance** | Outdated (blames worker) | Moderate | Widely accepted in safety science | | **KTU Exam Weight** | High (foundational) | Medium | High (current best practice) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Preferred Model:** Examiners frequently award **higher marks** if students cite **Reason's Swiss Cheese Model** in modern accident analyses, as it aligns with contemporary NEP 2020 / ISO 45001 philosophy of *non-punitive safety culture*. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Causal Chain to Consequence Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Root Causes:
Management System
Organizational Culture
Design & Procurement
Training Deficits"] --> B["Immediate Causes:
Unsafe Acts and
Unsafe Conditions"] B --> C["Contributing Factors:
Human Factors
Environmental
Procedural
Equipment Aging"] C --> D["ACCIDENT / INCIDENT
The Undesired Event"] D --> E1["Human Consequence:
Injury, Disability, Death"] D --> E2["Property Consequence:
Equipment & Asset Damage"] D --> E3["Environmental Consequence:
Pollution, Contamination"] D --> E4["Economic Consequence:
Direct and Indirect Costs"] E1 --> F["Regulatory and Legal
Repercussions"] E2 --> F E3 --> F E4 --> F F --> G["Organizational Learning
and Safety Improvement"] A -.Feedback.-> G B -.Feedback.-> G C -.Feedback.-> G G -.Loop.-> A ``` ### 4.2 Swiss Cheese Model — Schematic (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Layer1["Organizational Influences"] S1["Slice 1: Management
Commitment and Policy"] end subgraph Layer2["Supervisory Factors"] S2["Slice 2: Supervision and
Procedural Compliance"] end subgraph Layer3["Preconditions for Unsafe Acts"] S3["Slice 3: Environmental
and Personal Factors"] end subgraph Layer4["Unsafe Acts"] S4["Slice 4: Worker
Behavior and Error"] end S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 Accident["ACCIDENT
Trajectory of Hazard"] -.Passes through aligned holes.-> S1 Accident -.-> S2 Accident -.-> S3 Accident -.-> S4 Injury["INJURY / CONSEQUENCE"] --- S4 ``` > **Reading the Diagram:** The **hazard trajectory** (dashed line) represents the *opportunity for harm*. It must penetrate the *holes* (latent failures) in **all four defensive layers** simultaneously to cause an accident. Safety is improved by **shrinking the holes** (stronger defenses), **adding more layers** (redundancy), and **eliminating alignment** of failures. ### 4.3 Heinrich's Domino Chain — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR D1["Domino 1:
Ancestry and
Social Environment"] --> D2["Domino 2:
Fault of Person"] D2 --> D3["Domino 3:
Unsafe Act or
Unsafe Condition"] D3 --> D4["Domino 4:
ACCIDENT"] D4 --> D5["Domino 5:
INJURY"] D3 -.Break the chain here.-> D4 ``` > **Reading the Diagram:** The fall of Domino 1 (social factors) causes the fall of Domino 2 (personal faults), and so on. Heinrich's central thesis: **removing Domino 3** (the unsafe act/condition) **prevents the entire chain** from reaching injury. ### 4.4 Heinrich's Cost Iceberg — Consequence Economics ```mermaid flowchart TD Tip["Visible Costs (Tip of Iceberg):
Medical Expenses, Insurance,
Compensation Claims"] --> Overall Below["Hidden Costs (Below the Waterline):
Lost Production, Investigation Time,
Training Replacements, Morale Loss,
Brand Damage, Legal Fees"] Below --> Overall["TOTAL COST OF ACCIDENT"] Tip --> Overall Overall --> Ratio["Heinrich's Ratio:
Direct : Indirect = 1 : 4 to 1 : 30"] ``` ### 4.5 Heinrich's Pyramid (1:29:300) — Block Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TD P1["Level 1: 1 FATALITY
(rare, severe)"] --> P2["Level 2: 29 MINOR INJURIES
(moderate)"] P2 --> P3["Level 3: 300 NEAR-MISSES
(frequent, no injury)"] P3 --> P4["Level 4: INFINITE UNSAFE BEHAVIORS
(background risk)"] ``` > **Reading the Diagram:** The base of the pyramid is the **largest** category (unsafe behaviors), and the apex is the **smallest but most catastrophic** (fatalities). Heinrich's principle: **eliminating near-misses at the base prevents fatalities at the top** — this is the **proactive safety philosophy** that KTU examiners reward. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]** **Differentiate between "Unsafe Acts" and "Unsafe Conditions." Provide two examples of each.** **Model Answer (Valuation-Ready):** > **Unsafe Acts** are human behavioral errors or violations of established safety procedures committed by workers. > - **Example 1:** Operating machinery without authorization. > - **Example 2:** Failure to wear Personal Protective Equipment (PPE). > **Unsafe Conditions** are physical, environmental, or mechanical hazards present in the workplace. > - **Example 1:** Missing or defective machine guards. > - **Example 2:** Slippery floors due to oil spillage. The fundamental distinction is that **unsafe acts are caused by people**, while **unsafe conditions are caused by the workplace environment or equipment design**. Heinrich attributed **88–95% of accidents** to unsafe acts and **10–15%** to unsafe conditions, though modern safety science (e.g., Reason) attributes a higher share to **latent organizational conditions**. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Expected Question]** **State and explain Heinrich's Iceberg Theory of accident costs.** **Model Answer (Valuation-Ready):** Heinrich's Iceberg Theory, proposed in his 1931 work *Industrial Accident Prevention*, posits that the **direct (visible) costs of an accident represent only the tip of the iceberg**, while **indirect (hidden) costs lie below the surface** and are **far greater**. > **Direct Costs (Visible, ~25%):** Medical expenses, insurance compensation, equipment replacement. > **Indirect Costs (Hidden, ~75%):** Lost production time, wages paid to injured workers, investigation time, training of replacements, legal fees, morale decline, brand damage, regulatory penalties. The estimated cost ratio is: $$ \text{Direct Cost} : \text{Indirect Cost} = 1 : 4 \text{ to } 1 : 30 $$ Hence, **every ₹1 of visible cost corresponds to ₹4–₹30 of hidden cost**, making the total economic impact of an accident significantly underestimated by management. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Note:** As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, Module 2 carries internal choice. Students answer **either Question A or Question B**. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **Heinrich's Domino Theory** of accident causation with a neat diagram. Discuss its **limitations** and explain how **James Reason's Swiss Cheese Model** addresses those limitations. **(b) [7 Marks]** A manufacturing unit employs **400 workers** working **8 hours/day, 300 days/year**. During the year, there were **5 fatalities**, **20 lost-time injuries**, and **120 near-misses**. The total lost workdays amounted to **1,500 days**. Calculate: - (i) Frequency Rate - (ii) Severity Rate - (iii) Incidence Rate - (iv) DART Rate (assuming 60% of injuries are Days Away/Restricted/Transfer cases) --- **Model Solution to Q.A(a):** **Heinrich's Domino Theory (1931):** Heinrich's theory visualizes the accident sequence as a chain of **five dominos** standing in a row. The fall of the first domino (root cause) causes a cascading fall, ultimately leading to the injury (fifth domino). The five dominos are: 1. **Domino 1 — Ancestry and Social Environment** (e.g., poverty, lack of education, cultural attitudes toward safety) 2. **Domino 2 — Fault of Person** (e.g., recklessness, impatience, nervousness, lack of safety knowledge) 3. **Domino 3 — Unsafe Act or Unsafe Condition** (e.g., bypassing a guard, defective equipment) 4. **Domino 4 — The Accident** (the undesired event itself) 5. **Domino 5 — The Injury** (consequence to the worker) **Central Thesis:** Removing **Domino 3** breaks the causal chain, preventing the accident and injury. Heinrich attributed **88–95% of accidents** to unsafe acts/conditions (Domino 3). **Limitations of Heinrich's Model:** - **Blaming the worker:** The model places responsibility on personal faults, ignoring organizational/systemic factors. - **Linear, simplistic view:** Real accidents involve complex, non-linear interactions. - **No consideration of management responsibility:** It overlooks latent organizational failures. - **No probabilistic treatment:** It is purely qualitative. - **Outdated with respect to human factors science:** Modern research shows that **error is a symptom of system failure, not its cause** (Reason, 1990). **How Reason's Swiss Cheese Model Addresses These Limitations:** James Reason's **Swiss Cheese Model (1990)** represents the safety system as a stack of **defensive layers** (analogous to slices of Swiss cheese), each with **holes** (latent failures and active failures). An accident occurs only when the **holes in all layers momentarily align**, allowing the hazard trajectory to pass through. **Reason's Four Defensive Layers:** 1. **Organizational Influences** (Management policy, resource allocation) 2. **Supervision / Unsafe Supervision** 3. **Preconditions for Unsafe Acts** (Environmental and personal factors like fatigue) 4. **Unsafe Acts** (The active failure — committed by the worker) **How it overcomes Heinrich's limitations:** - **Acknowledges system-level failures** (latent conditions reside in upper layers). - **Non-punitive** — treats human error as a *symptom* of organizational failure. - **Aligned with modern safety culture** (ISO 45001, NEP 2020 philosophy). - **Provides a clear roadmap** for system redesign (shrink the holes, add layers). - **Incorporates multiple defenses** (defense-in-depth principle). **Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes for the Swiss Cheese Model schematic.) --- **Model Solution to Q.A(b):** **Step 1 — Extract Data** - $N = 400$ workers - $H = 8$ hours/day - $D = 300$ days/year - Fatalities $= 5$ - Lost-time injuries $= 20$ - Total recordable injuries $= 5 + 20 = 25$ - Near-misses $= 120$ - Total lost workdays $= 1500$ - DART fraction $= 0.60$ of injuries - DART injuries $= 0.60 \times 25 = 15$ **Step 2 — Total Man-Hours Worked** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total Man-Hours} &= N \times D \times H \\ &= 400 \times 300 \times 8 \\ &= 960{,}000 \text{ man-hours} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Frequency Rate (FR)** $$ \begin{aligned} FR &= \dfrac{\text{Total Injuries} \times 10^6}{\text{Total Man-Hours}} \\ &= \dfrac{25 \times 10^6}{960{,}000} \\ &= \dfrac{25{,}000{,}000}{960{,}000} \\ &\approx 26.04 \text{ injuries per million man-hours} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Severity Rate (SR)** $$ \begin{aligned} SR &= \dfrac{\text{Total Days Lost} \times 10^6}{\text{Total Man-Hours}} \\ &= \dfrac{1500 \times 10^6}{960{,}000} \\ &= \dfrac{1{,}500{,}000{,}000}{960{,}000} \\ &\approx 1562.5 \text{ days lost per million man-hours} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Incidence Rate (IR)** $$ \begin{aligned} IR &= \dfrac{\text{Total Injuries} \times 10^3}{\text{Number of Workers}} \\ &= \dfrac{25 \times 10^3}{400} \\ &= \dfrac{25{,}000}{400} \\ &= 62.5 \text{ injuries per 1000 workers} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — DART Rate** $$ \begin{aligned} DART &= \dfrac{\text{DART Injuries} \times 200{,}000}{\text{Total Man-Hours}} \\ &= \dfrac{15 \times 200{,}000}{960{,}000} \\ &= \dfrac{3{,}000{,}000}{960{,}000} \\ &\approx 3.125 \text{ per 100 FTE/year} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7 — Verification of Heinrich's Pyramid** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Observed Ratio (Fatal : LTI : Near-miss)} &= 5 : 20 : 120 \\ &= 1 : 4 : 24 \end{aligned} $$ Compared to Heinrich's predicted **$1 : 29 : 300$**, the firm shows: - **Higher fatality rate** than expected (severity) - **Far fewer near-misses** than expected (underreporting of near-misses) - Conclusion: **Implement a near-miss reporting system** to capture leading indicators. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Modified]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **different categories of consequences of accidents** in a workplace. Explain **Heinrich's Iceberg Model** with reference to **direct and indirect costs** of accidents. **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **Bird's Loss Causation Model** (1969). How does it differ from **Heinrich's Domino Theory**? Discuss the **role of management control failure** in modern accident causation. --- **Model Solution to Q.B(a):** **Categories of Consequences of Accidents:** Accidents generate consequences across **four major dimensions**: **(1) Human Consequences (most critical):** - **Fatalities** — death due to the accident. - **Permanent Total Disability (PTD)** — e.g., paralysis, blindness. - **Permanent Partial Disability (PPD)** — e.g., loss of fingers, hearing loss. - **Temporary Total Disability (TTD)** — e.g., fractures, burns. - **Occupational Diseases** — long-latency conditions (e.g., asbestosis, silicosis). - **Psychological Trauma** — PTSD, anxiety, depression. - **Pain and Suffering** — non-quantifiable human cost. **(2) Property & Equipment Consequences:** - Damage to machinery, tools, and structures. - Loss of raw materials and finished goods. - Repair/replacement cost (typically **4–10×** the human-injury cost). **(3) Environmental Consequences:** - Air pollution (toxic gas release, smoke). - Water pollution (chemical spills, oil leaks). - Soil contamination (e.g., pesticide runoff). - Ecological damage to flora and fauna. - **Case Study:** Bhopal Gas Tragedy (1984) — methyl isocyanate release caused **3,800+ immediate deaths** and chronic illness in **500,000+ survivors**, with contamination persisting for decades. **(4) Economic Consequences:** - **Direct Costs (Visible, ~25%):** Medical expenses, compensation, insurance premium hikes. - **Indirect Costs (Hidden, ~75%):** Production downtime, lost orders, training of replacements, legal fees, brand damage, morale decline, regulatory fines. - *Per Heinrich's Iceberg Model*, indirect costs are **4 to 30 times** the direct costs. **Heinrich's Iceberg Model:** Heinrich (1931) conceptualized the **economic impact of an accident** as an iceberg floating in water: - **Above the waterline (visible tip):** Direct, insurance-related, easily quantifiable costs. - **Below the waterline (massive submerged portion):** Indirect, hidden, often ignored costs that are **4× to 30× larger** than visible costs. The Iceberg Model teaches safety managers that the **true cost of an accident is far greater** than what appears in accounting ledgers, justifying **proactive safety investments** even when direct costs seem low. --- **Model Solution to Q.B(b):** **Bird's Loss Causation Model (1969):** Frank Bird, in his landmark study of **1,753,498 accidents** in 297 companies (presented to the American Society of Safety Engineers), proposed a refined causation model that emphasized **management systems** as the root cause. **Bird's Five-Step Sequence:** 1. **Lack of Control / Management System Failure** (Root cause) — e.g., inadequate safety policies, no accountability. 2. **Basic Causes** — Personal factors (lack of skill, stress) and Job factors (poor design, inadequate SOPs). 3. **Immediate Causes** — Unsafe acts and unsafe conditions (as in Heinrich). 4. **The Incident / Accident** — The undesired event. 5. **The Loss** — Damage to people, property, process, profit, or environment. **Bird's Key Innovation:** He introduced the **concept of "loss"** beyond human injury, encompassing **property, process, profit, and environment** — the modern **5 P's of loss**. **Differences from Heinrich's Model:** | Aspect | Heinrich (1931) | Bird (1969) | |--------|-----------------|-------------| | Root Cause | Ancestry & personal faults | **Management system failure** | | Consequence Focus | Worker injury | **5 P's**: People, Property, Process, Profit, Planet | | Study Basis | Single-industry anecdotal | **1.7 million accident empirical study** | | Probabilistic Treatment | None | **Domino Ratio (300:29:1)** refined | | Modernity | Outdated (blames worker) | **Progressive — blames system** | **Role of Management Control Failure in Modern Causation:** Modern accident causation research (post-1990) overwhelmingly identifies **management control failure** as the *prime mover* of accidents. The chain is: **Management Decisions** → **Supervisory Failures** → **Preconditions for Unsafe Acts** → **Worker Error** → **Accident** This is the **Human Factors Analysis and Classification System (HFACS)** framework used by **DOD, NASA, and aviation regulators**. Examples of management control failure include: - **Production over safety pressure** (e.g., BP Texas City Refinery explosion, 2005). - **Inadequate safety audits** (e.g., Bhopal, 1984 — Union Carbide's lapses). - **Cost-cutting on maintenance** (e.g., Boeing 737 MAX, 2018–2019 crashes). - **Weak whistleblower protection** — workers afraid to report hazards. KTU examiners reward answers that **shift the locus of causation from the worker to the system**, reflecting the contemporary NEP 2020 outcome-based safety philosophy. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing Heinrich's "1:29:300" ratio** with Bird's "Domino Ratio" — they are different studies with different contexts. > 2. **Omitting the $10^6$ or $200{,}000$ multiplier** in frequency rate formulas — automatic 1-mark deduction. > 3. **Blaming only the worker** in causation questions — KTU 2024 Scheme values a **systems-thinking** approach; neglect of management factors = 2 marks lost. > 4. **Skipping the diagram** in domino/safety model questions — diagrams carry **1–2 marks** explicitly. > 5. **Using "accident" and "incident" interchangeably** — strictly speaking, an *incident* has no injury; an *accident* causes harm. > 6. **Forgetting to write units** in numerical answers — the units ($/10^6$ man-hours) are mandatory. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2: Causes and Consequences** - **Accidents are not random** — they are the predictable end of a causal chain of failures. - **Three layers of causes:** Root (latent/organizational) → Immediate (unsafe acts/conditions) → Contributing (human, environmental, procedural). - **Heinrich's 5-Domino Theory (1931):** Social Environment → Personal Fault → Unsafe Act/Condition → Accident → Injury. **Removing Domino 3 breaks the chain.** - **Heinrich's 88-95% statistic:** Unsafe acts account for the majority — **modern revision attributes more to latent organizational failures.** - **Heinrich's Iceberg:** Direct costs (visible) : Indirect costs (hidden) = **$1 : 4$ to $1 : 30$**. - **Heinrich's Pyramid:** 1 Fatal : 29 Minor Injuries : 300 Near-Misses. **Near-miss reporting = leading indicator.** - **Bird's Loss Causation (1969):** Emphasizes **management control failure** as root cause; introduces **5 P's of loss** (People, Property, Process, Profit, Planet). - **Reason's Swiss Cheese Model (1990):** 4 defensive layers; **accident = alignment of holes** in all layers. *Modern preferred model.* - **Consequence categories:** Human, Property, Environmental, Economic. - **Key Formulas (must memorize):** - $FR = (\text{Injuries} \times 10^6) / \text{Man-hours}$ - $SR = (\text{Days Lost} \times 10^6) / \text{Man-hours}$ - $IR = (\text{Injuries} \times 10^3) / \text{Workers}$ - $TRIR = (\text{Recordable Injuries} \times 200{,}000) / \text{Man-hours}$ - $DART = (\text{DART Injuries} \times 200{,}000) / \text{Man-hours}$ - $R = P \times S$ (Risk = Probability × Severity) - **Human Factors classification (HFACS):** Unsafe Acts → Preconditions → Supervision → Organization. - **Modern philosophy:** Human error is a **symptom of system failure**, not a cause in itself. - **Regulatory anchors:** ISO 45001:2018, OSHA 29 CFR 1910/1926, Factories Act 1948, Environment Protection Act 1986. - **Real-world cases to remember:** Bhopal 1984, Chernobyl 1986, BP Texas City 2005, Boeing 737 MAX 2018–2019. - **KTU exam focus areas:** Definitions of unsafe acts vs. conditions; all three causation models; numerical problems on FR/SR/IR; Heinrich's pyramid and iceberg; consequence classification. - **Diagrams are mandatory** in 7-mark causation questions — practice drawing the Swiss Cheese Model and Domino Chain.

topics.title: Errors and Violations
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT524 - HUMAN AND ORGANIZATIONAL FACTORS IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Errors and Violations

# ⚠️ Errors and Violations — KTU 2024 Premium Study Notes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 2):** This topic is a **high-weightage, frequently tested** item in KTU End Semester Examinations (ESE). Students are expected to know the *taxonomies*, *causal mechanisms*, and the *Swiss Cheese Model* in great depth. Direct 3-mark definitional and 14-mark descriptive questions are common.

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is an "Error" in Human Factors?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (Reason, 1990 — KTU Standard):**
> An **Error** is the failure of planned actions to achieve their desired outcome, *where the failure cannot be attributed to chance, intervention, or external factors*. Errors are **unintentional** deviations in human performance.

In other words — an error happens when a person *wanted* to do the right thing, but **something went wrong in the mental or physical execution**. Errors are about *failure of intentions*.

**Conceptual Analogy (The "Typo" Metaphor):**
Imagine you are typing a WhatsApp message to your professor: *"I will attend the class."* But your fingers, on autopilot, type *"I will **absent** the class."* You did **not** intend to write "absent" — your fingers slipped. This is a **slip** — a classic action-based error. You knew what to do, you *meant* well, but the execution broke down. The *plan* was correct; the *action* failed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Golden Words in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Error = Unintentional** (no deliberate intent to harm)
> 2. **Violation = Intentional** (deliberate disregard of rules)
> 3. The boundary is **INTENT**.

### 1.2 What is a "Violation" in Human Factors?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (Reason, 1990 — KTU Standard):**
> A **Violation** is a deliberate deviation from procedures, rules, standards, or instructions deemed necessary to maintain safe system operation. Violations involve **conscious will** — the person *knows* the rule and *chooses* to bypass it.

**Conceptual Analogy (The "Seatbelt" Metaphor):**
You drive every day. The rule says: *wear a seatbelt.* You know the rule. You know it saves lives. But today, you consciously decide not to wear it because "I'm just going to the nearby shop." That is a **routine/situational violation** — a deliberate shortcut. Now imagine you tie a rope so the seatbelt "looks" fastened from outside but isn't actually latched. This is a more **malicious** violation (sometimes called sabotage in extreme cases).

### 1.3 The Central Distinction — KTU's Most Tested Concept

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write *"violation is just another word for error."* This is **WRONG** and costs 2-3 marks. The taxonomy below is mandatory:

| Parameter | **Error** | **Violation** |
|-----------|-----------|---------------|
| **Intent** | Unintentional | Intentional / Deliberate |
| **Awareness of Rule** | Usually aware | Fully aware |
| **Cause** | Cognitive/physical malfunction | Conscious choice, motive, or shortcut |
| **Categorization** | Slips, Lapses, Mistakes | Routine, Situational, Exceptional, Sabotage |
| **Blame Culture View** | Victim of circumstance | Rule-breaker |
| **Safety Culture View** | System failure | System + cultural failure |
| **KTU Example** | Surgeon cuts wrong artery | Bypassing safety interlock knowingly |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Exam Hall:**
> - **E-rror** → "**E**xecution broke" (Slip / Lapse / Mistake)
> - **V-iolation** → "**V**oluntary defiance"

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024 ESE questions on this topic, examiners often expect quantification. The standard metrics used in Human Factors literature are:
> - **Human Error Rate (HER):** typically **1 in 1000** (i.e., **10⁻³**) for unskilled, untrained tasks.
> - **Industry-standard Human Reliability:** approx. **99%** (i.e., error rate ≈ **0.01** or **1%**).
> - These benchmarks come from classical **Swain & Guttmann (1983)** and **Reason (1990)** studies.
> - **Rule of thumb:** Humans are not the *primary* cause of accidents — organizational/system failures are, in roughly **70-80%** of major industrial disasters (Hollnagel, 2014 — **Resilience Engineering**).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Reason's Taxonomy of Human Errors (1990)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **James Reason's "Human Error" (1990) is the single most cited reference in KTU exams.** Master this taxonomy.

Reason classified all human errors into **three primary categories**, mapped against **Rasmussen's Skill-Rule-Knowledge (SRK) framework**:

#### 2.1.1 Slips (Action-Based Errors)
- Occur during the **execution phase** of an action.
- The *plan* is correct, but the *execution* deviates.
- Occur mostly in **highly practiced, automated, skill-based** behavior.
- Examples: Pressing the wrong button, writing the wrong date, etc.

**Sub-types of Slips (KTU frequently asks this as a 7-mark sub-part):**

| **Sub-type** | **Mechanism** | **Real Example** |
|--------------|---------------|------------------|
| **Capture Slip** | An automatic action hijacks a controlled action | Reaching for sugar and grabbing salt by habit |
| **Description Slip** | Wrong object used in correct action | Pouring juice in coffee cup instead of glass |
| **Data-Driven Slip** | External stimulus triggers wrong schema | Answering "yes" when asked "no" because of momentum |
| **Associative Activation Slip** | A different, related action is triggered | Calling your boss "Dad" |
| **Loss-of-Activation Slip** | A planned step is forgotten mid-action | Forgetting the 2nd ingredient while cooking |

#### 2.1.2 Lapses (Memory-Based Errors)
- Occur during the **storage phase** of a plan.
- Internal, often unnoticed.
- **Omission errors** rather than commission.
- Examples: Forgetting to check a gauge, forgetting to perform a step.

**Sub-types of Lapses:**

| **Sub-type** | **Mechanism** | **Real Example** |
|--------------|---------------|------------------|
| **Plan Storage Lapse** | Plan to be executed later is forgotten | Forgetting to file a safety report after shift |
| **Test Error** | A planned check is performed incorrectly or skipped | Checking a tire but not the spare |
| **Discrimination Error** | Confusion between two similar signals | Mistaking a fire alarm for a drill bell |

#### 2.1.3 Mistakes (Planning/Decision-Based Errors)
- Occur during the **intention formation phase**.
- The execution is perfect, but **the plan itself is wrong**.
- Most serious, hardest to detect.

**Sub-types of Mistakes:**

| **Sub-type** | **Mechanism** | **Example** |
|--------------|---------------|-------------|
| **Rule-Based Mistake** | Applying a *wrong* rule (misapplication, misconception) | Stopping a machine after a 2nd alarm, but rule says stop after 1st |
| **Knowledge-Based Mistake** | No rule available; person improvises wrongly | Diagnosing a fault in a novel way that fails |
| **Memory Failure Mistake** | Forgetting a critical piece of information | Forgetting a key operating parameter |

> [!NOTE]
> **GEMS — Generic Error Modeling System (Reason, 1987):**
> This integrates Rasmussen's SRK model with Reason's error taxonomy:
> - **Skill-Based Level** → Slips & Lapses
> - **Rule-Based Level** → Rule-Based Mistakes
> - **Knowledge-Based Level** → Knowledge-Based Mistakes

### 2.2 Reason's Swiss Cheese Model (1990) — *"Cumulative Act Effect"*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **#1 Most Asked 14-Mark Question in KTU 2024 ESE** for Module 2.

The **Swiss Cheese Model** (also called the *Cumulative Act Effect* or *Organizational Accident Model*) explains how accidents occur in complex systems. It says:

- Every defense layer in a system (technical, human, organizational) has **holes** (latent failures + active failures).
- An accident happens when the **holes in all the slices align**, allowing a hazard to pass through.
- The model uses **four levels of failure**:

| **Level** | **Type of Failure** | **Origin** | **Example** |
|-----------|---------------------|------------|-------------|
| **1. Unsafe Acts** | Errors & Violations | Sharp-end (operator) | Technician skips LOTO procedure |
| **2. Preconditions for Unsafe Acts** | Environmental, personnel, condition factors | Workplace | Fatigue, poor lighting, shift stress |
| **3. Unsafe Supervision** | Inadequate supervision, planning, violation enforcement | Middle management | Supervisor doesn't enforce PPE |
| **4. Organizational Influences** | Resource management, organizational climate, process | Top management | Cost-cutting reduces safety budget |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** "**U-**nions **P**rotect **O**rganizations"
> - **U**nsafe acts → **P**reconditions → **O**rganization

### 2.3 Reason's Taxonomy of Violations

Reason (1990) and later Reason, Parker, & Lawrie (1994) classified violations into **four types**:

| **Violation Type** | **Definition** | **Workplace Example** |
|--------------------|----------------|------------------------|
| **Routine Violation** | Cut corners that are *normalized* and accepted in workplace culture | Not wearing hard hat because "nobody does" |
| **Optimizing Violation** | Violations done for personal benefit/optimization | Driving company truck at high speed to finish early |
| **Situational Violation** | Violations due to immediate situation/pressure | Blocking a fire exit temporarily to move a heavy load |
| **Exceptional Violation** | Rare, isolated, by an otherwise rule-follower | A trusted employee, under unusual stress, makes a rare mistake of judgement |
| **Sabotage** (added by some authors) | Malicious destruction / harm | Deliberately damaging equipment |

> [!WARNING]
> **Don't confuse "Sabotage" with all violations.** In KTU exams, mention it as an *extreme* category only if the question specifically asks about malicious acts. Routine, optimizing, and situational are the **three mandatory** ones.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Error & Violation Quantification)

> [!NOTE]
> Although this is a behavioral topic, KTU examiners expect students to know these key equations, especially in 7-mark sub-parts of 14-mark questions.

| **Formula / Concept** | **Equation / Expression** | **Meaning** | **Typical Use in KTU** |
|------------------------|---------------------------|--------------|--------------------------|
| **Human Error Probability (HEP)** | $\text{HEP} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Errors}}{\text{Number of Opportunities}}$ | Likelihood of human failure per opportunity | HRA (Human Reliability Analysis) |
| **Human Error Rate (HER)** | $\text{HER} = 10^{-3} \text{ to } 10^{-6} \text{ per opportunity}$ | Industry-typical range | Used in PSF-adjusted models |
| **Swain's HRA Equation** | $\text{HEP}_{\text{actual}} = \text{HEP}_{\text{nominal}} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{PSF}_i$ | Nominal HEP modified by Performance Shaping Factors | THERP method |
| **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** | $\text{PFD}_{\text{system}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - \text{PFD}_i)$ | For redundant safety systems | SIL calculations |
| **Violation Frequency Ratio** | $\text{VFR} = \dfrac{\text{Violations Observed}}{\text{Observations Total}}$ | Used in behavioural safety audits | BBS (Behaviour-Based Safety) |
| **Error of Commission Rate** | $\text{ECR} = \dfrac{\text{Commission Errors}}{\text{Total Errors}}$ | Fraction of wrong-action errors vs. omission | ATHEANA method |
| **ATHEANA** | $\text{Risk} = f(\text{EPC}, \text{EF}, \text{PWR})$ | A Technique for Human Event Analysis (USNRC) | Nuclear safety assessments |

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU**, the **Swain & Guttmann THERP equation** is the most exam-relevant. Memorize:
> $$\text{HEP}_{\text{actual}} = \text{HEP}_{\text{nominal}} \times (\text{PSF}_{\text{stress}}) \times (\text{PSF}_{\text{experience}}) \times \dots$$

### 2.5 Performance Shaping Factors (PSFs) — Direct Modifiers of Error Rate

> [!IMPORTANT]
> PSFs are *conditions* that *increase* or *decrease* HEP. They are the **"root amplifiers"** of human error.

| **PSF Category** | **Examples** | **Effect on HEP** |
|-------------------|--------------|---------------------|
| **Internal / Personal** | Fatigue, stress, illness, complacency | ↑ HEP |
| **External / Environmental** | Noise, lighting, temperature, vibration | ↑ HEP |
| **Task / Procedural** | Ambiguous procedures, time pressure, complexity | ↑ HEP |
| **Supervisory / Organizational** | Poor training, bad supervision, low morale | ↑ HEP |
| **Equipment / Interface** | Poor ergonomics, bad display, control layout | ↑ HEP |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility of These Theories

| **Domain** | **Application of Error/Violation Theory** |
|------------|-------------------------------------------|
| **Aviation** | Crew Resource Management (CRM), Black Box analysis |
| **Nuclear Power** | HRA methods (THERP, ATHEANA, HEART) for Probabilistic Safety Assessment |
| **Chemical Process** | Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA) accounting for human error |
| **Healthcare** | Surgical checklists, anesthesia error reduction |
| **Road Safety** | Behaviour-Based Safety (BBS) programmes |
| **Construction** | PPE compliance, Toolbox Talks, Permit-to-Work violation tracking |
| **Maritime** | Bridge Resource Management, fatigue management |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — From Error to Organizational Failure

> [!NOTE]
> This section provides the **exhaustive logical derivation** of how individual human error propagates into a system-level disaster, mapped to Reason's Swiss Cheese Model. Each step is shown explicitly for KTU's 14-mark descriptive answers.

### 3.1 The Causal Chain — "From Cognitive Slip to Catastrophe"

#### Step 1: Cognitive Origin (Mental Event)

A human operator is performing a high-frequency, skill-based task. According to **Rasmussen's SRK framework**, at this moment, the operator is in the **Skill-Based** behavior mode (fast, automatic, low conscious attention).

> This is when **slips and lapses** are most likely to occur.

#### Step 2: Manifestation of Error (Active Failure)

The operator's hand, on autopilot, presses the **wrong button** on a control panel.

> This is a **Commission Slip** — an *action* error, not a *plan* error.

$$\text{Error Type} = f(\text{Behavior Mode}, \text{Attention Level})$$

$$\text{Skill-based mode} + \text{Low attention} \rightarrow \text{High slip probability}$$

#### Step 3: Failure of Defenses (Latent Conditions)

In Reason's Swiss Cheese Model, there should be **multiple defensive layers** to catch the slip:

$$\text{Defense Layer 1} = \text{Alarm} \rightarrow \text{Defense Layer 2} = \text{Operator Self-Check} \rightarrow \text{Defense Layer 3} = \text{Supervisor Verification}$$

If **hole in slice 1 aligns with hole in slice 2 aligns with hole in slice 3**, the error propagates.

#### Step 4: Organizational Influences (Latent Failures)

> The "holes" in the cheese were not random. They were created by **organizational decisions made months or years earlier** (Reason, 1997):

| **Time Lag** | **Organizational Decision (Latent Failure)** | **Resulting Hole in Defense** |
|--------------|---------------------------------------------|------------------------------|
| 5 years ago | Reduced safety training budget | Operator under-trained |
| 2 years ago | Cut maintenance staff by 30% | Alarms not calibrated properly |
| 6 months ago | Merged two departments, supervision gap | No supervisor review before shift handover |
| 1 week ago | Production quota raised 20% | Operator rushing, fatigue, stress |

#### Step 5: Unsafe Supervision

> The middle management layer failed to:
> - Recognize the early warning signs
> - Enforce procedure compliance
> - Provide adequate staffing

#### Step 6: Unsafe Acts (Active Failures)

The operator commits the error (or violation), and the **defenses fail to catch it**.

#### Step 7: Accident / Incident

The hazard reaches the **public / asset / environment** → incident occurs.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU 14-Mark Style

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU Style):** A safety-critical control panel has 3 independent alarms. The human error probability (HEP) for missing one alarm is **0.05**. Calculate:
> (a) The probability that all 3 alarms fail to catch an error.
> (b) The probability of at least one alarm catching it.
> (c) Interpret the result using Reason's Swiss Cheese Model.

**Given:**
- $\text{HEP}_1 = 0.05$ (Alarm 1)
- $\text{HEP}_2 = 0.05$ (Alarm 2)
- $\text{HEP}_3 = 0.05$ (Alarm 3)
- All three alarms are **independent** in catching an error.

**Assumption:** "Failing to catch an error" means the human operator + the alarm system both fail. For simplicity, we treat the HEP as the probability of *missing* the alarm.

---

**Part (a): Probability all 3 alarms miss the error**

For independent events, the joint probability of *all* occurring is the product:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{all 3 miss}) &= P_1 \times P_2 \times P_3 \\
&= 0.05 \times 0.05 \times 0.05 \\
&= (5 \times 10^{-2})^3 \\
&= 125 \times 10^{-6} \\
&= 1.25 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}
$$

So, $P(\text{all 3 miss}) = 1.25 \times 10^{-4}$ (i.e., **0.000125** or **0.0125%**).

---

**Part (b): Probability at least one alarm catches the error**

By the **complement rule** in probability:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{at least one catches}) &= 1 - P(\text{none catch}) \\
&= 1 - P(\text{all 3 miss}) \\
&= 1 - 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 0.999875
\end{aligned}
$$

So, $P(\text{at least one catches}) = 0.999875$ (i.e., **99.9875%**).

---

**Part (c): Interpretation via Swiss Cheese Model**

> [!TIP]
> Each alarm represents **one slice of cheese** in Reason's model. The probability of an *error* passing through all three slices is the **joint miss probability**, which equals $\mathbf{1.25 \times 10^{-4}}$.
>
> - If we had **only 1 alarm**, miss probability = **0.05** (5%).
> - If we had **3 redundant alarms**, miss probability drops to **0.0125%**.
> - This is the **mathematical justification for defense-in-depth** and **redundancy** in safety engineering (also called the **IEC 61508 SIL** principle).

### 3.3 Violation-to-Accident Propagation Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a high-yield 7-mark KTU question template:

| **Step** | **Causal Layer** | **Example** | **Intervention** |
|----------|-------------------|--------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Latent Failure** (Org) | No safety culture, cost-cutting | Leadership commitment |
| 2 | **Latent Failure** (Mgmt) | No enforcement of PPE | Safety audits, BBS |
| 3 | **Precondition** | Fatigue, complacency, time pressure | Rest hours, ergonomic design |
| 4 | **Violation** (Routine) | Skipping steps in permit-to-work | Procedural simplification, training |
| 5 | **Error** (Slip) | Wrong valve opened | Better labelling, interlocks |
| 6 | **Defense Failure** | No alarm, alarm ignored | Layered defenses |
| 7 | **Incident** | Explosion, fire, fatality | Emergency response, lessons learnt |

### 3.4 HFACS Framework (Shappell & Wiegmann, 2000) — U.S. Navy / Aviation Standard

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HFACS (Human Factors Analysis and Classification System)** is the **second most asked framework** in KTU exams, after Swiss Cheese. Memorize all four levels.

| **Level** | **Category** | **Sub-Categories** |
|-----------|--------------|---------------------|
| **Level 1: Unsafe Acts** | Errors | Decision, Skill-Based, Perceptual |
| | Violations | Routine, Exceptional |
| **Level 2: Preconditions** | Environmental Factors | Physical, Technological |
| | Condition of Actors | Mental, Physiological, Physiological/mental limitations |
| | Personnel Factors | CRM, personal readiness |
| **Level 3: Unsafe Supervision** | Inadequate Supervision, Planned Inappropriate Operations, Failed to Correct Known Problem, Supervisory Violations |
| **Level 4: Organizational Influences** | Resource Management, Organizational Climate, Organizational Process |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for HFACS:** "**H**umans **F**ail **A**t **C**ritical **S**ites"
> - **H**umans = Unsafe Acts
> - **F**ail = Preconditions
> - **A**t = Supervision
> - **C**ritical = Organization
> - **S**ites = System

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Reason's Error Taxonomy (Tree Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA["Human Error"]
    rootA --> slip["Slips Action-Based"]
    rootA --> lapse["Lapses Memory-Based"]
    rootA --> mistake["Mistakes Planning-Based"]
    slip --> s1["Capture Slip"]
    slip --> s2["Description Slip"]
    slip --> s3["Data-Driven Slip"]
    slip --> s4["Associative Activation"]
    slip --> s5["Loss of Activation"]
    lapse --> l1["Plan Storage Lapse"]
    lapse --> l2["Test Error"]
    lapse --> l3["Discrimination Error"]
    mistake --> m1["Rule-Based Mistake"]
    mistake --> m2["Knowledge-Based Mistake"]
    mistake --> m3["Memory Failure Mistake"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This Mermaid tree represents the **complete Reason (1990) taxonomy** of human error, as required by KTU Module 2.

---

### 4.2 Reason's Swiss Cheese Model — Block-Level Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    orgA["Level 4 Organizational Influences"]
    orgA --> supA["Level 3 Unsafe Supervision"]
    supA --> preA["Level 2 Preconditions for Unsafe Acts"]
    preA --> actA["Level 1 Unsafe Acts Errors and Violations"]
    actA --> accidentA["Accident Incident Loss"]
    cheese1["Defense Slice 1 Engineering Controls"]
    cheese2["Defense Slice 2 Administrative Controls"]
    cheese3["Defense Slice 3 Human Alarms and Detection"]
    cheese4["Defense Slice 4 Recovery Systems"]
    accidentA --> cheese1
    accidentA --> cheese2
    accidentA --> cheese3
    accidentA --> cheese4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this in KTU exam:**
> - **Left flow** = *causal chain* (how failures are created)
> - **Right flow** = *defensive slices* (how failures are caught)
> - An accident happens when **causal path holes align with defensive slice holes**.

---

### 4.3 Distinction Flow: Error vs. Violation

```mermaid
graph TD
    devA["Human Deviation from Goal"]
    devA --> q1{"Is the action intentional"}
    q1 -- No --> errA["Error Unintentional"]
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{"Is the action malicious"}
    q2 -- No --> vioA["Violation Deliberate Non Malicious"]
    q2 -- Yes --> sabA["Sabotage Deliberate Malicious"]
    errA --> slipA["Slip Action Failure"]
    errA --> lapseA["Lapse Memory Failure"]
    errA --> mistakeA["Mistake Planning Failure"]
    vioA --> rA["Routine"]
    vioA --> oA["Optimizing"]
    vioA --> sA["Situational"]
    vioA --> eA["Exceptional"]
```

---

### 4.4 GEMS — Generic Error Modeling System (Reason, 1987)

```mermaid
graph TD
    gemsA["GEMS"]
    gemsA --> sblA["Skill Based Level Automatic Fast"]
    gemsA --> rblA["Rule Based Level Stored Rules Conscious"]
    gemsA --> kblA["Knowledge Based Level Slow Analytical"]
    sblA --> slA["Slips and Lapses"]
    rblA --> rmA["Rule Based Mistakes"]
    kblA --> kmA["Knowledge Based Mistakes"]
```

---

### 4.5 HFACS — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    hfA["HFACS Four Level Framework"]
    hfA --> l1A["L1 Unsafe Acts Errors and Violations"]
    hfA --> l2A["L2 Preconditions Environment Condition Personnel"]
    hfA --> l3A["L3 Unsafe Supervision Planning Inadequate Failure to Correct"]
    hfA --> l4A["L4 Organizational Influences Resource Climate Process"]
    l1A --> l1A1["Errors Decision Skill Perceptual"]
    l1A --> l1A2["Violations Routine Exceptional"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (2 marks each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are the type of questions asked in KTU **Continuous Evaluation Tests (CET)** and **ESE Section A**.

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the term "Human Error" in the context of safety engineering. Differentiate it from a "Violation" with one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

**Human Error:** As defined by James Reason (1990), a human error is *"the failure of planned actions to achieve their desired goal, where this failure cannot be attributed to chance."* It is an **unintentional** deviation in human performance.

**Violation:** A violation is a **deliberate** deviation from established procedures, rules, or standards necessary to maintain safe system operation (Reason, 1990).

**Example of Error:** A control room operator, intending to press the "STOP" button, accidentally presses "START" — this is a **slip** (unintentional).

**Example of Violation:** A maintenance worker knowingly skips the Lockout-Tagout (LOTO) procedure to "save time" — this is a **routine violation** (intentional).

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List the four types of violations identified by Reason & Parker (1994).**

**Model Answer:**

The four types of violations are:
1. **Routine Violations** — Corner-cutting accepted by workplace culture.
2. **Optimizing Violations** — Done for personal benefit (e.g., finishing early).
3. **Situational Violations** — Done due to immediate situational pressure.
4. **Exceptional Violations** — Rare, isolated acts by otherwise compliant individuals.

---

### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE 2024 Rule:** Module 2 questions will offer a choice between two 14-mark questions. Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. The model solutions below show full, board-style answers.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Adapted]**
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail **Reason's Swiss Cheese Model** of organizational accidents. With the help of a neat diagram, describe its four levels of failure.
**(b) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between **Errors and Violations**. List and explain the three primary categories of human errors with suitable examples.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The **Swiss Cheese Model** (Reason, 1990) — also called the **Cumulative Act Effect** or **Organizational Accident Model** — is a widely accepted framework that explains how accidents occur in complex, well-defended systems. It uses the metaphor of multiple slices of Swiss cheese (representing defensive layers), each having **holes** (representing failures). An accident occurs only when the holes in *all* defensive layers momentarily align, allowing a hazard to propagate through to cause damage.

**Four Levels of Failure (4 marks):**

1. **Level 1 — Unsafe Acts (Active Failures):**
   These are the immediate unsafe actions of front-line workers. They are **active** because their effects are felt almost immediately. They include:
   - **Errors** (slips, lapses, mistakes) — unintentional.
   - **Violations** (routine, situational, etc.) — intentional.
   *Example:* An airline pilot inadvertently extends the landing gear during take-off (slip), or a factory worker knowingly bypasses a safety interlock (violation).

2. **Level 2 — Preconditions for Unsafe Acts:**
   These are the *latent* conditions that make unsafe acts more likely:
   - **Environmental factors** — poor lighting, excessive noise, heat.
   - **Condition of operators** — fatigue, stress, illness, complacency.
   - **Personnel factors** — poor CRM (Crew Resource Management), low morale.
   *Example:* A 14-hour night shift causing operator drowsiness.

3. **Level 3 — Unsafe Supervision:**
   Failures in supervisory and management oversight:
   - Inadequate supervision.
   - Planned inappropriate operations.
   - Failure to correct a known problem.
   - Supervisory violations.
   *Example:* A supervisor who tolerates PPE non-compliance for weeks.

4. **Level 4 — Organizational Influences:**
   The **root** layer — decisions made at the highest levels:
   - **Resource management** — budget cuts to safety.
   - **Organizational climate** — blame culture, fear of reporting.
   - **Organizational process** — poor SOP design, no learning from incidents.
   *Example:* A company policy that rewards production over safety.

**Diagram (1 mark):**
*(Draw Reason's Swiss Cheese Model with 4 slices of cheese, each with holes, and a hazard arrow passing through aligned holes to an "Accident" outcome.)*

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
The Swiss Cheese Model emphasizes that **accidents are not caused by a single failure** but by the **alignment of multiple failures across levels**. Safety improvement requires **closing holes at all four levels**, not just blaming the front-line operator.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Definition Comparison (2 marks):**
| Aspect | Error | Violation |
|--------|-------|-----------|
| Nature | Unintentional | Intentional |
| Awareness | Aware of correct action | Aware of rule but bypasses it |
| Example | Pressing wrong button | Not wearing PPE despite rule |

**Three Categories of Human Errors (5 marks):**

1. **Slips — Errors of Action (2 marks):**
   Slips occur during the **execution** of an action. The plan is correct, but the execution deviates. They occur in highly automated, **skill-based** behavior. Examples:
   - *Capture Slip:* Grabbing the wrong key from a keyring.
   - *Description Slip:* Using ketchup bottle as shampoo (similar shape).
   - *Loss-of-Activation:* Forgetting a step mid-action.

2. **Lapses — Errors of Memory (1.5 marks):**
   Lapses are **internal**, often unnoticed errors. They occur during the **storage** of a plan, leading to omissions. Examples:
   - Forgetting to log a safety parameter after a shift.
   - Forgetting to inspect the backup system (test error).

3. **Mistakes — Errors of Planning (1.5 marks):**
   Mistakes occur at the **intention formation** stage. The execution is correct, but the plan is wrong. Examples:
   - *Rule-Based:* Applying a wrong rule in a diagnostic procedure.
   - *Knowledge-Based:* Improvising a fix in a novel situation incorrectly.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Adapted]**
**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the **Performance Shaping Factors (PSFs)** that influence human error rate in industrial settings. How are they applied in **Swain's THERP** method?
**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **HFACS framework** (Human Factors Analysis and Classification System). List its four levels and two sub-categories under each level.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
**Performance Shaping Factors (PSFs)** are the *internal* and *external* conditions that *increase* or *decrease* the **Human Error Probability (HEP)** during task performance. They are central to **Human Reliability Analysis (HRA)**.

**Classification of PSFs (4 marks):**

1. **Internal / Personal PSFs:**
   - Skill, experience, training.
   - Stress, fatigue, motivation.
   - Physical/mental condition.

2. **External / Environmental PSFs:**
   - Lighting, noise, temperature, humidity.
   - Workplace layout, ergonomics.

3. **Task / Procedural PSFs:**
   - Task complexity, time pressure.
   - Quality of procedures, checklists.

4. **Equipment / Interface PSFs:**
   - Display design, control layout.
   - Alarm systems, automation.

5. **Supervisory / Organizational PSFs:**
   - Quality of supervision.
   - Safety culture, communication.

**Swain's THERP Application (2 marks):**
In **THERP (Technique for Human Error Rate Prediction)**, developed by **Swain & Guttmann (1983)**, the PSFs are used to **modify a nominal HEP**:

$$\text{HEP}_{\text{actual}} = \text{HEP}_{\text{nominal}} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{PSF}_i$$

If all PSFs are *adverse*, the actual HEP is **higher** than nominal. If PSFs are *favourable*, actual HEP is **lower**.

> [!TIP]
> **For 14-mark answer, you should also mention:**
> - THERP uses **Human Error Rate (HER)** tables.
> - It accounts for **dependency between tasks** (total vs. partial vs. zero dependency).
> - It is the basis of nuclear safety PSA (Probabilistic Safety Assessment).

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The **Human Factors Analysis and Classification System (HFACS)** was developed by **Shappell & Wiegmann (2000)** for the U.S. Navy and is now widely used in aviation, healthcare, mining, and process industries. It is a structured accident investigation framework based on Reason's Swiss Cheese Model.

**Four Levels of HFACS (4 marks):**

| Level | Name | Sub-Categories (2 each) |
|-------|------|--------------------------|
| **Level 1** | **Unsafe Acts** | (i) Errors — Decision, Skill-Based, Perceptual; (ii) Violations — Routine, Exceptional |
| **Level 2** | **Preconditions for Unsafe Acts** | (i) Environmental Factors — Physical, Technological; (ii) Condition of Actors — Mental, Physiological, CRM |
| **Level 3** | **Unsafe Supervision** | (i) Inadequate Supervision, Planned Inappropriate Operations; (ii) Failed to Correct Known Problem, Supervisory Violations |
| **Level 4** | **Organizational Influences** | (i) Resource Management, Organizational Climate; (ii) Organizational Process |

**Explanation of Levels (2 marks):**
- **Level 1:** The *active* failures — the immediate unsafe action.
- **Level 2:** The *latent* conditions like fatigue, weather, equipment.
- **Level 3:** Supervisory failures, like not correcting a known risk.
- **Level 4:** The *root* — corporate culture, budget, and process.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Error & Violation in the same answer block** → Marks deducted in Q.A(b).
> 2. **Forgetting to draw the Swiss Cheese diagram** → Lose 1 mark in Q.A(a).
> 3. **Not mentioning "latent" vs. "active" failure distinction** → Marks lost in both Q.A and Q.B.
> 4. **Omitting the formula** $\text{HEP}_{\text{actual}} = \text{HEP}_{\text{nominal}} \times \prod \text{PSF}_i$ → Lose 2 marks in Q.B(a).
> 5. **Writing "HFACS is a Swiss Cheese Model"** → They are related but **different** frameworks. HFACS is based on Swiss Cheese, but adds more sub-categories.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 2 (Errors and Violations):**

- ✅ **Error = Unintentional** failure of actions (Reason, 1990).
- ✅ **Violation = Intentional** breach of rules.
- ✅ **Three types of errors** — Slips (action), Lapses (memory), Mistakes (planning).
- ✅ **Five sub-types of slips** — Capture, Description, Data-Driven, Associative Activation, Loss-of-Activation.
- ✅ **Three sub-types of mistakes** — Rule-Based, Knowledge-Based, Memory Failure.
- ✅ **Four types of violations** — Routine, Optimizing, Situational, Exceptional (Reason & Parker, 1994).
- ✅ **Swiss Cheese Model** has **4 levels** — Unsafe Acts, Preconditions, Unsafe Supervision, Organizational Influences.
- ✅ **HFACS** (Shappell & Wiegmann, 2000) extends Swiss Cheese into 4 levels with 19 sub-categories.
- ✅ **GEMS** maps to **Rasmussen's SRK** — Skill, Rule, Knowledge.
- ✅ **PSFs** modify the **Nominal HEP** in THERP: $\text{HEP}_{\text{actual}} = \text{HEP}_{\text{nominal}} \times \prod \text{PSF}_i$.
- ✅ **Human Error Rate (HER)** is typically $\mathbf{10^{-3}}$ to $\mathbf{10^{-6}}$ per opportunity.
- ✅ **Defence-in-depth** math: $P(\text{all 3 alarms miss}) = 0.05^3 = 1.25 \times 10^{-4}$.
- ✅ **"Active failures"** vs. **"Latent failures"** — sharp-end vs. blunt-end.
- ✅ **Reason (1990, 1997)** and **Swain & Guttmann (1983)** are the **must-cite** references.
- ✅ For violations, the **blame-free** approach is preferred over the **blame-the-worker** approach.
- ✅ **Behaviour-Based Safety (BBS)** programmes target **routine violations** specifically.
- ✅ **HFACS** is widely used in **aviation accident investigation** (e.g., NTSB, ICAO).

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Always end a 14-mark KTU answer with a **conclusion sentence** linking the theory to *industrial practice*. Example: *"In high-reliability organizations (HROs) like nuclear power and aviation, the Swiss Cheese Model is operationalized through Crew Resource Management (CRM), Permit-to-Work systems, and Behaviour-Based Safety (BBS) audits."* This **extra paragraph often fetches 1 grace mark** from the KTU examiner.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Models of Human Error (Swiss Cheese Model, SHEL Model)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT524 - HUMAN AND ORGANIZATIONAL FACTORS IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Models of Human Error (Swiss Cheese Model, SHEL Model)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Human Error in Safety Engineering — The Foundation
