In safety-critical engineering domains (aviation, nuclear, process industries, healthcare), **Human Error** is formally defined as any action or inaction of a human operator that deviates from the expected, prescribed, or correct behavior, leading to an undesirable system outcome, increased risk, or reduced safety margin. Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, human error is studied not as a root cause in isolation, but as a **symptom of deeper systemic, organizational, and design deficiencies**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examination Standard):** Human Error is a *discrepancy between the mental model held by the human operator and the actual state of the system*, caused by cognitive, perceptual, ergonomic, or organizational mismatches. It is treated as the **"last link in a long chain of causal factors"**, never as the origin.

### Intuitive Analogy: The Domino Stack of Defenses
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — A Slice of Swiss Cheese:** Imagine your safety system as a stack of protective barriers (slice of cheese). Each barrier has small **holes** (weaknesses, latent failures, active mistakes). A single hole never causes disaster because the next layer catches the failure. **An accident happens ONLY when the holes in *every* slice momentarily align**, allowing a hazard to pass through all defenses. This is the *Swiss Cheese Model* of accident causation.

### Classification of Human Error (Skill-Rule-Knowledge Basis)
The three classical categories proposed by **Rasmussen (1983)** are:
1. **Skill-Based Errors (SBE):** Slip, lapse, unintended action in routine tasks. (e.g., pressing wrong button).
2. **Rule-Based Errors (RBE):** Misapplication of a memorized rule. (e.g., following an outdated checklist).
3. **Knowledge-Based Errors (KBE):** Cognitive reasoning failure in novel situations. (e.g., pilot solving an unfamiliar fault).

## 1.2 Swiss Cheese Model — Formal Definition

The **Swiss Cheese Model (SCM)**, formally called the **Cumulative Act Effect Model**, was developed by **Prof. James T. Reason in 1990** (University of Manchester). It is a *systemic accident causation model* that posits that accidents occur due to the **alignment of failures across multiple defensive layers** within a system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Board Standard):** The Swiss Cheese Model is a *latent-failure framework* that visualizes an organization's safety defenses as a series of imperfect barriers (slices of cheese), where each barrier contains **gaps (holes)** representing active failures, latent conditions, and systemic weaknesses. An accident propagates when the holes momentarily align across all layers.

### The Two Categories of Failures in SCM
| Failure Type | Description | Origin |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Active Failures** | Immediate unsafe acts by frontline operators (slips, mistakes, violations) | Sharp end (pilots, control room operators) |
| **Latent Failures** | Hidden organizational, managerial, or design flaws that lie dormant | Blunt end (management, designers, regulators) |

## 1.3 SHEL Model — Formal Definition

The **SHEL Model** was originally conceptualized by **Prof. Elwyn Edwards (1972)** and later refined by **Frank H. Hawkins (1975)** as a Human Factors framework for aviation safety. It describes the working environment of a human operator as a system of **four interfacing components**, with the human (Liveware) at the center.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Board Standard):** SHEL is a *descriptive Human Factors model* that identifies the four critical components of the operational environment — **Software, Hardware, Environment, and Liveware** — and emphasizes that **human error arises from mismatches at the interfaces** between the human and these components.

### Components of the SHEL Model
| Letter | Component | Description | Example |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| **S** | **Software** | Non-physical elements: procedures, manuals, checklists, SOPs, training, regulations | Outdated emergency checklist |
| **H** | **Hardware** | Physical machines, equipment, controls, displays, interfaces | Poorly designed cockpit display |
| **E** | **Environment** | Surrounding conditions: physical, economic, social, political, weather | Fog, vibration, noise, fatigue-inducing shift |
| **L** | **Liveware** | The human operator (central element) | Cognitive limitations, stress, fatigue |
| **L** | **Liveware (Other)** | Other humans in the system: crew, ATC, ground staff, supervisors | Poor crew communication |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The L (human) is depicted as a **rough-edged, irregular shape** at the center, while the surrounding components (S, H, E, other L) are shown with **straight edges**. This geometric mismatch is the visual representation of the **interface compatibility problem** that produces human error.

## 1.4 The Shared Philosophy of Both Models
Both models share a **paradigm shift** in safety thinking:
- They move beyond the **"blame-the-operator"** (person model) approach.
- They adopt a **systems-theoretic** view, where human error is a *consequence*, not a *cause*.
- They emphasize **defense-in-depth** and **latent organizational factors**.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Aid for KTU Exam:** *Swiss Cheese = "Holes align → accident."* *SHEL = "Mismatches at interfaces → error."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Swiss Cheese Model — Layered Architecture (Reason's Full Version)

Reason's mature model (often called the **"Organizational Accident Model"**) consists of **four hierarchical levels** of failure, with the accident at the top and the organizational roots at the bottom. Each level generates failures that propagate upward.

### The Four Levels (Top to Bottom)

**Level 1 — Unsafe Acts (Sharp End)**
- Errors: skill-based, decision, perceptual
- Violations: routine, exceptional, optimizing, situational
- Examples: pressing wrong switch, misreading gauge

**Level 2 — Preconditions for Unsafe Acts**
- **Environmental factors:** lighting, noise, temperature
- **Personnel factors:** fatigue, stress, boredom, illness
- **Task factors:** workload, time pressure, ambiguity
- **Individual factors:** competence, motivation, crew composition

**Level 3 — Unsafe Supervision**
- Inadequate supervision, training, planning
- Failure to correct known problems
- Supervisory violations, cultural blindness

**Level 4 — Organizational Influences (Blunt End)**
- Resource management, organizational climate
- Management decisions, economic pressures
- Process standardization, regulatory compliance

### The Seven Conceptual Components of Reason's Swiss Cheese
1. **Hazards** — latent threats in the system
2. **Loss events** — actual accidents realized
3. **Defenses** — barriers (engineered, procedural, regulatory)
4. **Organizational factors** — corporate culture, processes
5. **Supervision** — management layer
6. **Preconditions** — psychological/physiological state, environmental
7. **Unsafe acts** — frontline human actions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Swiss Cheese"?** Each defensive layer is *imperfect* — full of *holes* representing latent and active failures. Hazards travel along trajectory, encountering each barrier. A successful accident requires **simultaneous alignment of holes** across ALL barriers — a probabilistic event, not a deterministic one.

## 2.2 Application: The "Trajectory of Accident Opportunity"
Reason describes the *trajectory of accident opportunity* as a continuous path through the system, originating at the organization's latent failures and terminating at the loss event. Defenses (slices) intercept this trajectory, and only when all defenses have aligned holes does the trajectory pass through.

## 2.3 The SHEL Model — Interface Analysis

The **SHEL Model** identifies **four critical interfaces** (or five, if the two L's are counted separately). Human error arises at the **boundaries** between these components.

### The Four (or Five) Interfaces of SHEL

| Interface | Name | Mismatch Example | Engineering Mitigation |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| **L–L** | Liveware – Liveware | Crew Resource Management failure, miscommunication | CRM training, standardized phraseology |
| **L–H** | Liveware – Hardware | Unreadable gauge, control force mismatch | Human-centered design, ergonomic displays |
| **L–S** | Liveware – Software | Obscure procedure, ambiguous checklist | User-friendly manuals, just-in-time training |
| **L–E** | Liveware – Environment | Vibration-induced reading error, fatigue from heat | Environmental control, rest cycles |

### Why the L (Human) is at the Center
The human is **irregularly shaped** (variable: size, capability, mood, training, fatigue, cultural background) and is the **only component that is not standardized** by the manufacturer. Therefore, the system must be *designed to fit the human*, not vice versa.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hawkins' Insight (1975):** "The SHEL model emphasizes that the human is the central element of the system, and the surrounding components must be designed to be compatible with the human's capabilities and limitations." This is the foundation of **Human-Centered Design (HCD)**.

## 2.4 Comparison Framework: Swiss Cheese vs. SHEL

| Aspect | Swiss Cheese Model | SHEL Model |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **Originator** | James T. Reason (1990) | Elwyn Edwards (1972), refined by Frank Hawkins (1975) |
| **Original Domain** | Generic industrial safety (nuclear, aviation) | Aviation (ICAO Human Factors Digest) |
| **Focus** | Organizational & systemic latent failures | Human–system interface mismatches |
| **Central Element** | Defenses (slices) and their holes | Human (Liveware) |
| **Visual Metaphor** | Stacked cheese slices with aligned holes | Human at center, surrounded by S, H, E, L |
| **Primary Cause of Error** | Alignment of holes across defenses | Mismatch at interfaces |
| **Application** | Accident investigation, Safety Management Systems (SMS) | Cockpit/control room design, CRM, training |
| **Strength** | Captures organizational/management failures | Highlights ergonomic and interface design |
| **Limitation** | Static; doesn't capture dynamic human behavior | Less focus on organizational hierarchy |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Model | Industry Application |
|:---|:---|
| Swiss Cheese | Nuclear plant accident investigation (Chernobyl, Three Mile Island), aviation SMS, healthcare (surgical errors), railway safety |
| SHEL | Aircraft cockpit design, ATC workstations, ICU monitoring, process control rooms, military command centers |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Conceptual Derivation

## 3.1 Logical Derivation of the Swiss Cheese Model

The Swiss Cheese Model is derived from a **probabilistic layered defense analysis**. The following step-by-step logic builds the model from first principles.

### Step 1 — Define the System
Let a system $S$ contain $n$ defensive layers (slices of cheese) $L_1, L_2, \ldots, L_n$ in series between a hazard $H$ and a loss event $E$.

### Step 2 — Define the "Hole" Probability
For each layer $L_i$, define $p_i$ as the probability that the layer has a "hole" (failure) at the moment the hazard attempts to pass. The probability that the layer *catches* the hazard is therefore $(1 - p_i)$.

### Step 3 — Series Defense Probability
The probability that the hazard is *successfully intercepted* (no accident) is the product of all individual interception probabilities:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{no accident}) &= \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left(1 - p_i\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Accident Probability
The probability that an accident *occurs* (all holes align) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{accident}) &= 1 - P(\text{no accident}) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left(1 - p_i\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Interpretation
- If all layers are perfect: $p_i = 0$ for all $i$, then $P(\text{accident}) = 0$. Safe system.
- If any one layer is perfect: $p_j = 0$, then $(1 - p_j) = 1$, and $P(\text{accident}) = 0$. **Defense-in-depth works.**
- Only when *all* layers fail simultaneously does the accident occur.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The model mathematically justifies the principle of **defense-in-depth** — multiple independent barriers dramatically reduce accident probability, even if each individual barrier is imperfect.

### Step 6 — Latent vs. Active Failures
Reason distinguishes:
- **Active failures ($p_i^{active}$):** time-varying, often transient (operator error, distraction).
- **Latent failures ($p_i^{latent}$):** relatively stable, embedded in system design (poor alarm, ambiguous procedure).

The effective hole probability is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_i &= p_i^{active} + p_i^{latent} - p_i^{active} \cdot p_i^{latent}
\end{aligned}
$$

This expresses that holes can be created by either or both failure types.

## 3.2 Logical Derivation of the SHEL Interface Mismatch

The SHEL Model can be expressed as an **interface compatibility function**. Define each component as a "shape" of behavioral characteristics, and the human is the *most irregular* shape.

### Step 1 — Component Definition
Let each component be represented by a characteristic vector:
- $S$ — software (procedure complexity, clarity, latency)
- $H$ — hardware (ergonomic rating, control force, display legibility)
- $E$ — environment (temperature, noise, vibration, illumination)
- $L$ — liveware (cognitive load, fatigue, training, perception)

### Step 2 — Define the Mismatch Function
At each interface, define a mismatch score $M_{XY} \in [0, 1]$ where 0 = perfect fit, 1 = total incompatibility. The total human error potential is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{potential} &= f\left(M_{LS}, M_{LH}, M_{LE}, M_{LL}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Simplest Linear Form
In a basic additive model:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{potential} &= w_1 M_{LS} + w_2 M_{LH} + w_3 M_{LE} + w_4 M_{LL}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $w_i$ are weighting factors representing the operational significance of each interface.

### Step 4 — Interpretation
- Minimizing any $M_{XY}$ reduces $E_{potential}$.
- The **most impactful mitigation** targets the interface with the highest weight $w_i$ *and* the highest mismatch $M_{XY}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Application:** In a control room redesign, if $M_{LH}$ (hardware mismatch) is high, redesigning displays and controls gives the greatest error reduction. If $M_{LL}$ (crew communication) is high, CRM training is prioritized.

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Model

| Scenario | Recommended Model | Reason |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| Investigating a nuclear plant accident (post-hoc) | Swiss Cheese | Captures latent organizational failures |
| Designing a new aircraft cockpit | SHEL | Highlights Liveware–Hardware interface |
| Building a Safety Management System (SMS) | Swiss Cheese | Provides the layered defense architecture |
| Crew Resource Management training | SHEL | Targets Liveware–Liveware interface |
| Hospital surgical safety analysis | Swiss Cheese | Reveals systemic holes in defense layers |
| Industrial ergonomics redesign | SHEL | Identifies Liveware–Environment mismatch |
| Regulatory auditing | Swiss Cheese | Evaluates multiple defense layers |
| Human–Automation interaction design | SHEL | Frames human as central element |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Swiss Cheese Model — Layered Defense Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the four levels of Reason's Swiss Cheese Model, the trajectory of accident opportunity, and the alignment of holes leading to the loss event.

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeA["LOSS EVENT (Accident)"] --> nodeB["LEVEL 1: UNSAFE ACTS - Skill Errors, Decisions, Violations"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["LEVEL 2: PRECONDITIONS - Fatigue, Stress, Environment, Task Load"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["LEVEL 3: UNSAFE SUPERVISION - Inadequate Training, Planning, Corrective Action"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["LEVEL 4: ORGANIZATIONAL INFLUENCES - Culture, Resources, Management"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["HAZARD ORIGIN - Latent Threats in System"]
    nodeA -.-> nodeG["HOLE ALIGNMENT - All Defenses Fail Simultaneously"]
    nodeB -.-> nodeG
    nodeC -.-> nodeG
    nodeD -.-> nodeG
    nodeE -.-> nodeG
    nodeG -.-> nodeH["SAFETY DEFENSES - Engineered, Procedural, Regulatory, Training"]
    style nodeA fill="#ff6b6b",stroke="#000",stroke-width="2px",color:#fff
    style nodeF fill="#ffd93d",stroke:#000",stroke-width:"2px"
    style nodeG fill:"#6bcf7f",stroke:"#000",stroke-width:"2px"
    style nodeH fill:"#4d96ff",stroke:"#000",stroke-width:"2px",color:#fff
```

**Diagram Reading Guide:**
- The vertical flow (top to bottom) shows the **hierarchical causation chain** from organizational roots to the frontline act.
- The dashed lines converging on `nodeG` represent the **"holes alignment"** condition required for accident propagation.
- `nodeH` represents the engineered, procedural, and training barriers that must be penetrated.

## 4.2 SHEL Model — Interface Architecture

The following block diagram shows the human (Liveware) at the center, interfacing with the four other system components.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeSW["S - SOFTWARE\nProcedures, Manuals,\nChecklists, Training"] -.->|Mismatch| nodeLV
    nodeHW["H - HARDWARE\nMachines, Displays,\nControls, Equipment"] -.->|Mismatch| nodeLV
    nodeEN["E - ENVIRONMENT\nPhysical, Weather,\nNoise, Vibration"] -.->|Mismatch| nodeLV
    nodeLL["L - LIVEWARE OTHER\nCrew, ATC, Supervisors,\nGround Personnel"] -.->|Mismatch| nodeLV
    nodeLV["L - LIVEWARE\nHuman Operator\nCentral Irregular Element"] --> nodeOUT["HUMAN ERROR\nManifestation"]
    style nodeLV fill:"#ff6b6b",stroke:"#000",stroke-width:"3px",color:#fff
    style nodeSW fill:"#4d96ff",stroke:"#000",color:#fff
    style nodeHW fill:"#4d96ff",stroke:"#000",color:#fff
    style nodeEN fill:"#4d96ff",stroke:"#000",color:#fff
    style nodeLL fill:"#4d96ff",stroke:"#000",color:#fff
    style nodeOUT fill:"#ffd93d",stroke:"#000",stroke-width:"2px"
```

**Diagram Reading Guide:**
- The four surrounding components (`nodeSW`, `nodeHW`, `nodeEN`, `nodeLL`) connect to the central `nodeLV` (human).
- Dashed edges labeled "Mismatch" represent the **four critical interfaces** identified by SHEL.
- `nodeOUT` represents the human error outcome that emerges from interface mismatches.

## 4.3 Combined Conceptual Map — Why Both Models?

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeRoot["HUMAN ERROR IN COMPLEX SYSTEMS"] --> nodeSC["SWISS CHEESE MODEL\nWhy accidents happen\nat the system level"]
    nodeRoot --> nodeSH["SHEL MODEL\nWhere human error\noriginates at interfaces"]
    nodeSC --> nodeDEEP["Defenses: Engineered, Procedural,\nTraining, Regulatory"]
    nodeSC --> nodeHOLE["Holes: Active and Latent Failures"]
    nodeSH --> nodeCENT["Liveware at center\nwith irregular shape"]
    nodeSH --> nodeINT["Four Interfaces: L-L, L-H, L-S, L-E"]
    nodeDEEP --> nodeOUT["ACCIDENT PROBABILITY"]
    nodeHOLE --> nodeOUT
    nodeCENT --> nodeOUT
    nodeINT --> nodeOUT
    style nodeRoot fill:"#ff6b6b",stroke:"#000",stroke-width:"3px",color:#fff
    style nodeOUT fill:"#ffd93d",stroke:"#000",stroke-width:"2px"
    style nodeSC fill:"#4d96ff",stroke:"#000",color:#fff
    style nodeSH fill:"#4d96ff",stroke:"#000",color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Define the **Swiss Cheese Model** of accident causation. Who developed it and what does each "slice" represent? **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The Swiss Cheese Model, developed by **Prof. James T. Reason in 1990**, is a systemic accident causation model that visualizes an organization's safety defenses as a series of **stacked slices of Swiss cheese**. Each slice represents a **defensive layer** (engineered, procedural, organizational, supervisory), and the **holes** in each slice represent **latent and active failures** (weaknesses, errors, violations). An accident occurs when the **holes in all the slices momentarily align**, allowing the trajectory of accident opportunity to pass through every defense, leading to a loss event. The model emphasizes that human error is rarely the root cause but is instead a symptom of deeper systemic and organizational failures. **(3 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** List the four components of the **SHEL Model** and explain why the human (Liveware) is placed at the center. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The **SHEL Model** consists of four components: **Software** (procedures, manuals, checklists, training), **Hardware** (machines, displays, controls), **Environment** (physical, economic, social conditions), and **Liveware** (the human operator and other humans). The human (Liveware) is placed at the center because **the human is the only component that is not standardized by the manufacturer** — it is the most variable, irregular, and fallible element in the system. Therefore, all other components must be *designed to fit the human's capabilities and limitations* (Human-Centered Design), and human error arises from **mismatches at the interfaces** between the human and the surrounding components. **(3 Marks)**

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**
> **Q. Module 2 / Choice A (14 Marks)**
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **Swiss Cheese Model** of human error. With the help of a suitable diagram, describe its four levels and the concept of latent and active failures. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Discuss the **SHEL Model** in detail. Identify all four interfaces and explain how each interface mismatch contributes to human error in a safety-critical operation. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
The Swiss Cheese Model (SCM), developed by **James T. Reason in 1990**, is a layered accident causation model that demonstrates how multiple, independent defensive failures must align for a catastrophic loss to occur.

**The Four Levels (4 Marks):**
The model has four hierarchical levels, from frontline acts (top) to organizational roots (bottom):
1. **Level 1 — Unsafe Acts:** Skill-based slips, decision errors, perceptual mistakes, and violations by frontline operators.
2. **Level 2 — Preconditions for Unsafe Acts:** Environmental factors (noise, heat), personnel factors (fatigue, stress), task factors (high workload, time pressure), and individual factors (low competence, poor communication).
3. **Level 3 — Unsafe Supervision:** Inadequate supervision, planning failures, failure to correct known issues, and supervisory violations.
4. **Level 4 — Organizational Influences:** Resource allocation, organizational climate, management decisions, and regulatory compliance — the "blunt end" of the system.

**Latent vs. Active Failures (2 Marks):**
- **Active failures** are unsafe acts committed by frontline operators with immediate consequences (e.g., pressing the wrong button).
- **Latent failures** are hidden organizational, managerial, or design flaws that lie dormant until they combine with active failures to breach defenses (e.g., ambiguous checklist, poor alarm design).
- Accidents occur only when the **holes (failures) align across all slices simultaneously**.

**Conclusion:** The SCM shifts the focus from individual blame to **systemic design and organizational improvement**.

### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
The SHEL Model, originally proposed by **Elwyn Edwards (1972)** and refined by **Frank Hawkins (1975)**, is a Human Factors framework that identifies four critical components of the operational environment with the human (Liveware) at the center.

**The Four Components (2 Marks):**
- **S — Software:** Procedures, manuals, checklists, training programs, SOPs.
- **H — Hardware:** Physical machines, instruments, displays, controls, equipment.
- **E — Environment:** Physical surroundings (weather, noise, vibration), and the broader economic/social context.
- **L — Liveware (Two L's):** The human operator (central) and other humans (crew, ATC, supervisors).

**Four Interface Mismatches (3 Marks):**
1. **Liveware–Software (L–S):** Outdated or ambiguous procedures cause operators to misinterpret required actions. *Example: Confusing checklist wording during an emergency.*
2. **Liveware–Hardware (L–H):** Poorly designed displays or controls lead to perceptual errors. *Example: An unreadable altimeter causing a controlled flight into terrain.*
3. **Liveware–Environment (L–E):** Extreme weather, noise, or fatigue-inducing conditions degrade human performance. *Example: Turbulence causing a missed radio call.*
4. **Liveware–Liveware (L–L):** Poor communication or coordination between crew members or with ATC. *Example: Misheard clearance leading to runway incursion.*

**Conclusion (1 Mark):**
The SHEL Model emphasizes **Human-Centered Design (HCD)** — the system must be designed to match the human's irregular capabilities, and **error mitigation must focus on interface compatibility**, not on blaming the operator.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**
> **Q. Module 2 / Choice B (14 Marks)**
> **(a)** Compare and contrast the **Swiss Cheese Model** and the **SHEL Model** of human error. Highlight the strengths and limitations of each. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Apply the **Swiss Cheese Model** to analyze a real-world industrial accident (e.g., the Chernobyl disaster, Bhopal, or a runway incursion). Identify the active and latent failures at each level. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Comparative Table (4 Marks):**
| Aspect | Swiss Cheese Model | SHEL Model |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| Originator | James T. Reason (1990) | Edwards (1972), Hawkins (1975) |
| Focus | Organizational and systemic latent failures | Human–system interface mismatches |
| Central Element | Layered defenses (slices) | Human (Liveware) |
| Cause of Error | Alignment of holes across all defenses | Mismatch at L–L, L–H, L–S, L–E interfaces |
| Application | Accident investigation, SMS | Design of human interfaces, training |
| Strength | Captures management and organizational roots | Highlights ergonomic and design issues |
| Limitation | Static; misses dynamic human behavior | Less focus on organizational hierarchy |

**Complementary Nature (2 Marks):**
The two models are **complementary, not competing**. The Swiss Cheese Model explains *why* accidents happen at the system level (defense-in-depth failure), while the SHEL Model explains *where* human error originates (interface mismatch). Together, they form a complete human factors framework: SHEL identifies the **proximate cause** (interface), and Swiss Cheese identifies the **systemic cause** (organizational holes).

**Conclusion (1 Mark):**
Modern safety engineering uses both models in an integrated manner — SHEL for design, SCM for accident analysis.

### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Case Study: Bhopal Gas Tragedy (1984) — Swiss Cheese Analysis**

**Level 4 — Organizational Influences (2 Marks):**
- Cost-cutting measures led to deferred maintenance.
- Inadequate safety audit and regulatory enforcement.
- Inadequate emergency planning and community preparedness.
- Decision to store large quantities of MIC (Methyl Isocyanate).

**Level 3 — Unsafe Supervision (1.5 Marks):**
- Supervisors ignored repeated alarms and instrument anomalies in the weeks before the accident.
- Inadequate operator training on emergency shutdown procedures.
- Failure to act on known design deficiencies in the flare tower and scrubber.

**Level 2 — Preconditions for Unsafe Acts (1.5 Marks):**
- Operator fatigue from long shifts.
- High cognitive workload during shift changeover.
- Ambiguous written procedures for handling runaway reactions.

**Level 1 — Unsafe Acts (1 Mark):**
- Operator failed to apply water curtain in time.
- Misdiagnosis of the nature of the leak.
- Inadequate communication between shift operators.

**Active vs. Latent Failures (1 Mark):**
- **Active failures:** Operator misjudgment, delayed water curtain activation.
- **Latent failures:** Poor design of relief valves, deferred maintenance, organizational cost-cutting, regulatory gaps.

**Conclusion:** The accident was not a single operator's mistake but the result of **aligned holes across all four levels of the Swiss Cheese Model**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse the two models.** Swiss Cheese focuses on *defenses failing*; SHEL focuses on *interfaces mismatching*. Mixing them is a 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Do NOT list the four SHEL components without explaining the interfaces.** Simply listing S, H, E, L without interface analysis loses 3 marks.
> 3. **Do NOT attribute SHEL to Reason** (he developed SCM). SHEL is by Edwards/Hawkins. Incorrect attribution = 1 mark deduction.
> 4. **Do NOT draw the Swiss Cheese diagram as a single slice.** Always show *multiple slices with aligned holes*. A single slice is worth 0 marks for the diagram question.
> 5. **In 14-mark answers, always include a labeled diagram** — KTU board examiners allocate 2 marks specifically for a clear, labeled schematic.
> 6. **In case-study applications, identify BOTH active and latent failures** explicitly. Mentioning only active failures (operator error) is the classic "blame-the-operator" trap that the Swiss Cheese Model was designed to prevent.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2**

- [x] **Swiss Cheese Model** is by **James T. Reason (1990)**; sometimes called the *Cumulative Act Effect Model*.
- [x] The model visualizes defenses as **stacked cheese slices** with **holes** representing failures.
- [x] An accident occurs **only when holes in ALL slices align**.
- [x] **Four levels:** Unsafe Acts → Preconditions → Unsafe Supervision → Organizational Influences.
- [x] **Active failures** = frontline, immediate (slips, mistakes, violations).
- [x] **Latent failures** = hidden, organizational, dormant (design flaws, management gaps).
- [x] **SHEL Model** is by **Edwards (1972)** and refined by **Frank Hawkins (1975)**.
- [x] SHEL stands for **Software, Hardware, Environment, Liveware** (with two L's — one for self, one for others).
- [x] The human is depicted as an **irregular shape at the center** — the most variable component.
- [x] Human error arises from **mismatches at four interfaces**: L–L, L–H, L–S, L–E.
- [x] The SHEL Model is the foundation of **Human-Centered Design (HCD)** and **Crew Resource Management (CRM)**.
- [x] Both models are **complementary**: SHEL = *proximate cause* (interface), Swiss Cheese = *systemic cause* (organizational holes).
- [x] **Defense-in-depth** is the engineering principle mathematically justified by the Swiss Cheese Model: $P(\text{accident}) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - p_i)$.
- [x] For KTU exam: **always draw a labeled diagram** in 14-mark answers.
- [x] For KTU exam: **always distinguish active vs. latent failures** in case-study applications.
- [x] Key industry applications: aviation, nuclear, chemical process, healthcare, railway, military.
- [x] Famous applications: **Chernobyl (1986)** — Swiss Cheese alignment of organizational, supervisory, and operator failures; **Bhopal (1984)** — similar Swiss Cheese pattern; **Air France 447 (2009)** — SHEL L–H and L–L interface mismatches.
- [x] Modern frameworks like **ICAO SMS (Safety Management System)** are direct descendants of the Swiss Cheese Model.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Techniques for Human Error Identification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT524 - HUMAN AND ORGANIZATIONAL FACTORS IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Techniques for Human Error Identification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Human Error Identification Techniques** are a systematic set of prospective (before-the-event) and retrospective (after-the-event) methodologies used in safety engineering, human factors, and risk assessment disciplines to anticipate, classify, quantify, and analyze human failures within man-machine-process systems. According to KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, these techniques form the analytical backbone of **Human Reliability Analysis (HRA)** — the discipline that estimates the **Human Error Probability (HEP)** for operator actions during both normal and abnormal plant conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Human Error Identification Techniques are structured qualitative and quantitative procedures that systematically identify, classify, and evaluate the types, causes, and consequences of human actions (or inactions) that may compromise the safety, reliability, or productivity of engineered systems.*

The discipline was formally institutionalized after the **Three Mile Island (1979)** and **Chernobyl (1986)** accidents, where investigators realized that mechanical failures alone could not explain catastrophic system losses — human and organizational decisions were equally culpable. This led to the development of first-generation techniques (THERP, HEART, SLIM-MAUD) in the 1980s, and second-generation context-aware methods (ATHEANA, CREAM, MERMOS) in the 1990s–2000s.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Blind Surgeon" Metaphor

Imagine a skilled surgeon performing an operation wearing a blindfold (an analogy for poor Human-System Interface design). The surgeon's *technical knowledge* is excellent, and the *surgical tools* are perfect. Yet the operation is almost certain to fail. To prevent this:

- **Retrospective techniques** (TRIPOD, Critical Incident Technique) would be like reviewing past surgeries to find *why* the patient bled out.
- **Prospective techniques** (SHERPA, THERP) would be like *predicting* the steps where the surgeon might slip *before* the surgery.
- **Context-based techniques** (ATHEANA, CREAM) would be like assessing *environmental* conditions — bad lighting, sleep-deprived surgeon, alarms sounding — that push the surgeon's performance downward.

> [!NOTE]
> The fundamental insight is: **humans are not the root cause of accidents — they are the final link** in a chain of latent organizational, design, and procedural weaknesses. Error identification techniques trace the chain back to its origin.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Is Critical for KTU Board Examinations

| KTU Relevance | Practical Impact |
|---------------|-----------------|
| Direct CO linkage: **CO2 – Apply human error identification techniques** | Used in nuclear, aviation, oil & gas, healthcare safety |
| Frequently asked as 14-mark comparison questions | Reduces accidents in high-hazard industries |
| High-weightage in Module 2 of PESFT524 | Regulatory mandate (AERB, IAEA, OSHA) |
| Foundation for Module 3 (Human Reliability Assessment) | Anchors Probabilistic Safety Assessments (PSAs) |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU 2024 board valuation, examiners expect students to know at least **3 techniques in depth** (typically THERP, SHERPA, and either HEART or CREAM) along with their **error classifications** and **limitations**. A 14-mark "Compare" question is the most common pattern.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Human Error Identification Techniques

Human error identification techniques are classified along two primary axes: **temporal axis** (when applied) and **quantitative axis** (output type).

### A. Prospective Techniques (Before-the-Event)

These techniques analyze the system *before* an accident occurs, predicting where errors are likely to happen.

1. **Task Analysis Techniques**
   - **Hierarchical Task Analysis (HTA)** — decomposes a task into goals → operations → plans.
   - **Cognitive Task Analysis (CTA)** — captures mental processes, decision points.
   - **Link Analysis (LA)** — diagrams communication and movement patterns.
   - **Timeline Analysis** — sequences events on a time axis.

2. **Human Error Analysis Techniques**
   - **SHERPA** (Systematic Human Error Reduction and Prediction Approach) — by **David Embrey (1986)**.
   - **Predictive Human Error Analysis (PHEA)** — by the UK Health & Safety Executive.
   - **SPEAR** (System for Predicting Human Error and Recovery).

3. **Quantitative HRA Techniques**
   - **THERP** (Technique for Human Error Rate Prediction) — by **Swain & Guttmann (1983)**.
   - **HEART** (Human Error Assessment and Reduction Technique) — by **J.C. Williams (1988)**.
   - **SLIM-MAUD** (Success Likelihood Index Methodology – Multi-Attribute Utility Decomposition).
   - **THERP+ / INTENT** — extensions of THERP.

### B. Retrospective Techniques (After-the-Event)

These techniques reconstruct the *causes* of an accident that has already occurred.

1. **Critical Incident Technique (CIT)** — by **John Flanagan (1954)**.
2. **TRIPOD** (and **TRIPOD Beta**) — by **Reason & Hudson (1990s)**.
3. **Accident Investigation Techniques** — MORT, SCAT, TapRooT.
4. **Human Factors Analysis and Classification System (HFACS)** — by **Shappell & Wiegmann (2000)**.

### C. Second-Generation / Context-Aware Techniques

1. **ATHEANA** (A Technique for Human Event Analysis) — US NRC (1990s).
2. **CREAM** (Cognitive Reliability and Error Analysis Method) — by **Erik Hollnagel (1998)**.
3. **MERMOS** — EDF (France).
4. **HERA** — UK HSE.

## 2.2 Detailed Walk-Through of Major Techniques

### 2.2.1 SHERPA (Systematic Human Error Reduction and Prediction Approach)

**Origin:** David Embrey, 1986, UK.
**Type:** Prospective, qualitative, task-based.
**Application:** Procedural task analysis in process industries, nuclear, rail.

**Eight-Step SHERPA Methodology:**

| Step | Activity | Output |
|------|----------|--------|
| 1 | Define the system under analysis | Scope statement |
| 2 | Perform Hierarchical Task Analysis (HTA) | Task hierarchy tree |
| 3 | Identify human tasks at each HTA node | Task catalog |
| 4 | Classify each task into one of 8 task types | Task-type codes |
| 5 | Identify likely errors for each task | Error catalog |
| 6 | Determine psychological error mechanisms | Root cause (C/D/O/T/I/F/S/A) |
| 7 | Assess consequences & recovery | Risk matrix |
| 8 | Recommend error reduction strategies | Mitigation list |

**SHERPA Task Type Classification (8 Types — KTU High-Yield):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SHERPA Task Types (Memorize for KTU):**
> 1. **P — Plan** (decision-making, strategy selection)
> 2. **R — Retrieve** (information retrieval, look-up)
> 3. **F — Formulate** (problem-solving, computation)
> 4. **A — Action** (physical manipulation, control)
> 5. **C — Check** (verification, comparison)
> 6. **I — Information transfer** (reporting, communication)
> 7. **Fe — Feedback** (response to system output)
> 8. **S — Selection** (choice between alternatives)

**SHERPA Error Classifications (Embrey's Generic Error Types):**

- **A1 — Action too early / too late**
- **A2 — Action in wrong direction**
- **A3 — Action on wrong object**
- **A4 — Action omitted**
- **A5 — Action incomplete**
- **A6 — Action wrong (intrusion)**
- **A7 — Action too much / too little**
- **A8 — Repetition / failure to repeat**

**SHERPA Psychological Error Mechanisms:**

- **C — Communication channel failure**
- **D — Display failure**
- **O — Operate control failure**
- **T — Training & knowledge failure**
- **I — Individual factors (stress, fatigue)**
- **F — Friendly failure (assumed correct by others)**
- **S — Stress (workload, time pressure)**
- **A — Alarm/event masking**

### 2.2.2 THERP (Technique for Human Error Rate Prediction)

**Origin:** Swain & Guttmann, Sandia National Labs, 1983 (NUREG/CR-1278).
**Type:** Prospective, quantitative.
**Output:** Human Error Probability (HEP) values between **0 and 1**.

**THERP Procedure:**

1. Define system events and operator actions.
2. Decompose actions into sub-tasks.
3. Estimate nominal HEP for each sub-task from THERP tables.
4. For dependencies between tasks, apply **Dependence Model (Z, D, L) — see formula sheet below**.
5. Combine sub-task HEPs into overall HEP using **event trees**.
6. Calculate **HEP for Recovery** = Probability that operator detects and corrects a failed action.

**THERP Task-Type Dependence Levels (KERN-HRA Tables):**

- **Zero Dependence (Z):** Tasks are independent; one does not influence the other.
- **High Dependence (HD):** The second task is highly influenced by the success/failure of the first (success probability for second task is 0.95 if first succeeded, but only **0.15** if first failed).
- **Low Dependence (LD):** The second task is moderately influenced (success probability = 0.5 if first failed).
- **Moderate Dependence (MD):** In between HD and LD.

### 2.2.3 HEART (Human Error Assessment and Reduction Technique)

**Origin:** J.C. Williams, 1988.
**Type:** Prospective, quantitative, generic.
**Output:** Human Error Probability (HEP) per task.

**Key Concept:** HEART is **"Error-Producing Condition (EPC) based"** — it multiplies a generic task's baseline human unreliability with multipliers for adverse conditions.

### 2.2.4 CREAM (Cognitive Reliability and Error Analysis Method)

**Origin:** Erik Hollnagel, 1998.
**Type:** Retrospective and prospective, context-aware (second generation).
**Core Concept:** All human actions are governed by four **Control Modes (CPC — Common Performance Conditions):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CREAM Four Control Modes (Board-Favorite):**
> 1. **Strategic** — Conscious planning, deliberate reasoning (most reliable).
> 2. **Tactical** — Action based on stored rules/procedures (moderately reliable).
> 3. **Opportunistic** — Reactive, data-driven actions (less reliable).
> 4. **Scrambled** — Loss of control, cognitive chaos (least reliable).

**CREAM's 9 Common Performance Conditions (CPCs):**

1. Adequacy of organization
2. Working conditions
3. Adequacy of MMI (Man-Machine Interface) and operational support
4. Availability of procedures/plans
5. Number of simultaneous goals
6. Available time
7. Time of day (circadian factor)
8. Adequacy of training and experience
9. Crew collaboration quality

**Cognitive Performance Function (CPF):**

$$ \text{CPF} = \text{Performance Shaping Factor (PSF)} \text{ product, bounded } [0, 1] $$

Each CPC is scored on a 5-point scale, and the function yields a predicted control mode → which maps to a HEP band (e.g., strategic ≈ 0.0001 to 0.01; scrambled ≈ 0.1 to 1.0).

### 2.2.5 ATHEANA (A Technique for Human Event Analysis)

**Origin:** US Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC), late 1990s.
**Type:** Retrospective, context-aware.
**Distinctive Feature:** Explicitly models **"Forcing Functions" (FF)** and **"Unsafe Acts" (UA)** within real operational contexts.

**ATHEANA Process Steps:**

1. Define the event of interest.
2. Identify the relevant operating context.
3. Identify Error-Producing Contexts (EPCs).
4. Identify Unsafe Acts (UAs).
5. Model the relationship between EPCs and UAs.
6. Quantify (or qualitatively assess) the HEP.

### 2.2.6 TRIPOD (and TRIPOD Beta)

**Origin:** Originally developed by the Shell Group (RIMTECH), popularized by **James Reason** and **Andrew Hudson** in the early 1990s.
**Type:** Retrospective, organizational, accident-investigation tool.
**Output:** Identifies **"Tripod"** of latent failure types and produces a **Basic Risk Factor (BRF)** score.

**TRIPOD's 11 General Failure Types (GFTs) — KTU Must-Know:**

| Code | Failure Type | Organizational Level |
|------|--------------|----------------------|
| **1** | Maintenance errors | Technical |
| **2** | Design errors | Technical |
| **3** | Procedural errors | Supervisory |
| **4** | Error-enforcing conditions | Workplace |
| **5** | Incompatible goals | Management |
| **6** | Communication failure | Supervisory |
| **7** | Training inadequacy | Training |
| **8** | Defences inadequate | Defensive |
| **9** | Unsafe supervision | Supervisory |
| **10** | Planned inappropriate operations | Management |
| **11** | Organisational factors | Strategic |

**TRIPOD Beta Extension:** Added recovery failure analysis, organizational culture, and quantification of BRF using a Bayesian-like Bayesian Belief Network.

### 2.2.7 Critical Incident Technique (CIT)

**Origin:** John C. Flanagan, 1954, originally for aviation psychology.
**Type:** Retrospective interview-based.
**Procedure:** Open-ended interview of frontline workers to identify incidents that **contributed positively OR negatively** to outcomes.

**Five Phases (Flanagan's CIT):**

1. **Establish the frame of reference** — what counts as an "incident"?
2. **Decide on data collection method** — interviews, surveys, archival.
3. **Collect data** — typically 50–100 incident reports.
4. **Analyze the data** — categorize by behavior, activity, cause.
5. **Interpret findings** — form hypotheses about human/system vulnerabilities.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the **mathematical backbone** of HRA quantification. Memorizing the HEART and THERP equations is **mandatory** for KTU 14-mark calculation questions.

### Table 2.1 — HRA Quantitative Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Variables & Units | KTU Use Case |
|---|---------|-------------------|--------------|
| 1 | **HEART Generic Equation:** $\text{HEP} = \text{Nominal} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} (\text{EPC}_i - 1 + 1) \times \text{AP} \times \text{W}$ | Nominal = Generic task unreliability (from Table A.1); EPC = Error Producing Condition multiplier; AP = Activity Profile coefficient; W = Workplace multiplier. | Estimate HEP for a single operator task. |
| 2 | **THERP Combined HEP (Independent tasks):** $\text{HEP}_{\text{total}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - \text{HEP}_i)$ | HEP$_i$ = sub-task HEP. | Combine probabilities for independent sub-tasks. |
| 3 | **THERP Combined HEP (High Dependence, HD):** $\text{HEP}_{\text{HD}} = \text{HEP}_1 + (1 - \text{HEP}_1) \cdot \text{HEP}_2^*$ | HEP$_2^*$ = HEP given previous task failed (typically 0.5 for HD). | Apply when second task is heavily influenced by the first. |
| 4 | **CREAM Control Mode Mapping:** $\text{HEP} = f(\text{CPC}_1, \text{CPC}_2, \ldots, \text{CPC}_9)$ | Each CPC scored –1, 0, or +1; result maps to strategic/tactical/opportunistic/scrambled. | Link context to a probabilistic band. |
| 5 | **SLIM-MAUD:** $\text{SLI} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i r_i$ | SLI = Success Likelihood Index; $w_i$ = PSF weight; $r_i$ = PSF rating. Then P(error) is obtained by a log-normal relationship. | Quantify HEP from expert PSF ratings. |
| 6 | **SHERPA Risk Score:** $\text{Risk} = \text{Consequence} \times \text{Likelihood}$ | Both scored 1–5 (low to high). | Prioritize errors for mitigation. |
| 7 | **PFD Calculation (Safety Function):** $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T \text{PFD}(t)\, dt$ | PFD = Probability of Failure on Demand; T = proof test interval. | Used in SIL (Safety Integrity Level) verification. |

### Table 2.2 — HEART Nominal Generic Task Unreliabilities (Sample — KTU-Relevant)

| Generic Task Type (A) | Nominal Unreliability (G) | Description |
|------------------------|-----------------------------|-------------|
| A1 | $5 \times 10^{-5}$ | Totally unfamiliar, complex task |
| A2 | $1.4 \times 10^{-4}$ | Shift or restore system to a new state on first attempt |
| A3 | $2.2 \times 10^{-4}$ | Complex task requiring high cognitive load |
| A4 | $6.7 \times 10^{-4}$ | Fairly simple task with little dependent variable |
| A5 | $1.3 \times 10^{-3}$ | Routine, highly practiced task |
| A6 | $3.5 \times 10^{-3}$ | Restoring or shifting a system to original state |
| A7 | $5 \times 10^{-2}$ | Completely familiar task performed with negligible effort |
| A8 | $1.6 \times 10^{-1}$ | Everyday task, no checking |
| A9 | $3.5 \times 10^{-1}$ | Completely familiar, no real checking, redundant cues |

### Table 2.3 — HEART Error Producing Conditions (EPC) — Selected High-Yield Examples

| EPC | Description | Max. Multiplier (HEART) |
|-----|-------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Unfamiliarity with situation requiring first-time response | 17 |
| 2 | Shortage of time for error detection & correction | 11 |
| 3 | Low signal-to-noise ratio | 10 |
| 4 | Means of displaying information to user | 8 |
| 5 | Information overload / out-of-cycle information | 6 |
| 6 | Little or no independent checking / cross-checking | 5 |
| 7 | Poor feedback on task progress | 4 |
| 8 | Age-related limitations on performance | 3 |
| 9 | Distraction / interruption | 2.5 |
| 10 | Stress (high workload, fatigue) | 1.6 |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** HEART is the **fastest** quantitative HRA method — it does not require detailed event trees like THERP. It is favored in **oil & gas, offshore, and rail** industries for time-constrained SIL assessments.

## 2.4 Comparative Analysis — Why Different Techniques Exist

| Dimension | First-Generation (THERP, HEART) | Second-Generation (ATHEANA, CREAM, TRIPOD Beta) |
|-----------|--------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------|
| Context awareness | Low | High |
| Cognitive modeling | Absent | Explicit |
| Quantification | Numerical HEP | Probabilistic bands / qualitative |
| Application | Early 1980s | Late 1990s–2000s |
| Strength | Mature, regulatory-accepted | Realistic PSF integration |
| Limitation | Assumes "rational operator" | More expert-judgment demanding |
| KTU exam weight | High | Rising (CO2) |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Human error identification is **predominantly qualitative**; however, several core quantitative derivations are essential for KTU 14-mark calculation sub-questions. This section demonstrates the **exhaustive step-by-step mathematical development** of HEART, THERP, and SLIM-MAUD, with no skipped transitions.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — HEART Generalized Human Error Probability Equation

The HEART method expresses the **Human Error Probability (HEP)** as a product of a generic task unreliability and a series of **Error-Producing Condition (EPC)** multipliers, modulated by an **Activity Profile (AP)** and a **Workplace (W)** factor.

### Step 1: Express the Generic Form

The basic HEART proposition is that a generic task has a baseline unreliability $G$ (a fixed probability from the standard HEART table), and each adverse condition inflates this baseline by an EPC multiplier $E_i$.

$$
\text{HEP}_{\text{base}} = G \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} E_i
$$

**Logic:** If no EPCs are present, $\text{HEP} = G$. For each adverse condition, multiply by $E_i \geq 1$. The HEP can never decrease below $G$.

### Step 2: Introduce the Activity Profile Coefficient (AP)

In HEART, the **Activity Profile** accounts for the proportion of the work-time spent in the actual task versus related subsidiary activities (e.g., logging, calibration, communication). The HEP is reduced by the AP factor because the operator is not continuously executing the critical task.

$$
\text{HEP}_{\text{AP-adjusted}} = G \times AP \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} E_i
$$

**Constraint:** $0 \leq AP \leq 1$. If $AP = 1$, the task is performed continuously; if $AP < 1$, the task is intermittent, reducing the integrated HEP.

### Step 3: Introduce the Workplace Multiplier (W)

The **Workplace multiplier** (typical value = **1.0** if no unusual conditions; can be increased up to **~3.0** for harsh environments like arctic offshore platforms) further inflates the HEP to account for aggregate, non-EPC-specific stressors.

$$
\boxed{\text{HEP} = G \times AP \times W \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} E_i}
$$

**Final Units:** HEP is **dimensionless** (probability, $0 \leq \text{HEP} \leq 1$).

### Step 4: Worked Example (KTU-Standard)

**Problem:** An operator must reset a circuit breaker after a fault. The task is "fairly simple with little dependent variable" (HEART A4, $G = 6.7 \times 10^{-4}$). Two EPCs apply:
- EPC1 (Information overload): $E_1 = 6$
- EPC2 (Time shortage): $E_2 = 11$

Activity profile $AP = 0.5$, Workplace $W = 1.5$. Compute HEP.

**Step-by-step evaluation:**

$$
\text{HEP} = (6.7 \times 10^{-4}) \times 0.5 \times 1.5 \times 6 \times 11
$$

$$
= 6.7 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.5 \times 1.5 \times 66
$$

$$
= 6.7 \times 10^{-4} \times 49.5
$$

$$
= 331.65 \times 10^{-4} = 3.3165 \times 10^{-2}
$$

**Result:** $\text{HEP} \approx 0.0332$ (about **3.3%** chance of failure on demand).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A HEP greater than $\sim 0.1$ signals that the design is unsafe. The HEART method **never caps** the HEP — it can exceed 1, which is interpreted as "virtually certain to fail."

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — THERP Combined Probability for Independent Tasks

### Step 1: Define the Independence Assumption

For **independent** sub-tasks, the probability of *success* on a sequence of $n$ sub-tasks is the product of the individual success probabilities. Therefore, the probability of *failure* on at least one sub-task is the complement.

### Step 2: Express Success Probability

Let $\text{HEP}_i$ be the failure probability of the $i$-th sub-task. The success probability of the $i$-th sub-task is $(1 - \text{HEP}_i)$.

The joint success probability for $n$ independent sub-tasks is:

$$
P(\text{success}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - \text{HEP}_i)
$$

### Step 3: Express Joint Failure Probability (Combined HEP)

$$
\boxed{\text{HEP}_{\text{total}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - \text{HEP}_i)}
$$

**Numerical example:** If two independent tasks have HEPs of $0.01$ and $0.05$:

$$
\text{HEP}_{\text{total}} = 1 - (1 - 0.01)(1 - 0.05) = 1 - (0.99 \times 0.95) = 1 - 0.9405 = 0.0595
$$

**Logical check:** Joint failure is slightly more than either individual HEP, confirming that the formula is consistent with engineering intuition.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — THERP Dependence Model (High Dependence)

### Step 1: Recognize the Conditional Probability Structure

When two tasks are **highly dependent**, the success of the second task is conditional on the success of the first. If the first task failed, the probability that the second task *succeeds* is reduced to a low value (typically $0.15$ for **HD**).

### Step 2: Define the Conditional HEP

Let $H_1 = \text{HEP}$ of first task; $H_2^* = \text{HEP}$ of second task given first task failed.

The probability that *both* tasks fail is:

$$
P(\text{both fail}) = H_1 + (1 - H_1) \cdot H_2^*
$$

The reasoning: either the first task failed (probability $H_1$) — leading to joint failure — *or* the first task succeeded (probability $1 - H_1$) **and** then the second task failed (probability $H_2^*$).

### Step 3: Closed-Form HEP under High Dependence

$$
\boxed{\text{HEP}_{\text{HD}} = H_1 + (1 - H_1) \cdot H_2^*}
$$

**Standard value:** $H_2^* \approx 0.5$ in THERP's tables for **HD** (meaning the second task succeeds only **50%** of the time if the first failed). Plug $H_1 = 0.01$:

$$
\text{HEP}_{\text{HD}} = 0.01 + (1 - 0.01) \times 0.5 = 0.01 + 0.495 = 0.505
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Dependency inflates HEP dramatically** — under High Dependence, the joint HEP can exceed 50% even when individual HEPs are tiny. This is why THERP always checks for **operator-action dependency**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — SLIM-MAUD Log-Linear Mapping

### Step 1: Compute the Success Likelihood Index (SLI)

The SLI is a **linear weighted sum** of $n$ Performance Shaping Factors (PSFs), each rated by an expert panel:

$$
\text{SLI} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i r_i
$$

where $w_i$ = weight of PSF $i$ (sum of all weights = 1); $r_i$ = expert rating on a 0–100 scale.

### Step 2: Map SLI to Log(Error Probability)

Empirically, error probability follows a **log-normal** distribution with respect to SLI. The log-linear relationship is:

$$
\log_{10}(\text{HEP}) = a \cdot \text{SLI} + b
$$

where $a$ (negative slope) and $b$ (intercept) are calibration constants derived from incident databases.

### Step 3: Solve for HEP

$$
\boxed{\text{HEP} = 10^{a \cdot \text{SLI} + b}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The calibration constants $a$ and $b$ are typically anchored at two extreme points: (1) **worst-case** operating conditions, where HEP is very high (e.g., $10^{-1}$); and (2) **best-case** conditions, where HEP is very low (e.g., $10^{-5}$).

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — CREAM's Cognitive Performance Function (CPF)

### Step 1: Score the 9 Common Performance Conditions (CPCs)

Each CPC is assigned a discrete level: **Improved (+), Not Significant (0), Reduced (–)**.

### Step 2: Aggregate CPC Effects

The **expected effect** on cognitive reliability is computed as:

$$
\Delta = \sum_{i=1}^{9} \text{CPC}_i^{\text{score}}
$$

where each score is +1, 0, or –1.

### Step 3: Map Aggregate to Control Mode

A lookup table maps $\Delta$ to a **Control Mode** ($C_1$ scrambled → $C_4$ strategic):

| $\Delta$ Range | Control Mode | HEP Band |
|----------------|--------------|----------|
| $\Delta \leq -4$ | Scrambled | $10^{-1} \leq \text{HEP} \leq 1$ |
| $-3 \leq \Delta \leq -1$ | Opportunistic | $10^{-2} \leq \text{HEP} \leq 10^{-1}$ |
| $0 \leq \Delta \leq 2$ | Tactical | $10^{-3} \leq \text{HEP} \leq 10^{-2}$ |
| $\Delta \geq 3$ | Strategic | $10^{-5} \leq \text{HEP} \leq 10^{-3}$ |

### Step 4: Final HEP

$$
\boxed{\text{HEP}_{\text{CREAM}} \in [\text{HEP}_{\min}(\text{mode}), \text{HEP}_{\max}(\text{mode})]}
$$

**Example:** If 3 CPCs are "reduced" and 1 is "improved": $\Delta = -3 + 1 = -2$ → **Opportunistic** control mode → $\text{HEP} \in [0.01, 0.1]$.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Technique

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This decision matrix is **frequently asked in KTU Part B questions** as an "open-choice 14-marker." Memorize the trade-off columns.

| Selection Criterion | SHERPA | THERP | HEART | CREAM | TRIPOD | ATHEANA |
|---------------------|--------|-------|-------|-------|--------|---------|
| **Stage** | Design | Operations | Operations | Design/Op | Investigation | Investigation |
| **Output Type** | Qualitative | Quantitative | Quantitative | Mixed | Qualitative | Mixed |
| **Time Required** | Low | High | Medium | High | Medium | High |
| **Expertise Needed** | Low | High | Medium | High | High | Very High |
| **Suitable For** | Procedural tasks | Step-by-step procedures | Quick estimates | Context-rich tasks | Accident analysis | Accident analysis |
| **Handles Recovery?** | Yes | Yes (explicit) | No | Yes (basic) | Yes (TRIPOD Beta) | Yes |
| **Quantification Unit** | Risk score (1–25) | HEP ($0$–$1$) | HEP ($0$–$1$) | HEP band | BRF (0–7) | HEP (qualitative) |
| **Industry Adoption** | Process, nuclear, rail | Nuclear (NRC) | Oil & gas, rail | Aviation, medical | Aviation, military | Nuclear |
| **KTU Exam Priority** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ |

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Critical Incident Technique (CIT) and list its five phases. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, CO2, Remember]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Critical Incident Technique (CIT)**, developed by **John C. Flanagan in 1954**, is a retrospective, interview-based procedure used to collect direct behavioural observations of human actions that contribute to successful or unsuccessful outcomes in operational settings. It is widely used in aviation, healthcare, and process industries to identify latent human-factors vulnerabilities.

> The **five phases of Flanagan's CIT** are:
> 1. **Establishing the general frame of reference** — defining what constitutes a "critical incident."
> 2. **Deciding on the data collection method** — interviews, surveys, archival records.
> 3. **Collecting the data** — typically through structured open-ended interviews with frontline personnel.
> 4. **Analyzing the data** — categorizing the incidents by behavior type, activity, and causal factor.
> 5. **Interpreting the findings and reporting** — formulating hypotheses about safety vulnerabilities.

> [!NOTE]
> CIT focuses on **specific incidents** rather than general opinions, making it a high-fidelity qualitative tool. However, it is **memory-dependent** and may suffer from recall bias.

---

### Question 2: List any **three Error Producing Conditions (EPCs)** used in the HEART method with their maximum multipliers. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, CO2, Understand]`

**Model Answer:**

**Error Producing Conditions (EPCs)** in HEART are contextual factors that increase the baseline human unreliability of a generic task. Three key EPCs (from Williams, 1988) are:

| EPC | Description | Max. Multiplier |
|-----|-------------|------------------|
| **EPC1** | Unfamiliarity with a novel situation requiring a first-time response | **17** |
| **EPC2** | Shortage of available time for error detection and correction | **11** |
| **EPC3** | Low signal-to-noise ratio (poor display contrast, weak auditory cue) | **10** |

The HEART equation incorporates these as multiplicative factors:

$$
\text{HEP} = G \times AP \times W \times E_1 \times E_2 \times E_3 \times \ldots \times E_n
$$

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE pattern: each module offers an internal choice of two 14-mark questions. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are **independent alternatives** — examiners will offer both, and you answer one. Each carries 7 + 7 = 14 marks, mapped to **CO2** and Bloom's **Apply / Analyze** levels.

---

### 🔹 Question A — 14 Marks
**"With a neat schematic, describe the THERP methodology for human error rate prediction. Explain the dependence model used in THERP with a suitable example."** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, CO2, Apply/Analyze]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Describe the THERP methodology.

**Model Solution:**

**THERP (Technique for Human Error Rate Prediction)** was developed by **A.D. Swain and H.E. Guttmann** at **Sandia National Laboratories** in 1983, published as NUREG/CR-1278 by the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission. It is a **prospective, quantitative HRA technique** that estimates the **Human Error Probability (HEP)** for each operator action in a procedural sequence.

> **Key Steps in THERP Methodology:**

> **Step 1 — Define the System and Operator Actions:**
> Identify the operator tasks to be analyzed (e.g., manually closing a valve after an alarm). This is done by reviewing operating procedures, control room layouts, and emergency operating instructions.

> **Step 2 — Task Decomposition:**
> Each operator action is subdivided into **sub-tasks** at the lowest level of detail. For example, "manually close valve V-101" may be decomposed into:
> (i) Recognize the alarm,
> (ii) Diagnose the fault,
> (iii) Locate the valve,
> (iv) Operate the valve (turn handwheel 5 rotations clockwise),
> (v) Verify closure on indicator.

> **Step 3 — Assign Nominal HEPs:**
> For each sub-task, look up the **nominal HEP** from THERP's tables (Table 20-1 of NUREG/CR-1278). For example, the HEP for "step on a switch" is $5 \times 10^{-3}$; for "read a digital display", it is $1 \times 10^{-3}$.

> **Step 4 — Check for Dependency Between Sub-Tasks:**
> Apply the **Dependence Model (Z, LD, MD, HD)** to determine if the success of one sub-task influences the next. This is the most critical step — see Part (b).

> **Step 5 — Build an HRA Event Tree:**
> Represent the operator action as a binary tree: success or failure of each sub-task. Branches are labelled with HEPs.

> **Step 6 — Compute Combined HEP:**
> For independent tasks, use $\text{HEP}_{\text{total}} = 1 - \prod (1 - \text{HEP}_i)$. For dependent tasks, apply the THERP dependence model.

> **Step 7 — Recovery Analysis:**
> Compute the probability that a *failed* operator action is detected and corrected (either by another operator, an automatic system, or procedure revision). Recovery HEPs are multiplicative.

> **Step 8 — Integrate into System-Level PSA:**
> The combined HEP is fed into a **Probabilistic Safety Assessment (PSA)** event tree, contributing to the **Core Damage Frequency (CDF)** and **Large Early Release Frequency (LERF)** of the plant.

> **Key Strengths of THERP:**
> - Mature, regulatory-accepted (NRC, IAEA).
> - Provides numerical HEPs suitable for PSA Level-1/2.
> - Explicit handling of dependencies and recovery.

> **Key Limitations:**
> - Data-intensive and time-consuming.
> - Treats human as a "component" — limited cognitive modeling.
> - Assumes rational, well-trained operator (low context awareness).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Explain the Dependence Model with a numerical example.

**Model Solution:**

In THERP, **dependency** refers to the statistical dependence between successive operator actions. Even if two sub-tasks are procedurally separate, the *psychological* state of the operator (stress, confusion after a failure) influences the next action. THERP classifies dependency into four levels:

> **1. Zero Dependence (Z):**
> The two sub-tasks are completely independent. The success/failure of the first has no influence on the second. The combined HEP is:

$$
\text{HEP}_{\text{total}} = 1 - (1 - H_1)(1 - H_2)
$$

> **2. Low Dependence (LD):**
> Slight influence; conditional success probability of task 2, given task 1 failed, is typically **0.5**. The combined HEP becomes:

$$
\text{HEP}_{\text{LD}} = H_1 + (1 - H_1) \cdot H_2^{\text{LD}}
$$

where $H_2^{\text{LD}} \approx 0.5$.

> **3. Moderate Dependence (MD):**
> Conditional success probability = 0.2–0.5 range; the THERP table provides specific values.

> **4. High Dependence (HD):**
> Strong coupling; conditional success probability drops to **0.15**. The combined HEP is:

$$
\text{HEP}_{\text{HD}} = H_1 + (1 - H_1) \cdot H_2^{\text{HD}}
$$

where $H_2^{\text{HD}} \approx 0.5$ (meaning the second task succeeds only 50% of the time even if the first succeeded — the failure of the first is psychologically devastating).

> **Numerical Example (HD Case):**
> A reactor operator must (1) acknowledge an alarm (HEP = $H_1 = 0.01$) and (2) manually trip a pump (HEP = $H_2 = 0.001$ in isolation). Under **High Dependence**, the failure of the alarm acknowledgement degrades the operator's cognitive state drastically.

> **Step-by-Step Computation:**

> **Step 1:** Compute the probability that task 1 fails:

$$
P(\text{task 1 fails}) = H_1 = 0.01
$$

> **Step 2:** Apply the THERP dependence value $H_2^{\text{HD}} = 0.5$:

$$
H_2^{\text{HD}} = 0.5
$$

> **Step 3:** Combine using the HD formula:

$$
\text{HEP}_{\text{HD}} = 0.01 + (1 - 0.01) \times 0.5 = 0.01 + 0.495 = 0.505
$$

> **Result:** Under **High Dependence**, the joint HEP balloons from a near-zero $0.011$ (independent case) to **0.505** — a **50% chance of failure**. This dramatic inflation justifies why THERP's dependence check is a *mandatory* step.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Many students **forget the dependence check** and simply multiply or add HEPs naively. This is a guaranteed 2–3 mark loss. Also, do **not** use the HD formula for Z-dependence tasks — always check the dependency level first.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake 2:** Conflating SHERPA's 8 task types with HEART's 9 generic task types. They are different schemes — SHERPA = P/R/F/A/C/I/Fe/S; HEART = A1–A9. Mixing them up loses marks in comparison questions.

---

### 🔹 Question B — 14 Marks
**"Compare the SHERPA and HEART techniques for human error identification with respect to origin, methodology, output type, and application. Illustrate with a worked HEART example."** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, CO2, Analyze]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Compare SHERPA and HEART.

**Model Solution:**

> **Comparison Table — SHERPA vs HEART:**

| Dimension | SHERPA | HEART |
|-----------|--------|-------|
| **Origin & Year** | David Embrey, UK, **1986** | J.C. Williams, UK, **1988** |
| **Type** | Prospective, qualitative | Prospective, quantitative |
| **Primary Output** | Error catalog + risk score | Human Error Probability (HEP) |
| **Input** | Hierarchical Task Analysis (HTA) | Generic task + EPCs |
| **Procedure** | 8-step systematic process | 5-step EPC multiplication |
| **Error Classification** | 8 error modes (A1–A8) | 9 generic task types (A1–A9) |
| **Strengths** | Easy to learn, minimal data, comprehensive error taxonomy | Quick numerical estimate, suitable for SIL |
| **Weaknesses** | Subjective, not suitable for PSA integration | EPC multipliers can be over-conservative |
| **Best Application** | Procedural task design in process industries | Early-design SIL verification in oil & gas |
| **Recovery Analysis** | Explicit step (Step 7) | Not built-in |
| **Cognitive Modeling** | Limited (psychological error mechanism codes) | None |
| **KTU Exam Relevance** | ★★★★★ (board favorite) | ★★★★ (calculation questions) |

> **Narrative Explanation:**

> **SHERPA** was developed as a **task-based, qualitative** technique to systematically predict human errors in procedural tasks. It uses the **HTA** as its foundation and classifies each task into one of 8 task types (Plan, Retrieve, Formulate, Action, Check, Information transfer, Feedback, Selection). For each task, errors are catalogued from Embrey's generic error modes (A1–A8), and a **psychological error mechanism** (C, D, O, T, I, F, S, A) explains *why* the error is likely. The output is a **risk matrix** combining consequence severity (1–5) and likelihood (1–5), prioritizing errors for mitigation.

> **HEART**, in contrast, is a **quantitative** technique. It does not decompose the task into sub-actions; instead, the entire task is mapped to a **generic task type (A1–A9)** with a fixed nominal unreliability $G$. The method then identifies the **Error Producing Conditions (EPCs)** — adverse contextual factors — and multiplies their EPC multipliers with $G$ to yield the final HEP. The result is a single **probability value** (e.g., HEP = 0.03) that can be directly fed into SIL calculations or PSAs.

> **Similarities:**
> - Both are **prospective** — applied during design or pre-operations.
> - Both are **task-based** — require task description as input.
> - Both are **widely adopted in process industries**.

> **Differences:**
> - SHERPA is **qualitative**; HEART is **quantitative**.
> - SHERPA's output is a **risk score**; HEART's output is a **HEP**.
> - SHERPA has **explicit recovery analysis**; HEART does not.
> - HEART is **faster**; SHERPA is **more detailed**.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Worked HEART Example.

**Problem Statement:**

An operator must perform a routine valve lineup changeover in a refinery distillation column. The task is "completely familiar, no real checking, redundant cues" — HEART **Generic Task A9**, with nominal unreliability $G = 3.5 \times 10^{-1}$. Two EPCs apply:

- **EPC 9 (Distraction / interruption):** $E_1 = 2.5$
- **EPC 6 (Little or no independent checking):** $E_2 = 5.0$

The operator is engaged in this task only **40%** of the shift (so Activity Profile $AP = 0.4$). The workplace is a **standard indoor control room**, so $W = 1.0$. Compute the HEART HEP.

> **Step 1: Identify the Generic Task and Nominal G.**

$$
G = 3.5 \times 10^{-1} = 0.35
$$

> **Step 2: List the EPCs and their multipliers.**

$$
E_1 = 2.5, \quad E_2 = 5.0
$$

> **Step 3: Identify the Activity Profile and Workplace Multiplier.**

$$
AP = 0.4, \quad W = 1.0
$$

> **Step 4: Apply the HEART Equation.**

$$
\text{HEP} = G \times AP \times W \times \prod E_i = 0.35 \times 0.4 \times 1.0 \times 2.5 \times 5.0
$$

> **Step 5: Multiply Sequentially.**

$$
\text{HEP} = 0.35 \times 0.4 \times 1.0 \times 12.5
$$

$$
= 0.35 \times 0.4 \times 12.5 = 0.35 \times 5.0 = 1.75
$$

> **Step 6: Interpret the Result.**

Since the computed HEP exceeds **1.0**, this is a **physically meaningful signal**: the task as designed is **virtually certain to fail**. HEART does not cap HEP at 1.0; the excess above 1.0 is a **qualitative warning** that the design assumptions (low checking, distraction-prone environment, routine task) are incompatible with safe operation.

> **Step 7: Recommend Error Reduction Strategies.**

- **Reduce EPC impact:** Provide a checklist for independent verification (reduces $E_2$ from 5.0 to ~1.5).
- **Modify generic task:** Add a verification step (reclassify from A9 to A6, lowering $G$ from 0.35 to $3.5 \times 10^{-3}$).
- **Improve workplace:** Add alarm-suppression logic to reduce distractions (reduces $E_1$ to 1.2).

> **Revised HEP after mitigation:**

$$
\text{HEP}_{\text{new}} = 0.0035 \times 0.4 \times 1.0 \times 1.5 \times 1.2 = 0.00252
$$

> This represents a **1000-fold reduction** in HEP — a typical outcome of HEART-guided design optimization.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — HEART Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Activity Profile (AP)** — losing 2 marks. AP is *not optional*; it accounts for the fraction of time the operator actually performs the task.
> 2. **Capping HEP at 1.0** — wrong. HEART intentionally allows HEP > 1 to signal design untenability.
> 3. **Using HEART for novel unfamiliar tasks without adjusting for unfamiliarity EPC** — the EPC1 multiplier (17) is the largest single driver; ignoring it causes severe underestimation.
> 4. **Confusing HEART's $G$ with THERP's nominal HEP** — they are different values from different databases.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake in SHERPA Questions:**
> 1. **Mixing SHERPA's 8 task types (P/R/F/A/C/I/Fe/S) with HEART's 9 generic task types (A1–A9).** These are **different taxonomies** for different methods.
> 2. **Skipping Step 6 (Psychological Error Mechanism)** — this is where examiners look for the "why" behind the error. Omitting it loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting to score Consequence and Likelihood** in the risk matrix — Step 7.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Final 5-minute Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2 (Human Error Identification Techniques)**

- ✅ **Definition:** Human Error Identification Techniques are systematic prospective or retrospective methods to find, classify, and quantify human failures in socio-technical systems.
- ✅ **Three major families:**
  - **Prospective (before-event):** HTA, SHERPA, THERP, HEART, SLIM-MAUD
  - **Retrospective (after-event):** CIT (Flanagan), TRIPOD (Reason), HFACS (Shappell)
  - **Context-aware / Second Generation:** CREAM (Hollnagel), ATHEANA (NRC), MERMOS, HERA
- ✅ **SHERPA:** 8 task types (P, R, F, A, C, I, Fe, S); 8 generic error modes (A1–A8); 8 psychological mechanisms (C, D, O, T, I, F, S, A); output is **qualitative risk matrix**.
- ✅ **THERP:** NUREG/CR-1278, 1983, Swain & Guttmann; output is **numerical HEP**; uses **Dependence Model (Z, LD, MD, HD)**; HD inflates joint HEP dramatically.
- ✅ **HEART:** Williams 1988; equation $\text{HEP} = G \times AP \times W \times \prod E_i$; EPC1 (unfamiliarity) = 17×, EPC2 (time pressure) = 11×, EPC3 (low SNR) = 10×; output is **numerical HEP**.
- ✅ **CREAM:** Hollnagel 1998; **4 control modes** (Strategic, Tactical, Opportunistic, Scrambled); **9 CPCs**; HEP is a **band**, not a point value.
- ✅ **ATHEANA:** NRC method; explicitly models **Error-Producing Contexts (EPCs)** and **Unsafe Acts (UAs)**; emphasizes real operating context.
- ✅ **TRIPOD:** 11 General Failure Types (GFTs); covers **organizational, management, supervisory, preconditions, defences**; uses **Swiss Cheese model** of latent failures.
- ✅ **CIT (Flanagan 1954):** 5 phases — Frame of Reference, Data Collection, Collection, Analysis, Interpretation; qualitative interview-based.
- ✅ **HEART Equation (Most Important Formula):**

$$
\boxed{\text{HEP} = G \times AP \times W \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} E_i}
$$

- ✅ **THERP Independent Tasks (Most Important Formula):**

$$
\boxed{\text{HEP}_{\text{total}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - \text{HEP}_i)}
$$

- ✅ **THERP High Dependence (Most Important Formula):**

$$
\boxed{\text{HEP}_{\text{HD}} = H_1 + (1 - H_1) \cdot H_2^*}
$$

- ✅ **Key constant values for board exam:**
  - HEART Generic Task A4: $G = 6.7 \times 10^{-4}$
  - HEART Generic Task A5: $G = 1.3 \times 10^{-3}$
  - HEART EPC1 multiplier: **17**
  - THERP HD value: $H_2^* \approx 0.5$
- ✅ **Comparison to remember (board-favorite 14-marker):** SHERPA (qualitative, 8 task types) vs HEART (quantitative, 9 generic tasks) vs THERP (quantitative, dependence model) vs CREAM (context-aware, control modes).
- ✅ **Two-generation taxonomy:** First generation (1980s: THERP, HEART, SLIM) = low context; Second generation (1990s+: ATHEANA, CREAM, TRIPOD Beta) = high context.
- ✅ **KTU Course Outcome mapping:** CO2 (Apply human error identification techniques) — direct coverage.
- ✅ **Bloom's levels tested:** Remember (definitions), Understand (EPCs, SHERPA task types), Apply (HEART calculation), Analyze (compare techniques).

> 🎯 **Final KTU Tip:** In a 14-mark comparison question, **always draw a comparison TABLE** (with 6–8 rows) — it visually structures your answer, satisfies the "neat schematic" instruction, and earns you 2–3 extra marks. Then follow with a 3–4 paragraph narrative expansion. This is the **highest-scoring answer pattern** in KTU 2024 ESE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Error-Producing Conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT524 - HUMAN AND ORGANIZATIONAL FACTORS IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Error-Producing Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **Error-Producing Conditions (EPCs)** are specific characteristics of the **task, environment, or operator** that, when present, significantly *increase the probability* of an operator committing a human error during the performance of a system task. They are the latent, situational, and psychological/physiological "shapers" of human unreliability.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, EPCs are formally categorized as **Error-Promoting Factors (EPFs)** in the Swain & Guttmann THERP (Technique for Human Error Rate Prediction) Handbook, and are mathematically treated as **multipliers** applied to a **Basic Human Error Probability (BHEP)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE] 
> **Analogy — The Rainy Highway:** Imagine driving on a familiar highway. On a clear, dry afternoon, you almost never skid. But introduce Error-Producing Conditions — *heavy rain (environmental), rush-hour traffic (workload), a crying child in the back seat (distraction), and a flat tire you ignored yesterday (poor maintenance)* — and the probability of an error (a skid or a crash) skyrockets. The car (the human operator) hasn't changed; the *conditions* around the car have. EPCs are the "rain on the highway" of human performance.

### 1.3 Core Distinction

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Human Error** | The act itself (e.g., pressing the wrong button) |
| **Error-Producing Condition (EPC)** | The *circumstance* that made the error more likely (e.g., button labels are faded) |
| **Performance Shaping Factor (PSF)** | A broader category covering *all* influences (training, procedure, EPC) |
| **Latent Failure** | Organizational/systemic conditions (Reason's model) that allow EPCs to persist |

### 1.4 Significance in Safety Engineering

- Reducing EPCs is a **proactive, upstream** safety intervention — cheaper and more effective than reactive error correction.
- EPC quantification allows **probabilistic safety assessment (PSA)** and human reliability analysis (HRA) in industries like nuclear, aviation, petrochemical, and process control.
- The standard reference metric is the **Basic Human Error Probability (BHEP)**, typically ranging from **1×10⁻⁴ (simple task)** to **1×10⁻¹ (complex, cognitively demanding task)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->
