<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Error-Producing Conditions

EPCs are classified into **four primary categories** based on their origin:

**(A) Task-Related EPCs**
- Unfamiliarity with the task or situation
- Time shortage / time pressure
- High task complexity
- Monotonous or repetitive tasks
- Simultaneous multiple goals (divided attention)
- Inadequate or ambiguous procedures/instructions

**(B) Environmental EPCs**
- Poor signal-to-noise ratio
- Disruption of normal work–sleep cycle (shift work)
- Extreme temperature, noise, vibration, illumination
- Confusing or poorly designed displays and controls (poor HMI design)
- Inadequate workspace layout

**(C) Operator-Related (Psychological/Physiological) EPCs**
- Stress, anxiety, emotional upset
- Fatigue (physical and mental)
- Boredom / monotony
- Memory load / cognitive overload
- Poor physical or mental health
- Substance influence (alcohol, drugs, medication)

**(D) Organizational/Systemic EPCs**
- Inadequate training and supervision
- Poor feedback systems
- Inadequate communication
- Cultural / managerial pressure to bypass safety
- Lack of standardization

### 2.2 The Logic: Why EPCs Matter

1. **Human reliability is not constant** — it is conditional on context.
2. A *basic* error rate, derived from generic task data, must be **adjusted** for the specific conditions.
3. The **adjusted** human error probability determines the actual risk contribution of human actions in a fault tree or event tree.
4. Mitigation strategies **must target the EPC**, not the operator (posters saying "be more careful" are useless).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE] 
> The following equation and table are **core KTU high-yield content** for numerical and theory questions on EPCs.

**Master Equation (THERP / Swain's EPC Model):**

$$
\text{HEP}_{\text{adjusted}} \;=\; \text{BHEP} \;\times\; \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{EPF}_i
$$

Where:
- $\text{HEP}_{\text{adjusted}}$ = Human Error Probability under the given set of conditions
- $\text{BHEP}$ = Basic Human Error Probability (the baseline rate for the task in a reference condition)
- $\text{EPF}_i$ = Error-Producing Factor multiplier for condition *i*
- $n$ = total number of applicable EPCs
- The operator $\prod$ denotes the product of all applicable EPC multipliers

**Swain's Standard EPC Multipliers (Examination-Relevant):**

| # | Error-Producing Condition | Multiplier Range | Typical Value |
|:---:|---|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | Unfamiliarity with task/situation | 1.2 – 10.0 | 3.0 |
| 2 | Time shortage / time pressure | 1.1 – 11.0 | 3.0 |
| 3 | Poor signal-to-noise ratio | 1.2 – 10.0 | 3.0 |
| 4 | Misperception of risk | 1.1 – 4.0 | 2.0 |
| 5 | Poor feedback / unclear information | 1.2 – 4.0 | 2.0 |
| 6 | Inadequate / ambiguous instructions | 1.1 – 10.0 | 3.0 |
| 7 | Memory limitation / cognitive overload | 1.1 – 5.0 | 2.0 |
| 8 | Stress (high) | 1.1 – 9.0 | 2.0 |
| 9 | Fatigue (high) | 1.1 – 8.0 | 2.0 |
| 10 | Task complexity (very high) | 1.1 – 5.0 | 2.0 |
| 11 | High mental workload | 1.1 – 6.0 | 2.5 |
| 12 | Monotony / boredom | 1.1 – 4.0 | 2.0 |
| 13 | Poor health | 1.1 – 5.0 | 2.0 |
| 14 | Alcohol / drug influence | 1.1 – 13.0 | 5.0 |
| 15 | Distraction | 1.1 – 9.0 | 2.5 |

**Supporting Equations:**

$$
\text{Risk Increase Factor (RIF)} \;=\; \frac{\text{HEP}_{\text{adjusted}}}{\text{BHEP}} \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{EPF}_i
$$

$$
\text{Relative Risk Reduction} \;=\; 1 - \frac{1}{\text{RIF}}
$$

**Real-World Utility:** This framework is used in **Probabilistic Safety Assessment (PSA)** of nuclear power plants, **Human Reliability Analysis (HRA)** in aviation, and **Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)** in chemical process safety to quantify how working conditions shift the probability of operator failure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Adjusting HEP for Multiple EPCs

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **Problem Statement (KTU-Style Numerical):** An operator in a process control room must perform a manual valve-closure task following a high-temperature alarm. The **Basic Human Error Probability (BHEP)** for this task is **2 × 10⁻³** under reference (ideal) conditions. During the actual incident, the following **five Error-Producing Conditions** are identified: **(i) high time pressure (EPF = 3.0), (ii) stress (EPF = 2.0), (iii) high mental workload (EPF = 2.5), (iv) poor feedback from the alarm (EPF = 2.0), and (v) unfamiliarity with the upgraded HMI panel (EPF = 3.0)**. Calculate the adjusted Human Error Probability (HEP) and the Risk Increase Factor (RIF).

---

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation.**

The Swain THERP equation for human error probability under multiple EPCs is:

$$
\text{HEP}_{\text{adjusted}} \;=\; \text{BHEP} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{EPF}_i
$$

This is the canonical form used in the THERP handbook and the KTU syllabus.

---

**Step 2 — Extract the given values.**

$$
\text{BHEP} \;=\; 2 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\text{EPF}_1 \;=\; 3.0 \quad \text{(time pressure)}
$$

$$
\text{EPF}_2 \;=\; 2.0 \quad \text{(stress)}
$$

$$
\text{EPF}_3 \;=\; 2.5 \quad \text{(mental workload)}
$$

$$
\text{EPF}_4 \;=\; 2.0 \quad \text{(poor feedback)}
$$

$$
\text{EPF}_5 \;=\; 3.0 \quad \text{(unfamiliarity)}
$$

Number of EPCs: $n = 5$.

---

**Step 3 — Compute the product of EPC multipliers.**

We compute the product step-by-step to avoid arithmetic errors:

$$
\prod_{i=1}^{5} \text{EPF}_i \;=\; 3.0 \times 2.0 \times 2.5 \times 2.0 \times 3.0
$$

$$
= \;(3.0 \times 3.0) \times (2.0 \times 2.0) \times 2.5
$$

$$
= \;9.0 \times 4.0 \times 2.5
$$

$$
= \;36.0 \times 2.5
$$

$$
= \;90.0
$$

So the cumulative multiplier is **90.0**.

---

**Step 4 — Compute the adjusted HEP.**

Substitute BHEP and the product back into the master equation:

$$
\text{HEP}_{\text{adjusted}} \;=\; (2 \times 10^{-3}) \times 90.0
$$

$$
\text{HEP}_{\text{adjusted}} \;=\; 180 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\text{HEP}_{\text{adjusted}} \;=\; 1.8 \times 10^{-1}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{HEP}_{\text{adjusted}} \;=\; 0.18 \;\;(\text{i.e., an 18\% error probability})}
$$

This means the operator now has an 18% chance of failing the task — a *catastrophically high* value, justifying an immediate HMI redesign and operator retraining.

---

**Step 5 — Compute the Risk Increase Factor (RIF).**

$$
\text{RIF} \;=\; \frac{\text{HEP}_{\text{adjusted}}}{\text{BHEP}} \;=\; \frac{1.8 \times 10^{-1}}{2 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
\text{RIF} \;=\; 90
$$

The risk of error is **90 times** the baseline.

---

**Step 6 — Compute the Relative Risk Reduction achievable by removing ALL EPCs.**

If all five EPCs are eliminated (returning to reference conditions):

$$
\text{Relative Risk Reduction} \;=\; 1 - \frac{1}{\text{RIF}} \;=\; 1 - \frac{1}{90}
$$

$$
= \;1 - 0.01111
$$

$$
= \;0.9889 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 98.89\%
$$

Eliminating these five EPCs reduces error probability by ~98.9%.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Each EPC

| EPC Removed | New Product | New HEP | Reduction vs. 0.18 |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| None (all 5 present) | 90.0 | 0.180 | 0% |
| Remove time pressure | 30.0 | 0.060 | 66.7% |
| Remove stress | 45.0 | 0.090 | 50.0% |
| Remove mental workload | 36.0 | 0.072 | 60.0% |
| Remove poor feedback | 45.0 | 0.090 | 50.0% |
| Remove unfamiliarity | 30.0 | 0.060 | 66.7% |

> [!NOTE] 
> **Engineering Insight:** The largest single-point gains come from removing **time pressure** and **unfamiliarity** (each giving ~66.7% reduction). A safety manager should therefore prioritize (a) better scheduling, and (b) more frequent refresher training.

---

### 3.3 Decision Rule for EPC Multipliers

$$
\text{If } \text{EPF}_i > 10.0 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Task must be redesigned or automated.}
$$

This is a standard THERP threshold: any single multiplier above 10 indicates the human should not be the primary executor.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of EPCs

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA["ERROR PRODUCING CONDITIONS"] --> nodeB["TASK RELATED EPCs"]
    rootA --> nodeC["ENVIRONMENTAL EPCs"]
    rootA --> nodeD["OPERATOR EPCs"]
    rootA --> nodeE["ORGANIZATIONAL EPCs"]
    nodeB --> nodeB1["Unfamiliarity"]
    nodeB --> nodeB2["Time Pressure"]
    nodeB --> nodeB3["Task Complexity"]
    nodeB --> nodeB4["Monotony"]
    nodeB --> nodeB5["Divided Attention"]
    nodeC --> nodeC1["Poor Signal to Noise"]
    nodeC --> nodeC2["Extreme Temperature"]
    nodeC --> nodeC3["Poor HMI Design"]
    nodeC --> nodeC4["Shift Disruption"]
    nodeD --> nodeD1["Stress"]
    nodeD --> nodeD2["Fatigue"]
    nodeD --> nodeD3["Cognitive Overload"]
    nodeD --> nodeD4["Poor Health"]
    nodeD --> nodeD5["Substance Influence"]
    nodeE --> nodeE1["Inadequate Training"]
    nodeE --> nodeE2["Poor Feedback"]
    nodeE --> nodeE3["Bad Communication"]
    nodeE --> nodeE4["Safety Culture Failure"]
```

### 4.2 Causal Chain: From EPC to Accident

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["Latent Organisational Failures"] --> nodeB["ERROR PRODUCING CONDITIONS"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["HUMAN ERROR"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["INITIATING EVENT"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["SYSTEM FAILURE"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["ACCIDENT OR LOSS"]
    nodeA -.- nodeG["Training Policy"]
    nodeA -.- nodeH["Design Standards"]
    nodeA -.- nodeI["Maintenance Culture"]
    nodeB -.- nodeJ["Stress Fatigue Noise"]
    nodeB -.- nodeK["Time Pressure Complexity"]
    nodeC -.- nodeL["Wrong Action Omission"]
```

### 4.3 EPC Multiplier Application Flow (THERP Method)

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA["Start: Identify Task"] --> stepB["Obtain BHEP from THERP Table"]
    stepB --> stepC["List All Applicable EPCs"]
    stepC --> stepD["Assign EPF Value to Each EPC"]
    stepD --> stepE["Multiply All EPFs Together"]
    stepE --> stepF["Multiply BHEP by EPF Product"]
    stepF --> stepG{"Any Single EPF greater than 10"}
    stepG -->|"Yes"| stepH["RECOMMEND REDESIGN OR AUTOMATION"]
    stepG -->|"No"| stepI["Use Adjusted HEP in PSA or LOPA"]
    stepI --> endJ["End: Quantified Human Error Probability"]
    stepH --> endJ
```

### 4.4 Reason Swiss Cheese Model in EPC Context

```mermaid
graph TD
    layerA["Organisational Influences"] -->|"Latent Failures"| layerB["Unsafe Supervision and Preconditions"]
    layerB -->|"EPCs Emerge"| layerC["Preconditions for Unsafe Acts"]
    layerC -->|"Errors Occur"| layerD["Unsafe Acts"]
    layerD -->|"Holes Align"| outcomeE["ACCIDENT"]
    layerA -.- noteA["Policy Resource Allocation"]
    layerB -.- noteB["Training Fatigue Schedule"]
    layerC -.- noteC["Stress Noise Time Pressure"]
    layerD -.- noteD["Slips Lapses Mistakes Violations"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **Block-Level Functional Insight:** In the Swiss Cheese model, EPCs are exactly the *holes* in the preconditions layer. They form because the layers above (organisational and supervisory) failed. The accident occurs only when the holes in *all* layers momentarily align.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
**Define Error-Producing Conditions (EPCs). List any four common categories of EPCs with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Error-Producing Conditions (EPCs) are characteristics of the task, environment, or operator state that *increase the probability* of a human error during task performance (1 mark). They are mathematically treated as multipliers (Error-Promoting Factors) in the Swain THERP model (1 mark). Four categories with examples (1 mark total):
- **Task-related:** Time pressure during an emergency shutdown
- **Environmental:** Poor signal-to-noise ratio in a noisy plant
- **Operator-related:** Operator fatigue due to 12-hour shift
- **Organizational:** Inadequate training on a new system

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest]**
**What is the Basic Human Error Probability (BHEP)? Why is the adjusted HEP always greater than or equal to the BHEP?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Basic Human Error Probability (BHEP) is the *generic, baseline* probability of an operator committing an error on a given task under **reference (ideal) conditions**, as tabulated in the THERP Handbook (1 mark). The adjusted HEP is obtained by multiplying the BHEP by the product of all applicable Error-Promoting Factor (EPF) values, each of which is by definition ≥ 1.0 (1 mark). Since all EPFᵢ ≥ 1.0, their product is ≥ 1.0, and therefore $\text{HEP}_{\text{adjusted}} \geq \text{BHEP}$ (1 mark).

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, End Semester, Model Paper]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the **classification of Error-Producing Conditions** into task, environmental, operator, and organizational categories. For each category, give **two examples with EPF multiplier values** from the Swain THERP table. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A complex chemical plant operator must execute an emergency isolation procedure. The **BHEP** for the procedure under reference conditions is **5 × 10⁻⁴**. During the actual emergency, the following four EPCs are present: high time pressure (EPF = 3.0), operator fatigue (EPF = 2.0), high mental workload (EPF = 2.5), and unfamiliarity with the upgraded control panel (EPF = 3.0). Calculate the **adjusted HEP**, the **Risk Increase Factor (RIF)**, and recommend whether the task should be redesigned. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification (7 marks):**

- **Task-related EPCs** are inherent to the activity itself. *Example 1:* Time pressure during an emergency response (EPF = 1.1 to 11.0, typical 3.0). *Example 2:* High task complexity in multi-step diagnostics (EPF = 1.1 to 5.0, typical 2.0). (2 marks)

- **Environmental EPCs** arise from the physical workspace. *Example 1:* Poor signal-to-noise ratio in a high-noise plant floor (EPF = 1.2 to 10.0, typical 3.0). *Example 2:* Extreme temperature affecting dexterity (EPF = 1.1 to 4.0, typical 2.0). (1.5 marks)

- **Operator-related EPCs** stem from the human's physical and mental state. *Example 1:* Fatigue from prolonged shift (EPF = 1.1 to 8.0, typical 2.0). *Example 2:* Stress due to high-stakes decision (EPF = 1.1 to 9.0, typical 2.0). (1.5 marks)

- **Organizational EPCs** emerge from management and system design. *Example 1:* Inadequate training on new HMI (EPF = 1.1 to 10.0, typical 3.0). *Example 2:* Poor feedback systems (EPF = 1.2 to 4.0, typical 2.0). (2 marks)

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Identify given values:

$$
\text{BHEP} = 5 \times 10^{-4}, \quad \text{EPF}_1=3.0, \quad \text{EPF}_2=2.0, \quad \text{EPF}_3=2.5, \quad \text{EPF}_4=3.0
$$

Step 2 — Compute EPC product:

$$
\prod \text{EPF}_i = 3.0 \times 2.0 \times 2.5 \times 3.0 = 45.0
$$

Step 3 — Adjusted HEP:

$$
\text{HEP}_{\text{adjusted}} = (5 \times 10^{-4}) \times 45.0 = 2.25 \times 10^{-2} = 0.0225
$$

Step 4 — Risk Increase Factor:

$$
\text{RIF} = \frac{2.25 \times 10^{-2}}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 45
$$

Step 5 — Redesign Decision:

Since all individual EPF values are ≤ 10.0, automatic redesign is *not yet mandatory* under THERP rules. However, with RIF = 45, the risk is 45× the baseline. **Recommendation:** Reduce EPCs by improving training (removes unfamiliarity, EPF 3.0) and providing better alarm clarity (reduces mental workload). After mitigation, recalculate. If still > 10, redesign the HMI or automate the isolation step. (2 marks)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, End Semester]**
**(a)** With the aid of a **neat labelled diagram**, explain the **Swiss Cheese Model** of accident causation. Show how EPCs fit into this model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **practical engineering strategies** an organization can adopt to **eliminate or reduce at least six different EPCs**. For each strategy, identify the EPC removed and the corresponding EPF reduction. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Swiss Cheese Model (7 marks):**

The Swiss Cheese Model, proposed by James Reason (1990), represents accident causation as a series of **defensive layers** (cheese slices), each containing *holes* (failures). An accident occurs only when holes in **all layers align momentarily** (3 marks). The four layers from outer to inner are:
1. **Organisational influences** (policy, resource allocation) — Latent failures
2. **Unsafe supervision / preconditions** (training, scheduling) — Creates preconditions for unsafe acts
3. **Preconditions for unsafe acts** — *This is exactly where EPCs manifest* (stress, fatigue, noise, time pressure)
4. **Unsafe acts** (errors and violations) — Immediate trigger

A labelled Mermaid or block diagram is mandatory (2 marks). EPCs are the *holes in the third layer*; they arise from latent failures in layers 1 and 2, and they enable the unsafe acts in layer 4. **Engineering implication:** Eliminate the holes — not by blaming the operator at layer 4, but by fixing training (layer 2) and policy (layer 1). (2 marks)

**(b) Practical Strategies (7 marks):**

| # | EPC | Strategy | Effect on EPF |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | Unfamiliarity | Conduct regular hands-on simulator training | EPF 3.0 → 1.0 |
| 2 | Time pressure | Standardize procedures with check-lists; pre-position tools | EPF 3.0 → 1.0 |
| 3 | Poor signal/noise | Use visual + auditory redundant alarms; soundproof control rooms | EPF 3.0 → 1.0 |
| 4 | Stress | Implement rotation, peer support, and counselling programs | EPF 2.0 → 1.0 |
| 5 | Fatigue | Enforce maximum shift lengths; mandatory rest periods | EPF 2.0 → 1.0 |
| 6 | Poor feedback | Install clear, real-time HMI feedback with confirmation tones | EPF 2.0 → 1.0 |

Each correctly linked strategy = 1 mark (6 marks). Concluding statement: by removing 6 EPCs with typical combined multiplier of 3×3×3×2×2×2 = 216, the BHEP of any task is reduced by 99.5% (1 mark).

---

### 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] 
> **Where students commonly lose marks in EPC questions:**
> 1. **Confusing EPCs with PSFs (Performance Shaping Factors):** EPCs are a *subset* of PSFs — specifically the negative, error-promoting ones. PSFs can be positive (e.g., good training reduces error rate).
> 2. **Forgetting that EPF multipliers are ≥ 1.0.** If you use a value below 1.0, you are claiming EPCs *reduce* error — physically impossible.
> 3. **Arithmetic slip in the product $\prod \text{EPF}_i$.** Always multiply *step-by-step*, not all at once. Carrying errors costs 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Omitting the decision rule:** A single EPF > 10 ⇒ redesign. Examiners specifically look for this.
> 5. **Ignoring units and orders of magnitude:** Mixing $10^{-3}$ with $10^{-5}$ gives a result 100× off. Always write the exponent explicitly.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions:** THERP assumes EPCs are *independent*; examiners love to deduct marks if you don't acknowledge this.

---

### 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Error-Producing Conditions**

- **Definition:** EPCs = conditions that *increase* the probability of human error. They are multipliers, not causes in isolation.
- **Master Equation:** $\text{HEP}_{\text{adjusted}} = \text{BHEP} \times \prod \text{EPF}_i$
- **BHEP Range:** typically $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-1}$, depending on task complexity.
- **EPF Constraint:** Every EPF ≥ 1.0; the *product* of EPFs is the Risk Increase Factor.
- **Single-EPF Threshold:** EPF > 10 ⇒ task must be redesigned or automated.
- **Four Categories:** Task, Environmental, Operator, Organizational — *memorize 2 examples per category*.
- **Swiss Cheese Model:** EPCs = holes in the *preconditions* layer; caused by latent organizational failures.
- **Most Common EPCs in Industry (in order):** Time pressure, poor HMI, fatigue, inadequate training, stress, poor signal-to-noise.
- **Most Effective Interventions:** Training, procedure redesign, HMI improvement, fatigue management, alarm rationalization.
- **Common Mistake:** Treating EPCs as "blame the operator" issues. EPCs are *systemic* and must be *engineered out*.
- **Key Names to Know (No Author Invention):** James Reason (Swiss Cheese Model), A.D. Swain (THERP Handbook, 1983), H.G. Heinrich (Domino Theory precursor).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Human Error in System Design and Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT524 - HUMAN AND ORGANIZATIONAL FACTORS IN SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Human Error in System Design and Operations # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Human Error** can be defined as a generic term that encompasses all those occasions in which a planned sequence of mental or physical activities fails to achieve its intended outcome, and when these failures cannot be attributed to the intervention of some chance agency (Reason, 1990). In the context of **system design and operations**, human error refers to the systematic deviation of human action from the intended design specifications, operational procedures, or expected performance standards, leading to unintended outcomes that may compromise safety, efficiency, or productivity. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Board Standard):** > Human error is any action or inaction of a person in a system that: > (i) Deviates from the prescribed or expected performance > (ii) Leads to, or has the potential to lead to, an adverse effect on system safety > (iii) Is traceable to either latent system deficiencies or active operator failures ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a perfectly designed highway system. The road, the signage, the lane markings, the traffic signals, the divider, the streetlights — every component has been engineered. Now, **a driver** uses this system every day. Human error occurs when: - The driver **misreads** a sign (mistake in interpretation) - The driver **presses the accelerator instead of the brake** under panic (slip of action) - The driver **forgets** to check the rear-view mirror (lapse of memory) - The driver knowingly **ignores** the speed limit (violation) - The signage is **too small to read** at 100 km/h — the system "designed" an error trap > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Takeaway:** Human error is rarely *just* the operator's fault. The designer of the road, the placement of the sign, the lighting, the vehicle ergonomics — *all* contribute. In safety engineering, we treat human error as a **symptom** of deeper system flaws, not as the *root cause* itself. ## 1.3 Key Metrics and Standard Indices - **Human Error Rate (HER):** Typically measured as errors per million operations, with a baseline human error probability of **1 × 10⁻³** per operation (one error in every 1000 operations) in routine tasks under normal conditions. - **THERP (Technique for Human Error Rate Prediction):** A widely used probabilistic method, established by **A.D. Swain & H.E. Guttmann (1983)**, to quantify human reliability. - **HEART (Human Error Assessment and Reduction Technique):** Proposed by **J.C. Williams (1988)** to predict error probabilities. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Three Levels of Human Failure (KTU High-Yield):** > 1. **Unsafe Acts** — Slips, lapses, mistakes, violations (the immediate trigger) > 2. **Preconditions for Unsafe Acts** — Fatigue, stress, poor communication, misperception > 3. **Latent Failures** — Design flaws, procedures, organizational culture (the *real* root cause) ## 1.4 The Human as a System Component In modern safety engineering, humans are no longer viewed as the *cause* of accidents but as **the most flexible, adaptable, and yet the most fallible component** within a socio-technical system. The **SHELL Model** (Software, Hardware, Environment, Liveware) — proposed by **Frank Hawkins (1984)** — treats the human operator as a central "Liveware" component interfacing with: - **Software** (procedures, manuals, training) - **Hardware** (machines, displays, controls) - **Environment** (noise, temperature, lighting) - **Other Liveware** (other people — crew, supervisors, controllers) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Human Errors Human errors are classified by **James Reason (1990)** into the following categories — this is *the most important* framework for KTU 2024 Scheme examinations. ### 2.1.1 Errors of Execution (Slips and Lapses) These occur when there is a *correct plan* but the execution fails. - **Slip:** An *observable* error in the execution of an action. The intention was correct, but the action didn't match. *Example:* A pilot intends to flip the right switch but flips the left one — both look identical. - **Lapse:** A *non-observable* error of memory or attention. *Example:* A technician forgets to close a valve after restarting a pump; the action is internal, the error is noticed only by the consequence. > [!NOTE] > **Memory Aid:** **Slip = Skill-based** (action); **Lapse = Memory-based** (forgetting). Both are *execution* failures where the *plan was correct*. ### 2.1.2 Errors of Planning (Mistakes) These occur when the plan itself is wrong, even if executed perfectly. - **Rule-based Mistake:** The wrong rule is selected for a given situation. *Example:* Applying a fire-fighting rule intended for electrical fires to an oil fire. - **Knowledge-based Mistake:** No pre-learned rule exists; the person improvises a wrong solution. *Example:* A junior engineer applying textbook (but contextually wrong) thermodynamic reasoning to a non-standard reactor condition. ### 2.1.3 Violations Deliberate deviations from prescribed procedures, rules, or standards. They are **not errors** in the cognitive sense — they are *choices*. - **Routine Violation:** Corner-cutting has become normalized. *Example:* Skipping checklist items because "we always do it this way." - **Optimising Violation:** To get the job done faster/rewarding. *Example:* Bypassing a safety interlock to meet a production target. - **Exceptional/Situational Violation:** Necessary under unique circumstances, but breaks protocol. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Examiner's Note:** > Slips and lapses are *internal to the operator's mind*; mistakes are *cognitive planning failures*; violations are *behavioural, intentional, and driven by organizational and cultural factors*. The same observable failure (e.g., a switch flipped wrong) can be a slip, a mistake, or a violation — the *intent* decides the category. ## 2.2 James Reason's Swiss Cheese Model The **Swiss Cheese Model** is the most influential accident causation model in modern safety engineering. - An organization is represented as **multiple slices of Swiss cheese**. - Each slice represents a **layer of defence** (engineering controls, administrative controls, supervision, training, management, regulation). - Each slice has **holes** — latent failures, active failures, or weaknesses. - An accident occurs when the **holes in all slices momentarily align**, allowing the hazard to reach the target (people, equipment, environment). - The **active failure** is the last slice (the operator); the **latent failures** are the deeper organizational and design layers. ### 2.2.1 Why this matters for System Design - System designers should **add more slices** (defence in depth). - They should **reduce the size of holes** by addressing root causes. - They should **prevent alignment** by introducing diversity and independence between layers. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Description | Typical Units / Values | |---|---|---|---| | Basic Human Error Probability | $P_{e,\text{base}}$ | Baseline error rate for routine tasks | $\approx 1 \times 10^{-3}$ per operation | | THERP Generic Error Rate | $P_{e,\text{THERP}}$ | Swain & Guttmann nominal error rate (most tasks) | $1 \times 10^{-3}$ to $1 \times 10^{-2}$ | | HEART Error Probability | $P_{HEART} = P_{b} \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{EPC}_{i} \cdot \text{APOC}$ | Nominal + Error Producing Conditions + Assessed Proportion of Occasions | Dimensionless (probability) | | HEART Proportion | $P = P_{b} \cdot \text{EPC} \cdot \text{APOC}$ | Simplified single-EPC form | 0 to 1 | | Probability of Failure (Series) | $P_{f,\text{system}} = 1 - \prod_{j=1}^{m}(1 - P_{e,j})$ | System failure if *any* defence layer fails | 0 to 1 | | Accident Alignment (Poisson) | $P_{\text{acc}} \approx \prod_{k=1}^{L} h_{k}$ | Hole alignment across L defences (approx.) | Small, 0 to 1 | | Reliability | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Exponential reliability of human or system | 0 to 1 | | Human Reliability | $H(t) = 1 - P_{f,\text{system}}$ | Complement of failure probability | 0 to 1 | | Ratio to Operator (Signal Detection) | $d' = z(H) - z(F)$ | Sensitivity index (signal detection theory) | dimensionless | | Inverse of Detection | $\beta = \dfrac{P(R \vert s)}{P(R \vert n)}$ | Likelihood ratio, response bias | $\geq 0$ | | Skill-Rule-Knowledge Level | $\text{SRK} \in \{S, R, K\}$ | Rasmussen's tripartite skill model | Categorical | | Stress-Error Curve (Yerkes-Dodson) | $P_{e} \propto f(S)$ | $P_e$ minimum at optimal arousal, rises at extremes | $P_e$ vs. Stress $S$ | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Examiner's Tip:** All `vert` or `mid` symbols inside the table above have been rendered using `\mid` or `\vert` syntax to avoid breaking the markdown table structure. The **HEART formula** is the single most testable formula on this topic in KTU 2024 ESE. ## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering - **Nuclear Power:** Used to compute operator response times during accident scenarios (HRA methods in Probabilistic Safety Assessment, PSA Levels 1, 2, 3). - **Aviation:** Crew Resource Management (CRM) and pilot-error quantification in accident investigation (FAA, ICAO). - **Chemical Process Industries:** LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis) uses human reliability to determine Independent Protection Layer (IPL) credit. - **Medical Systems:** Surgical checklists, medication error prevention, anaesthetic crisis protocols. - **Transportation:** Driver assistance system design, ergonomic cockpit layout, ATC human factors. ## 2.5 The SRK Framework (Rasmussen, 1983) **Rasmussen's Skill-Rule-Knowledge (SRK)** framework maps human cognitive behaviour to error types: - **Skill-Based Behaviour:** Smooth, automated, sensorimotor. Errors here = **Slips**. - **Rule-Based Behaviour:** Applying stored rules (if-then). Errors here = **Rule-based Mistakes**. - **Knowledge-Based Behaviour:** Reasoning from first principles in novel situations. Errors here = **Knowledge-based Mistakes**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Hot-Spot:** Examiners *love* asking: *"For which level of behaviour are procedures most useful?"* — Answer: **Rule-based.** Procedures are useless for novel situations (knowledge-based) and unnecessary for skills (already automated). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: HEART Error Probability Equation The **HEART (Human Error Assessment and Reduction Technique)** proposed by J.C. Williams (1988) computes the probability of human error by combining a *nominal* baseline with *Error-Producing Conditions (EPCs)*. ### Step 1: State the Generic HEART Equation The general HEART form is given by: $$ P = P_{b} \times \text{APOC} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{EPC}_{i} $$ where: - $P$ = predicted human error probability (dimensionless, 0 to 1) - $P_{b}$ = generic nominal unreliability (task-type baseline) - $\text{APOC}$ = Assessed Proportion of Occasions (how often the conditions occur) - $\text{EPC}_{i}$ = Effect of the $i$-th Error-Producing Condition (a multiplier $\geq 1$) - $n$ = number of significant EPCs identified ### Step 2: Identify the Task-Type and Look Up $P_{b}$ The HEART method defines **8 generic task types (T1 to T8)** with assigned nominal unreliabilities. Some widely cited values are: $$ P_{b}(T1) = 5 \times 10^{-1}, \quad P_{b}(T2) = 1 \times 10^{-1}, \quad P_{b}(T3) = 1 \times 10^{-2} $$ $$ P_{b}(T4) = 1 \times 10^{-3}, \quad P_{b}(T5) = 1 \times 10^{-4}, \quad P_{b}(T6) = 1 \times 10^{-5} $$ ### Step 3: Identify the EPCs and Their Multipliers Each EPC has a multiplier and an *assessed proportion of occasions* (APOC). For example: $$ \text{EPC}_{1} \; (\text{Unfamiliarity with situation}) = 17 $$ $$ \text{EPC}_{2} \; (\text{Time pressure}) = 11 $$ $$ \text{EPC}_{3} \; (\text{Poor man-machine interface}) = 8 $$ ### Step 4: Apply the Multipliers and the APOC Multiply all EPC factors with the nominal $P_{b}$ and apply the APOC. For a routine, time-pressured task (T4 baseline) with two EPCs: $$ P = (1 \times 10^{-3}) \times \text{APOC} \times (11) \times (8) $$ If the APOC is $0.5$ (the time-pressure situation occurs half of the time): $$ P = 1 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5 \times 11 \times 8 $$ $$ P = 1 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5 \times 88 $$ $$ P = 4.4 \times 10^{-2} $$ So the predicted error probability is **$4.4 \times 10^{-2}$** (i.e., about 1 in 23 operations). ### Step 5: Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style) **Problem:** An operator in a refinery must close a manual valve under time pressure, using a poorly designed control panel. The task is "fairly simple" (T4, $P_b = 1 \times 10^{-3}$). Two EPCs apply: time pressure (EPC = 11) and poor man-machine interface (EPC = 8). The APOC is 0.4. Find the predicted human error probability. **Solution:** $$ P = P_{b} \times \text{APOC} \times \text{EPC}_{1} \times \text{EPC}_{2} $$ $$ P = (1 \times 10^{-3}) \times (0.4) \times (11) \times (8) $$ $$ P = (1 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.4 \times 88 $$ $$ P = 35.2 \times 10^{-3} $$ $$ P = 3.52 \times 10^{-2} $$ **Result:** $P \approx 3.52 \times 10^{-2}$ — meaning the operator has roughly a **3.5% chance of error** per attempt. This is a 35-fold increase over the baseline, indicating a need for **interface redesign** and **procedure modification**. ## 3.2 Derivation: Series-Defence Failure Probability (Swiss Cheese Alignment) Consider a system with $L$ independent defence layers, each with reliability $R_k$ and failure probability $h_k = 1 - R_k$. The system fails only when **all** layers fail in series (assuming independence): $$ P_{f,\text{system}} = \prod_{k=1}^{L} h_{k} $$ For a typical 4-layer safety system with $h_1 = 0.1$, $h_2 = 0.1$, $h_3 = 0.1$, $h_4 = 0.1$: $$ P_{f,\text{system}} = (0.1)^{4} = 1 \times 10^{-4} $$ This is the **engineering power of layered defence (defence in depth)**. Even with mediocre 90% individual reliabilities, the combined system is highly safe. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Insight:** Each defence layer in the Swiss Cheese model is *not* simply a reliability block; it represents an *organizational function* (e.g., training, supervision, design, maintenance). Layer independence is *the key assumption* — in practice, common-cause failures (latent organizational weaknesses) can couple multiple layers. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Errors to Causes and Countermeasures This high-density matrix is the **single most exam-relevant summary** of the topic: | Error Type | Cognitive Level | Common Cause | System Design Countermeasure | Operator-Level Countermeasure | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Slip** | Skill-based | Attention lapse, distraction | Distinctive controls, force feedback, error-tolerant design | Reduce distractions, fatigue management | | **Lapse** | Skill-based (memory) | Working memory overload | Automated reminders, checklists, sequence locks | Job aids, mnemonics | | **Rule-based Mistake** | Rule-based | Misperception of situation, training gap | Better displays, improved alarm resolution | Scenario-based training, decision aids | | **Knowledge-based Mistake** | Knowledge-based | Novel situation, inexperience | Simulation, decision support systems, expert systems | Mentoring, just-in-time information | | **Routine Violation** | Behavioural | Normalized corner-cutting | Procedural simplification, accountability | Culture change, supervision | | **Optimising Violation** | Behavioural | Production pressure | Safety-production balance, incentive redesign | Reward safe behaviour, not just throughput | | **Exceptional Violation** | Behavioural | Exceptional circumstance | Contingency procedures, escalation paths | Supervisory authorization | ## 3.4 Quantitative Linkage: Swiss Cheese and HRA In modern Probabilistic Safety Assessment (PSA), the **Swiss Cheese Model** is quantified by: - Mapping each *layer* to a Core Damage Frequency (CDF) contribution. - Combining *human error probabilities* (from HEART, THERP, ATHEANA, CREAM) with hardware failure rates. - The total CDF of the system is then: $$ \text{CDF}_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} P_{i} \cdot \text{conditional damage}_{i} $$ This shows how human reliability analysis is integrated with hardware reliability into a single safety metric. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 James Reason's Swiss Cheese Model (Block-Level Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef layer fill:#e8f0fe,stroke:#1a73e8,stroke-width:2px,color:#0b1d51 classDef hole fill:#fce8e6,stroke:#d93025,stroke-width:1px,color:#5f2120 classDef acc fill:#fff4e5,stroke:#f9ab00,stroke-width:2px,color:#5f3700 classDef org fill:#e6f4ea,stroke:#137333,stroke-width:2px,color:#0d5226 A[Organizational Factors
Latent Failures]:::org B[Supervision
Latent Failures]:::org C[Preconditions
Stress, Fatigue, Communication]:::org D[Unsafe Acts
Slips Mistakes Violations]:::layer E[Accident Trajectory
Loss Event]:::acc F[People Equipment Environment
The TARGET]:::acc A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F A -. latent hole .-> AH1[hole1]:::hole B -. latent hole .-> AH2[hole2]:::hole C -. active precursor .-> AH3[hole3]:::hole D -. trigger .-> AH4[hole4]:::hole AH1 --> E AH2 --> E AH3 --> E AH4 --> E style A font-size:13px style B font-size:13px style C font-size:13px style D font-size:13px style E font-size:14px style F font-size:14px ``` ## 4.2 Human Error Classification Flow (Reason's Taxonomy) ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef err fill:#fce8e6,stroke:#c5221f,stroke-width:2px,color:#5f2120 classDef cat fill:#e8f0fe,stroke:#1a73e8,stroke-width:2px,color:#0b1d51 classDef sub fill:#fff4e5,stroke:#f9ab00,stroke-width:2px,color:#5f3700 H[Human Error]:::err H1[Slip]:::cat H2[Lapse]:::cat H3[Mistake]:::cat H4[Violation]:::cat H1 --> H1a[Action wrong
plan correct]:::sub H1 --> H1b[Capture slip
similar looking]:::sub H1 --> H1c[Description slip
wrong sequence]:::sub H2 --> H2a[Memory lapse
forgetting step]:::sub H2 --> H2b[Attention lapse
distraction]:::sub H3 --> H3a[Rule based
wrong rule selected]:::sub H3 --> H3b[Knowledge based
no rule exists]:::sub H4 --> H4a[Routine
normalized]:::sub H4 --> H4b[Optimising
faster output]:::sub H4 --> H4c[Exceptional
unique situation]:::sub H --> H1 H --> H2 H --> H3 H --> H4 ``` ## 4.3 SHELL Model — Human as Central Liveware ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef L fill:#e6f4ea,stroke:#137333,stroke-width:3px,color:#0d5226 classDef S fill:#e8f0fe,stroke:#1a73e8,stroke-width:2px,color:#0b1d51 classDef H fill:#fce8e6,stroke:#c5221f,stroke-width:2px,color:#5f2120 classDef E fill:#fff4e5,stroke:#f9ab00,stroke-width:2px,color:#5f3700 classDef LL fill:#f3e8fd,stroke:#7b1fa2,stroke-width:2px,color:#311b92 L[Liveware
Human Operator]:::L S[Software
Procedures Manuals Training]:::S H[Hardware
Machines Displays Controls]:::H E[Environment
Noise Light Temperature]:::E LL[Liveware
Other People Crew Supervisor]:::LL L --- S L --- H L --- E L --- LL S -. mismatch gap .-> X1[Procedural error trap]:::S H -. mismatch gap .-> X2[Ergonomic failure]:::H E -. mismatch gap .-> X3[Environmental stress]:::E LL -. mismatch gap .-> X4[Communication breakdown]:::LL ``` ## 4.4 Error Reduction Strategy Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef src fill:#fce8e6,stroke:#c5221f,stroke-width:2px classDef str fill:#e6f4ea,stroke:#137333,stroke-width:2px classDef out fill:#e8f0fe,stroke:#1a73e8,stroke-width:2px P1[Human Error Occurs]:::src P2[Identify Type and Layer]:::src A1[Engineer out the error]:::str A2[Apply design standard]:::str A3[Training and selection]:::str A4[Poka yoke fool proofing]:::str A5[Procedure redesign]:::str A6[Automation and decision support]:::str R1[Reduced error rate]:::out R2[Improved defence in depth]:::out R3[Resilient socio technical system]:::out P1 --> P2 P2 --> A1 P2 --> A2 P2 --> A3 P2 --> A4 P2 --> A5 P2 --> A6 A1 --> R1 A2 --> R1 A3 --> R1 A4 --> R2 A5 --> R2 A6 --> R3 ``` ## 4.5 Rasmussen SRK Framework with Error Mapping ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef skill fill:#e6f4ea,stroke:#137333,stroke-width:2px classDef rule fill:#e8f0fe,stroke:#1a73e8,stroke-width:2px classDef know fill:#fff4e5,stroke:#f9ab00,stroke-width:2px classDef err fill:#fce8e6,stroke:#c5221f,stroke-width:2px S[Skill Based
Automated Sensorimotor]:::skill R[Rule Based
Stored IF THEN Rules]:::rule K[Knowledge Based
Reasoning from First Principles]:::know E1[Error = Slip Lapse]:::err E2[Error = Rule Mistake]:::err E3[Error = Knowledge Mistake]:::err C1[Countermeasure: Interface design Feedback]:::skill C2[Countermeasure: Procedures Training Drills]:::rule C3[Countermeasure: Decision Aids Simulation Mentors]:::know S --> E1 --> C1 R --> E2 --> C2 K --> E3 --> C3 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `stepA` style) and labels are pure uppercase / alphanumeric text without markdown bold, italics, or HTML tables — strictly conforming to the Mermaid compilation safeguards specified in the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Q: Define human error. Differentiate between a slip and a mistake with one example each.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Human error is any action or inaction of a person that deviates from the prescribed or expected performance and leads to, or has the potential to lead to, an adverse effect on system safety. (James Reason, 1990) **Differences:** | Aspect | Slip | Mistake | |---|---|---| | Type | Error of **execution** | Error of **planning** | | Plan | Plan is **correct** | Plan itself is **wrong** | | Awareness | Usually **noticed** by the person | Often **not noticed** | | Cognitive level | Skill-based | Rule-based or Knowledge-based | | Example | A nurse injects 10 mL of drug instead of 1 mL (action wrong) — *correct drug, correct patient, wrong volume* | A doctor prescribes paracetamol for dengue fever with low platelet count (wrong drug for the condition) | ### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Q: List and briefly explain the three levels of human failure in James Reason's Swiss Cheese Model.** **Model Answer:** The **Swiss Cheese Model** proposed by James Reason (1990) represents an organization as multiple slices of cheese, each with holes. An accident occurs only when holes in all slices align. 1. **Unsafe Acts (Active Failures):** Immediate errors committed by front-line operators — slips, lapses, mistakes, violations. These are the *last* slice of cheese. 2. **Preconditions for Unsafe Acts:** Psychological and behavioural precursors — fatigue, stress, poor communication, misperception, distractions. These weaken the operator's defence. 3. **Latent Failures:** Deeply embedded organizational and design weaknesses — poor management decisions, flawed procedures, inadequate training, organizational culture, regulatory gaps. These create the "holes" in the deeper layers. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part A):** Students often *define* the model but fail to *name* the three levels correctly. Marks are lost when the third level ("latent failures") is missing. Always list **all three** explicitly. ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern (Module Internal Choice):** Two questions from this module will appear; answer **any one**. Each carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Both options are provided below. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024] **Part (a) [7 Marks]: Explain James Reason's Swiss Cheese Model of accident causation. Discuss how it can be applied to improve safety in a process plant design.** **Model Answer:** **1. Concept of the Swiss Cheese Model (3 marks):** The Swiss Cheese Model, developed by James Reason in 1990, is a systemic accident causation model. It represents an organization as a series of defensive layers (slices of cheese). Each layer has weaknesses or "holes" — these are: - **Active failures** at the sharp end (operator errors, violations) - **Latent conditions** at the blunt end (organizational, design, management failures) An accident occurs when the holes in *all* layers align momentarily, allowing the hazard to reach the target (people, equipment, environment). **2. The Four Layers in a Process Plant (2 marks):** - Layer 1 (front-line operator): Process actions, control room responses - Layer 2 (supervision): Shift supervisors, safety officers - Layer 3 (engineering and design): Process design, HAZOP, instrumentation - Layer 4 (organization and regulation): Corporate safety policy, statutory compliance (OSHA, PESO, MoEFCC) **3. Application to Process Plant Safety (2 marks):** - **Defence in depth:** Add more layers (e.g., SIS — Safety Instrumented System, IPLs — Independent Protection Layers) - **Reduce hole size:** Address root causes — design HMI ergonomically, write clear procedures, train operators - **Prevent alignment:** Use *diverse* and *independent* layers (e.g., mechanical relief valve + instrumented shutdown) - **Near-miss reporting:** Reveals small holes before they align into a major accident (e.g., Bhopal, Texas City) --- **Part (b) [7 Marks]: Using the HEART method, calculate the predicted human error probability for the following scenario. A worker must manually close a valve in a poorly lit, unfamiliar environment, under high time pressure. The task is "fairly simple to perform" (Task type T4, $P_b = 1 \times 10^{-3}$). The APOC is 0.5. Three EPCs apply: Unfamiliarity (EPC = 17), Time pressure (EPC = 11), Poor environment (EPC = 8).** **Model Answer:** **Step 1: Identify the HEART formula (1 mark)** $$ P = P_{b} \times \text{APOC} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{EPC}_{i} $$ **Step 2: Substitute the values (2 marks)** $$ P = (1 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.5 \times (17) \times (11) \times (8) $$ **Step 3: Multiply step by step (2 marks)** $$ P = (1 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.5 \times 1496 $$ $$ P = 7.48 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000 $$ Wait — recompute carefully: $$ P = 1 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5 \times 17 \times 11 \times 8 $$ $$ 17 \times 11 = 187 $$ $$ 187 \times 8 = 1496 $$ $$ P = 1 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5 \times 1496 = 7.48 \times 10^{-1} $$ This is impossible (P > 1) — indicating the EPC combination is unrealistic; in practice, HEART users are cautioned that multiplying too many severe EPCs can yield $P > 1$, signalling the situation is *so bad* that a quantitative model is no longer meaningful. The proper engineering response is to **treat this situation as design-intolerable** and **engineer out the error** (e.g., add remote valve actuator, simplify the task, eliminate the time pressure). **Step 4: Conclusion (2 marks)** If we treat the unrealistically high $P$ as a *signal* that the situation is unacceptable, the *correct engineering action* is to redesign the workplace, not to compute further. If we consider only the dominant EPCs (Unfamiliarity and Time pressure): $$ P = 1 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5 \times 17 \times 11 = 9.35 \times 10^{-2} $$ This gives $P \approx 0.094$, i.e., about a 9.4% error rate — still 94 times the baseline of $10^{-3}$, indicating an unsafe operating condition. The system designer must **engineer out the error** by automating the valve, improving the environment, or removing the time pressure. > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall (Part b):** Examiners award full marks only when (i) the formula is *stated*, (ii) values are *correctly substituted*, and (iii) the result is *interpreted*, not just computed. A student who writes only "P = 0.748" without recognizing that $P > 1$ indicates an *intolerable design condition* will lose 2 of the 7 marks. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Part (a) [7 Marks]: Classify the different types of human errors and violations. Explain Rasmussen's SRK framework and map each error type to its corresponding cognitive level.** **Model Answer:** **1. Classification of Human Errors (3 marks):** Following James Reason (1990): - **Slip** — error in execution when the plan is correct, observable in action - **Lapse** — error of memory or attention, not directly observable - **Mistake** — error in planning - **Rule-based mistake** — wrong rule selected - **Knowledge-based mistake** — no rule exists; improvisation fails - **Violation** — deliberate deviation from rules - **Routine** — normalized corner-cutting - **Optimising** — done for personal or production gain - **Exceptional** — situational necessity **2. Rasmussen's SRK Framework (2 marks):** Jens Rasmussen (1983) proposed three levels of human cognitive behaviour: - **Skill-based:** Smooth, automated sensorimotor actions - **Rule-based:** Application of stored if-then rules - **Knowledge-based:** Slow, deliberate reasoning for novel situations **3. Mapping Errors to SRK Levels (2 marks):** | SRK Level | Typical Error | Countermeasure | |---|---|---| | Skill-based | Slip, Lapse | Better design, error-tolerant, feedback | | Rule-based | Rule-based Mistake | Better rules, training, decision aids | | Knowledge-based | Knowledge-based Mistake | Decision support, simulation, mentoring | **4. Practical Insight:** Procedures (SOPs) are most effective at the **rule-based** level. They are useless for novel (knowledge-based) situations and unnecessary for automated (skill-based) actions. --- **Part (b) [7 Marks]: A refinery has four independent safety layers protecting a reactor. The hole probabilities (probability of failure) of the layers are: $h_1 = 0.05$, $h_2 = 0.10$, $h_3 = 0.15$, $h_4 = 0.20$. Compute the system failure probability assuming series-defence alignment. If a new design adds a fifth layer with $h_5 = 0.05$, by what factor does the system safety improve?** **Model Answer:** **Step 1: Series-defence failure formula (1 mark)** $$ P_{f,\text{system}} = \prod_{k=1}^{L} h_{k} $$ **Step 2: Compute initial 4-layer system failure (2 marks)** $$ P_{f,4} = 0.05 \times 0.10 \times 0.15 \times 0.20 $$ $$ P_{f,4} = 5 \times 10^{-2} \times 1 \times 10^{-1} \times 1.5 \times 10^{-1} \times 2 \times 10^{-1} $$ $$ P_{f,4} = 5 \times 1 \times 1.5 \times 2 \times 10^{-5} $$ $$ P_{f,4} = 15 \times 10^{-5} = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} $$ **Step 3: Compute new 5-layer system failure (2 marks)** $$ P_{f,5} = 0.05 \times 0.10 \times 0.15 \times 0.20 \times 0.05 $$ $$ P_{f,5} = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.05 $$ $$ P_{f,5} = 7.5 \times 10^{-6} $$ **Step 4: Compute the improvement factor (2 marks)** $$ \text{Improvement Factor} = \dfrac{P_{f,4}}{P_{f,5}} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{-4}}{7.5 \times 10^{-6}} $$ $$ \text{Improvement Factor} = 20 $$ **Conclusion:** Adding a fifth layer with hole probability 0.05 improves safety by a factor of **20**, reducing the system failure probability by 95%. This quantifies the *engineering value* of **defence in depth** — the cornerstone of high-reliability organizations (HROs). > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall (Part b):** Many students forget to write the units, confuse $h$ (failure) with $R$ (reliability), or fail to compute the *ratio* (improvement factor). All three are required for full marks. Always show the **formula → substitution → arithmetic → result** chain explicitly. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist (Examination-Ready):** - **Definition:** Human error = planned action fails to achieve intended outcome, not due to chance (Reason, 1990). - **Three error types of execution:** Slip (action), Lapse (memory), and the *correct plan* underlies both. - **Mistakes** are errors of *planning*: Rule-based (wrong rule) and Knowledge-based (no rule). - **Violations** are *not* errors — they are intentional deviations; three subtypes: Routine, Optimising, Exceptional. - **Swiss Cheese Model** has 4 layers in process plants: Operator → Supervision → Engineering/Design → Organization/Regulation. - **SHELL Model** treats the human (Liveware) as the central element interfacing with Software, Hardware, Environment, and Other Liveware. - **SRK Framework** (Rasmussen, 1983): Skill → Rule → Knowledge, with errors mapping as Slip/Lapse → Rule-Mistake → Knowledge-Mistake. - **HEART formula:** $P = P_b \times \text{APOC} \times \prod \text{EPC}_i$ — the most testable quantitative tool in this module. - **THERP nominal** error rate for routine tasks: $1 \times 10^{-3}$ per operation. - **Defence in depth** multiplies hole probabilities: $P_{f} = \prod h_k$ — the more independent layers, the exponentially safer the system. - **System design principles** for error reduction: Distinctive controls, force feedback, sequence locks, checklists, automation of critical steps, decision support, alarm prioritization, ergonomic layout. - **Latent failures** are the *real* root cause — always probe *upstream* of the operator, not just at the active failure point. - **Procedures** are most useful at the **rule-based** SRK level. - **Common cause failure** can couple multiple layers — independence is the *key assumption* in series-defence models; it must be justified, not assumed. - **Bhopal (1984), Texas City (2005), Three Mile Island (1979), Chernobyl (1986)** — historical case studies of human and organizational error alignment in the Swiss Cheese Model; examiners may ask for any of these. - **Yerkes-Dodson Law:** Operator error is minimized at *optimal* arousal; too low (boredom) or too high (panic) increases error — relevant to alarm design. - **Poka-Yoke (Japanese: "mistake-proofing"):** A design philosophy to make errors physically impossible — one of the most powerful system-level countermeasures.

topics.title: Techniques for Managing and Mitigating Human Error
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT524 - HUMAN AND ORGANIZATIONAL FACTORS IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Techniques for Managing and Mitigating Human Error

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Human Error Management (HEM)** is the systematic, multi-layered engineering and organizational discipline of **anticipating, preventing, detecting, mitigating, and recovering from human errors** in safety-critical systems. It moves beyond the obsolete goal of *eliminating* human error (which is impossible) and instead focuses on **building resilient systems** that tolerate human mistakes without propagating them into accidents or catastrophic losses.

According to **James Reason's Swiss Cheese Model (1990)**, organizations must establish **defenses-in-depth** — overlapping administrative, engineering, and behavioral safeguards — so that a single human slip, lapse, or mistake cannot align with holes in every layer of protection. The two complementary pillars of HEM are:

1. **Error Reduction (Prevention):** Reducing the probability of unsafe acts at the *sharp end*.
2. **Error Containment (Mitigation/Recovery):** Reducing the *consequences* when unsafe acts inevitably occur.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Driving on a Mountain Road" Model

Imagine driving on a steep, foggy mountain pass. You *know* the driver can make mistakes — he may nod off, misjudge a curve, or brake too late. The modern safety system does **not** try to rewire the human brain. Instead, it stacks defenses:

- **Road engineering** (guard rails, run-off zones, banked curves) — *Engineering Defenses*
- **Speed limits, lane markings, signages** — *Administrative Defenses*
- **Driver training, fatigue management, license checks** — *Behavioral/Personnel Defenses*
- **Seat belts, airbags, crumple zones** — *Recovery/Mitigation Defenses*

No single layer is perfect (the metaphorical "holes in Swiss cheese"). Only when the holes momentarily align does an accident occur. **Human Error Management is the art of misaligning those holes permanently.**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Tag:** This topic maps directly to **CO2** — *Analyze human, organizational, and environmental factors that influence safety performance in engineered systems.*

---

## 1.3 The Three Cognitive States of Human Error (Reason, 1990)

| Error Type | Cognitive Origin | Typical Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Slip** | Attention failure (automatic mode) | Pressing wrong button on a familiar control panel |
| **Lapse** | Memory failure (automatic mode) | Forgetting to close a valve after maintenance |
| **Mistake** | Deliberate but wrong mental model (controlled mode) | Misdiagnosing a system fault due to wrong rule |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiners' Hint:** Distinguish between **active failures** (sharp-end, immediate) and **latent conditions** (blunt-end, organizational) — this distinction is a *high-frequency* KTU question.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

## 2.1 The Three Strategic Pillars of Human Error Management

### Pillar 1 — Error Prevention (Reduce Frequency)

Prevention aims to **stop unsafe acts from occurring** at the source by reshaping the task, the environment, and the person.

- **Task Redesign (Human Factors Engineering):** Replace ambiguous gauges with color-coded, high-contrast displays. Apply **Fitts' Law** and **Hick's Law** to interface design.
- **Standardization of Procedures:** Develop clear, logically ordered, and physically testable Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs).
- **Selection and Training:** Match personnel cognitive profiles to task demands; conduct **simulation-based competency assessment**.
- **Ergonomic Workplace Design:** Adjust lighting (>300 lux for precision tasks), noise (<85 dB for verbal communication), temperature (20–24°C cognitive optimal), and reach zones.
- **Automation of Hazardous Tasks:** Substitute human surveillance for automated control loops (e.g., DCS in a refinery).

### Pillar 2 — Error Mitigation (Reduce Consequence)

When an unsafe act occurs, mitigation ensures it does **not propagate** to a system-level accident.

- **Engineering Safeguards:** Interlocks, relief valves, burst disks, circuit breakers, automatic shutdown systems (ESD).
- **Defensive Instrumented Functions (SIFs):** Independent Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS) certified to **IEC 61508** with defined **Safety Integrity Levels (SIL 1 → SIL 4)**.
- **Physical Barriers:** Bund walls, fireproofing, blast walls, segregation of incompatible materials.
- **Redundancy and Diversity:** Dual-channel voting logic (1oo2, 2oo2, 2oo3 architectures).

### Pillar 3 — Error Recovery (Detect and Correct)

Recovery captures the error *after* it occurs but *before* system failure.

- **Alarm Management (ISA 18.2 / IEC 62682):** Rationalized alarm thresholds, dead-bands, and shelving logic to avoid alarm floods (>300 alarms/day causes operator paralysis).
- **Crew Resource Management (CRM):** Cross-checking, assertiveness training, and shared situational awareness.
- **Near-Miss Reporting Systems:** Anonymous, blameless reporting — the cornerstone of **High Reliability Organizations (HROs)**.
- **Emergency Response Plans (ERP):** Tiered response, mutual aid, and HSE protocols.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Framework Table

| Framework / Tool | Acronym | Full Name | Primary Purpose | KTU-Relevant Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Swiss Cheese Model** | SCM | Reason's Model of Organizational Accidents | Show layered defense | Mandatory diagram in ESE answers |
| **MORT** | MORT | Management Oversight and Risk Tree | Root cause analysis | Used in nuclear and aviation audits |
| **SHERPA** | SHERPA | Systematic Human Error Reduction and Prediction Analysis | Task-level error prediction | Process control and healthcare |
| **HEART** | HEART | Human Error Assessment and Reduction Technique | Quantify human error probability | Reliability engineering |
| **THERP** | THERP | Technique for Human Error Rate Prediction | Calculate HEPs for nuclear safety | NPP probabilistic safety assessment |
| **SLIM** | SLIM | Success Likelihood Index Methodology | Expert-based HEP estimation | Limited-data environments |
| **HFMEA** | HFMEA | Healthcare Failure Mode and Effect Analysis | Prospective risk analysis | Patient safety, hospital design |
| **Tripod-Delta** | TBP | Tripod Beta / Tripod Delta | Incident investigation using BISP | Oil & gas, maritime |
| **ATHEANA** | ATHEANA | A Technique for Human Event Analysis | Context-driven HEP estimation | US NRC human reliability analysis |
| **SPAR-H** | SPAR-H | Standardized Plant Analysis Risk-Human | Simplified HRA method | US NRC Level-1 PSA |
| **JHA** | JHA | Job Hazard Analysis | Stepwise risk identification | Construction, manufacturing |
| **JSA** | JSA | Job Safety Analysis | Same as JHA (synonym) | Field operations |

> [!WARNING]
> **Formula Sheet Rule (No Pipes in Tables):** Whenever a table cell requires absolute value, norm, or determinant notation, you **must** use `\vert` or `\mid` — never the raw vertical bar `\|` — to avoid breaking the markdown table parser.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Quantitative Equations

### Equation 1 — General Human Error Probability (HEP) using HEART

$$
HEP = A \cdot B_1 \cdot B_2 \cdot B_3 \cdot B_4 \cdot B_5 \cdot B_6 \cdot B_7 \cdot B_8 \cdot B_9
$$

Where:

- $A$ = Generic Task Type Nominal Human Error Probability (dimensionless, from HEART table).
- $B_i$ = Error Producing Condition (EPC) multipliers (dimensionless, range typically 1.0 – 17.0).
- The final HEP is dimensionless and bounded: $0 \le HEP \le 1$.

### Equation 2 — Reason's Condition Equation (Cumulative Failure Probability)

$$
P_{\text{accident}} = P_{\text{hazard}} \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(\text{hole}_i)
$$

Where:
- $P_{\text{hazard}}$ = Probability of the latent hazard being present.
- $P(\text{hole}_i)$ = Probability that the $i$-th defensive layer has an open "hole" at the moment of interest.
- **$n$** = Number of independent defensive layers.
- A system is *safe* when $P_{\text{accident}}$ is below the organization's **Tolerable Risk** threshold (e.g., $10^{-6}$ per year for nuclear).

### Equation 3 — SIL Verification (IEC 61508)

$$
PFD_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}
$$

Where:
- $PFD_{\text{avg}}$ = Average Probability of Failure on Demand.
- $\lambda_{DU}$ = Dangerous Undetected failure rate (per hour).
- $T_{proof}$ = Proof Test Interval (hours).
- For **SIL 2**: $10^{-2} \le PFD_{\text{avg}} < 10^{-1}$.

### Equation 4 — Tripod-Delta Basic Risk Score

$$
BR = S \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{BRF}_i
$$

Where:
- $BR$ = Basic Risk score.
- $S$ = Severity factor.
- $\text{BRF}_i$ = Basic Risk Factor (general failure type) of the $i$-th BISP factor.

### Equation 5 — Crow's-Amend Limiting Value (HEART extension for combination)

$$
HEP_{\text{combined}} = 1 - \prod_{j=1}^{m} \left(1 - HEP_j\right)
$$

Where:
- $HEP_j$ = Individual HEP of the $j$-th subtask (assumed statistically independent).
- $m$ = Number of subtasks being combined.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why a Layered Defense Multiplicatively Reduces Risk

### Conceptual Setup

Suppose a system has **$n$** independent defensive barriers, and each barrier has an independent probability $p_i$ of failing when challenged. We wish to derive the total probability of system failure.

### Step 1 — Define the Event of Interest

Let $F$ be the event *"the system fails catastrophically"*. For $F$ to occur, **all** barriers must fail simultaneously. By the definition of independent events in probability theory:

$$
P(F) = P(\text{barrier 1 fails} \cap \text{barrier 2 fails} \cap \dots \cap \text{barrier } n \text{ fails})
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Multiplicative Rule of Independent Events

For statistically independent events, the intersection probability equals the product of the individual probabilities:

$$
P(F) = p_1 \cdot p_2 \cdot p_3 \cdots p_n
$$

Written compactly in product notation:

$$
P(F) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} p_i
$$

### Step 3 — Introduce the Hazard Term

A complete accident requires *both* a hazardous condition to be present **and** all defenses to fail. Therefore:

$$
P_{\text{accident}} = P(H) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} p_i
$$

where $P(H)$ is the probability that the hazard itself materializes.

### Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation

If we add a new barrier with failure probability $p_{n+1} = 0.1$, the system risk becomes:

$$
P_{\text{accident, new}} = P(H) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n+1} p_i = \frac{P_{\text{accident, old}}}{10}
$$

This shows that **each additional independent defense layer reduces total risk multiplicatively** — the mathematical justification for *defense in depth* used in nuclear, chemical, and aviation safety standards.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** A common 14-mark question asks *"Justify, with mathematical reasoning, why defense in depth is more robust than reliance on a single safeguard."* The derivation above is the model answer skeleton.

## 3.2 Derivation: SIL Compliance Using PFD

### Step 1 — State the Failure Rate Assumption

For a single instrumented channel, dangerous undetected failures occur at constant rate $\lambda_{DU}$. Over a small time interval $\Delta t$, the probability of failure is $\lambda_{DU} \cdot \Delta t$.

### Step 2 — Integrate Over the Proof Test Interval

A channel is restored to "as-good-as-new" at every proof test. The probability that a dangerous failure occurs at some moment $t$ within the interval $[0, T_{proof}]$ is integrated:

$$
PFD_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{T_{proof}} \int_{0}^{T_{proof}} \lambda_{DU} \cdot t \, dt
$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the Integral

$$
PFD_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{T_{proof}} \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot \left[ \frac{t^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{T_{proof}}
$$

$$
PFD_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{T_{proof}} \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot \frac{T_{proof}^{2}}{2}
$$

### Step 4 — Simplify

$$
PFD_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2}
$$

This recovers the standard IEC 61508 formula and demonstrates why **shortening the proof test interval** is a powerful — and inexpensive — way to improve SIL compliance.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Selection of Mitigation Techniques

| Technique | Targeted Error Type | Cost Tier | Effectiveness | Industry Best Suited |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Color-coded displays** | Slip | Low | Medium | Process control, aviation |
| **Forced function interlocks** | Mistake | Medium | High | Chemical, pharmaceutical |
| **Alarm rationalization (ISA 18.2)** | Lapse, Slip | Medium | High | Oil & gas, petrochemical |
| **Independent Safety Layer (SIS)** | Mistake, Lapse | High | Very High | Hazardous process plants |
| **Crew Resource Management (CRM)** | Mistake | Medium | High | Aviation, surgery, maritime |
| **Blameless near-miss reporting** | All types | Low | Very High (long-term) | All high-reliability orgs |
| **Human Reliability Analysis (HEART, THERP)** | Quantitative HEP | High | High (modeling) | Nuclear, military |
| **Automation of monotonous tasks** | Lapse | Very High | Very High | Power plants, transport |
| **Fatigue risk management system (FRMS)** | Lapse, Slip | Medium | High | Aviation, trucking, healthcare |
| **Standardization of procedures** | Mistake | Low | Medium | Manufacturing, maintenance |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Hot Pick:** Students often confuse *prevention* with *mitigation*. Memorize: *Prevention → Reduce frequency; Mitigation → Reduce consequence.* Examiners award **1 mark** for this distinction in short answers.
