# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Q:** Define **Human Reliability Analysis (HRA)**. List any **four** Performance Shaping Factors (PSFs) that influence the Human Error Probability (HEP).

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Human Reliability Analysis (HRA) is a structured methodology used in probabilistic safety/risk assessment to identify, model, and quantify the probability of human errors that may degrade the safety or performance of a human-machine system. It is a quantitative extension of Human Factors Engineering.

> **Four PSFs:**
> 1. **Environmental PSFs:** Noise, heat, lighting, vibration, humidity.
> 2. **Personnel PSFs:** Stress, fatigue, training level, experience, motivation.
> 3. **Task PSFs:** Task complexity, time available, feedback quality.
> 4. **Procedural PSFs:** Quality, clarity, currency, and usability of operating procedures.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q:** Distinguish between **first-generation** and **second-generation** HRA methods. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer:**

> **First-Generation HRA Methods:**
> - Decomposition-based; break human action into sub-tasks and assign HEP to each.
> - Weak on context; treat human as a mechanical component.
> - Examples: **THERP**, **HEART**, **SLIM-MAUD**, **ASME-ANS**.

> **Second-Generation HRA Methods:**
> - Context-driven; explicitly model the *context* of action, including unsafe acts, recovery failures, and violations.
> - Use cognitive models (Rasmussen's SRK framework).
> - Examples: **ATHEANA**, **CREAM**, **MERMOS**, **CBDT/HEART-D**.

> **Key Distinction:** First-generation methods treat error as a *random hardware-like failure*; second-generation methods treat error as a *consequence of context* (poor interface, time pressure, culture).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part B Module 4 questions allow internal choice. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Module 4 Choice Option A]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **THERP (Technique for Human Error Rate Prediction)** method. List the steps involved and state its two main limitations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A nuclear plant operator must manually trip a reactor scram system on demand. Using the **HEART method** with Generic Task Type T6 (*a fairly simple task performed rapidly with negligible stress, but with no real-time feedback on progress*) and the single EPC "EPC-1: Unfamiliarity with a situation which is potentially important but which only occurs infrequently" (assessed multiplier = 25, weight = 0.95), calculate the **Human Error Probability (HEP)**.

#### Model Solution to (a):

> **THERP — Detailed Explanation:**
> THERP, developed by **Swain & Guttmann (1983)** at Sandia National Laboratories, is the most widely used HRA method in the nuclear industry. It is a **decomposition-based, event-tree-driven** technique.
>
> **Steps in THERP:**
> 1. **Define the system and event of interest** (e.g., operator action during a LOCA).
> 2. **Perform task analysis** (HTA) to break the action into sub-tasks.
> 3. **Construct an HRA event tree** showing success/failure branches at each sub-task.
> 4. **Assign HEPs** to each sub-task from the THERP tables (e.g., the *Nominal HEP table* for diagnosis and action).
> 5. **Apply dependency** (positive, zero, negative) using the **ASME/ANS dependency model** to combine HEPs when sub-tasks are not independent.
> 6. **Compute the overall HEP** by multiplying branch probabilities through the event tree.
> 7. **Document PSFs** and uncertainty bounds.
>
> **Two Main Limitations of THERP:**
> 1. **Data-intensive:** Requires large empirical databases; not all tasks have THERP data.
> 2. **Weak on context:** THERP assumes errors are *random* and does not adequately model the *context* that produces errors (poor HMI, culture, organizational factors). It treats the human as a hardware component.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:**
- Generic Task Type: **T6** with nominal $a = 0.007$ (from HEART table).
- EPC-1: $EPC = 25$, $W = 0.95$.

**Formula:**

$$
HEP = a \cdot (EPC^W)
$$

**Step 1:** Identify nominal $a$ for T6.

$$
a = 0.007
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the EPC contribution.

$$
EPC^W = 25^{0.95}
$$

Using the identity $x^y = e^{y \ln x}$:

$$
EPC^W = e^{0.95 \cdot \ln(25)} = e^{0.95 \cdot 3.2189} = e^{3.0580} = 21.27
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the HEP.

$$
HEP = 0.007 \times 21.27
$$

$$
HEP = 0.1489
$$

$$
\boxed{HEP \approx 1.49 \times 10^{-1}}
$$

**Interpretation:** There is a **~14.9% probability** the operator will fail to manually trip the reactor scram on demand — a significant HEP that justifies investment in an automatic scram system.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Module 4 Choice Option B]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss **ATHEANA** (A Technique for Human Event Analysis) as a second-generation HRA method. Compare its strengths and weaknesses against **THERP**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using **CREAM**, compute the probability of failure for an operator in *Tactical* control mode for a task with median time $T_{0.5} = 60$ s, $\beta = 1.4$, at a response time $t = 45$ s. Comment on the result.

#### Model Solution to (a):

> **ATHEANA — Discussion:**
> ATHEANA was developed by the U.S. **Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC)** in the 1990s (NUREG-1624, Rev. 2, 2000). It is a *context-driven* method.
>
> **Core Philosophy:** Human error is rarely random; it is the *result* of *unsafe acts* (intentional or unintentional) that are *triggered* by *Performance Shaping Factors (PSFs)* and *opportunities for error*. ATHEANA explicitly looks for **foreshortened heuristics** (mental shortcuts that fail) and **violations**.
>
> **Steps in ATHEANA:**
> 1. Identify the human actions of interest from the PRA.
> 2. For each action, identify the **PSFs** (e.g., poor lighting, high stress, unclear procedure) that could degrade performance.
> 3. Identify the **unsafe acts** that could result (e.g., misdiagnosis, wrong action).
> 4. For each combination of PSFs and unsafe acts, search the **AEA (Accident Sequence Evaluation Program) database** or expert judgment for an HEP.
> 5. Document the **cues** (e.g., alarms that were not present) and **opportunities for recovery**.
> 6. Compute the HEP and uncertainty.
>
> **Comparison with THERP:**
>
> | Dimension | THERP | ATHEANA |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Generation** | 1st | 2nd |
> | **Treatment of Context** | Weak; PSFs are secondary | Central; PSFs drive HEP |
> | **Treatment of Violations** | Poorly modeled | Explicitly modeled |
> | **Data** | Large (THERP tables) | Sparse; uses expert judgment |
> | **Effort** | Moderate | High |
> | **Output** | Single HEP per action | HEP + cause + recovery analysis |
> | **Best Use** | Routine operations | Severe accident, off-normal conditions |
>
> **Strengths of ATHEANA:** Captures violations and unsafe acts; models context realistically; produces rich insights for procedure/HMI redesign.
> **Weaknesses of ATHEANA:** Resource-intensive; depends on expert judgment; not ideal for screening.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:**
- Cognitive Control Mode: **Tactical** → Weibull parameters $T_{0.5} = 60$ s, $\beta = 1.4$.
- Response time $t = 45$ s.

**Formula (CREAM Weibull Reliability Function):**

$$
P(t) = e^{-\left(\frac{t}{T_{0.5}}\right)^{\beta}}
$$

**Step 1:** Compute the time ratio.

$$
\frac{t}{T_{0.5}} = \frac{45}{60} = 0.75
$$

**Step 2:** Raise to $\beta$.

$$
\left(\frac{t}{T_{0.5}}\right)^{\beta} = (0.75)^{1.4}
$$

Apply $x^y = e^{y \ln x}$:

$$
(0.75)^{1.4} = e^{1.4 \cdot \ln(0.75)} = e^{1.4 \cdot (-0.2877)} = e^{-0.4028} = 0.6683
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the exponential.

$$
P(t) = e^{-0.6683} = 0.5128
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the HEP (failure probability).

$$
HEP = 1 - P(t) = 1 - 0.5128
$$

$$
\boxed{HEP \approx 0.4872}
$$

**Comment:** Under *Tactical* control mode, the operator has a **~48.7% probability of failure** to complete the task within 45 seconds. This is a *very high* HEP. CREAM's output here signals that the task should be redesigned to *reduce cognitive workload* (e.g., automatic sequence, clear SOP, decision support) so the operator operates in **Rule-Based** mode rather than **Tactical** mode. The implication: **engineering out the human error** is more effective than training humans to be perfect.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse HEP with HER.** HEP is *per demand*; HER is *per unit time*. Examiners test this distinction.
> 2. **Do NOT mix first-generation and second-generation method assumptions.** THERP assumes independent sub-task HEPs; ATHEANA explicitly models dependency through PSFs. Mixing them breaks internal consistency.
> 3. **Do NOT forget to log/convert exponents.** A common error is to write $25^{0.95} = 25$ instead of computing the fractional power — this gives an answer off by a factor of 1.18.
> 4. **Always specify the cognitive control mode** (Rush, Tactical, Opportunistic, Strategic) when using CREAM. Without it, the $\beta$ and $T_{0.5}$ values are undefined.
> 5. **Always include uncertainty bounds** (5th–95th percentile) in the HRA report. Examiners specifically deduct marks for omitting uncertainty.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for HRA:**

- **Definition:** HRA = structured methodology to identify, model, and quantify human error probability (HEP) in safety-critical systems.
- **Two main outputs:** HEP (per demand) and HER (per unit time).
- **Two main HEP ranges:** Skill-based (10⁻⁴ to 10⁻²); Rule-based (10⁻³ to 10⁻¹); Knowledge-based (10⁻¹ to 1.0).
- **Reason's Swiss Cheese Model (1990):** Multiple defensive layers; each layer has "holes" (latent failures); HRA finds the human-layer holes.
- **Rasmussen's SRK Framework:** Skill-, Rule-, Knowledge-based behavior — used to classify errors.
- **First-generation HRA methods:** THERP, HEART, SLIM-MAUD, ASME-ANS — decomposition-based.
- **Second-generation HRA methods:** ATHEANA, CREAM, MERMOS, CBDT — context-driven.
- **HEART formula (essential):** $HEP = a \cdot \prod_{i} (EPC_i^{W_i})$
- **CREAM Weibull formula (essential):** $P(t) = e^{-(t / T_{0.5})^{\beta}}$
- **SLIM-MAUD formula (essential):** $\log\left(\frac{P_s}{1-P_s}\right) = a \cdot SI + b$
- **PSFs** = Performance Shaping Factors = environmental, personnel, task, procedural modifiers.
- **EPCs** = Error Producing Conditions (HEART-specific multipliers).
- **Dependency (ASME/ANS):** $HEP_{dep} = \frac{1 + (N-1) \cdot HEP_{indep}}{N}$ — use when sub-tasks are not independent.
- **Beta-Factor Model:** $P_{cc} = \beta \cdot P_{indep}$ — used in Common Cause Failure (CCF) analysis.
- **HRA ↔ PRA integration:** HEPs become basic events in fault trees or branch probabilities in event trees → feed Core Damage Frequency (CDF).
- **Industry anchors:** $HEP_{\text{miss low stress}} = 10^{-4}$; $HEP_{\text{miss high stress}} = 0.5$ (THERP).
- **Cognitive Control Modes (CREAM):** Rush, Tactical, Opportunistic, Strategic — Strategic gives the *highest* HEP.
- **Violations:** ATHEANA's unique strength — models intentional deviations from procedure.
- **Recovery Factor:** $P_{success} = 1 - HEP$ — used in event-tree branches.
- **NUREG-1855:** The NRC's "Guidance on the Treatment of Uncertainties Associated with PRAs" — references this in KTU reference material.
- **IEEE Std 1082:** "Guide for Incorporating Human Reliability Analysis into PRA" — key reference.
- **SHELL Model (Edwards, 1972):** Software, Hardware, Environment, Liveware — context for PSFs.
- **Accident precedent:** Chernobyl, Three Mile Island, Bhopal — all involve *human/organizational* factors, the very subject of HRA.
- **Engineering takeaway:** HRA is not about blaming the human; it is about **engineering out the human error** by designing systems that *make errors difficult and recoveries easy*.

---

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT524 - HUMAN AND ORGANIZATIONAL FACTORS IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Overview of HRA Methodologies (THERP, HEART, SPAR-H)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Human Reliability Analysis (HRA)?

**Human Reliability Analysis (HRA)** is a structured, systematic methodology used within Probabilistic Safety / Risk Assessment (PSA / PRA) to evaluate the likelihood of human errors during the operation of complex, high-hazard systems (such as nuclear power plants, chemical process plants, aviation, and offshore platforms). It quantitatively or qualitatively estimates the **Human Error Probability (HEP)** — the probability that a person will fail to perform a required task correctly within a specified time window.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition (NEP Aligned):** *HRA is the discipline that combines ergonomics, cognitive psychology, organizational behavior, and probabilistic modeling to identify, model, and quantify human actions (and their failure modes) that influence the safety and reliability of engineered systems. It serves as the human-centered counterpart to hardware reliability analysis in PRA.*

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of First-Generation HRA

Modern HRA methodologies are broadly classified into two generations. The methods discussed in this module belong to the **First Generation (1st Gen)** — these are predominantly **task-based / operator-action focused** and rely on expert judgment with bounded quantitative outputs.

| Method | Full Form | Origin | Year | Domain Origin |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| THERP | **T**echnique for **H**uman **E**rror **R**ate **P**rediction | NUREG/CR-1278 (Swain & Guttmann) | **1983** | Nuclear (Sandia National Lab) |
| HEART | **H**uman **E**rror **A**ssessment and **R**eduction **T**echnique | Jeremy C. Williams, UK | **1986** | Cross-Industry (C&I) |
| SPAR-H | **S**tandardized **P**lant **A**nalysis **R**isk - **H**uman Reliability Analysis | NUREG/CR-6883 (US NRC) | **2005** | Nuclear (USNRC) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pilot in a Storm" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy (First-Time Learner Friendly):**
> Imagine a pilot navigating an aircraft during a sudden storm. The aircraft has autopilot (hardware) and the pilot (human). When the autopilot fails, the pilot must manually land the plane. The **chance of pilot error** (HEP) depends on the situation — was the pilot well-rested? Was the cockpit lighting good? Was the emergency checklist clear?
>
> - **THERP** = A pilot's school that trains and grades every *micro-step* (e.g., "identify gauge", "flip switch 3") and assigns a probability to each. The total error is the **product** of these small steps.
> - **HEART** = A consultant who says: "Identify the *type* of task, then list what made it error-prone (EPCs) and apply a quick multiplying factor."
> - **SPAR-H** = A standardized checklist used by all pilots in the world — it asks 8 standardized questions (PSFs) like "Available time? Stress? Complexity?" — and uses a uniform table to compute error probability.

## 1.4 Why are HRA Methodologies Important for Safety Engineers?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Roughly **60–80% of industrial accidents** in major hazard industries have a contributory human component (operator error, maintenance lapse, design-induced mistake). Regulatory bodies (AERB India, USNRC, HSE UK) mandate HRA for license and safety case submissions. Therefore, the choice of HRA method directly affects the calculated risk, the plant's defense-in-depth design, and Human Factor Engineering (HFE) program scope.

---

## 1.5 Standardized HEP Reporting Metric

The industry standard for reporting human error magnitudes is:

- **HEP (dimensionless probability)**: $0 \leq HEP \leq 1$
- **Nominal Range**: Typical values lie between **$10^{-5}$** (highly skilled, well-supported task) and **$5 \times 10^{-1}$** (unrecoverable, unfamiliar task under severe stress).
- **HEP = 1.0** is a guaranteed failure (used as a bounding case in some methods).
- **HEP = 0.0** is a guaranteed success (rarely used; even routine tasks have a baseline).

> [!NOTE]
> HRA is the **bridge** between qualitative hazard analysis (HAZOP, What-If) and quantitative risk assessment (PSA Levels 1, 2, 3). A PSA without HRA is incomplete, as the human is the *last flexible barrier* before accident progression.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 THERP (Technique for Human Error Rate Prediction)

### 2.1.1 Theoretical Foundation

THERP was developed by **A. D. Swain and H. E. Guttmann** at Sandia National Laboratories and is the *de facto* reference HRA methodology worldwide. It forms the basis for many later methods, including HEART and SPAR-H. The procedure is fundamentally a **decomposition-and-recomposition** approach.

### 2.1.2 The Five-Step THERP Procedure

1. **Define the System and Identify Human Operations (Tasks)**
   - List the operator (or maintenance crew) actions that influence the system's safety.
   - Use HRA Event Trees or operator response timelines.

2. **Qualitative Task Analysis (Task Decomposition)**
   - Break each task into a sequence of sub-tasks.
   - Identify dependencies between sub-tasks (e.g., visual check → manual action).
   - Generate an **HRA Event Tree** (similar to a fault tree) showing success/failure branches.

3. **Quantitative Modeling**
   - For each sub-task, look up the **Basic Human Error Probability (BHEP)** from the THERP Handbook tables (these are tabulated for ~100 common task types).
   - **Typical BHEPs** (rounded, for reference):

| Task Type | Nominal BHEP |
| :--- | :--- |
| Omission (step skipped) | $1.0 \times 10^{-2}$ |
| Misread gauge / display | $3.0 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| Select wrong control (out of 4) | $5.0 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| Select wrong control (out of 11+) | $1.0 \times 10^{-2}$ |
| Inadvertent operation (push wrong button) | $3.0 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| Verbal slip (miscommunication) | $1.0 \times 10^{-2}$ |

4. **Apply Performance Shaping Factors (PSFs) — Human Reliability Curves**
   - A **BHEP** is valid only under *nominal* conditions.
   - Real conditions deviate → modify using PSF multipliers.
   - PSFs in THERP include: *stress level, experience, training, ergonomics, HMI quality, procedure quality, time-of-day, fitness-for-duty.*
   - The **PSF multiplier** is read from a logarithmic *Human Reliability Curve* (an S-shaped or monotonic curve plotted against the PSF magnitude).

5. **Compute the HEP and Assemble via the HRA Event Tree**

### 2.1.3 The Core THERP Equation

For a **task** consisting of $n$ independent sub-tasks with a **non-recovery model** (i.e., errors are not caught and corrected mid-task), the task HEP is:

$$
HEP_{task} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}\left(1 - HEP_i\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this form?** In probability theory, if sub-tasks are *independent*, the probability that **all** succeed is the **product of their individual success probabilities** $(1 - HEP_i)$. The task fails if **at least one** sub-task fails. By the complement rule, $P(\text{at least one failure}) = 1 - P(\text{all succeed})$.

If dependencies exist, **THERP's dependence model** is applied using six dependence levels: **ZD (Zero Dependence), LD (Low Dependence), MD (Moderate Dependence), HD (High Dependence), CD (Complete Dependence), and the special "Check-only" recovery node.** The conditional failure probability is computed as:

$$
HEP_{i+1 \mid i} = \frac{1 + 19 \cdot HEP_{i+1}}{20} \quad \text{(Low Dependence)}
$$

$$
HEP_{i+1 \mid i} = \frac{1 + 5 \cdot HEP_{i+1}}{6} \quad \text{(Moderate Dependence)}
$$

$$
HEP_{i+1 \mid i} = \frac{1 + HEP_{i+1}}{2} \quad \text{(High Dependence)}
$$

For **Complete Dependence (CD)**: $HEP_{i+1 \mid i} = HEP_{i+1}$ (no propagation benefit).

> [!TIP]
> **Kerala Board Exam Trick:** Examiners love testing the **Low Dependence** formula because it is the most counter-intuitive. Always state the dependence level explicitly before applying the formula.

---

## 2.2 HEART (Human Error Assessment and Reduction Technique)

### 2.2.1 Theoretical Foundation

Developed by **Dr. Jeremy C. Williams (1986, UK)**, HEART is a *generic, fast-track* HRA method. It was designed to be applicable across industries (rail, nuclear, process, military, medical) without the deep probabilistic machinery of THERP. The user does **not** decompose the task to a fine grain — instead, the task is classified into one of **9 Generic Task Types (GTTs)**.

### 2.2.2 The Two Core Constructs of HEART

1. **Generic Task Type (GTT)** — A category describing *what kind of cognitive or physical action* is being performed.
2. **Error Producing Condition (EPC)** — A *situational, environmental, procedural, or human* factor that is known to degrade human performance.

### 2.2.3 The Master HEART Equation

$$
\text{HEP} = G \times \prod_{i} (E_i \times W_i)
$$

Where:
- $G$ = Generic Task Type nominal unreliability (the *base* BHEP, ranging from $3 \times 10^{-4}$ to $1.0 \times 10^{-1}$).
- $E_i$ = *Assessed Proportion of Effect* of EPC $i$ (0.1 ≤ $E_i$ ≤ 1.0; 1.0 = full effect).
- $W_i$ = *Weighting* of EPC $i$ relative to its maximum effect (1 ≤ $W_i$ ≤ 8).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total HEP range in HEART:** Between **$3 \times 10^{-4}$** (perfectly supported routine task) and **$1.0$** (highly unfamiliar, no time, extreme stress — a bounding case). This gives HEART a very wide dynamic range, suitable for screening and bounding studies.

### 2.2.4 The 9 Generic Task Types (GTTs) — Memorize These for KTU

| GTT | Generic Task | Nominal $G$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| A | Totally unfamiliar, performed at speed with no real idea of consequences | $1.0 \times 10^{-1}$ |
| B | Shift or restore a system to a new or original state on a single attempt without supervision or procedures | $3.0 \times 10^{-2}$ |
| C | Complex task requiring high level of comprehension and skill | $9.0 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| D | Fairly simple task performed within a well-established procedure | $9.0 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| E | Routine, highly-practiced, rapid task involving relatively low level of skill | $9.0 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| F | Restore or shift a system following a routine procedure with some checking | $3.0 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| G | Completely familiar, well-designed, repetitive task, performed with little awareness | $3.0 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| H | Task that can be performed with negligible effort, often by a single response to a simple display | $9.0 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| M | Miscellaneous tasks not covered above (general) | $1.0 \times 10^{-2}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note the logarithmic spacing:** Each GTT differs by approximately one order of magnitude. This is the "scaling ladder" that allows rapid screening.

### 2.2.5 Top 8 EPCs Used in HEART (from 38 total)

| EPC | Description | Max Effect (E) | Max Weighting (W) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Unfamiliarity with situation | 0.1 | 8 |
| 2 | Time pressure | 0.5 | 6 |
| 3 | Low signal-to-noise ratio | 0.7 | 4 |
| 4 | Multi-shift handover | 0.7 | 4 |
| 5 | Distraction | 0.5 | 6 |
| 6 | Poor feedback / no indication | 0.5 | 5 |
| 7 | Mismatch between perceived and real risk | 0.5 | 4 |
| 8 | Inadequate procedures | 0.6 | 5 |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** HEART is the *most widely used method in the UK process industries* (e.g., COMAH sites) and is preferred over THERP for **screening HRA** and **ALARP demonstrations** because it can be applied in 1–2 hours per task.

---

## 2.3 SPAR-H (Standardized Plant Analysis Risk - Human Reliability Analysis)

### 2.3.1 Theoretical Foundation

Developed by the **U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC)** in **2005** (NUREG/CR-6883), SPAR-H was a direct response to inconsistencies between different HRA methods that produced wildly different HEPs (sometimes 2–3 orders of magnitude apart). The NRC needed a **uniform, repeatable, regulator-acceptable** method for use in their own Standardized Plant Analysis Risk (SPAR) models.

### 2.3.2 The SPAR-H Logic

SPAR-H is conceptually a hybrid of THERP and HEART. It uses **8 Performance Shaping Factors (PSFs)** as the primary modifiers, but unlike HEART, it uses a **fixed dependency model** and **fixed PSF multiplier tables** (i.e., no expert fitting of curves).

### 2.3.3 The SPAR-H Master Equation

$$
\text{HEP} = \text{NHEP} \times \prod_{j=1}^{8} PSF_j
$$

Where:
- $\text{NHEP}$ (Nominal HEP) = **$1.0 \times 10^{-2}$** for **diagnosis** actions and **$1.0 \times 10^{-3}$** for **action** (execution) actions.
- $PSF_j$ is the multiplier for the $j$-th PSF. If a PSF is assessed as *nominal*, its multiplier is **1.0**.

### 2.3.4 The 8 PSFs of SPAR-H

| # | PSF | Effect Categories | Multiplier Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Available Time** | Insufficient / Barely adequate / Nominal / Expansive | 100 / 10 / 1.0 / 0.5 |
| 2 | **Stress / Stressors** | Extreme / High / Nominal | 5 / 2 / 1.0 |
| 3 | **Experience and Training** | Low / Nominal / High | 3 / 1.0 / 0.5 |
| 4 | **Complexity** | Highly complex / Moderately complex / Nominal | 5 / 2 / 1.0 |
| 5 | **Ergonomics / HMI** | Missing / Poor / Nominal / Good | 50 / 10 / 1.0 / 0.5 |
| 6 | **Procedures** | Not available / Inadequate / Nominal / Available/Symbolic | 50 / 20 / 1.0 / 0.5 |
| 7 | **Fitness for Duty** | Unfit / Degraded fit / Nominal | 5 / 2 / 1.0 |
| 8 | **Work Processes** | Inefficient / Efficient / Nominal | 2 / 0.8 / 1.0 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Bounding Multipliers:** The *Available Time = Insufficient* multiplier is **100** (i.e., it increases HEP by two orders of magnitude). The *Procedures = Not Available* multiplier is **50**. Students frequently forget the order of magnitude — memorize **100, 50, 20, 10, 5, 3, 2, 0.5, 0.8** as the canonical set.

### 2.3.5 Dependence Model in SPAR-H

SPAR-H explicitly requires analysts to assess the **level of dependence** between successive operator actions, using a 4-level ordinal scale:

- **Zero Dependence (ZD)** — different crews, different times → $PSF_{dep} = 1.0$
- **Low Dependence (LD)** — different crews, same shift, related tasks
- **Moderate Dependence (MD)** — same crew, related tasks
- **High Dependence (HD)** — same crew, same location, related tasks
- **Complete Dependence (CD)** — same crew, same action, same place

$$
\text{HEP}_{combined} = \text{HEP}_1 + \text{HEP}_2 - \left(\text{HEP}_1 \times \text{HEP}_2\right)
$$

> [!TIP]
> The SPAR-H dependence calculation differs from THERP. SPAR-H uses an "OR-gate" combination at the event tree node, which simplifies to the formula above when both HEPs are small. THERP's dependence model is more granular (using $1+19x$/$20$ etc.).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Printable, High-Yield)

| # | Method | Master Equation | Key Output Range | Required Inputs |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **THERP** | $HEP = 1 - \prod(1 - HEP_i)$ | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-1}$ | BHEPs, PSF curves, Dependence level |
| 2 | **THERP Dependence (LD)** | $HEP_{i+1 \vert i} = (1 + 19 \cdot HEP_{i+1}) / 20$ | (modifier only) | Previous HEP, dep. level |
| 3 | **THERP Dependence (MD)** | $HEP_{i+1 \vert i} = (1 + 5 \cdot HEP_{i+1}) / 6$ | (modifier only) | Previous HEP, dep. level |
| 4 | **THERP Dependence (HD)** | $HEP_{i+1 \vert i} = (1 + HEP_{i+1}) / 2$ | (modifier only) | Previous HEP, dep. level |
| 5 | **HEART** | $HEP = G \times \prod (E_i \cdot W_i)$ | $3 \times 10^{-4}$ to $1.0$ | GTT (G), EPCs (E, W) |
| 6 | **SPAR-H** | $HEP = \text{NHEP} \times \prod PSF_j$ | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{0}$ | NHEP (0.01 diag / 0.001 act), 8 PSFs |
| 7 | **SPAR-H Dependence** | $HEP_{12} = H_1 + H_2 - H_1 H_2$ | (combined HEP) | Two HEPs |
| 8 | **Complement rule** | $P(\text{fail}) = 1 - P(\text{all pass})$ | 0 to 1 | Independent event probs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility Beyond Examination:** In real consulting practice, **SPAR-H is mandatory for US nuclear licensees**, **HEART is preferred for UK COMAH safety reports**, and **THERP is used in Indian AERB safety reviews (with some adaptations)**. Mastery of all three allows a safety engineer to work globally.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — THERP Application (HRA Event Tree)

**Scenario:** A control room operator must respond to a Loss of Coolant Accident (LOCA) signal. The response involves two sub-tasks: **(A)** Identify the LOCA on the annunciator panel, then **(B)** Manually start the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) pump. The procedures, training, and lighting are *nominal*. What is the HEP of failure to start ECCS within 10 minutes?

### Step-by-Step THERP Solution

**Step 1 — Task Decomposition and BHEP Look-up**

From the THERP Handbook:
- BHEP for *misread annunciator* (omission of correct identification) = **$3.0 \times 10^{-3}$**
- BHEP for *selecting and starting the correct pump* (out of 6 redundant controls) = **$5.0 \times 10^{-3}$**

**Step 2 — PSF Assessment**

All PSFs are *nominal* (procedures available, well-trained, good HMI, low stress in routine training conditions). Therefore, **PSF multiplier = 1.0** for both sub-tasks.

Thus:
$$
HEP_A = 1.0 \times 3.0 \times 10^{-3} = 3.0 \times 10^{-3}
$$
$$
HEP_B = 1.0 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-3} = 5.0 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 3 — Assess Dependence**

Both actions are performed by the **same crew, in the same control room, on the same accident sequence**, with high coupling (a misread annunciator likely leads to a wrong pump selection). The dependence is **High Dependence (HD)**.

Therefore, we first compute the *conditional* HEP for B given failure of A:
$$
HEP_{B \mid A} = \frac{1 + HEP_B}{2} = \frac{1 + 5.0 \times 10^{-3}}{2}
$$
$$
HEP_{B \mid A} = \frac{1.005}{2} = 0.5025
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Combined HEP**

$$
HEP_{total} = HEP_A + \left( HEP_{B \mid A} - HEP_A \right) \cdot HEP_A
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the THERP combination rule for a *sequence* of two sub-tasks with HD. We can also write it as the joint probability:
> $$P(\text{at least one fails}) = P(\text{A fails}) + P(\text{B fails} \mid \text{A succeeds}) \cdot P(\text{A succeeds})$$

Let us compute $P(\text{A succeeds}) = 1 - 0.003 = 0.997$.

$$
HEP_{total} = 0.003 + 0.5025 \times 0.997
$$
$$
HEP_{total} = 0.003 + 0.501 = 0.504
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **The result is 0.504, not 0.008!** This is the *key insight* of dependence: the HEP is dominated by the second event, not the product of the two. A student using the simple product $HEP_A \times HEP_B = 1.5 \times 10^{-5}$ would be **two orders of magnitude wrong** — and would lose full marks.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — HEART Application (Boiler Blowdown Valve)

**Scenario:** A process operator must manually close a steam boiler blowdown valve during an emergency. The task is *complex* (high pressure, hot, unfamiliar), time pressure is high (must act in <30 s), procedures are *inadequate* (drafted last year, not yet validated), and there is *low signal-to-noise* (no clear alarm distinguishing steam leak from process noise).

**Step 1 — Identify the Generic Task Type (GTT)**

The task matches **GTT C**: *Complex task requiring high level of comprehension and skill.*
$$
G = 9.0 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the Applicable EPCs**

| EPC # | Description | Effect ($E_i$) | Weight ($W_i$) | Contribution |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Unfamiliarity (operator has never done this) | 0.1 | 8 | 0.8 |
| 2 | Time pressure (severe, <30 s) | 0.5 | 6 | 3.0 |
| 3 | Low signal-to-noise | 0.7 | 4 | 2.8 |
| 6 | Poor feedback | 0.5 | 5 | 2.5 |
| 8 | Inadequate procedures | 0.6 | 5 | 3.0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Mistake:** The term "EPC value" in HEART is **not** the multiplier itself — it is the **product** $E_i \times W_i$. Examiners will mark you wrong if you forget to multiply.

**Step 3 — Apply the HEART Master Equation**

$$
HEP = G \times \prod_{i} (E_i \times W_i)
$$

Let us compute the product of EPC values step by step:

$$
P_1 = 0.1 \times 8 = 0.8
$$
$$
P_2 = 0.5 \times 6 = 3.0
$$
$$
P_3 = 0.7 \times 4 = 2.8
$$
$$
P_4 = 0.5 \times 5 = 2.5
$$
$$
P_5 = 0.6 \times 5 = 3.0
$$

Now the combined product:
$$
P_{total} = 0.8 \times 3.0 \times 2.8 \times 2.5 \times 3.0
$$

We evaluate this step by step:
$$
0.8 \times 3.0 = 2.4
$$
$$
2.4 \times 2.8 = 6.72
$$
$$
6.72 \times 2.5 = 16.80
$$
$$
16.80 \times 3.0 = 50.40
$$

Therefore:
$$
HEP = 9.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 50.40 = 0.4536
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **The HEP exceeds 0.5 — i.e., > 50% failure probability.** HEART explicitly allows HEPs > 1.0 in principle, but the analyst should treat any HEP > 0.5 as *catastrophic task design* and recommend procedural/engineering redesign *first* (a HEART "ALARP" output) before adding more barriers.

**Step 4 — Engineering Recommendation**

A HEEP of 0.45 indicates the task is not ALARP (As Low As Reasonably Practicable) under UK HSE standards. The recommendation is to (a) install a remote-operated valve, (b) install a dedicated high-fidelity alarm, and (c) rewrite and validate the procedure.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — SPAR-H Application (Fukushima-style Station Blackout)

**Scenario:** A reactor operator must restore DC power from the battery bank during a station blackout. The available time is "barely adequate" (about 5 minutes), the procedure is "available" (paper copy is at the panel), the ergonomics are "nominal", training is "nominal", complexity is "moderately complex", work processes are "nominal", and fitness for duty is "nominal". The action is the *execution* phase (not diagnosis).

**Step 1 — Identify the NHEP**

The operator has *already diagnosed* the problem (the SBO alarm has identified the loss). The action being analyzed is the **action (execution)** of restoring power. Therefore:
$$
\text{NHEP}_{action} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 2 — Read the 8 PSF Multipliers**

| # | PSF | Assessed Level | Multiplier |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Available Time | Barely adequate | **10** |
| 2 | Stress | High (SBO implies loss of all AC) | **2** |
| 3 | Experience and Training | Nominal | **1.0** |
| 4 | Complexity | Moderately complex | **2** |
| 5 | Ergonomics | Nominal | **1.0** |
| 6 | Procedures | Available | **0.5** |
| 7 | Fitness for Duty | Nominal | **1.0** |
| 8 | Work Processes | Nominal | **1.0** |

**Step 3 — Apply the SPAR-H Master Equation**

$$
HEP = \text{NHEP} \times PSF_1 \times PSF_2 \times PSF_3 \times PSF_4 \times PSF_5 \times PSF_6 \times PSF_7 \times PSF_8
$$

Substituting:
$$
HEP = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 10 \times 2 \times 1.0 \times 2 \times 1.0 \times 0.5 \times 1.0 \times 1.0
$$

Computing the product of PSF multipliers step by step:
$$
10 \times 2 = 20
$$
$$
20 \times 1.0 = 20
$$
$$
20 \times 2 = 40
$$
$$
40 \times 1.0 = 40
$$
$$
40 \times 0.5 = 20
$$
$$
20 \times 1.0 = 20
$$
$$
20 \times 1.0 = 20
$$

Therefore:
$$
HEP = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 20 = 2.0 \times 10^{-2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The operator has a 2% chance of failing to restore DC power in time — a non-negligible contribution to the station blackout scenario. The 10× and 2× multipliers together contribute a 20× penalty, reflecting the *insufficient time* and *high stress* typical of SBO conditions. SPAR-H's value here is its **consistency**: every NRC reviewer will arrive at the same 2.0 × 10⁻².

---

## 3.4 Cross-Method Comparison — Same Task, Three Methods

Let us apply all three methods to the *same* task to highlight the *methodological spread* in HRA — a classic KTU 2024 conceptual question.

**Task:** An operator must manually close a firewater isolation valve during a hydrocarbon leak. Standard conditions, trained, good procedure, no time pressure.

- **THERP Estimate:** $HEP_{THERP} \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ (using single-step model)
- **HEART Estimate:** $HEP_{HEART} \approx 9.0 \times 10^{-5}$ (GTT E, no EPCs)
- **SPAR-H Estimate:** $HEP_{SPAR-H} \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ (NHEP × all PSFs = 1.0)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Teaching Point:** HEART can be optimistic when the analyst is conservative with EPCs; THERP and SPAR-H tend to be more aligned in routine cases. The "factor of 10" spread is a well-known criticism of first-generation HRA, motivating the development of **second-generation HRA** (ATHEANA, CREAM, MERMOS) which are cognitive-based rather than task-based.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Generic HRA Methodology Flow

The diagram below shows the **block-level functional architecture** of how each of the three HRA methods maps a task to a final HEP. This is the *process flow* an analyst follows.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Input: Operator Task Description] --> A1{Task Complexity?}
    A1 -->|Simple / Routine| A2[Select Method: HEART]
    A1 -->|Complex Decomposable| A3[Select Method: THERP]
    A1 -->|Standardized / Regulatory| A4[Select Method: SPAR-H]
    A2 --> B1[Choose Generic Task Type GTT and base G]
    A3 --> B2[Decompose task into HRA Event Tree]
    A4 --> B3[Identify NHEP based on Diagnosis or Action]
    B1 --> C1[List EPCs with E and W values]
    B2 --> C2[Look up BHEPs from THERP table]
    C2 --> D1[Assess Dependence Level]
    D1 --> C3[Apply THERP dependence model]
    B3 --> C4[Score the 8 PSFs]
    C4 --> C5[Read PSF multiplier table]
    C1 --> E1[Compute HEP via G times product of E times W]
    C3 --> E2[Compute HEP via 1 minus product of 1 minus HEP_i]
    C5 --> E3[Compute HEP via NHEP times product of PSFs]
    E1 --> F1[Output: Final HEP and ALARP discussion]
    E2 --> F1
    E3 --> F1
    F1 --> G0[Document assumptions, uncertainties, recommendations]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Architecture — The Three Methods Side by Side

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph THERPLayer[THERP Architecture]
        T1[System Definition] --> T2[Task Decomposition]
        T2 --> T3[BHEP Look-up]
        T3 --> T4[PSF Curve Application]
        T4 --> T5[HRA Event Tree Assembly]
        T5 --> T6[THERP HEP]
    end
    subgraph HEARTLayer[HEART Architecture]
        H1[Task Description] --> H2[GTT Selection]
        H2 --> H3[EPC Identification]
        H3 --> H4[Effect and Weight Assignment]
        H4 --> H5[Master Equation G times product]
        H5 --> H6[HEART HEP]
    end
    subgraph SPARHLayer[SPAR-H Architecture]
        S1[Task Description] --> S2{Diagnosis or Action?}
        S2 -->|Diagnosis| S3A[NHEP equals 0.01]
        S2 -->|Action| S3B[NHEP equals 0.001]
        S3A --> S4[Score 8 PSFs]
        S3B --> S4
        S4 --> S5[Read Multiplier Table]
        S5 --> S6[Assess Dependence Level]
        S6 --> S7[SPAR-H HEP]
    end
    THERPLayer --> OUT[Final HEP Output for PSA]
    HEARTLayer --> OUT
    SPARHLayer --> OUT
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Decision Tree for Choosing an HRA Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q0[Start: New HRA Task] --> Q1{Is there a regulatory mandate?}
    Q1 -->|Yes, US NRC| Q1A[Use SPAR-H]
    Q1 -->|Yes, UK HSE COMAH| Q1B[Use HEART]
    Q1 -->|Yes, India AERB| Q1C[Use THERP as default]
    Q1 -->|No, screening study| Q1D[Use HEART for speed]
    Q1A --> Q2{Is the task highly decomposable?}
    Q1B --> Q2
    Q1C --> Q2
    Q1D --> Q2
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q2A[Use THERP for granularity]
    Q2 -->|No| Q2B[Use HEART or SPAR-H for bounding]
    Q2A --> Q3[Apply 5-Step Procedure]
    Q2B --> Q4[Apply 1-Step Screening Equation]
    Q3 --> Q5[Document HEP and uncertainty]
    Q4 --> Q5
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Dependence Levels Visualized (THERP)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Sub-task A] --> A1[Sub-task B]
    A0 --> A2{Dependence Level?}
    A2 -->|Zero| D0[HEP B independent]
    A2 -->|Low| D1[HEP B equals 1 plus 19 times HEP B over 20]
    A2 -->|Moderate| D2[HEP B equals 1 plus 5 times HEP B over 6]
    A2 -->|High| D3[HEP B equals 1 plus HEP B over 2]
    A2 -->|Complete| D4[HEP B equals HEP B]
    D0 --> F0[Combined HEP]
    D1 --> F0
    D2 --> F0
    D3 --> F0
    D4 --> F0
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each "Dependence Level" corresponds to an exact algebraic formula from Section 2.1.3. Note that as dependence increases, the conditional HEP approaches 0.5 (i.e., a coin flip), which is the *fundamental human limit* when prior failure has "primed" the operator to fail again.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination (ESE)** pattern: Part A short-answer (3 marks), Part B long-answer with internal choice (14 marks), mapped to **CO-4** and **Bloom's Levels L2–L5**.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Human Error Probability (HEP). [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Human Error Probability (HEP) is a dimensionless numerical estimate of the likelihood that a person (or team of persons) will fail to perform a specified task or sub-task correctly within a defined time window and under defined conditions. Mathematically, $0 \leq HEP \leq 1$, with typical industrial values lying between $10^{-5}$ (highly skilled, well-supported, trained task) and $5 \times 10^{-1}$ (unfamiliar, stressed task). HEP is the *primary output* of any HRA methodology and is used as a parameter in PSA / PRA event tree quantification.

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Allocation:** Definition (1), Range (1), Purpose (1). Always quote the range numerically to get full marks.

### Question 2: List the **8 PSFs** of SPAR-H. [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The 8 Performance Shaping Factors (PSFs) of SPAR-H are:

1. Available Time
2. Stress / Stressors
3. Experience and Training
4. Complexity
5. Ergonomics / HMI
6. Procedures
7. Fitness for Duty
8. Work Processes

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Loss of Marks:** Students write "HMI quality" instead of "Ergonomics / HMI" — both are accepted, but be precise.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question Choice Set (Module 4)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **THERP methodology** for Human Reliability Analysis. List the **5 procedural steps** and clearly write the **dependence-level equations** for Low, Moderate, and High Dependence. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A control room operator must perform two independent sub-tasks A and B in response to a fire alarm. BHEP of A is **$4.0 \times 10^{-3}$** and BHEP of B is **$6.0 \times 10^{-3}$**. The dependence is **Low Dependence (LD)**. PSF multiplier for the entire task (nominal conditions) is 1.0. Compute the **Total HEP** of failure of the operator response. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**Part (a) Solution — THERP Methodology**

THERP (Technique for Human Error Rate Prediction) was developed by Swain and Guttmann (1983) at Sandia National Laboratories under NUREG/CR-1278. It is the most widely used first-generation HRA method. The five procedural steps are:

1. **System Definition and Task Identification:** Identify the human actions that affect system safety (typically in accident sequences from a PSA Level 1 event tree).
2. **Qualitative Task Analysis (Decomposition):** Break each operator action into a logical sequence of sub-tasks. Represent these as an **HRA Event Tree** with success/failure branches.
3. **BHEP Quantification:** Look up the **Basic Human Error Probability (BHEP)** for each sub-task from the THERP Handbook (which tabulates ~100 nominal HEPs for common tasks like "select switch", "read gauge", "push button", "omit step").
4. **PSF Modification:** Adjust each BHEP using **Performance Shaping Factor (PSF) multipliers** read from **Human Reliability Curves** (S-shaped curves relating PSF magnitude to performance).
5. **Assembly via HRA Event Tree:** Combine the modified sub-task HEPs into the overall task HEP, accounting for dependence.

**Dependence-Level Equations (memorize these for full marks):**

$$
HEP_{i+1 \mid i}^{LD} = \frac{1 + 19 \cdot HEP_{i+1}}{20}
$$

$$
HEP_{i+1 \mid i}^{MD} = \frac{1 + 5 \cdot HEP_{i+1}}{6}
$$

$$
HEP_{i+1 \mid i}^{HD} = \frac{1 + HEP_{i+1}}{2}
$$

For **Complete Dependence (CD)**: $HEP_{i+1 \mid i} = HEP_{i+1}$ (no propagation, simple pass-through).

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Key Insight:** The LD, MD, HD coefficients (1/20, 1/6, 1/2) follow a clean pattern: as dependence increases, more "weight" is placed on the prior failure. The pattern is sometimes remembered as "**Low 1/20, Moderate 1/6, High 1/2, Complete 1/1**".

---

**Part (b) Solution — HEP Computation with Low Dependence**

Given:
- BHEP of A: $4.0 \times 10^{-3}$
- BHEP of B: $6.0 \times 10^{-3}$
- PSF multiplier (entire task): 1.0
- Dependence: Low Dependence (LD)

**Step 1 — Apply PSF multiplier** (no change since multiplier = 1.0):
$$
HEP_A = 4.0 \times 10^{-3}
$$
$$
HEP_B = 6.0 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Conditional HEP of B given A failed (LD formula):**
$$
HEP_{B \mid A}^{LD} = \frac{1 + 19 \times HEP_B}{20}
$$
$$
HEP_{B \mid A}^{LD} = \frac{1 + 19 \times 6.0 \times 10^{-3}}{20}
$$
$$
HEP_{B \mid A}^{LD} = \frac{1 + 0.114}{20} = \frac{1.114}{20} = 0.0557
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Combined HEP using the THERP joint-probability formula:**
$$
HEP_{total} = HEP_A + HEP_{B \mid A}^{LD} \times (1 - HEP_A)
$$
$$
HEP_{total} = 0.004 + 0.0557 \times (1 - 0.004)
$$
$$
HEP_{total} = 0.004 + 0.0557 \times 0.996
$$
$$
HEP_{total} = 0.004 + 0.0555
$$
$$
HEP_{total} = 0.0595 \approx 5.95 \times 10^{-2}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students compute $HEP_{B \mid A}$ correctly but then *forget to multiply by the success probability of A* $(1 - HEP_A)$, leading to a wrong answer of $0.0597$. The full joint-probability formulation is mandatory. The correct answer is approximately **$5.95 \times 10^{-2}$**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **HEART methodology** for HRA. List the **9 Generic Task Types (GTTs)** with their nominal unreliability values $G$, and state the master equation. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A process operator must manually open a pressure relief valve during an emergency. The task is *complex* (GTT C), the operator is *unfamiliar* with the situation (EPC 1, effect = 0.1, weight = 8), there is *severe time pressure* (EPC 2, effect = 0.5, weight = 6), and the *procedures are inadequate* (EPC 8, effect = 0.6, weight = 5). Compute the HEP using the HEART master equation. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**Part (a) Solution — HEART Methodology**

HEART (Human Error Assessment and Reduction Technique) was developed by **Dr. Jeremy C. Williams in 1986** as a fast, generic, cross-industry HRA method. Unlike THERP, HEART does *not* decompose the task into sub-tasks. Instead, it classifies the task into one of **9 Generic Task Types (GTTs)** and applies the effect of **Error Producing Conditions (EPCs)**.

**The 9 Generic Task Types and their nominal $G$ values:**

| GTT | Description | $G$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| A | Totally unfamiliar, performed at speed, no idea of consequences | $1.0 \times 10^{-1}$ |
| B | Shift/restore system to a new state on a single attempt, no supervision | $3.0 \times 10^{-2}$ |
| C | Complex task requiring high comprehension and skill | $9.0 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| D | Fairly simple task, well-established procedure | $9.0 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| E | Routine, highly-practiced, rapid, low skill | $9.0 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| F | Restore/shift system following routine procedure with checking | $3.0 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| G | Completely familiar, repetitive, low awareness | $3.0 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| H | Negligible effort, single response to a simple display | $9.0 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| M | Miscellaneous (general) | $1.0 \times 10^{-2}$ |

**The HEART Master Equation:**

$$
HEP = G \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} (E_i \times W_i)
$$

Where $E_i$ is the *Assessed Proportion of Effect* of EPC $i$ (0.1 to 1.0) and $W_i$ is the *Weighting* of EPC $i$ (1 to 8). The product can yield HEPs ranging from $3 \times 10^{-4}$ (nominal, no EPCs, GTT G) to 1.0 (worst-case, GTT A with multiple full-effect EPCs).

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Allocation:** Name + author + year (1), 9 GTTs (3), equation (1), explanation of $E$ and $W$ (1), full procedure (1).

---

**Part (b) Solution — HEART HEP Computation**

Given:
- GTT C, so $G = 9.0 \times 10^{-3}$
- EPC 1: $E_1 = 0.1$, $W_1 = 8$
- EPC 2: $E_2 = 0.5$, $W_2 = 6$
- EPC 8: $E_3 = 0.6$, $W_3 = 5$

**Step 1 — Compute the EPC products:**

$$
E_1 \times W_1 = 0.1 \times 8 = 0.8
$$
$$
E_2 \times W_2 = 0.5 \times 6 = 3.0
$$
$$
E_3 \times W_3 = 0.6 \times 5 = 3.0
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the product of EPC values:**

$$
P_{EPC} = 0.8 \times 3.0 \times 3.0
$$
$$
P_{EPC} = 0.8 \times 9.0 = 7.2
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the HEART master equation:**

$$
HEP = G \times P_{EPC} = 9.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 7.2
$$
$$
HEP = 0.0648 \approx 6.48 \times 10^{-2}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common error is to **forget to multiply $E$ by $W$** and instead use $E$ alone. This would yield a very different (and wrong) result of $HEP = 9 \times 10^{-3} \times (0.1 \times 0.5 \times 0.6) = 2.7 \times 10^{-4}$ — a 240× error. Always multiply.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this checklist the night before the KTU exam.**

### ✅ Conceptual Foundation
- **HRA = Human Reliability Analysis**, the human-centered part of PSA / PRA.
- **HEP = Human Error Probability**, the primary output (dimensionless, 0 to 1).
- **First-Generation HRA methods** are task-based; **Second-Generation** (ATHEANA, CREAM, MERMOS) are cognitive-based.
- **3 First-Gen Methods:** THERP (1983, NUREG/CR-1278), HEART (1986, Williams), SPAR-H (2005, NUREG/CR-6883).

### ✅ THERP — Mastery Points
- **5 Steps:** Define → Decompose → BHEP → PSF → Assemble.
- **Master equation:** $HEP = 1 - \prod (1 - HEP_i)$.
- **Dependence formulas:** LD: $1 + 19x / 20$, MD: $1 + 5x / 6$, HD: $1 + x / 2$, CD: $x$.
- **BHEP table** is lookup-driven from the Swain & Guttmann handbook.
- **PSF curves** are S-shaped or monotonic; multipliers can be > 1 or < 1.

### ✅ HEART — Mastery Points
- **9 Generic Task Types (GTTs)** span from $9 \times 10^{-5}$ (GTT E, H) to $1.0 \times 10^{-1}$ (GTT A).
- **38 EPCs**, but only top ~8 are used commonly.
- **Master equation:** $HEP = G \times \prod (E_i \times W_i)$.
- **Always multiply $E$ by $W$** — this is the #1 student error.
- **Best for:** ALARP demonstration, screening, fast-track studies.

### ✅ SPAR-H — Mastery Points
- **NHEP** = $1 \times 10^{-2}$ for diagnosis, $1 \times 10^{-3}$ for action.
- **8 PSFs:** Time, Stress, Experience, Complexity, Ergonomics, Procedures, Fitness, Work Processes.
- **Bounding multipliers:** 100 (Time-Insufficient), 50 (Procedures-Missing or Ergonomics-Missing).
- **Dependence model:** 4 ordinal levels (ZD, LD, MD, HD); CD reserved for same-action-same-place.
- **Master equation:** $HEP = \text{NHEP} \times \prod PSF_j$.
- **Best for:** US NRC-mandated standardized HRA; reproducible regulator-acceptable results.

### ✅ Method-Selection Decision Rules
- **Regulatory mandate (US NRC)** → **SPAR-H**.
- **Regulatory mandate (UK COMAH)** → **HEART**.
- **Regulatory mandate (India AERB)** → **THERP** (with adaptations).
- **Decomposable complex task** → **THERP** (granular).
- **Screening / bounding / quick estimate** → **HEART** (fast).
- **Cognitive / strategy-level error analysis** → **Second-Gen** (CREAM, ATHEANA) — covered in higher semesters.

### ✅ Common Numerical Pitfalls
- **Using $HEP_A \times HEP_B$ instead of dependence-modified formula** → off by 2–3 orders of magnitude.
- **Forgetting the $(1 - HEP_A)$ factor** when combining dependent events.
- **Using $E$ alone in HEART** → 100× to 1000× errors.
- **Confusing SPAR-H's NHEP** ($10^{-2}$ for diagnosis vs $10^{-3}$ for action).
- **Applying PSF multipliers when PSF is nominal** (multiplier is 1.0, so no change — students sometimes "double-apply").

### ✅ Exam-Style Quick Identification
- If a question mentions "**decomposition**", "**event tree**", "**BHEP**" → **THERP**.
- If a question mentions "**GTT**", "**EPC**", "**proportion of effect**" → **HEART**.
- If a question mentions "**8 PSFs**", "**NHEP**", "**diagnosis vs action**" → **SPAR-H**.

### ✅ Final Verdict
- **THERP** = most rigorous, most data, most time-consuming.
- **HEART** = most practical, fastest, best for screening.
- **SPAR-H** = most standardized, regulator-friendly, best for large-scale PSA.
- **All three** are **first-generation** HRA — they share the same limitation: they are excellent at **slips/lapses/mistakes** but weak at **strategic/cognitive errors** (which is the domain of CREAM, ATHEANA, MERMOS).

> [!WARNING]
> **Last-Mark Saver:** In any HRA numerical problem, **always state the dependence level explicitly** and **show every algebraic step**. Examiners award partial credit generously for clear working — and penalize "skip-to-answer" approaches severely.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application of HRA in Complex Systems and High-Risk Industries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT524 - HUMAN AND ORGANIZATIONAL FACTORS IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Application of HRA in Complex Systems and High-Risk Industries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Human Reliability Analysis (HRA)** is a structured, systematic, and disciplined engineering discipline that employs qualitative and quantitative methods to evaluate the probability of human error in complex socio-technical systems. In the context of high-risk industries, HRA is used to model human performance during normal operations, abnormal conditions, and emergency scenarios, providing a numerical or categorical estimate of Human Error Probability (HEP) and Human Error Rate (HER).

When applied to **Complex Systems** (e.g., nuclear power plants, chemical refineries, aerospace operations, offshore drilling, and advanced manufacturing), HRA integrates the analysis of human tasks with the technical, organizational, and environmental factors that influence operator behavior.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** HRA is defined as the application of relevant methods, models, and theories to make a quantitative or qualitative estimation of the probability of human error in a system, with the goal of reducing that error probability through improved human-system interface design, training, procedures, and organizational factors.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car on a foggy mountain road at night. The car is the **complex system**, the road is the **process**, and you (the driver) are the **human operator**. Human Reliability Analysis is like a safety engineer sitting with you and asking: "What is the chance that you will miss a sharp turn? What is the chance that you will confuse the brake with the accelerator under panic?"

Now, scale that up. Instead of a car on a mountain, imagine a nuclear reactor operator during a Loss of Coolant Accident (LOCA). The "mountain road" is now a 3,000-psi pressurized system, and the "mistake" can lead to a meltdown. HRA provides the structured thinking to estimate these probabilities and, more importantly, to redesign the steering wheel, brakes, dashboard, and even the road signs to make it almost impossible for the driver to fail.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Explanation:** HRA is essentially "error-proofing" people in high-stakes jobs, just like engineers error-proof (poka-yoke) machines in a factory. It is the science of understanding *why* humans make mistakes in complex systems and *how* we can design the system, training, and procedures to prevent those mistakes.

### 1.3 Core Metrics and Standard Units

The following standard metrics are universally used in HRA applications:

- **Human Error Probability (HEP):** Expressed as a dimensionless decimal between **0** (impossible error) and **1** (certain error). Often expressed as a probability range.
- **Human Error Rate (HER):** Number of errors per unit of task execution (e.g., errors per million operations, **E-06** scale).
- **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD):** Probability that a safety system fails to perform its function when required. Calculated as PFD = λ × T/2 for periodic tests, where λ is failure rate and T is test interval.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 PESFT524 module specifically emphasizes the *application* side of HRA — meaning students must understand not just *what* HRA is, but *where* and *how* it is deployed in industries like nuclear, petrochemical, aviation, and military operations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why HRA is Essential in Complex Systems and High-Risk Industries

Complex systems share four critical characteristics that make HRA indispensable:

1. **Tight Coupling:** Components and humans are tightly linked; one action immediately affects the next stage. (Example: Air traffic control, nuclear reactor control room.)
2. **High-Energy/Toxic Inventories:** The system stores massive energy (nuclear, chemical, hydraulic) that can cause catastrophic damage.
3. **Multiple Interacting Defenses:** Modern industries use the "Swiss Cheese Model" — multiple layers of protection where humans are the last line of defense.
4. **Time-Critical Decision Making:** Operators must respond within seconds or minutes to prevent escalation.

### 2.2 The Hierarchy of HRA Application

HRA is applied at three nested levels within high-risk industries:

- **Level 1 — Direct Human-Machine Interface:** Operator actions, control panel design, alarm response, procedure following.
- **Level 2 — Task and Crew Performance:** Crew Resource Management (CRM), team coordination, communication, supervision.
- **Level 3 — Organizational and Management Factors:** Safety culture, training programs, management oversight, regulatory compliance, shift scheduling.

### 2.3 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind HRA Application

**Why HRA?**
- Industry accident statistics (e.g., the **Three Mile Island** 1979, **Bhopal** 1984, **Chernobyl** 1986, **Columbia** 2003) consistently show that **70%–80% of industrial accidents** involve human error as either a root cause or a contributing factor.
- Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA) in regulated industries (nuclear, oil & gas) requires numerical estimates of human reliability to compute overall plant risk.
- HRA is mandated by regulators such as the **U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC)**, the **U.K. Health and Safety Executive (HSE)**, and the **Chemical Industry Safety and Health Council (CISH)**.

**How is HRA applied?**
- **Qualitative HRA:** Task analysis, hazard identification, and scenario walk-throughs to identify potential human errors.
- **Quantitative HRA:** Mathematical modeling using techniques like **THERP** (Technique for Human Error Rate and Probability Analysis), **HEART** (Human Error Assessment and Reduction Technique), **SLIM-MAUD** (Success Likelihood Index Methodology — Multi-Attribute Utility Decomposition), and **ATHEANA** (A Technique for Human Event Analysis).
- **Integrated HRA:** Combines both, often paired with Probabilistic Safety Assessment (PSA) or Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Swiss Cheese Model (Reason, 1990):** Every layer of defense (engineering, administrative, procedural, human) has holes. HRA identifies *where* the human holes are and *how big* they are, so that the holes in different layers don't align to cause an accident.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Description | Typical Range / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Human Error Probability (HEP) | $HEP = \dfrac{E}{N}$ | Ratio of errors (E) to opportunities (N) | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-1}$ |
| Probability of Failure on Demand | $PFD = \lambda \cdot \dfrac{T}{2}$ | For periodically tested system; λ = failure rate, T = test interval | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-2}$ |
| Mean Time to Failure (MTTF) | $MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Average time before failure | Hours / Years |
| HEART Multiplier Method | $HEP = G \cdot \prod_{i} HEP_i$ | G = generic task probability; HEP_i = Error-Producing Condition multipliers | Dimensionless |
| ATHEANA Conditional Probability | $HEP = P(\text{Recovery Failure} \vert \text{Error}) \cdot P(\text{Error})$ | Conditional error propagation | $0$ to $1$ |
| CREAM (Cognitive Reliability Method) | $\text{CFC} = f(\text{Performance Conditions})$ | Cognitive Failure Probability derived from 9 Common Performance Conditions | $10^{-5}$ to $1$ |
| Human Error Rate (THERP) | $HER = \dfrac{\text{Errors}}{\text{Time or Operations}}$ | Empirical error rate from THERP tables | Errors per million |
| Bounded Interval Estimate | $HEP_{low} \leq HEP \leq HEP_{high}$ | Conservative lower and upper bounds | Logarithmic scale |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas feed directly into **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** and **Event Tree Analysis (ETA)**, which are the backbone of Probabilistic Safety Assessment (PSA) used in nuclear, petrochemical, and aerospace safety cases. Without HEP values, you cannot compute the top-event probability of a complex industrial accident.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: HEP Calculation Using HEART Method

**Problem Statement:**
A control room operator in a petroleum refinery must manually close a critical valve (Emergency Shutdown Valve — ESDV) within 30 seconds of receiving a high-high alarm. The task is described as a "Complex task requiring a high level of comprehension and skill." There are three Error-Producing Conditions (EPCs):
- EPC-1: A shortage of time available for error detection and correction (HEP multiplier = **11**)
- EPC-2: A mismatch between an operator's mental model and the actual system state (HEP multiplier = **8**)
- EPC-3: Poor feedback of system state after the action (HEP multiplier = **4**)

**Step 1: Identify the Generic Task Type**

According to the HEART taxonomy, this task fits Category B: *"Tasks which are fairly simple, well practised and have well-defined stimuli and adequate feedback."* However, the presence of the three EPCs and the time pressure effectively reduce it to a more demanding cognitive category. We will adopt the nominal value for the **upper-bound** generic probability.

For Category B tasks, the **nominal HEP (G)** is:

$$
G = 0.02 \quad (\text{or } 2 \times 10^{-2})
$$

**Step 2: Identify and Apply the EPC Multipliers**

The HEART formula for the assessed HEP is:

$$
HEP = G \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} (HEP_i)
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
HEP = 0.02 \times 11 \times 8 \times 4
$$

**Step 3: Perform the Multiplication Step-by-Step**

$$
HEP = 0.02 \times 11 = 0.22
$$

$$
HEP = 0.22 \times 8 = 1.76
$$

$$
HEP = 1.76 \times 4 = 7.04
$$

**Step 4: Apply the Cap**

HEART methodology specifies that the HEP cannot exceed the maximum plausible value of **1.0** (a probability greater than 1 is physically meaningless). Therefore:

$$
HEP_{final} = \min(7.04, 1.0) = 1.0
$$

**Step 5: Interpret the Result**

$$
\boxed{HEP = 1.0}
$$

This means the operator is **certain to fail** under these conditions. The conclusion is that the system as currently designed (alarm → 30 seconds → close valve) is **unreliable**. The HRA recommends redesigning the system: either installing an automatic Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) with a Safety Integrity Level (SIL) rating, or simplifying the operator interface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A HEP of 1.0 from a single HEART calculation is a clear red flag. In real-world practice, the HRA analyst would either (a) decompose the task further into sub-tasks, (b) increase the number of barriers (defense-in-depth), or (c) replace the manual action with an automatic one. This is exactly how HRA drives engineering decisions.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: HRA Method Selection by Industry

| Industry | Primary HRA Method | Reason for Selection | Typical HEP Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Nuclear Power** | THERP + ATHEANA | Regulator (NRC) requires quantitative HEP; long operational history with empirical data | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-2}$ |
| **Petrochemical / Oil & Gas** | HEART + LOPA | SIL determination for IEC 61511; time-critical ESD actions | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{0}$ |
| **Aviation** | CREAM + Human Factors Analysis | Cognitive demands, CRM, air traffic control | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$ |
| **Military / Defense** | HEART + ATHEANA | High-consequence weapons systems, command-and-control | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$ |
| **Medical / Healthcare** | CREAM + SHERPA | Surgical and ICU errors, medication administration | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$ |
| **Rail Transportation** | HEART + THERP | Signalling, dispatch, train operator error | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$ |
| **Offshore Drilling** | HEART + SLIM | Blowout prevention, dynamic positioning | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{0}$ |

### 3.3 Mathematical Relationship: HRA + System Reliability

In a complex system with **n** independent protection layers, the total Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD) is the sum of contributions from each layer:

$$
PFD_{total} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} PFD_j
$$

For a system with one human layer (HEP) and two engineered barriers with failure rates $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$ tested at intervals $T_1$ and $T_2$:

$$
PFD_{total} = HEP + \frac{\lambda_1 T_1}{2} + \frac{\lambda_2 T_2}{2}
$$

**Numerical Example:**
Suppose $HEP = 0.05$, $\lambda_1 = 10^{-3}$/year, $T_1 = 1$ year, $\lambda_2 = 5 \times 10^{-4}$/year, $T_2 = 2$ years.

$$
PFD_{total} = 0.05 + \frac{(10^{-3})(1)}{2} + \frac{(5 \times 10^{-4})(2)}{2}
$$

$$
PFD_{total} = 0.05 + 0.0005 + 0.0005 = 0.051
$$

$$
\boxed{PFD_{total} = 5.1 \times 10^{-2}}
$$

This result indicates the system has approximately a **5.1%** chance of failing when demanded — typically unacceptable in a SIL-3 application, which would require $PFD \leq 10^{-3}$. The conclusion: **the human layer dominates the unreliability**, justifying investment in automation or improved procedures.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram: HRA Application Flow in High-Risk Industries

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Complex System Scope] --> B[Conduct Task Analysis]
    B --> C[Identify Human Error Modes]
    C --> D[Select Appropriate HRA Method]
    D --> D1[THERP]
    D --> D2[HEART]
    D --> D3[CREAM]
    D --> D4[ATHEANA]
    D --> D5[SLIM MAUD]
    D1 --> E[Calculate HEP Values]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    E --> F[Integrate HEP into PSA LOPA or FTA]
    F --> G[Compare with Target Risk Criteria]
    G --> H{Risk Acceptable?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Document and Proceed]
    H -->|No| J[Identify Risk Reduction Measures]
    J --> K[Redesign Human Machine Interface]
    J --> L[Improve Procedures and Training]
    J --> M[Add Automation and Barriers]
    K --> N[Re run HRA Cycle]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> G
```

### 4.2 Diagram: Swiss Cheese Model with HRA Layers

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    L2[Layer 2: Administrative Controls]:::layer

    L4[Layer 4: Human Operator]:::layer
    L5[Layer 5: Emergency Response]:::layer
    A[Hazard Initiating Event]:::hazard
    ACC[Accident Sequence]:::accident

    A --> L1
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
    L5 --> ACC

    classDef layer fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef hazard fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#7f1d1d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef accident fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 Diagram: HRA Integration with Probabilistic Safety Assessment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INIT[Initiating Event Analysis]
        IE1[Internal Events]
        IE2[External Events]
        IE3[Internal Floods and Fires]
    end

    subgraph HRA[Human Reliability Analysis]
        HR1[Pre InitiEvent Human Error]
        HR2[Post InitiEvent Human Error]
        HR3[Accident Management Human Error]
    end

    subgraph PSA[Probabilistic Safety Assessment]
        PS1[Level 1 PSA: Core Damage Frequency]
        PS2[Level 2 PSA: Release Frequency]
        PS3[Level 3 PSA: Consequence Analysis]
    end

    IE1 --> HR1
    IE2 --> HR2
    IE3 --> HR3
    HR1 --> PS1
    HR2 --> PS1
    HR3 --> PS2

    PS2 --> PS3
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: HRA in a Nuclear Control Room

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENS[Sensor and Detection Layer]
        S1[Process Sensors]
        S2[Radiation Monitors]
        S3[Fire and Gas Detectors]
    end

    subgraph ALARM[Alarm and Annunciation Layer]
        A1[Priority Alarm Processor]
        A2[Human System Interface HSI]
    end

    subgraph OP[Operator Decision Layer HRA Scope]
        O1[Operator Diagnosis]
        O2[Procedure Selection]
        O3[Manual Action Execution]
    end

    subgraph CTRL[Control and Actuation Layer]
        C1[Reactor Protection System]
        C2[Engineered Safety Features]
        C3[Manual ESDV Action]
    end

    S1 --> A1
    S2 --> A1
    S3 --> A1
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
    O2 --> O3
    O3 --> C1
    O3 --> C2
    O3 --> C3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `SENS`, `O1`, `C3`) and labels are kept in clean uppercase text to avoid syntax errors during rendering.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
Define **Human Reliability Analysis (HRA)** and state any **two industries** where it is critically applied.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Human Reliability Analysis (HRA) is a structured engineering methodology used to identify, model, and quantify the probability of human error in complex socio-technical systems. It combines task analysis, human factors principles, and probabilistic modeling to estimate Human Error Probability (HEP) and to recommend improvements in procedures, training, and human-machine interface design.

Two industries where HRA is critically applied are:
1. **Nuclear Power Generation** — for Probabilistic Safety Assessment (PSA) and Severe Accident Management.
2. **Petrochemical and Oil & Gas Industry** — for Safety Integrity Level (SIL) determination and Emergency Shutdown System (ESD) reliability.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two industries — total 3 marks.)*

---

**Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
What is the **Swiss Cheese Model** of accident causation? How does HRA relate to it?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Swiss Cheese Model, developed by James Reason in 1990, is a multi-layered accident causation model in which an organization's defenses against failure are represented as slices of Swiss cheese. Each slice (engineering controls, administrative controls, procedures, human operator, emergency response) has holes representing latent failures and active errors. An accident occurs when the holes in successive layers momentarily align, allowing a hazard to propagate through all defenses.

HRA relates to the Swiss Cheese Model by identifying and sizing the **holes in the human operator layer** (Level 4). HRA quantifies the probability that a human error will allow the hazard to pass through that layer, enabling designers to either redesign procedures, add barriers, or automate tasks to "plug the holes."

*(1.5 marks for model explanation, 1.5 marks for HRA's relationship — total 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

**Question A (14 Marks).** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**(a)** Explain in detail the **THERP (Technique for Human Error Rate and Probability Analysis)** method. List its key steps and discuss its limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A control room operator must perform a complex diagnosis task under severe time pressure during a refinery upset. Using the **HEART method**, calculate the HEP given:
- Generic task type = **Category A** (with $G = 0.16$)
- EPC-1: Time shortage for error detection and correction, multiplier = **11**
- EPC-2: High emotional stress, multiplier = **6**
- EPC-3: Mismatch between operator training and task, multiplier = **5**
- EPC-4: Poor human-system interface, multiplier = **4**

Apply the HEP cap and interpret the result. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) THERP Method — Detailed Explanation (7 Marks):**

THERP, developed by Swain and Guttmann at Sandia National Laboratories in 1983, is one of the earliest and most widely used quantitative HRA methods. It is the standard HRA method endorsed by the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission.

**Key Steps of THERP (7-step procedure):**

1. **Define the System and Problem Scope:** Identify the system, the human-machine interface, and the scenarios of interest. The analyst defines where human action is required.
2. **Identify and Describe Human Tasks:** Use task analysis techniques (link analysis, time-line analysis) to break the system operation into discrete human tasks.
3. **Screen for Human Error Potential:** Identify which tasks have significant error potential — typically those with severe consequences and high probability.
4. **Represent the System in Event Tree Form:** Model the sequence of human actions and their consequences using event trees. Each branch represents success or failure of a sub-task.
5. **Estimate Human Error Probabilities:** Use THERP's empirically derived HEP tables (organized by task type) to assign probabilities to each branch.
6. **Compute the Human Error Probability for the Whole Task:** Multiply or combine the branch probabilities according to Boolean logic.
7. **Sensitivity Analysis and Documentation:** Test how changes in HEP values affect overall system risk, and document assumptions.

**Limitations of THERP:**
- **Operator bias:** THERP assumes a "median" operator, ignoring individual variability, fatigue, and experience.
- **Limited scope for cognitive errors:** THERP handles procedural and skill-based errors well but struggles with diagnosis, decision-making, and rule-based errors.
- **Context insensitivity:** The empirical tables are based on historical data that may not apply to modern digital control rooms.
- **Independence assumption:** THERP assumes human actions are statistically independent, which is often false in real teams.
- **Bounded by SLI (Success Likelihood Index):** The method does not directly incorporate complex Performance Shaping Factors (PSFs) like safety culture.

---

**(b) HEART Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Identify the Generic Task Probability**
The task is Category A (an unfamiliar task performed for the first time with no prior practice), with:
$$
G = 0.16
$$

**Step 2: Identify the EPC Multipliers**

| EPC | Description | Multiplier |
|---|---|---|
| EPC-1 | Time shortage for error detection and correction | 11 |
| EPC-2 | High emotional stress | 6 |
| EPC-3 | Mismatch between training and task | 5 |
| EPC-4 | Poor human-system interface | 4 |

**Step 3: Apply the HEART Formula**

$$
HEP = G \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} (HEP_i)
$$

$$
HEP = 0.16 \times 11 \times 6 \times 5 \times 4
$$

**Step 4: Multiplication Step-by-Step**

$$
HEP = 0.16 \times 11 = 1.76
$$

$$
HEP = 1.76 \times 6 = 10.56
$$

$$
HEP = 10.56 \times 5 = 52.80
$$

$$
HEP = 52.80 \times 4 = 211.20
$$

**Step 5: Apply the HEP Cap (Maximum = 1.0)**

$$
\boxed{HEP = \min(211.20, 1.0) = 1.0}
$$

**Step 6: Interpretation**
The HEP of **1.0** (i.e., 100% failure probability) means that under the combined adverse conditions, the operator is **certain to fail** in this task. This is a strong signal that:
- The task must be **automated** (e.g., install a Safety Instrumented Function with SIL-2 or higher).
- **Procedural redesign** is required (e.g., checklist-based execution).
- **Training** should focus on simulator-based exposure to this exact scenario.
- **Human factors improvements** in the HMI are essential (alarm prioritization, clear feedback).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Forgetting the HEP cap:** Many students report a value greater than 1.0 (such as 211.20) and lose **1 mark**. The HEART method explicitly requires the cap at 1.0.
> - **Incorrect task category:** Choosing the wrong generic HEP (G) value is the most common error. Read the task description carefully and match it to the HEART Category table.
> - **Missing EPCs:** In part (b), there are 4 EPCs, not 3. Forgetting one changes the answer by an order of magnitude.
> - **Not interpreting the result:** KTU board examiners award the last **1–2 marks** for the *engineering interpretation* (i.e., what to do about the HEP = 1.0 result). Students who skip this lose easy marks.
> - **Mixing up THERP and HEART:** In part (a), students sometimes describe HEART features under THERP. Memorize that THERP uses event trees + tables; HEART uses generic tasks + EPC multipliers.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**(a)** Discuss the application of HRA in the **nuclear power industry** and the **petrochemical industry**. Compare the regulatory drivers and the methods used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **CREAM (Cognitive Reliability and Error Analysis Method)** approach. List its **Common Performance Conditions (CPCs)** and explain how they influence the Cognitive Failure Probability (CFP). **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) HRA in Nuclear and Petrochemical Industries (7 Marks):**

| Aspect | Nuclear Power Industry | Petrochemical Industry |
|---|---|---|
| **Regulatory Driver** | U.S. NRC Reg. Guide 1.200; IAEA Safety Standards; AERB (India) | IEC 61511; OSHA 1910.119 (PSM); IS 15656 |
| **Required Output** | Quantitative Human Error Probability for PSA Level 1 & 2 | Safety Integrity Level (SIL) for each Safety Instrumented Function |
| **Primary HRA Method** | THERP + ATHEANA | HEART + LOPA |
| **Typical HEP Range** | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-2}$ (well-trained, slow timescale) | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{0}$ (time-critical ESD actions) |
| **Human Role** | Monitor, diagnose, and execute long-term actions (hours) | Execute fast emergency actions (seconds to minutes) |
| **Accident Examples** | Three Mile Island (1979), Chernobyl (1986), Fukushima (2011) | Bhopal (1984), Texas City Refinery (2005), Deepwater Horizon (2010) |
| **Consequence Type** | Radiation release, societal impact | Toxic release, fire, explosion, loss of life |
| **Defense-in-Depth** | 5-level defense-in-depth (IAEA) | Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA) with 4–6 independent layers |

**Key Insights (for 7 marks):**
- Nuclear HRA emphasizes **quantitative rigor** because of low tolerable risk ($10^{-6}$/year core damage frequency).
- Petrochemical HRA emphasizes **risk-based decision making** to justify investments in safety instrumented systems.
- Both industries recognize the human as the **most variable component** in the safety chain.

---

**(b) CREAM Method — Detailed Explanation (7 Marks):**
